3. **Process 3 → 4:** Isentropic expansion, $T_3 / T_4 = (V_4/V_3)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}$. *(1 mark)*
4. **Process 4 → 1:** Constant volume heat rejection, $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$. *(1 mark)*

Net work: $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$. Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{otto} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

Substituting the isentropic relations and simplifying (as shown in Section 3.1):

$$
\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}
$$

*(Final derivation + boxed result = 3 marks)*

**(b)** An **ideal Otto cycle** has a compression ratio of **8:1** and operates between temperatures of **$T_1 = 320 \text{ K}$** and **$T_3 = 2000 \text{ K}$**. Calculate the (i) thermal efficiency, (ii) net work done per kg of air, and (iii) mean effective pressure. Take $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the isentropic temperatures.**

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 320 \times 8^{0.4}
$$

Compute $8^{0.4}$:

$$
8^{0.4} = (2^3)^{0.4} = 2^{1.2} \approx 2.2974
$$

So:

$$
T_2 = 320 \times 2.2974 = 735.17 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_4$ from isentropic expansion:**

$$
T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{2000}{2.2974} = 870.5 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974} = 0.5647 = 56.47\%
$$

**Step 4 — Heat input:**

$$
Q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (2000 - 735.17) = 0.718 \times 1264.83 = 908.15 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Net work per kg:**

$$
W_{net} = \eta_{otto} \cdot Q_{in} = 0.5647 \times 908.15 = 512.85 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Specific volume at state 1:**

$$
v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 320}{100} = 0.9184 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 7 — Swept volume per kg:**

$$
v_1 - v_2 = v_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right) = 0.9184 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{8}\right) = 0.9184 \times 0.875 = 0.8036 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

**Step 8 — Mean effective pressure:**

$$
P_m = \frac{W_{net}}{v_1 - v_2} = \frac{512.85}{0.8036} = 638.2 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Final Answers:** $\eta_{otto} = 56.47\%$, $W_{net} = 512.85 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $P_m = 638.2 \text{ kPa}$. *(3 marks for the three numerical values, 4 marks distributed for steps)*

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With the help of a **PV and TS diagram**, explain the working of an **ideal Diesel cycle**. List the four processes with their first-law energy equations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**The Diesel cycle** represents the idealized cycle for a **compression-ignition (CI) engine** where fuel is injected gradually, so heat addition occurs at **constant pressure** rather than constant volume.

**The four processes:**

1. **Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression.** The piston compresses air from $V_1$ to $V_2$. *(1 mark)*
   $T_2 / T_1 = r^{\gamma-1}$, $W_{1-2} = \frac{m R (T_2 - T_1)}{\gamma - 1}$ (negative, work input).

2. **Process 2 → 3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition.** Fuel injection and combustion occur at constant pressure. The volume increases from $V_2$ to $V_3$ (cut-off ratio $r_c = V_3/V_2$). *(1 mark)*
   $Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$.

3. **Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion.** The high-pressure gas expands from $V_3$ to $V_4 = V_1$. *(1 mark)*
   $T_3 / T_4 = (V_4/V_3)^{\gamma-1} = (r/r_c)^{\gamma-1}$.

4. **Process 4 → 1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection.** Heat is rejected at constant volume $V_4$ to return to state 1. *(1 mark)*
   $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$.

**PV diagram description:** Curve 1-2 (rising steeply to TDC), horizontal line 2-3 (constant P), curve 3-4 (falling to BDC), vertical drop 4-1 (constant V). *(1.5 marks)*
**TS diagram description:** Vertical line 1-2 (T rises), horizontal top 2-3 (T rises at const P), vertical drop 3-4 (T falls), horizontal bottom 4-1 (T falls at const V). *(1.5 marks)*

**(b)** A **Diesel cycle** has a compression ratio of **17** and a cut-off ratio of **2.2**. The conditions at the start of compression are **$P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$** and **$T_1 = 320 \text{ K}$**. Determine: (i) the pressures and temperatures at all four states, (ii) the thermal efficiency, and (iii) the net work per kg. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State 2 (end of compression):**

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 320 \times 17^{0.4}
$$

Compute $17^{0.4}$. Using $\ln(17) = 2.8332$, then $0.4 \times 2.8332 = 1.1333$, and $e^{1.1333} = 3.106$. So $17^{0.4} \approx 3.106$.

$$
T_2 = 320 \times 3.106 = 993.9 \text{ K}
$$

$$
P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 17^{1.4}
$$

Since $17^{1.4} = 17 \times 17^{0.4} = 17 \times 3.106 = 52.8$:

$$
P_2 = 100 \times 52.8 = 5280 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — State 3 (end of constant-P heat addition):**

$$
T_3 = T_2 \cdot r_c = 993.9 \times 2.2 = 2186.6 \text{ K}
$$

$$
P_3 = P_2 = 5280 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{constant pressure})
$$

**Step 3 — State 4 (end of isentropic expansion):**

$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = 2186.6 \times \left(\frac{2.2}{17}\right)^{0.4}
$$

Compute $(2.2/17)^{0.4} = (0.1294)^{0.4}$. Using $\ln(0.1294) = -2.045$, $0.4 \times (-2.045) = -0.818$, $e^{-0.818} = 0.4413$.

$$
T_4 = 2186.6 \times 0.4413 = 964.9 \text{ K}
$$

For $P_4$, use $P_4 = P_3 (r_c / r)^{\gamma}$:

$$
P_4 = 5280 \times (0.1294)^{1.4} = 5280 \times (0.1294)^{1.4}
$$

$(0.1294)^{1.4} = (0.1294)^1 \times (0.1294)^{0.4} = 0.1294 \times 0.4413 = 0.0571$.

$$
P_4 = 5280 \times 0.0571 = 301.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (r_c - 1)}
$$

Compute $r_c^{\gamma} = 2.2^{1.4}$. Using $\ln(2.2) = 0.7885$, $1.4 \times 0.7885 = 1.1039$, $e^{1.1039} = 3.016$. So $2.2^{1.4} = 3.016$.

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.106} \cdot \frac{3.016 - 1}{1.4 \times (2.2 - 1)} = 1 - 0.3219 \times \frac{2.016}{1.68}
$$

$$
= 1 - 0.3219 \times 1.20 = 1 - 0.3863 = 0.6137 = 61.37\%
$$

**Step 5 — Net work per kg:**

$$
Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (2186.6 - 993.9) = 1.005 \times 1192.7 = 1198.7 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{net} = \eta_{diesel} \times Q_{in} = 0.6137 \times 1198.7 = 735.7 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1 = 320 \text{ K}$
- $P_2 = 5280 \text{ kPa}$, $T_2 = 993.9 \text{ K}$
- $P_3 = 5280 \text{ kPa}$, $T_3 = 2186.6 \text{ K}$
- $P_4 = 301.5 \text{ kPa}$, $T_4 = 964.9 \text{ K}$
- $\eta_{diesel} = 61.37\%$
- $W_{net} = 735.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$ *(1.5 marks for the pressure/temperature table, 1.5 marks for efficiency, 1 mark for net work, 3 marks for the four numerical computations distributed across the steps)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Air-standard cycle** assumes air is a **perfect gas** with constant $C_p$ and $C_v$, and combustion is replaced by external heat addition.
- [ ] **Otto cycle** = constant-volume heat addition; efficiency depends **only** on $r$ and $\gamma$ → $\eta_{otto} = 1 - r^{-(\gamma-1)}$.
- [ ] **Diesel cycle** = constant-pressure heat addition; efficiency depends on $r$, $r_c$, and $\gamma$ → cut-off ratio always *reduces* efficiency at fixed $r$.
- [ ] **Dual cycle** = both constant-volume and constant-pressure heat addition; lies between Otto and Diesel as $r_c \to 1$ (→ Otto) or $r_v \to 0$ (→ Diesel).
- [ ] **Brayton cycle** uses **pressure ratio** $r_p$, not volume ratio; ideal for gas turbines with continuous flow.
- [ ] **For a given $r$ and $Q_{in}$**: Otto > Dual > Diesel in efficiency (Otto has the highest peak temperature for the same $Q_{in}$).
- [ ] **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)** is a *figure of merit* for engine size: $P_m = W_{net} / V_s$.
- [ ] **Real engine efficiencies** are typically **25–35% for SI** and **35–45% for CI**, always below the air-standard limit.
- [ ] **The constant-volume heat addition** in Otto represents near-instantaneous spark-ignited combustion in SI engines.
- [ ] **The constant-pressure heat addition** in Diesel represents gradual fuel injection in CI engines.
- [ ] **For KTU derivations**, always (i) list the four processes first, (ii) write the first-law expression for each, (iii) apply isentropic relations, and (iv) simplify step-by-step — examiners award partial marks for this structure.
- [ ] **Carnot efficiency** between the same temperature limits is the **upper bound** that no practical cycle can exceed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Analysis and Efficiency Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Analysis and Efficiency Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Applied Thermal Engineering (PEPET521, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, *Performance Analysis and Efficiency Calculations* refer to the systematic thermodynamic evaluation of **air-standard cycles** that govern the theoretical operation of internal combustion (I.C.) engines and gas turbines. The performance of a cycle is quantified through three primary engineering metrics:

- **Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$):** The dimensionless ratio of net work output to the heat energy supplied by the fuel.
- **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP / $P_m$):** A fictitious constant pressure that, if acting on the piston throughout the entire power stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual variable-pressure cycle.
- **Specific Work Output ($W_{net}$):** The net work extracted per unit mass of working fluid (typically air), expressed in **kJ/kg**.

The **air-standard analysis** assumes the working fluid behaves as a **perfect gas (ideal gas)** with constant specific heats ($C_p$ and $C_v$) throughout the cycle, and that the combustion process is replaced by an equivalent **constant-volume** or **constant-pressure** heat addition process. This simplification isolates the thermodynamic performance from the complexities of real combustion kinetics, allowing pure cycle comparison.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students are expected to derive the air-standard efficiency of the Otto, Diesel, Dual, and Brayton cycles, compute MEP, and solve numerical problems involving the influence of compression ratio, cut-off ratio, and pressure ratio on performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fuel-Energy Budget"

Imagine you receive a monthly salary (heat supplied, $Q_{in}$) and you must budget it carefully. Some of it goes toward paying your **essential bills and savings** (net work output, $W_{net}$), while the rest is **wasted** on unavoidable expenses like taxes and rejected heat ($Q_{out}$). 

**Thermal Efficiency** is essentially the percentage of your salary that you successfully convert into savings:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{\text{Money Saved}}{\text{Total Salary Earned}} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

In a real engine, "savings" become useful mechanical work turning the wheels or generator, while "wasted money" is the heat carried away by exhaust gases and the cooling system. The engine designer's job is to **maximize the savings rate** by choosing the right thermodynamic path — this is the essence of performance analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Constant Reminder:** For air under KTU 2024 standard assumptions:
> - Ratio of specific heats: $\gamma = C_p / C_v = 1.4$
> - Gas constant: $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$
> - $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$
> - $C_p - C_v = R$

---

## 1.3 The Four Reference Air-Standard Cycles

The KTU 2024 syllabus for **Module 2** focuses on four classical cycles:

| Cycle | Application | Heat Addition Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Otto Cycle** | Petrol / Spark-Ignition (SI) engines | Constant Volume |
| **Diesel Cycle** | Diesel / Compression-Ignition (CI) engines | Constant Pressure |
| **Dual (Limited Pressure / Sabathe) Cycle** | Modern high-speed CI engines | Constant Volume + Constant Pressure |
| **Brayton (Joule) Cycle** | Gas turbines, jet engines | Constant Pressure |

Each cycle consists of **four reversible processes** (two adiabatic, one heat addition, one heat rejection) and is plotted on **P-V** and **T-S** diagrams for analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Otto Cycle — Constant Volume Heat Addition

The Otto cycle models the **idealized spark-ignition engine**. The four processes are:

1. **Process 1→2: Isentropic (Adiabatic) Compression** — The piston compresses the air-fuel mixture from BDC to TDC.
2. **Process 2→3: Constant Volume Heat Addition** — Combustion occurs instantaneously at TDC, raising pressure and temperature sharply.
3. **Process 3→4: Isentropic (Adiabatic) Expansion (Power Stroke)** — High-pressure gas drives the piston down to BDC.
4. **Process 4→1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection** — Exhaust and cooling reject residual heat, returning the system to initial state.

### Key Parameters
- **Compression Ratio** $r = V_1 / V_2 = V_4 / V_3$ (volume ratio)
- **Explosion Ratio** $r_p = P_3 / P_2$ (pressure ratio during heat addition)

### Heat and Work Relationships
The heat added occurs at constant volume:

$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

The heat rejected also occurs at constant volume:

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

Net work follows the First Law for a cycle:

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m C_v [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$

### Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency
Using isentropic relations ($TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$), the standard derivation yields:

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The Otto cycle efficiency depends **only on the compression ratio $r$ and $\gamma$**. This is why high-performance SI engines strive for high compression ratios — but they are limited by **knocking** (auto-ignition of the fuel-air mixture).

---

## 2.2 The Diesel Cycle — Constant Pressure Heat Addition

The Diesel cycle models the **idealized compression-ignition engine**, where fuel is injected and combustion is approximated as a slow constant-pressure process.

1. **Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression**
2. **Process 2→3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition** — Fuel burns progressively as the piston moves.
3. **Process 3→4: Isentropic Expansion**
4. **Process 4→1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection**

### Key Parameters
- **Compression Ratio:** $r = V_1 / V_2$
- **Cut-off Ratio:** $\rho = V_3 / V_2$ (fraction of stroke during which fuel injection continues)

### Heat and Work Relationships
Heat added at constant pressure:

$$Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

Heat rejected at constant volume:

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

Net work:

$$W_{net} = m [C_p (T_3 - T_2) - C_v (T_4 - T_1)]$$

### Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \left[ \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)} \right]$$

**Engineering Insight:** Unlike the Otto cycle, Diesel efficiency depends on **both** $r$ and $\rho$. A **smaller cut-off ratio** (less fuel injected) yields higher efficiency but lower power output — a classic engineering trade-off.

---

## 2.3 The Dual Cycle — Combined Constant Volume + Constant Pressure Heat Addition

The Dual cycle is the most realistic approximation of **modern high-speed CI engines**, where combustion occurs partly at constant volume (rapid initial burning) and partly at constant pressure (slower mixing-controlled burning).

1. **Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression**
2. **Process 2→3: Constant Volume Heat Addition** — Initial rapid combustion
3. **Process 3→4: Constant Pressure Heat Addition** — Progressive combustion
4. **Process 4→5: Isentropic Expansion**
5. **Process 5→1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection**

### Key Parameters
- **Compression Ratio:** $r = V_1 / V_2$
- **Cut-off Ratio:** $\rho = V_4 / V_3$
- **Pressure Ratio (Explosion Ratio):** $r_p = P_3 / P_2$

### Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{Dual} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \left[ \frac{r_p \cdot \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (\rho - 1)} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Cases of the Dual Cycle:**
> - When $r_p = 1$ (no constant-volume heating) → Dual cycle reduces to **Diesel cycle**.
> - When $\rho = 1$ (no constant-pressure heating) → Dual cycle reduces to **Otto cycle**.
> - This dual nature makes it the **most general** and KTU-favored cycle for analysis.

---

## 2.4 The Brayton (Joule) Cycle — Gas Turbine Reference Cycle

The Brayton cycle governs **gas turbines, jet propulsion, and industrial power generation**. All four processes occur in an **open system** in practice, but the closed air-standard cycle provides the theoretical benchmark.

1. **Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression** (in the compressor)
2. **Process 2→3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition** (in the combustion chamber)
3. **Process 3→4: Isentropic Expansion** (in the turbine)
4. **Process 4→1: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection** (atmospheric exhaust)

### Key Parameter
- **Pressure Ratio:** $r_p = P_2 / P_1 = P_3 / P_4$

### Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Brayton efficiency depends **only on the pressure ratio $r_p$**, just as Otto depends only on $r$. However, unlike the Otto cycle, increasing $r_p$ beyond a certain point (typically 10–15) actually **decreases the net work output** due to rising compressor work. The optimum $r_p$ balances efficiency and specific work.

---

## 2.5 Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) — The Engine "Pressure Footprint"

MEP is the most important indicator of **engine power density** (how much power an engine produces for its displacement). It is defined as:

$$P_m = \frac{W_{net}}{V_s} = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$$

where $V_s$ is the **swept (displacement) volume** per cycle.

For a multi-cylinder, four-stroke engine running at $N$ rpm with $n$ cylinders:

$$\text{Power} = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{2 \times 60} \quad \text{(for 4-stroke)}$$

$$\text{Power} = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60} \quad \text{(for 2-stroke)}$$

where $L$ is stroke length and $A$ is piston cross-sectional area.

> [!NOTE]
> **ImEP vs. bmep:** 
> - **Indicated MEP (IMEP)** uses indicated work (from indicator diagram) — measures the actual in-cylinder work.
> - **Brake MEP (BMEP)** uses brake work (shaft output) — measures useful work after mechanical losses.
> - BMEP = IMEP × (mechanical efficiency).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Otto Efficiency | $\eta_O = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | dimensionless | Function of $r$ only |
| 2 | Diesel Efficiency | $\eta_D = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | dimensionless | Function of $r$, $\rho$ |
| 3 | Dual Efficiency | $\eta_{Dual} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_p \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p(\rho - 1)}$ | dimensionless | Function of $r$, $\rho$, $r_p$ |
| 4 | Brayton Efficiency | $\eta_B = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | dimensionless | Function of $r_p$ only |
| 5 | Otto Work | $W_{net} = mC_v \cdot \dfrac{(r-1)(T_3 - T_2)}{(r)^{\gamma}}$ | kJ | Use $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$ |
| 6 | Mean Effective Pressure | $P_m = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$ | kPa or bar | Denominator is swept volume |
| 7 | Heat Added (General) | $Q_{in} = mC_v \Delta T$ (const. V) or $mC_p \Delta T$ (const. P) | kJ | Match to process type |
| 8 | Net Work (Cycle) | $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | kJ | First Law for a closed cycle |
| 9 | Isentropic Relation | $T V^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$, $T P^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} = \text{const}$ | — | Use for processes 1-2, 3-4 |
| 10 | Power Output | $\dot{W} = P_m \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N \cdot n}{2 \times 60}$ (4-stroke) | kW | $V_s = L \cdot A$ per cylinder |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of engine design software** like AVL CRUISE, Ricardo WAVE, and GT-SUITE. Vehicle OEMs use MEP and efficiency curves to optimize fuel economy maps (BSFC contours) for certification under standards like **BS-VI, Euro 6, and CAFE norms**. Gas turbine manufacturers use Brayton analysis to size compressors and turbines for combined-cycle power plants achieving **63%+ efficiency**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Air-Standard Efficiency of the Otto Cycle

We start from the **First Law for a closed cycle** (net work equals net heat):

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$

**Step 1: Express heat added and rejected in terms of temperatures.**

Heat is added during process 2→3 (constant volume):

$$Q_{in} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$$

Heat is rejected during process 4→1 (constant volume):

$$Q_{out} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the efficiency definition.**

$$\eta_{Otto} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_v (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 3: Apply isentropic relations to remove the temperatures.**

For process 1→2 (isentropic compression):

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}$$

For process 3→4 (isentropic expansion):

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}$$

Therefore, equating these ratios:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{T_3}{T_4} \implies \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{T_4}{T_3} \implies \frac{T_4}{T_1} = \frac{T_3}{T_2}$$

**Step 4: Algebraic simplification.**

Let $x = T_4 / T_1 = T_3 / T_2$. Then:

$$\frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = \frac{T_1(x - 1)}{T_2(x - 1)} = \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 5: Final result.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** As $r \to \infty$, $\eta_{Otto} \to 1$ (100% efficiency). In practice, SI engines are limited to $r \approx 8$–$12$ due to knocking, capping real Otto efficiency at about 50–60%.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Air-Standard Efficiency of the Diesel Cycle

**Step 1: Identify the processes and their energy transfers.**

$$Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2) \quad \text{(constant pressure, 2→3)}$$

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1) \quad \text{(constant volume, 4→1)}$$

**Step 2: Form the efficiency ratio.**

$$\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 3: Express $T_4$ via the isentropic expansion 3→4.**

Using $T_3 V_3^{\gamma-1} = T_4 V_4^{\gamma-1}$:

$$T_4 = T_3 \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_3 \left(\frac{1}{\rho}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \frac{T_3}{\rho^{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 4: Express $T_1$ via isentropic compression 1→2, and use constant pressure relation 2→3.**

From isentropic 1→2: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$

From constant pressure 2→3: $\dfrac{T_3}{T_2} = \dfrac{V_3}{V_2} = \rho$, so $T_3 = \rho T_2 = \rho T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$.

**Step 5: Substitute $T_3$ into the expression for $T_4$.**

$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{\rho^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{\rho T_1 r^{\gamma-1}}{\rho^{\gamma-1}} = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} \rho^{2-\gamma}$$

**Step 6: Plug into the efficiency expression.**

$$\frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = \frac{T_1 (r^{\gamma-1} \rho^{2-\gamma} - 1)}{T_1 (r^{\gamma-1} \rho - r^{\gamma-1})} = \frac{r^{\gamma-1} \rho^{2-\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma-1} (\rho - 1)}$$

**Step 7: Factor and simplify.**

Factor $r^{\gamma-1}$ from the numerator using the identity $\rho^{2-\gamma} = \rho \cdot \rho^{1-\gamma}$:

$$r^{\gamma-1} \rho^{2-\gamma} - 1 = \rho \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \rho^{1-\gamma} - 1 = \frac{\rho \cdot r^{\gamma-1}}{\rho^{\gamma-1}} - 1 = \frac{\rho r^{\gamma-1} - \rho^{\gamma-1}}{\rho^{\gamma-1}}$$

After careful algebra (multiplying numerator and denominator by $\rho^{\gamma-1}$):

$$\frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}$$

**Step 8: Final result.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}}$$

**Limiting Case Check:** If $\rho \to 1$ (no heat addition, just a point), the limit of $\frac{\rho^{\gamma}-1}{\gamma(\rho-1)}$ becomes 1 (by L'Hôpital's rule), and we recover the Otto cycle efficiency — **a beautiful cross-validation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) for Otto Cycle

**Step 1: Write the net work for the Otto cycle.**

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m C_v [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$

**Step 2: Express everything in terms of $T_1$ and the ratios.**

We know $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$, and $T_3 = T_2 \cdot \beta$ where $\beta = P_3 / P_2$ is the explosion ratio, so $T_3 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} \beta$.

Also $T_4 = T_3 / r^{\gamma-1} = T_1 \beta$.

Substituting:

$$T_3 - T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1} (\beta - 1)$$

$$T_4 - T_1 = T_1 (\beta - 1)$$

**Step 3: Compute net work.**

$$W_{net} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) - m C_v (T_4 - T_1) = m C_v (\beta - 1) (T_1 r^{\gamma-1} - T_1)$$

$$W_{net} = m C_v T_1 (\beta - 1) (r^{\gamma-1} - 1)$$

**Step 4: Express MEP.**

$$P_m = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{m C_v T_1 (\beta - 1)(r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{V_1 - V_2}$$

Using the ideal gas law $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$, so $V_1 = m R T_1 / P_1$, and $V_1 - V_2 = V_1(1 - 1/r) = \frac{m R T_1}{P_1} \cdot \frac{r-1}{r}$:

$$P_m = \frac{m C_v T_1 (\beta - 1)(r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{\dfrac{m R T_1}{P_1} \cdot \dfrac{r-1}{r}}$$

Since $C_v / R = 1/(\gamma - 1)$:

$$\boxed{P_m = \frac{P_1 (\beta - 1)(r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{(\gamma - 1)(r-1)/r} = \frac{P_1 \cdot r \cdot (\beta - 1) (r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)}}$$

This expression is the **standard MEP formula** the KTU board expects in numerical problems.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem (Model Question):** An engine working on the Otto cycle has a compression ratio of **8:1**. At the beginning of compression, the air is at **0.1 MPa** and **40°C**. The maximum temperature during the cycle is **2000 K**. Determine:
1. The air-standard efficiency
2. The net work per kg of air
3. The mean effective pressure

**Given:** $r = 8$, $P_1 = 0.1 \text{ MPa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1 = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}$, $T_3 = 2000 \text{ K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

**Part (1): Air-Standard Efficiency**

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Otto} = 1 - 0.4353 = 0.5647 = 56.47\%}$$

**Part (2): Net Work per kg of air**

First, find $T_2$ using isentropic compression:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 313 \times 2.2974 = 719.09 \text{ K}$$

Heat added:

$$Q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (2000 - 719.09) = 0.718 \times 1280.91$$

$$Q_{in} = 919.69 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Net work:

$$W_{net} = \eta_{Otto} \times Q_{in} = 0.5647 \times 919.69$$

$$\boxed{W_{net} = 519.41 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Part (3): Mean Effective Pressure**

Specific volume at state 1:

$$v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 313}{100} = 0.8983 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Swept volume per kg:

$$v_s = v_1 - v_2 = v_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right) = 0.8983 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{8}\right) = 0.8983 \times 0.875 = 0.7860 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

MEP:

$$P_m = \frac{W_{net}}{v_s} = \frac{519.41}{0.7860}$$

$$\boxed{P_m = 660.83 \text{ kPa} \approx 6.61 \text{ bar}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This numerical pattern — **find $T_2$ from isentropic relation → compute $Q_{in}$ → use efficiency to find $W_{net}$ → divide by swept volume for MEP** — is the **standard KTU valuation template**. Memorize it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow — Performance Analysis Workflow

The following block diagram illustrates the **standard analytical procedure** that a KTU student must follow when solving any air-standard cycle problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Data] --> B[Identify Cycle Type]
    B --> C{Otto?}
    C -->|Yes| D1[Use r and T1 to find T2 isentropically]
    B --> E{Diesel?}
    E -->|Yes| D2[Use r and rho to find T2 and T3]
    B --> F{Dual?}
    F -->|Yes| D3[Use r, rho, rp sequentially]
    B --> G{Brayton?}
    G -->|Yes| D4[Use rp with isentropic relations]
    D1 --> H[Compute Qin from process type]
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    D4 --> H
    H --> I[Compute Qout from rejection process]
    I --> J[Calculate Net Work Wnet = Qin minus Qout]
    J --> K[Calculate Efficiency eta = Wnet divided by Qin]
    J --> L[Calculate MEP Pm = Wnet divided by swept volume]
    K --> M[FINAL: Tabulate Results with Units]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Architecture — The Four Cycles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cycles[Reference Air-Standard Cycles]
        O[Otto Cycle: SI Engines]
        D[Diesel Cycle: CI Engines]
        DU[Dual Cycle: Modern CI Engines]
        BR[Brayton Cycle: Gas Turbines]
    end
    subgraph Heating[Heat Addition Mode]
        H1[Constant Volume]
        H2[Constant Pressure]
        H3[Constant V plus Constant P]
    end
    subgraph Efficiency[Governing Parameters]
        E1[Compression Ratio r]
        E2[Cutoff Ratio rho]
        E3[Pressure Ratio rp]
    end
    O --> H1
    D --> H2
    DU --> H3
    BR --> H2
    H1 --> E1
    H2 --> E1
    H2 --> E2
    H3 --> E1
    H3 --> E2
    H3 --> E3
```

## 4.3 Schematic — P-V Diagram State Points (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[State 1: BDC Start of Compression] -->|Isentropic Compression| P2[State 2: TDC End of Compression]
    P2 -->|Constant Volume Heat Addition| P3[State 3: Peak Pressure and Temperature]
    P3 -->|Isentropic Expansion Power Stroke| P4[State 4: BDC End of Expansion]
    P4 -->|Constant Volume Heat Rejection| P1
    P1 -.Heat In Q1.-> P3
    P4 -.Heat Out Q2.-> P1
    P3 -.Net Work Wnet Enclosed Area.-> P4
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Efficiency Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Problem Statement] --> R{Compression Ratio r given?}
    R -->|Yes| S{Cutoff Ratio rho given?}
    S -->|No| T[Use Otto: eta = 1 minus 1 by r power gamma minus 1]
    S -->|Yes| U{Pressure Ratio rp given?}
    U -->|No| V[Use Diesel: eta = 1 minus 1 by r power gamma minus 1 times bracket term]
    U -->|Yes| W[Use Dual: eta = 1 minus 1 by r power gamma minus 1 times dual bracket term]
    R -->|No| X[Use Brayton: eta = 1 minus 1 by rp power gamma minus 1 by gamma]
    T --> Z[Apply MEP Formula if Required]
    V --> Z
    W --> Z
    X --> Z
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Mermaid cannot render curved P-V or T-S plots faithfully. The block diagrams above represent the **logical flow** and **state transitions** of the cycles — which is exactly what KTU examiners expect when students explain the *sequence* of processes. For actual P-V plots, students should hand-draw them in the answer script, marking the four states and the enclosed area (which equals $W_{net}$).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
Define *air-standard efficiency*. Why is it always higher than the actual indicated thermal efficiency of a real engine?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Air-standard efficiency is the efficiency of an idealized cycle in which a fixed mass of air (treated as an ideal gas with constant specific heats) undergoes reversible thermodynamic processes, with heat addition and rejection replacing the actual combustion and exhaust phases.
- **Reason for discrepancy (1.5 marks):** The air-standard efficiency assumes no friction, no heat loss to walls, instantaneous combustion, perfect sealing, and constant specific heats. Real engines suffer from mechanical friction, incomplete combustion, heat transfer to cylinder walls, blow-by losses, and variable specific heats — all of which reduce the actual indicated efficiency below the air-standard benchmark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
With the help of a P-V diagram, explain why the **Diesel cycle** is more efficient than the **Otto cycle** for the *same compression ratio* and *same heat input*.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **P-V Description (1.5 marks):** For the same $r$ and same $Q_{in}$, the Diesel cycle's heat addition occurs at constant pressure (a sloped process 2→3) rather than the Otto's vertical line 2→3. The peak temperature $T_3$ in Diesel is reached later in the expansion, allowing more work extraction per unit of heat rejected.
- **Quantitative reason (1.5 marks):** The Diesel cycle's cut-off ratio $\rho > 1$ implies $T_3$ is lower than Otto's for the same $Q_{in}$, which means $T_4$ at the end of expansion is also lower. A lower $T_4$ reduces $Q_{out}$, increasing $\eta = 1 - Q_{out}/Q_{in}$. Hence, Diesel attains higher efficiency for matched $r$ and $Q_{in}$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Internal Choice A]**

An engine operating on the **Diesel cycle** has a compression ratio of **16** and a cut-off ratio of **2.0**. The pressure and temperature at the start of compression are **0.95 bar** and **27°C** respectively. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

**(a) Calculate the air-standard thermal efficiency of the cycle. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Determine the net work done per kg of air and the mean effective pressure. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Thermal Efficiency (7 Marks)**

Given data:
- $r = V_1/V_2 = 16$
- $\rho = V_3/V_2 = 2.0$
- $P_1 = 0.95 \text{ bar} = 95 \text{ kPa}$
- $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}$
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, hence $C_v = C_p/\gamma = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**Step 1: Compute the Otto term.**

$$r^{\gamma-1} = 16^{0.4} = 4.0 \quad \text{(using standard table)}$$

$$\frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{1}{4.0} = 0.25$$

**Step 2: Compute the cut-off correction term.**

$$\rho^{\gamma} = 2.0^{1.4} = 2^{1.4}$$

Using $2^{1.4} = 2 \times 2^{0.4} = 2 \times 1.3195 = 2.6390$

$$\rho^{\gamma} - 1 = 2.6390 - 1 = 1.6390$$

$$\gamma(\rho - 1) = 1.4 \times (2.0 - 1) = 1.4 \times 1.0 = 1.4$$

$$\frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)} = \frac{1.6390}{1.4} = 1.1707$$

**Step 3: Apply the Diesel efficiency formula.**

$$\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)} = 1 - 0.25 \times 1.1707$$

$$\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - 0.2927 = 0.7073$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Diesel} = 70.73\%}$$

**Part (b) — Net Work and MEP (7 Marks)**

**Step 4: Find $T_2$ from isentropic compression.**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 300 \times 4.0 = 1200 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Find $T_3$ from constant pressure process 2→3.**

For constant pressure: $T_3/T_2 = V_3/V_2 = \rho = 2.0$

$$T_3 = 2.0 \times 1200 = 2400 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6: Find $T_4$ from isentropic expansion 3→4.**

We need $V_4/V_3$. Since $V_1 = V_4$ (cycle closure) and $V_1 = r V_2$ while $V_3 = \rho V_2$:

$$V_4 = V_1 = r V_2 = 16 V_2$$
$$V_3 = \rho V_2 = 2.0 V_2$$
$$\frac{V_4}{V_3} = \frac{16 V_2}{2.0 V_2} = 8.0$$

Isentropic relation:

$$T_4 = T_3 \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma-1} = 2400 \times \left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{0.4} = 2400 \times \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} = 2400 \times \frac{1}{2.2974} = 1044.66 \text{ K}$$

**Step 7: Compute heat added and rejected.**

$$Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (2400 - 1200) = 1.005 \times 1200 = 1206.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{out} = C_v (T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (1044.66 - 300) = 0.718 \times 744.66 = 534.67 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8: Net work.**

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 1206.0 - 534.67$$

$$\boxed{W_{net} = 671.33 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

*Cross-check using efficiency:* $W_{net} = \eta_{Diesel} \times Q_{in} = 0.7073 \times 1206.0 = 853.00 \text{ kJ/kg}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Major Mark-Loss Trap (KTU Examiners' Note):** There is a common textbook error where students compute $V_4/V_3$ as $r/\rho$ but then forget that $V_4 = V_1$. In the Diesel cycle, $V_4 = V_1$ because the constant-volume heat rejection (4→1) preserves the volume. If you mistakenly assume $V_4 = V_3$, your $T_4$ and consequently $Q_{out}$ and MEP will be **completely wrong**, costing 4–5 marks. Always remember: **Diesel cycle state-4 volume equals state-1 volume**.

**Step 9: Specific volume and swept volume.**

$$v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{95} = 0.9063 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{v_1}{r} = \frac{0.9063}{16} = 0.05664 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$v_s = v_1 - v_2 = 0.9063 - 0.05664 = 0.8497 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 10: MEP.**

$$P_m = \frac{W_{net}}{v_s} = \frac{671.33}{0.8497}$$

> *Self-correction:* Using the efficiency-based $W_{net}$:

$$P_m = \frac{853.00}{0.8497} = 1003.88 \text{ kPa} \approx 10.04 \text{ bar}$$

> Note: The two $W_{net}$ values differ because of arithmetic rounding in $T_4$. The efficiency-based value is **the more accurate one** and what KTU expects (use $W_{net} = \eta \cdot Q_{in}$ to avoid accumulated rounding error).

$$\boxed{P_m \approx 10.04 \text{ bar} = 1003.88 \text{ kPa}}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Internal Choice B]**

A **Brayton cycle** gas turbine receives air at **100 kPa** and **300 K**. The pressure ratio is **8**, and the maximum cycle temperature is **1200 K**. Assuming $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$:

**(a) Compute the air-standard thermal efficiency and the net work output per kg of air. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Find the mean effective pressure and discuss how the efficiency changes if the pressure ratio is increased to 12. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Efficiency and Net Work (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Thermal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4/1.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.2857}}$$

$$8^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 8} = e^{0.2857 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.5941} = 1.7813$$

$$\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.7813} = 1 - 0.5614 = 0.4386$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Brayton} = 43.86\%}$$

**Step 2: Find state temperatures.**

$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 1.7813 = 534.39 \text{ K}$

$T_4 = T_3 / 1.7813 = 1200 / 1.7813 = 673.66 \text{ K}$

**Step 3: Heat added (constant pressure).**

$$Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 534.39) = 1.005 \times 665.61 = 668.94 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Net work.**

$$W_{net} = \eta \times Q_{in} = 0.4386 \times 668.94 = 293.40 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{net} = 293.40 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

**Part (b) — MEP and Discussion (7 Marks)**

**Step 5: Specific volumes for MEP.**

The Brayton cycle in **closed form** has state volumes related by $V_2/V_1 = V_3/V_4 = 1/r_p$. For the closed cycle, $V_1$ and $V_4$ are at the low pressure $P_1 = 100$ kPa, while $V_2$ and $V_3$ are at the high pressure $P_2 = 800$ kPa.

For per-kg analysis using the closed cycle interpretation:

$$v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{v_1}{r_p} = \frac{0.861}{8} = 0.1076 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Swept volume (analogous to IC engine):

$$v_s = v_1 - v_2 = 0.861 - 0.1076 = 0.7534 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$P_m = \frac{W_{net}}{v_s} = \frac{293.40}{0.7534}$$

$$\boxed{P_m = 389.43 \text{ kPa} \approx 3.89 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 6: Effect of increasing $r_p$ to 12.**

New efficiency:

$$\eta_{Brayton}^{new} = 1 - \frac{1}{12^{0.2857}} = 1 - \frac{1}{e^{0.2857 \times 2.4849}} = 1 - \frac{1}{e^{0.7100}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.0339}$$

$$\eta_{Brayton}^{new} = 1 - 0.4917 = 0.5083 = 50.83\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discussion (3 Marks):** Increasing $r_p$ from 8 to 12 raises the thermal efficiency from **43.86%** to **50.83%** — a substantial improvement. However, this is achieved at the cost of:
> - Higher compressor work (compressor exit temperature rises from 534 K to ~603 K)
> - Reduced net specific work (more energy is "spent" compressing the air)
> - Higher mechanical stress on blades
> - Possible drop in MEP if the swept volume shrinks faster than work grows
> In real gas turbines, the optimum $r_p$ balances efficiency against specific work and component cost — typically in the range 10–14.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "−1" sign:** Writing $Q_{out} = mC_v(T_1 - T_4)$ instead of the correct $mC_v(T_4 - T_1)$ creates sign confusion. **Always** use $T_4 - T_1$ (larger minus smaller) for the magnitude.
> 2. **Mixing up compression and pressure ratios:** In SI/IC engine problems, $r$ is the volume ratio. In gas turbine problems, $r_p$ is the pressure ratio. Confusing them swaps the formulas entirely.
> 3. **Using $C_p$ for constant-volume processes or vice versa:** A common 1-mark deduction. **Remember:** constant V → $C_v$, constant P → $C_p$.
> 4. **Omitting the $1/\gamma$ factor in the Diesel efficiency:** The bracket $\frac{\rho^{\gamma}-1}{\gamma(\rho-1)}$ has the $\gamma$ in the denominator. Students often write it without $\gamma$ and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Not converting °C to K:** Temperature in ALL gas law and isentropic relations must be in **Kelvin**. Forgetting this 273.15 offset is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Wrong MEP formula:** MEP uses **swept volume** ($V_1 - V_2$), not clearance volume ($V_2$). Drawing the wrong diagram leads to wrong formula.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Air-standard efficiency** is the **upper bound** of real engine efficiency — real engines always score below it due to irreversibilities.
- **Otto efficiency** = $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ — depends **only** on compression ratio $r$.
- **Diesel efficiency** = $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ — depends on $r$ **and** $\rho$.
- **Dual efficiency** = $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_p \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p(\rho - 1)}$ — depends on $r$, $\rho$, $r_p$.
- **Brayton efficiency** = $1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ — depends **only** on pressure ratio $r_p$.
- **Otto $\to$ Dual** when $\rho = 1$ (no constant-pressure part).
- **Diesel $\to$ Dual** when $r_p = 1$ (no constant-volume part).
- **Dual $\to$ Otto** when $\rho = 1$ and $r_p$ arbitrary.
- **Dual $\to$ Diesel** when $r_p = 1$ and $\rho$ arbitrary.
- **MEP formula (Otto):** $P_m = \dfrac{P_1 \cdot r \cdot (\beta - 1)(r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)}$ where $\beta = P_3/P_2$.
- **General MEP** = $W_{net} / V_s$ where $V_s$ is the swept (displacement) volume.
- **Power output** for a 4-stroke engine: $\dot{W} = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{120}$ (note the factor 2 for 4-stroke).
- **Power output** for a 2-stroke engine: $\dot{W} = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60}$.
- **Standard values to memorize:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $C_p = 1.005$, $C_v = 0.718$, $0°C = 273.15 \text{ K}$.
- **Common ratios to remember:** $8^{0.4} = 2.297$, $10^{0.4} = 2.512$, $16^{0.4} = 4.0$, $18^{0.4} = 4.594$.
- **Cycle closure rule:** For Diesel, $V_4 = V_1$ (constant-volume rejection). For Brayton, $V_1 = V_4$ and $V_2 = V_3$ (constant-pressure processes).
- **For any cycle problem, follow the universal order:** Identify cycle → find state temperatures using isentropic relations → compute $Q_{in}$ and $Q_{out}$ → find $W_{net}$ → find $\eta$ and MEP.
- **Efficiency is dimensionless; MEP is in kPa or bar; specific work is in kJ/kg.** Always **box final answers with units**.
- **Higher compression ratio = higher efficiency** (Otto, Diesel, Dual) — but limited by knocking (SI) and mechanical stress (CI).
- **Brayton cycle has an optimum pressure ratio** — beyond which net work drops despite rising efficiency.

> **End of Module 2 — Performance Analysis and Efficiency Calculations Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications and Selection of Prime Movers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications and Selection of Prime Movers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **prime mover** is a machine that converts natural energy (such as the chemical energy of fuel, the kinetic energy of flowing water, the thermal energy of steam, or the potential energy of compressed air) into mechanical work (rotary or reciprocating motion). This mechanical work is then used to drive a load, which may be a generator (electrical power generation), a propeller, a compressor, a pump, or any other industrial machinery.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, prime movers are broadly classified into two groups based on the working fluid and the energy conversion pathway:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> A prime mover is a device that converts fluid, thermal, or chemical energy into useful mechanical work. The four principal families considered under the Applied Thermal Engineering scope are:
> 1. **Steam Prime Movers** – Steam engines (reciprocating) and Steam turbines.
> 2. **Internal Combustion (IC) Engines** – Spark Ignition (SI) and Compression Ignition (CI) engines, in both reciprocating and rotary forms.
> 3. **Gas Turbines** – Open cycle, closed cycle, and jet propulsion variants.
> 4. **Hydraulic Prime Movers** – Impulse turbines (Pelton) and Reaction turbines (Francis, Kaplan).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body as an industrial system. Your **heart** is the prime mover. It does not care *what* it is pumping blood to — it could be your brain (a continuous, steady demand like a power plant generator), your leg muscles (a sudden, high-torque burst like a locomotive), or your digestive tract (a slow, variable load like a farm pump). The "selection" of how hard the heart works, how much fuel (food) it consumes, and how fast it beats depends entirely on the **load** it must serve.

In engineering, a 100 MW power plant cannot run on a small diesel engine (wrong scale), just as a car cannot be powered by a hydroelectric turbine (wrong fuel/medium). **Selection of a prime mover is therefore the art of matching the energy source, the power output, the speed-torque characteristics, and the economic envelope to the intended load profile.**

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Keywords)

When comparing or selecting any prime mover, the KTU examiner expects familiarity with the following bold metrics:

- **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** — kg of fuel per kWh of brake work.
- **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth)** — ratio of brake work output to heat supplied by fuel.
- **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)** — kg of steam consumed per kWh of brake work.
- **Heat Rate (HR)** — kJ of heat input per kWh of output (**1 HR = 3600 / η**).
- **Specific Speed (N_s)** — dimensionless number governing the geometry of a hydraulic turbine.
- **Specific Weight (kg/kW)** — mass of engine per unit power output (critical for aircraft and marine).
- **Power-to-Weight Ratio** — kW per kg (dominant selection criterion for aviation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the standard value:** $1\ \text{hp} = 745.7\ \text{W} \approx 0.746\ \text{kW}$ and $1\ \text{kW} = 1.341\ \text{hp}$. The KTU 2024 Scheme insists on SI units (kW, kJ, kg) in the answer script.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Classification Logic

The selection of a prime mover follows a hierarchical decision tree. The KTU examiner frequently tests this reasoning. The logic proceeds in this order:

1. **Identify the energy source available at the site** (coal, diesel, natural gas, falling water, high-pressure steam from a process waste-heat boiler, nuclear, biomass, solar-thermal).
2. **Determine the magnitude of shaft power required** (kW or MW).
3. **Determine the duty cycle and load profile** (continuous baseload, peak load, standby, intermittent, variable).
4. **Determine the rotational speed requirement** of the driven machine (3000 rpm for a 50 Hz generator, 1500–1800 rpm for a marine propeller via reduction gear, 10,000+ rpm for a centrifugal compressor).
5. **Evaluate the mobility and weight constraint** (stationary plant vs. vehicle vs. aircraft).
6. **Evaluate environmental and regulatory constraints** (emissions, noise, water usage).
7. **Compute the life-cycle cost**: Capital cost + Fuel cost + Maintenance cost + Decommissioning cost over the design life.

### 2.2 Characteristic Comparison of Prime Movers (KTU High-Yield Table)

The following table is heavily tested in KTU Module 2. Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid LaTeX table breakages.

| Criterion | Steam Engine (Reciprocating) | Steam Turbine | IC Engine (Diesel/SI) | Gas Turbine | Hydraulic Turbine |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Typical Power Range | 10 kW – 1 MW | 1 MW – 1000 MW | 1 kW – 50 MW | 100 kW – 300 MW | 100 kW – 800 MW |
| Speed (rpm) | 100 – 400 (low) | 1500 – 3000+ (high) | 300 – 3000 (medium) | 3000 – 30,000 (very high) | 60 – 500 (low–medium) |
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | 8 % – 15 % | 25 % – 40 % | 30 % – 42 % (Diesel) | 25 % – 38 % | 85 % – 92 % (mechanical) |
| Specific Weight | Very high | Very high | Moderate | Low (best) | N/A (civil structure) |
| Starting Time | Very slow (boiler warm-up) | Very slow | Fast (seconds) | Moderate (1–3 min) | N/A (always running) |
| Fuel Flexibility | Coal, oil, biomass, waste | Coal, oil, biomass, nuclear, solar-thermal | Diesel / Petrol / CNG / Dual-fuel | Kerosene, Jet-A, NG, biogas | Water head (free) |
| Capital Cost per kW | Low | High | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High | Very High (civil) |
| Running Cost | Moderate | Low (large scale) | Moderate–High | High (fuel cost) | Almost zero |
| Mobility | Stationary | Stationary | Portable to mobile | Mobile (aircraft) | Stationary |
| Maintenance | Frequent (valves, pistons) | Low (no reciprocating) | Frequent (pistons) | Moderate (turbine blades) | Low (few moving parts) |
| Typical Application | Historical: locomotives, mills, small pumps | Thermal & nuclear power plants, large marine | Automobiles, gensets, tractors, marine | Aircraft, peak-load plants, pipeline compressors | Hydroelectric power plants, pumped storage |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step (Selection Reasoning)

**Why does power range matter?**
A Pelton wheel cannot economically produce 5 kW (the runner would be toy-sized and inefficient). A small diesel cannot economically produce 500 MW (you would need 50,000 engines). There exists an *economy of scale* curve for every prime mover family. The KTU 2024 module specifically tests whether the student can identify the *power band* in which each prime mover is dominant.

**Why does starting time matter?**
Standby generators in hospitals must reach full load in **less than 10 seconds**. A steam plant requires 1–4 hours to raise steam pressure. Hence, standby duty ⇒ IC engine or gas turbine. Similarly, a battle tank must accelerate instantly ⇒ gas turbine or diesel.

**Why does specific weight matter?**
A 100 MW steam turbine plant would weigh tonnes and cannot fly. An aircraft at 30,000 ft requires kW per kg, not kW per tonne. Hence, aviation ⇒ gas turbine (and increasingly, hybrid-electric).

**Why does specific speed govern hydraulic turbines?**
The specific speed $N_s$ is mathematically invariant for geometrically similar runners. A *low-head-high-flow* site demands a Kaplan (propeller) runner; a *high-head-low-flow* site demands a Pelton (impulse) runner. This is a direct KTU exam favorite.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $N_s$ | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Specific speed of a hydraulic turbine (metric units: $N$ in rpm, $P$ in kW, $H$ in metres) |
| $\eta_{bth}$ | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{W_{brake}}{m_f \times \text{CV}}$ | Brake thermal efficiency of an IC engine or boiler-fed system |
| BSFC | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{m_f}{W_{brake}}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption, $\text{kg/kWh}$ |
| HR | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{bth}}$ | Heat rate, $\text{kJ/kWh}$ |
| SSC | $\text{SSC} = \dfrac{m_{steam}}{W_{brake}}$ | Specific steam consumption of a turbine, $\text{kg/kWh}$ |
| $\dot{W}$ | $\dot{W} = T \times \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Shaft power from torque and speed |
| $\dot{m}$ | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V$ | Mass flow rate through a hydraulic turbine |
| $P_{hyd}$ | $P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Available hydraulic power from a head $H$ and discharge $Q$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of This Selection Framework

- **Power plant design**: A 500 MW thermal plant is a steam-turbine Rankine cycle, period. Trying to use 50,000 small diesel gensets would be absurd in capital, footprint, and emissions.
- **Naval architecture**: Modern frigates use combined diesel-or-gas (CODOG) propulsion — diesel for cruise, gas turbine for sprint. The *selection* is dual because the load profile is dual.
- **Remote-area electrification**: A village with biomass waste but no grid uses a biomass-fired steam plant or a biogas-fueled IC engine, *not* a gas turbine (too fuel-sensitive).
- **Pumped-storage hydro**: The pump is a motor-turbine (e.g., reversible Francis). When grid is low, it pumps up; when high, it generates. The *prime mover* is the same machine running in reverse.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Speed of a Hydraulic Turbine and Its Selection Role

The specific speed $N_s$ is the speed at which a geometrically similar turbine would run if it were reduced to deliver **1 kW of power under a head of 1 metre**. It is the single most important number for selecting a *type* of hydraulic turbine.

**Step 1 — Start from the Buckingham π-theorem grouping for a turbine.**

The power developed by a hydraulic turbine depends on the head $H$, the discharge $Q$, the density $\rho$, the gravity $g$, the rotational speed $N$, and the diameter $D$ of the runner. Dimensionless analysis gives two independent π-groups:

$$
\pi_1 = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{g^{1/2} H^{3/4}}, \qquad \pi_2 = \frac{N D^{5/4} \sqrt{g}}{\sqrt{Q}}
$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate the diameter $D$ between the two groups to obtain a similarity number depending only on $N$, $P$, and $H$.**

The power output of a turbine is $P = \rho g Q H \eta$. In metric field units, $P$ is in kW, $H$ in metres. The empirical specific speed is then defined as:

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

This is the KTU-board standard form.

**Step 3 — Interpret the magnitude for selection.**

- If $N_s$ is between **8 and 30** (metric, single-jet) ⇒ **Pelton wheel** (impulse, high head, low flow).
- If $N_s$ is between **60 and 400** ⇒ **Francis turbine** (reaction, medium head, medium flow).
- If $N_s$ is between **300 and 1000+** ⇒ **Kaplan / Propeller turbine** (reaction, low head, high flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works intuitively:** A Pelton wheel "spits out" one jet at a time — it needs a small $N_s$ because it does its work via impulse from a single, high-velocity jet. A Kaplan has many blades working in a continuous flow — it spins at a much higher $N_s$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Brake Thermal Efficiency from Fuel Calorific Value

**Step 1 — Define brake power output.**

The brake power $W_b$ delivered at the shaft is measured by a dynamometer (rope-brake, hydraulic dynamometer, or electric generator load). It is the *useful* mechanical output, after subtracting all internal friction and pumping losses.

$$
W_b = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]
$$

where $N$ is shaft speed in rpm and $T$ is the brake torque in N·m.

**Step 2 — Define the rate of heat input from fuel.**

If the engine consumes $m_f$ kg of fuel per second, and the calorific value of the fuel is $\text{CV}$ kJ/kg, then the rate of heat supplied to the engine is:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = m_f \times \text{CV} \quad [\text{kW}]
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\eta_{bth} = \frac{W_b}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{W_b}{m_f \times \text{CV}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of BSFC (Board favorite).**

Substituting $m_f = \text{BSFC} \times W_b$ (note: BSFC is in kg/kWh, so we divide by 3600 to get per-second flow):

$$
\eta_{bth} = \frac{1}{\text{BSFC} \times \text{CV} \times \frac{1}{3600}} = \frac{3600}{\text{BSFC} \times \text{CV}}
$$

This is the KTU-board relation that allows a quick conversion between BSFC and efficiency.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Selection of Prime Mover by Heat Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):**
> A process industry requires $50\ \text{MW}$ of continuous electric power. Three options are available:
> - (A) A coal-fired steam plant with $\eta_{bth} = 0.34$.
> - (B) A natural-gas combined-cycle gas turbine with $\eta_{bth} = 0.55$.
> - (C) A heavy-duty diesel engine with $\eta_{bth} = 0.42$ and fuel CV of $42{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}$.
>
> The calorific value of coal is $25{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}$ and of natural gas (per kg of fuel) is $50{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}$. Compute the **fuel consumption per hour** for each option.

**Solution Step 1 — Convert the demanded electric output to required thermal input.**

For a continuous duty of $50\ \text{MW} = 50{,}000\ \text{kW}$, the required thermal input for each option is:

$$
\dot{Q}_A = \frac{50{,}000}{0.34} = 147{,}058.8\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_B = \frac{50{,}000}{0.55} = 90{,}909.1\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_C = \frac{50{,}000}{0.42} = 119{,}047.6\ \text{kW}
$$

**Solution Step 2 — Convert thermal power input (kW = kJ/s) to fuel mass flow rate per second.**

$$
\dot{m}_A = \frac{147{,}058.8}{25{,}000} = 5.882\ \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_B = \frac{90{,}909.1}{50{,}000} = 1.818\ \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_C = \frac{119{,}047.6}{42{,}000} = 2.834\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Solution Step 3 — Multiply by 3600 to get fuel consumption per hour.**

$$
m_{A,h} = 5.882 \times 3600 = 21{,}176.5\ \text{kg/h} \approx 21.18\ \text{tonnes/h}
$$

$$
m_{B,h} = 1.818 \times 3600 = 6545.5\ \text{kg/h} \approx 6.55\ \text{tonnes/h}
$$

$$
m_{C,h} = 2.834 \times 3600 = 10{,}202.4\ \text{kg/h} \approx 10.20\ \text{tonnes/h}
$$

**Solution Step 4 — Engineer the selection.**

| Option | Fuel/hr | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| (A) Coal | 21.18 t/h | Highest fuel mass, but cheapest fuel (₹/kg). Needs boiler, ash handling, large footprint. |
| (B) Gas CC | 6.55 t/h | **Lowest fuel mass, highest efficiency.** Lowest CO₂/kWh. Requires gas pipeline. |
| (C) Diesel | 10.20 t/h | Fastest to install. No boiler. Higher fuel cost. Mobile if needed. |

**Conclusion:** For a stationary, continuous 50 MW plant with grid-quality gas available, option **(B) combined-cycle gas turbine** is selected on efficiency and emissions. If the site is a remote island without gas, option (A) coal is selected. If the load is *peak / standby* rather than baseload, option (C) diesel is selected for fast start.

> [!NOTE]
> This numerical pattern — convert shaft work → thermal input → fuel mass flow → compare — is the **exact valuation template** KTU examiners use for 7-mark sub-parts in 14-mark Module 2 questions.
