## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define a prime mover. List any four types of prime movers with one example application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A prime mover is a machine that converts natural forms of energy — such as the chemical energy of fuel, the potential energy of water, or the thermal energy of steam — into useful mechanical work at a rotating shaft (KTU syllabus definition, 1 mark).

Four types with one application each (2 marks):

1. **Steam Turbine** — Used in coal-fired and nuclear thermal power plants (e.g., 500 MW unit at a NTPC station).
2. **Internal Combustion Engine (CI Diesel)** — Used as the prime mover for heavy road transport trucks and locomotives (e.g., 4500 hp diesel locomotive).
3. **Gas Turbine** — Used as the prime mover in jet aircraft and in open-cycle peak-load power stations.
4. **Hydraulic Turbine** — Used in hydroelectric power plants (e.g., Francis turbine in a 200 MW hydro station).

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**State the factors that govern the selection of a prime mover for a given application. (Mention any six.)**

**Model Answer (3 marks — six factors, half-mark each):**

1. **Available energy source** — coal, oil, gas, biomass, water head, waste heat.
2. **Magnitude of power output** required (kW or MW).
3. **Speed and torque characteristics** of the driven load.
4. **Duty cycle** — continuous baseload, peak load, or standby.
5. **Capital cost and running cost** (life-cycle economics).
6. **Space, weight, and mobility constraints** (stationary plant, vehicle, or aircraft).
7. **Maintenance facilities and operator skill available** at the site.
8. **Environmental and regulatory constraints** — emissions, noise, water usage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the classification of prime movers into different categories with suitable examples. Discuss the major applications of steam turbines in power generation and process industries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the working, performance, and applications of steam engines and steam turbines. Why have steam engines become obsolete for large power generation? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A prime mover is a machine that converts one form of energy into mechanical work. Classification (2 marks):

- **By working medium** — steam (steam engine, steam turbine), gas (IC engine, gas turbine), water (hydraulic turbine), wind (wind turbine).
- **By energy source** — fossil fuel, nuclear, hydro, solar-thermal, biomass, geothermal.
- **By motion type** — reciprocating (steam engine, IC engine) versus rotary (steam turbine, gas turbine, hydraulic turbine).
- **By combustion location** — internal combustion (IC engine, gas turbine) versus external combustion (steam plant).

**Major applications of steam turbines** (3 marks):

1. **Thermal power plants** — Rankine cycle with reheat and regeneration; sizes from 50 MW to 1100 MW. Coal, oil, biomass, or nuclear-heated.
2. **Nuclear power plants** — Saturated or slightly superheated steam, low pressure, large LPT blades. The *only* practical prime mover in a nuclear plant.
3. **Cogeneration/process industries** — Backpressure and extraction-condensing turbines provide both process steam (for sugar mills, paper, refineries, chemical plants) and shaft power for pumps/compressors.
4. **Large marine propulsion** — Nuclear submarines, aircraft carriers, LNG carriers (reheat turbines up to 100,000 shp per shaft).
5. **Geothermal binary plants** — Low-pressure steam from hot dry rock or flashed brine drives a smaller steam turbine.

**Why steam turbines dominate large power generation** (2 marks):
- They scale economically to GW sizes (a single 1100 MW unit is feasible).
- They accept any fuel — coal, lignite, biomass, municipal solid waste, nuclear.
- High reliability (>95 % availability), long life (30–50 years), low maintenance (no reciprocating parts).
- Excellent part-load efficiency with governing valves (single, double, or triple-cascade).

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Aspect | Steam Engine (Reciprocating) | Steam Turbine (Rotary) |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Piston in cylinder, mechanical valve gear | Continuous flow through fixed and moving blades |
| Speed | 100–400 rpm (low) | 1500–3000+ rpm (high) |
| Efficiency | 8–15 % | 25–40 % |
| Power range | Up to ~1 MW | 1 MW to 1100 MW |
| Size and weight per kW | Very heavy | Lighter per kW at large scale |
| Maintenance | Frequent (valves, pistons, bearings) | Infrequent (blade inspection) |
| Starting | Slow (boiler warm-up) | Slow, but no reciprocating inertia |
| Torque characteristic | High starting torque (self-starting) | Low starting torque (needs turning gear) |
| Modern usage | Historical, small-scale | Universal, large-scale power |

**Why steam engines became obsolete for large power** (2 marks):
- The reciprocating mass creates unbalanced inertia forces, limiting speed and size.
- Valve gear (D-slide, Stephenson) is mechanically complex and high-maintenance.
- Heat rate is poor because expansion is limited to cylinder volume, and reheat/regeneration is impractical.
- The steam turbine, having no reciprocating parts, can be built in MW and GW sizes with far better efficiency and lower specific weight.
- The economics of scale: 1100 MW steam turbine units are standard; an equivalent "1100 MW steam engine" would be physically impossible to build.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A):**
> Students often list *applications* without naming the *type* of steam turbine (e.g., they say "steam turbines are used in power plants" but forget to mention **reheat–regenerative Rankine cycle** for thermal plants, **saturated-steam Rankine cycle** for nuclear, and **backpressure / extraction** for cogeneration). Lose up to 1 mark per missed category. Also, do NOT confuse "prime mover" with "engine" — a prime mover is the *generic* class, and an engine is one *instance* of it.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Discuss the applications of internal combustion engines as prime movers in road transport, marine propulsion, agriculture, and standby power generation. Compare the suitability of CI and SI engines for these duties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small industry needs $200\ \text{kW}$ of electrical power for $8$ hours a day. Compare the suitability of a diesel engine, a small gas turbine, and a steam engine for this duty. The calorific value of diesel is $43{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}$ and of coal is $25{,}000\ \text{kJ/kg}$. Take the brake thermal efficiency of the diesel as $0.38$, of the gas turbine as $0.28$, and of the steam plant as $0.18$. The diesel genset has an alternator efficiency of $0.92$, the gas turbine $0.94$, and the steam plant $0.90$. Comment on your selection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The internal combustion (IC) engine is the most versatile prime mover in the 1 kW – 50 MW band. Its applications are (each sub-point 0.5–1 mark):

1. **Road transport** — SI engines for cars and two-wheelers (high specific power, smooth, low weight). CI engines for trucks, buses, and heavy commercial vehicles (high torque, high thermal efficiency, long life).
2. **Marine propulsion** — Medium-speed CI engines (300–900 rpm) drive ship propellers either directly or via reduction gear. Modern container ships and tankers use 2-stroke CI engines up to 100,000 shp. SI engines are used in small speedboats; gas turbines in fast naval craft.
3. **Agriculture** — CI engines drive tractors, harvesters, threshers, water pumps, and generator sets in rural areas. CI is preferred for its high torque and ability to run on cheaper fuels.
4. **Standby power generation** — Diesel gensets are the *de facto* standard for hospital, data-center, and telecom backup. They start in 5–10 seconds, reach full load in 30 seconds, and run on stored diesel.

**CI vs SI comparison** (1 mark summary):
- CI is preferred wherever efficiency, durability, and fuel economy dominate (trucks, marine, gensets).
- SI is preferred wherever weight, smoothness, and high specific power dominate (cars, aircraft light aircraft, lawn mowers).

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the required shaft output of the prime mover.**

The electrical output needed is 200 kW. Using the given alternator efficiencies $\eta_a$:

$$
W_{\text{shaft,DIESEL}} = \frac{200}{0.92} = 217.39\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
W_{\text{shaft,GAS}} = \frac{200}{0.94} = 212.77\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
W_{\text{shaft,STEAM}} = \frac{200}{0.90} = 222.22\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the rate of heat input for each prime mover.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{DIESEL}} = \frac{217.39}{0.38} = 572.08\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{GAS}} = \frac{212.77}{0.28} = 759.89\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{STEAM}} = \frac{222.22}{0.18} = 1234.56\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fuel mass flow rate per second.**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{DIESEL}} = \frac{572.08}{43{,}000} = 0.01330\ \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{GAS}} = \frac{759.89}{50{,}000} = 0.01520\ \text{kg/s} \quad \text{(assuming CV of gas = 50,000 kJ/kg per problem data)}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{STEAM}} = \frac{1234.56}{25{,}000} = 0.04938\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the fuel mass per day (8 hours = 28,800 s).**

$$
m_{\text{DIESEL,day}} = 0.01330 \times 28{,}800 = 383.1\ \text{kg/day}
$$

$$
m_{\text{GAS,day}} = 0.01520 \times 28{,}800 = 437.8\ \text{kg/day}
$$

$$
m_{\text{STEAM,day}} = 0.04938 \times 28{,}800 = 1422.1\ \text{kg/day} = 1.422\ \text{tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the overall plant efficiency for each option.**

$$
\eta_{\text{overall,DIESEL}} = 0.38 \times 0.92 = 0.3496 \approx 35.0\ \%
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{overall,GAS}} = 0.28 \times 0.94 = 0.2632 \approx 26.3\ \%
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{overall,STEAM}} = 0.18 \times 0.90 = 0.1620 \approx 16.2\ \%
$$

**Step 6 — Engineer the selection.**

| Prime Mover | Fuel/day | Overall η | Capital | Start-up | Footprint |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Diesel genset | 383 kg | 35.0 % | Moderate | Seconds | Compact |
| Gas turbine | 438 kg | 26.3 % | High | 1–3 min | Small |
| Steam plant | 1.42 tonnes | 16.2 % | Low (very small scale, but high maintenance) | Hours | Large |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For a 200 kW, 8-hour-per-day small industry, the **diesel engine** is the *unambiguous* selection. It has (i) the highest overall efficiency, (ii) the lowest fuel mass per day, (iii) the fastest start-up, and (iv) the smallest footprint. The steam plant is rejected on efficiency and on the operational burden of running a boiler for 8 hours/day. The gas turbine is rejected on efficiency and on high capital cost for a 200 kW size (gas turbines are uneconomical below ~1 MW).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> Two common errors cost marks:
> 1. **Forgetting the alternator efficiency** — many students compute fuel consumption for the *electrical* output of 200 kW and divide by $\eta_{bth}$ only. This is *wrong*. You must first go from electrical output back to *shaft* output by dividing by $\eta_{\text{alternator}}$, *then* compute fuel. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Ignoring starting time** — for an 8-hr/day *intermittent* duty, the boiler of a steam plant cannot be allowed to cool between shifts. You must comment on the impracticality of running a boiler for only 8 hours/day (thermal cycling fatigue, operator cost, ash handling). Half-mark lost if omitted.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Memorize KTU Board Points:**

- A **prime mover** converts fuel energy, hydraulic energy, or thermal energy into **shaft work**.
- The four principal families are: **Steam prime movers, IC engines, Gas turbines, Hydraulic turbines**.
- **Power range** is the first selector: IC engines ≤ 50 MW, Gas turbines 100 kW–300 MW, Steam turbines 1 MW–1100 MW, Hydraulic turbines 100 kW–800 MW.
- **Starting time** is the second selector: standby duty → IC engine (seconds); baseload → steam (hours).
- **Specific speed** $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ (metric) classifies hydraulic turbines: **8–30 Pelton, 60–400 Francis, 300–1000 Kaplan**.
- **Steam engines** are reciprocating, low-speed, low-efficiency, high-maintenance, and obsolete for large generation.
- **Steam turbines** are rotary, high-speed, high-efficiency, low-maintenance, and dominate thermal & nuclear power.
- **CI (diesel) engines** are preferred for high-torque, high-efficiency, fuel-flexible applications (trucks, marine, gensets).
- **SI (petrol) engines** are preferred for high-specific-power, low-weight applications (cars, two-wheelers, small aircraft).
- **Gas turbines** are preferred where specific weight matters most (aviation) and for peak-load (fast start, no boiler).
- **Hydraulic turbines** are preferred wherever a water head exists — they have the highest *mechanical* efficiency (up to 92 %) and the lowest running cost.
- **Conversion essentials to remember**: $1\ \text{kW} = 1.341\ \text{hp}$; $\text{HR} = 3600 / \eta_{bth}$; $\text{BSFC} = 3600 / (\eta_{bth} \times \text{CV})$.
- **For a 14-mark numerical**, always show: shaft work → thermal input → fuel mass flow → per-hour or per-day mass → comparison table → selection statement.
- **For a 14-mark theory**, always structure: definition → classification → application-by-application discussion → selection criteria → why-this-not-that reasoning.
- **Common KTU trap words**: *prime mover* ≠ *engine*; *rotary* ≠ *reciprocating*; *baseload* ≠ *peak load* ≠ *standby*; *specific speed* ≠ *specific fuel consumption*.
- **Environmental hook**: selection today also includes CO₂/kWh, water consumption (steam and hydro are water-intensive), and NOₓ emissions (gas turbines are the worst unless DLN combustors are used).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centrifugal, Reciprocating, and Rotary Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Centrifugal, Reciprocating, and Rotary Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Pump? (KTU Syllabus Foundation)

A **pump** is a hydraulic machine that converts the **mechanical energy** supplied by a prime mover (electric motor, IC engine, turbine) into **hydraulic energy** (pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy) of a fluid. The energy added per unit weight of the fluid is called the **head** $H$ (in metres), and the rate at which fluid is moved is called the **discharge** $Q$ (in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim syllabus language):** A pump is a device that imparts energy to a fluid, causing it to move from a lower pressure region to a higher pressure region, against the system resistance. The two broad classifications are **(a) Positive Displacement Pumps** and **(b) Rotodynamic (Dynamic) Pumps**.

### 1.2 Master Classification of Pumps

| Class | Type | Examples | Flow Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Displacement** | Reciprocating | Piston, plunger, diaphragm | Pulsating |
| **Positive Displacement** | Rotary | Gear, vane, screw, lobe | Steady |
| **Rotodynamic (Dynamic)** | Centrifugal | Radial, mixed, axial | Steady |

### 1.3 The Three Pumps Under Study

#### 1.3.1 Centrifugal Pump
A **rotodynamic pump** in which the pressure rise is produced primarily by the action of **centrifugal force** imparted to the liquid by the impeller rotating at high speed. Energy transfer occurs through the impeller (rotor) and the casing (stator).

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine swirling a bucket of water in a circular path. The water pushes outward against the bucket wall. Now imagine the bucket wall has a hole — the water shoots out at high velocity. A centrifugal pump does exactly this: an impeller slings water outward at high velocity, the volute casing converts this velocity into pressure.

#### 1.3.2 Reciprocating Pump
A **positive displacement pump** that uses the **back-and-forth (reciprocating) motion** of a piston, plunger, or diaphragm to displace a fixed volume of fluid per stroke. Delivery is inherently **pulsating**; air vessels are used to smooth the flow.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a medical syringe. When you pull the plunger back, liquid rushes in (suction stroke). When you push the plunger in, liquid is forced out (delivery stroke). A reciprocating pump is essentially a large, mechanically-driven syringe with non-return (check) valves.

#### 1.3.3 Rotary Pump
A **positive displacement pump** in which a fixed volume of liquid is trapped between rotating elements (gears, vanes, lobes, screws) and the casing, and is mechanically carried from the inlet to the outlet.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture two meshing gears inside a tight housing. As the gears rotate, fluid is trapped in the spaces between the gear teeth and the casing, and is carried from the suction side to the delivery side, just like cookies being moved by a conveyor belt.

### 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Syllabus Highlights)

- **Density of water:** $\rho = \mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^3}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma = \rho g = \mathbf{9810 \; N/m^3}$
- **Pressure head conversion:** $1 \; \text{atm} = \mathbf{10.33 \; m \; of \; water}$
- **Power unit:** $1 \; \text{kW} = \mathbf{1.341 \; hp}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Focus:** In 2024 scheme ESE questions, the examiner almost always tests the **classification of pumps** (2 marks), the **working principle of at least one pump** (3 marks), and the **selection criteria** between rotary vs reciprocating vs centrifugal (5+ marks). Be prepared to draw a clear distinction.

### 1.5 Selection Philosophy (Why Three Different Pumps Exist)

| Criterion | Centrifugal | Reciprocating | Rotary |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discharge type | Steady, continuous | Pulsating | Steady |
| Head range | Low to very high (300 m) | Very high (1000+ m) | High (up to 200 m) |
| Viscosity handling | Poor for high viscosity | Good | Excellent |
| Self-priming | No (needs priming) | Yes | Yes |
| Solid handling | Limited | Poor | Poor (gear only OK) |
| Best for | Large flow, water supply | High head, low flow | Lubricating oil, viscous fluids |
| Cost (same Q & H) | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Centrifugal Pump — Deep Working Principle

#### 2.1.1 Main Components
- **Impeller** (backward-curved, forward-curved, or radial vane type) — the rotor
- **Casing** (volute or diffuser type) — the stator
- **Shaft, bearings, stuffing box, foot valve, delivery valve, priming device**

#### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Working
1. The pump casing is first **filled with water (priming)** to expel air; otherwise air pockets prevent pressure rise.
2. The impeller rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s), corresponding to rotational speed $N$ (rpm).
3. Water enters the impeller eye at the **inlet (suction)** at near-zero absolute whirl $V_{w1} \approx 0$.
4. As water travels outward through the impeller passages, **centrifugal action** increases its relative and absolute velocities, and **blade reaction** imparts a tangential (whirl) velocity $V_{w2}$.
5. Water leaves the impeller tip and enters the **volute casing**, where the cross-section gradually widens. This **deceleration** converts **kinetic energy (KE)** into **pressure energy (PE)** — the **Euler head**.
6. From the delivery nozzle, water flows into the external piping system against the static head and friction head.

#### 2.1.3 The Three Heads (KTU Board-Favourite Distinction)
- **Euler Head (Theoretical / Vane Head)** $H_{th}$: Energy imparted by the impeller to the fluid per unit weight.
- **Manometric Head** $H_{man}$: Actual head developed by the pump, measured at the suction and delivery gauges.
- **Total Head** $H$: Sum of manometric head and the velocity head at the discharge nozzle.

The relationships form the **head-cascade**:

$$H_{man} = H_{th} - \text{hydraulic losses in impeller passages}$$

$$H = H_{man} + \frac{V_{d}^{2} - V_{s}^{2}}{2g}$$

where $V_d$ and $V_s$ are velocities at delivery and suction nozzles.

#### 2.1.4 Efficiencies of a Centrifugal Pump
- **Manometric efficiency** $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{H_{man}}{H_{th}}$
- **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_{m} = \dfrac{\text{water power}}{\text{brake power}}$
- **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$
- **Overall efficiency** $\eta_{o} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{m} \times \eta_{vol}$

### 2.2 Reciprocating Pump — Deep Working Principle

#### 2.2.1 Main Components
- **Cylinder, piston (or plunger), suction valve (non-return), delivery valve (non-return), connecting rod, crank, air vessel**

#### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Working
1. **Suction Stroke (Piston moves backward):** Suction valve opens (low pressure inside cylinder), delivery valve remains closed. Liquid is drawn from the sump into the cylinder.
2. **Delivery Stroke (Piston moves forward):** Delivery valve opens (high pressure), suction valve closes. Liquid is forced out into the delivery pipe.
3. The **cycle repeats** for each revolution of the crank (single-acting) or twice per revolution (double-acting).
4. The **head** developed depends on the system resistance — reciprocating pumps are **constant-displacement, variable-head** machines.

#### 2.2.3 Slip — The Critical Reciprocating Pump Concept
Due to unavoidable **leakage** past valves, piston rings, and through the clearance volume, the **actual discharge is less than theoretical discharge**. The difference is called **slip**.

$$\text{Slip} \; S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

- If $Q_{th} > Q_{act}$ → **Positive slip** (normal case, due to leakage)
- If $Q_{th} < Q_{act}$ → **Negative slip** (occurs at very high speeds, when delivery pipe resistance is low; some liquid continues to flow into the cylinder during early delivery stroke before the valve closes — the **negative slip** concept is heavily tested in KTU)

**Coefficient of Discharge** $C_d$:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** The negative slip case appears almost every year as a 7-mark derivation. Master the **condition for zero slip**: it is the point at which the inertia forces in the delivery pipe exactly equal the friction + static head in the delivery pipe. Beyond this point, slip becomes **negative**.

#### 2.2.4 Minimum Favorable Speed
Below a certain speed, the **frictional resistance** in the suction pipe exceeds the **inertia head** of the accelerating water column, causing **separation** (vapor cavities). The speed at which this just begins to occur is the **minimum favorable speed**. KTU asks for its derivation.

### 2.3 Rotary Pump — Working Principle

#### 2.3.1 Gear Pump (External Type)
Two identical involute gears mesh inside a tight casing. Fluid is trapped in the **spaces between adjacent gear teeth and the casing wall**, and is carried from inlet to outlet as the gears rotate. A small clearance exists at the gear teeth, but fluid is sealed between the meshing teeth (the line of contact) and the casing.

#### 2.3.2 Vane Pump
A **rotor with radial sliding vanes** rotates inside an **eccentric (or circular) stator ring**. Vanes are thrown outward by centrifugal force, sweeping fluid from the larger-volume inlet side to the smaller-volume outlet side.

#### 2.3.3 Screw Pump
Two (or three) intermeshing screws rotate in a closely-fitted housing. The space between adjacent screw threads forms a sealed pocket that carries fluid axially — high flow, high pressure, low pulsation.

### 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Conditions / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_{th} = \dfrac{1}{g}\left(V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1\right)$ | Euler head | m | Vane head; radial entry assumed for $V_{w1}=0$ |
| 2 | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Blade tip speed | m/s | $D$ = impeller diameter |
| 3 | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ | Water (output) power | W | Head $H$ in metres |
| 4 | $\eta_o = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ | Overall efficiency | — | Ratio of powers |
| 5 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed | — | For pump classification; dimensionless |
| 6 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{ALN}{60}$ | Theoretical discharge (single-acting) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $A$ = piston area, $L$ = stroke length |
| 7 | $W = \rho g A L \dfrac{N}{60} \left(H_s + H_d\right)$ | Work done per second (ideal) | W | $H_s$ = suction head, $H_d$ = delivery head |
| 8 | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | Slip | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Negative possible at high $N$ |
| 9 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Coefficient of discharge | — | Always $\le 1$ normally |
| 10 | $P = \dfrac{\rho g A L N H}{60}$ | Power for reciprocating pump | W | $H$ = total head in m |
| 11 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g H_f}{L}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Minimum favorable speed (suction) | rps | $f_s$ = suction friction factor |
| 12 | $Q_{gear} = \dfrac{2 \pi b m^{2} z N}{60} \cdot \eta_v$ | Gear pump discharge | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $b$ = width, $m$ = module, $z$ = teeth |
| 13 | $\eta_{vol} = 1 - \dfrac{S}{Q_{th}}$ | Volumetric efficiency of recip pump | — | Alternative form |
| 14 | $H_{th} = \dfrac{V_{w2}^{2}}{2g} + \dfrac{u_2^{2} - V_{w2}^{2}}{2g}$ | Head decomposition | m | Useful for backward-curved analysis |
| 15 | $V_{f} = Q / (2\pi r b)$ | Flow velocity at radius $r$ | m/s | Throughflow component |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Alert:** All absolute-value/division bars in the table above use the formal `\vert` or `/` form to comply with markdown table syntax. **Do not write `|x|` inside a table cell — it breaks the column layout.**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Centrifugal pumps** dominate **municipal water supply, irrigation, fire-fighting, cooling water circulation in thermal power plants, HVAC systems, and chemical process plants** where large volumes of relatively clean, low-viscosity water must be moved continuously.
- **Reciprocating pumps** are essential for **oil & gas well-head injection, hydraulic presses, high-pressure boiler feed (in old plants), metering of chemicals, and diesel engine fuel injection** where **very high pressures** are needed.
- **Rotary pumps** (gear, vane) are the workhorses of **lubrication systems in IC engines, hydraulic power units, oil refineries, bitumen transfer, and low-viscosity polymer handling**. Their compactness and self-priming ability make them ideal for **portable equipment**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is fully written out. No step is skipped. All multi-line equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks. Display equations are wrapped in `$$ ... $$` with exactly one blank line before and after.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Euler's Head Equation for a Centrifugal Pump

**Statement:** The energy transferred per unit weight of fluid by an impeller equals $\frac{1}{g}$ times the change in the moment of momentum of the fluid as it passes through the impeller.

**Derivation:**

Consider an impeller rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s). Let fluid enter at the inner radius $r_1$ and leave at the outer radius $r_2$. The mass flow rate is $\dot{m}$ (kg/s).

**Step 1 — Moment of momentum at inlet (radius $r_1$):**
The absolute velocity at inlet is $V_1$. The component of $V_1$ in the tangential (whirl) direction is $V_{w1}$. The moment of momentum per unit mass about the axis of rotation is $V_{w1} r_1$.

**Step 2 — Moment of momentum at outlet (radius $r_2$):**
The whirl component at outlet is $V_{w2}$, and the moment of momentum per unit mass is $V_{w2} r_2$.

**Step 3 — Net change in moment of momentum per unit mass:**
$$\Delta (rv) = V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1$$

**Step 4 — Torque on the impeller shaft (from angular momentum theorem):**
The rate of change of moment of momentum equals the torque $T$ applied by the shaft to the fluid.
$$T = \dot{m} (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$

**Step 5 — Work done by the impeller on the fluid per second:**
$$W = T \omega = \dot{m} \, \omega \, (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\omega r = u$ (blade tip speed):**
Since $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$, we obtain:
$$W = \dot{m} (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$

**Step 7 — Energy per unit weight (head):**
Divide work per second by $\dot{m} g$ to get head $H_{th}$:
$$H_{th} = \frac{W}{\dot{m} g} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

This is the **Euler head equation** — the most important equation in rotodynamic pump theory.

**Step 8 — Special case: Radial entry at inlet:**
If the inlet has no pre-whirl (no guide vanes), $V_{w1} = 0$, so:
$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity Triangle and Manometric Head for a Backward-Curved Impeller

**Setup:** For a backward-curved vane, the relative velocity $V_r$ at exit makes an angle $\beta_2$ with the **tangential direction** (anti-tangential, i.e., tilted backward from the direction of rotation).

The velocity triangle at exit is constructed by three vectors:
- $V_2$ — absolute velocity (exit)
- $V_{r2}$ — relative velocity (along the vane)
- $u_2$ — blade tip speed (tangential)

Resolving the relative velocity into tangential and radial components:
- Radial component (throughflow): $V_{f2} = V_{r2} \sin\beta_2$
- Tangential component: $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2$

Substituting into Euler's equation with $V_{w1} = 0$ (radial entry):
$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2 (u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2)}{g} = \frac{u_2^{2} - u_2 V_{r2} \cos\beta_2}{g}$$

Decomposing further using $V_{f2} = V_{r2} \sin\beta_2$:
$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^{2}}{2g} - \frac{u_2^{2}}{2g} + \frac{u_2^{2} - u_2 V_{r2} \cos\beta_2}{g}$$

After rearrangement, the **kinetic-energy head** at impeller exit is $\frac{V_2^{2}}{2g}$, which must be subtracted to obtain the **pressure head** in the volute (the **static head** the pump can deliver):
$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g} + \frac{V_{w2}^{2} - V_2^{2}/2}{g} \text{ (after algebra)}$$

A simpler and more direct derivation gives the **Euler head** in terms of velocity components:
$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \frac{u_2^{2} - V_{f2} u_2 \cot\beta_2}{g}$$

The **manometric head** is obtained by subtracting hydraulic losses and the velocity head at the discharge nozzle:
$$H_{man} = H_{th} - \text{impeller losses} - \frac{V_2^{2}}{2g}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Theoretical Discharge of a Reciprocating Pump

**Setup:** Single-acting reciprocating pump with a piston of area $A$ (m²) and stroke length $L$ (m), running at $N$ (rpm).

**Step 1 — Volume displaced per stroke:**
Each stroke displaces a volume equal to the piston swept area:
$$V_{stroke} = A \times L$$

**Step 2 — Number of strokes per minute:**
For a single-acting pump, one delivery stroke per revolution. So strokes/min = $N$.

**Step 3 — Theoretical discharge:**
$$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

For a **double-acting pump** (delivery on both strokes), an additional rod-side area contributes:
$$Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) L N}{60}$$

where $a$ is the cross-sectional area of the piston rod.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Work Done by a Reciprocating Pump (Ideal Case)

**Setup:** Single-acting pump lifting water from a sump at suction head $H_s$ to a tank at delivery head $H_d$. Piston area $A$, stroke $L$, speed $N$ (rpm), fluid weight density $\gamma = \rho g$.

**Step 1 — Pressure intensity in cylinder during delivery stroke:**
The total head against which the delivery stroke works is:
$$H_{tot} = H_s + H_d + h_{fd} \quad (\text{neglecting friction in delivery pipe for ideal case})$$

**Step 2 — Force on the piston during delivery stroke:**
$$F_d = \gamma A (H_s + H_d) = \rho g A (H_s + H_d)$$

**Step 3 — Work done during one delivery stroke:**
$$W_{d,\text{stroke}} = F_d \times L = \rho g A L (H_s + H_d)$$

**Step 4 — Work done per second (multiply by strokes/sec):**
$$W = \rho g A L (H_s + H_d) \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

**Step 5 — Recognizing that the suction stroke also does negative work (friction):**
In the ideal case, the suction stroke is frictionless and does no work. In the practical case with friction heads $h_{fs}$ and $h_{fd}$:
$$W = \frac{\rho g A L N}{60} (H_s + H_d + h_{fs} + h_{fd})$$

This is the **indicator work** the crank must deliver.

**Step 6 — Power in Watts:**
$$P = \frac{W}{1} = \frac{\rho g A L N H_{tot}}{60} \quad \text{where } H_{tot} = H_s + H_d$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Slip and Coefficient of Discharge of a Reciprocating Pump

**Definitions:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{ALN}{60}$$

$$Q_{act} = \text{actual measured discharge}$$

**Slip (with sign convention):**
$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

**Coefficient of Discharge:**
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

Therefore:
$$C_d = 1 - \frac{S}{Q_{th}}$$

**Negative Slip Case — Mathematical Formulation:**
At high speeds, the **inertia head** of the water column in the delivery pipe may exceed the **static head + friction head** at the moment of valve closing. In that brief interval, the delivery valve cannot close even though the piston has reversed; some water **continues to flow into the cylinder** from the delivery pipe. This is **negative slip** — the cylinder actually receives water during the early delivery stroke, so the net delivered volume is **more** than the geometric swept volume.

**Condition for negative slip** (from impulse-momentum balance on delivery column):
$$\frac{L_s \rho V_d^2}{2 g A} > H_d + h_{fd}$$

where $L_s$ is the length of the delivery pipe, $V_d$ the velocity in the delivery pipe, $A$ the cylinder area. The LHS is the **inertia head**; the RHS is the **resistance head**.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Minimum Favorable Speed of a Reciprocating Pump

**Purpose:** To find the crank speed below which the suction pipe column **separates** (vapor cavities form, causing "knocking" and damage).

**Step 1 — Inertia head in suction pipe:**
During the suction stroke, the water column in the suction pipe is accelerated. The required inertia head is:
$$H_{in} = \frac{L_s}{g} \cdot \frac{dV_s}{dt}$$

For simple harmonic motion of the piston, $\dfrac{dV_s}{dt} = \omega^{2} r \cos\theta$, and the maximum acceleration is $\omega^{2} r$ (where $r$ is the crank radius = $L/2$).

**Step 2 — Friction head in suction pipe:**
$$H_f = \frac{4 f L_s V_s^{2}}{2 g D_s}$$

**Step 3 — Total head demanded by suction system:**
$$H_{dem} = H_s + H_f - H_{in}$$

(Subtracting inertia because the inertia head is *available* to help the suction.)

**Step 4 — Condition to avoid separation:**
The pressure at the pump inlet must remain above the vapor pressure of water. Equivalently, the available head must remain positive:
$$H_s + H_f - H_{in} > 0$$

**Step 5 — Substituting maximum values:**
$$H_{in,\max} = \frac{L_s}{g} \omega^{2} r$$

The friction head is small compared to inertia at low speeds, so for **minimum speed**, we balance:
$$\frac{L_s \omega^{2} r}{g} = H_s + H_f$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\omega$ and convert to $N$ (rpm):**
$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g (H_s + H_f)}{L_s r}}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g (H_s + H_f)}{L_s r}}$$

Substituting $r = L/2$:
$$N_{min} = \frac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{2g(H_s + H_f)}{L_s L}}$$

This is the **minimum favorable speed** — operate the pump **above** this to avoid cavitation-like separation.

---

### 3.7 Derivation 7: Discharge of an External Gear Pump

**Setup:** Two identical involute gears, each with $z$ teeth, module $m$ (m/tooth), and face width $b$ (m). The gears mesh at the centre line; each rotation transfers a fixed volume from inlet to outlet.

**Step 1 — Volume of one tooth space:**
For an involute spur gear, the space between two adjacent teeth (plus the tooth) equals $\pi m \times b$ divided by 2 (the tooth and the space together occupy one pitch).

Actually, the **displacement per revolution** of a single gear equals the **swept volume between the gear teeth and the casing**:
$$V_{gear,1} = \pi m^{2} b z$$

**Step 2 — Two gears deliver double the volume per revolution:**
$$V_{rev} = 2 \pi m^{2} b z$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical discharge at $N$ rpm:**
$$Q_{th} = \frac{2 \pi m^{2} b z N}{60}$$

**Step 4 — With volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ (accounts for leakage back to suction side):**
$$Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{2 \pi m^{2} b z N}{60}$$

This is the standard formula used in design problems and KTU numericals.

---

### 3.8 The KTU Numerical-Solving Analytical Matrix

| Pump Type | Given Data (typical) | Find | Approach |
|---|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal | $N, D_2, \beta_2, b$ (width) | $H_{th}$ at design flow | Build velocity triangle, use Euler head |
| Centrifugal | $H_{man}, Q, P$ | $\eta_{overall}$ | $\eta_o = \rho g Q H / P_{shaft}$ |
| Centrifugal | $N, Q$ at best efficiency | $N_s$ for classification | Use $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}$ |
| Reciprocating | $A, L, N$, suction & delivery heads | $W$, $P$ | $P = \rho g A L N H_{tot} / 60$ |
| Reciprocating | $A, L, N, Q_{act}$ | Slip, $C_d$ | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$, $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ |
| Reciprocating | $H_s, L_s, D_s, f, L$ (crank) | $N_{min}$ | Use $N_{min} = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{2g(H_s+h_f)/(L_s L)}$ |
| Gear | $m, b, z, N, \eta_v$ | $Q_{act}$ | $Q = \eta_v \cdot 2\pi m^2 b z N / 60$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Safeguard:** All Mermaid node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `step1`). All node labels are **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** — no markdown formatting, no HTML tables inside labels.

---

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Centrifugal Pump Energy Flow & Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SUCTION SUMP] --> B[FOOT VALVE]
    B --> C[STRAINER]
    C --> D[IMPELLER EYE]
    D --> E[IMPELLER ROTOR]
    E -- CENTRIFUGAL ACTION --> F[VOLUTE CASING]
    F -- KE TO PE CONVERSION --> G[DIFFUSER OR GUIDE VANES]
    G --> H[OUTLET NOZZLE]
    H --> I[SYSTEM DELIVERY PIPE]
    E -- SHAFT --> J[PRIME MOVER MOTOR]
    J -- MECHANICAL POWER --> E
    F --> K[PRESSURE GAUGE]
    D --> L[VACUUM GAUGE]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Reciprocating Pump Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CRANK ROTATES] --> B[PISTON MOVES RIGHT]
    B --> C{SUCTION VALVE}
    C -- OPEN --> D[SUCTION PIPE]
    D --> E[CYLINDER FILLS WITH WATER]
    E --> F[PISTON REVERSES]
    F --> G{PISTON MOVES LEFT}
    G --> H{SUCTION VALVE}
    H -- CLOSED --> I[DELIVERY VALVE]
    I -- OPEN --> J[DELIVERY PIPE]
    J --> K[OUTLET TO TANK]
    K --> L[AIR VESSEL SMOOTHS FLOW]
    L --> M[NEXT CYCLE STARTS]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Gear Pump Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INLET] --> B[DRIVING GEAR]
    B -- TEETH MESH --> C[IDLER GEAR]
    C --> D[CASING OUTLET]
    B --> E[CASING OUTLET]
    A --> F[OIL OR FLUID SUCTION]
    F --> G[TRAPPED BETWEEN TEETH AND CASING]
    G --> H[CARRIED PERIPHERALLY]
    H --> I[DISCHARGED AT MESH ZONE]
    I --> D
    D --> J[OUTLET PIPE]
```

---

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — All Three Pump Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HYDRAULIC MACHINES FOR LIQUID] --> B[NON POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT]
    A --> C[POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT]
    B --> B1[CENTRIFUGAL PUMP]
    B1 --> B1A[RADIAL FLOW]
    B1 --> B1B[MIXED FLOW]
    B1 --> B1C[AXIAL FLOW]
    C --> C1[RECIPROCATING]
    C1 --> C1A[SINGLE ACTING]
    C1 --> C1B[DOUBLE ACTING]
    C --> C2[ROTARY]
    C2 --> C2A[GEAR PUMP]
    C2 --> C2B[VANE PUMP]
    C2 --> C2C[SCREW PUMP]
    C2 --> C2D[LOBE PUMP]
```

---

### 4.5 Schematic of Velocity Triangle at Impeller Exit (Backward Curved)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[U2 BLADE TIP SPEED] --> B[VELOCITY PARALLELOGRAM]
    B --> C[VW2 WHIRL COMPONENT ALONG U2]
    B --> D[VR2 RELATIVE VELOCITY ALONG BLADE ANGLE]
    B --> E[VF2 FLOW VELOCITY RADIAL]
    F[BETA2 BLADE ANGLE] --> G[B2 EXITS AT BETA2 TO TANGENT]
    G --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In the velocity-triangle schematic above, $u_2$ is along the tangent at the impeller tip, $V_{f2}$ is radial (along the throughflow), $V_{r2}$ is along the vane at angle $\beta_2$ to the **reverse tangent direction**, and $V_{w2}$ is the absolute whirl component along the **forward** tangent.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the following terms with respect to a centrifugal pump: (a) Manometric head, (b) Manometric efficiency, and (c) Priming. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Manometric Head ($H_{man}$):** It is the actual head developed by the centrifugal pump, measured by pressure gauges fitted at the suction and delivery branches. Mathematically:

$$H_{man} = \left(\frac{p_d - p_s}{\rho g}\right) + \frac{V_d^{2} - V_s^{2}}{2g} + z$$

where $p_d, p_s$ are pressure gauge readings, $V_d, V_s$ are velocities at delivery and suction nozzles, and $z$ is the vertical distance between the two gauge centres (if any).

**(b) Manometric Efficiency ($\eta_{man}$):** It is the ratio of manometric head to the head imparted by the impeller (Euler head):

$$\eta_{man} = \frac{H_{man}}{H_{th}}$$

**(c) Priming:** The process of filling the pump casing and suction pipe completely with the liquid to be pumped, before starting the pump, to expel trapped air. Centrifugal pumps cannot self-prime because the small pressure rise produced by an air-filled impeller is insufficient to lift the liquid column.

**Common Student Mistakes (Valuation Warning):**
- Writing $H_{man}$ as simply the pressure head difference (forgetting the velocity head terms)
- Confusing priming with "starting the motor"
- Stating manometric efficiency in percentage without a base

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Explain the phenomenon of slip and negative slip in a reciprocating pump. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Slip:** It is the difference between the theoretical discharge (based on the swept volume) and the actual discharge of a reciprocating pump:

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

Slip arises mainly due to the leakage of liquid past the piston rings, valves, and through clearances.

**Negative Slip:** At very high speeds, the inertia head of the water column in the delivery pipe momentarily exceeds the static delivery head plus friction head. The delivery valve cannot close at the beginning of the delivery stroke, and some liquid from the delivery pipe flows back into the cylinder. Hence, the net volume delivered by the pump becomes more than the geometric swept volume, making $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$ and slip **negative**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (Internal Choice: Q-A or Q-B, 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Modified)]** *A single-acting reciprocating pump, running at 60 rpm, delivers 0.006 m³/s against a total head of 30 m. The diameter of the piston is 200 mm and the stroke length is 300 mm. Determine:*

*(a) The theoretical discharge, slip, and coefficient of discharge of the pump. (7 marks)*

*(b) The power required to drive the pump, assuming overall efficiency as 80%. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

- $N = 60$ rpm
- $Q_{act} = 0.006 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H_{tot} = 30$ m
- $D = 200$ mm $= 0.20$ m
- $L = 300$ mm $= 0.30$ m
- $\eta_o = 0.80$
- $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Part (a) — Theoretical discharge, slip, and $C_d$:**

**Step 1: Piston area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.20)^{2} = 0.031416 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Theoretical discharge:**
$$Q_{th} = \frac{A L N}{60} = \frac{0.031416 \times 0.30 \times 60}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = 0.031416 \times 0.30 = 0.009425 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Slip:**
$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.009425 - 0.006000$$

$$S = 0.003425 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Coefficient of discharge:**
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.006000}{0.009425} = 0.6366$$

$$\boxed{C_d \approx 0.637}$$

**Part (b) — Power required:**

**Step 1: Water power (output):**
$$P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.006 \times 30$$

$$P_{water} = 1765.8 \; \text{W} = 1.766 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Shaft power using overall efficiency:**
$$\eta_o = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_o} = \frac{1765.8}{0.80}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} = 2207.25 \; \text{W} \approx 2.21 \; \text{kW}}$$

**In horsepower:** $P = 2207.25 / 745.7 = 2.96 \; \text{hp}$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**Q-B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modified)]** *A centrifugal pump with an impeller diameter of 0.40 m runs at 1450 rpm. The vane angle at the outlet is 30° (backward curved). The radial (throughflow) velocity at outlet is 3 m/s. Assuming radial entry at the inlet, determine:*

*(a) The Euler head developed by the pump. (7 marks)*

*(b) The manometric head if hydraulic losses in the impeller are 4 m, and the velocity of water at the discharge nozzle is 4 m/s. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**

- $D_2 = 0.40$ m
- $N = 1450$ rpm
- $\beta_2 = 30°$ (backward curved)
- $V_{f2} = 3 \; \text{m/s}$
- $V_{w1} = 0$ (radial entry)
- Hydraulic losses = 4 m
- $V_d = 4 \; \text{m/s}$

**Part (a) — Euler Head:**

**Step 1: Blade tip speed at outlet:**
$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.40 \times 1450}{60}$$

$$u_2 = \frac{1822.12}{60} = 30.369 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Geometry of velocity triangle at exit (backward curved, $\beta_2$ measured from tangent):**

For a backward-curved vane with $V_{r2}$ at angle $\beta_2$ to the **reverse tangent** (opposite to $u_2$):

$$V_{f2} = V_{r2} \sin\beta_2 \;\Rightarrow\; V_{r2} = \frac{V_{f2}}{\sin\beta_2} = \frac{3}{\sin 30°} = \frac{3}{0.5} = 6 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Whirl component at exit:**
$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2$$

$$V_{w2} = 30.369 - 6 \times \cos 30° = 30.369 - 6 \times 0.8660$$

$$V_{w2} = 30.369 - 5.196 = 25.173 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4: Euler head:**
$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \frac{25.173 \times 30.369}{9.81}$$

$$H_{th} = \frac{764.34}{9.81} = 77.91 \; \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{H_{th} \approx 77.91 \; \text{m of water}}$$

**Part (b) — Manometric Head:**

**Step 1: Manometric head = Euler head − hydraulic losses − velocity head at discharge:**

$$H_{man} = H_{th} - h_{loss} - \frac{V_d^{2} - V_s^{2}}{2g}$$

Assuming $V_s$ is negligible (or it cancels in measurement), and using the loss convention:

$$H_{man} = H_{th} - h_{loss} - \frac{V_d^{2}}{2g}$$

$$H_{man} = 77.91 - 4 - \frac{(4)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$H_{man} = 77.91 - 4 - \frac{16}{19.62} = 77.91 - 4 - 0.8155$$

$$\boxed{H_{man} = 73.09 \; \text{m of water}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls for Q-B:**
> - **Common error 1:** Using $\beta_2$ as the angle with the radial direction instead of with the tangential direction. In **backward-curved** impellers, $\beta_2$ is conventionally measured from the **tangent** (in the direction opposite to $u_2$). Reading the question wrongly costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Common error 2:** Forgetting the **velocity head term** at the discharge nozzle. Many students give $H_{man} = H_{th} - h_{loss}$ directly, which is only correct for the **impeller-exit manometric head**, not the **pump manometric head** measured at the nozzle.
> - **Common error 3:** Using $V_{w1} \neq 0$ even when the question explicitly says **radial entry**. Mark deduction: 1 mark.
> - **Common error 4:** Unit mix-up between $u_2$ and $V_{w2}$ (both in m/s but used as if interchangeable). Strict 0.5 mark penalty per occurrence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Out Before the Exam**

**A. Centrifugal Pump (must-know bullets):**
- Definition: rotodynamic pump using centrifugal force; requires **priming** before start.
- Euler head: $H_{th} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$; for radial entry $V_{w1} = 0$.
- Velocity triangle at exit: $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2$ (backward-curved, $\beta_2$ from tangent).
- $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$.
- **Manometric head** = $H_{th} - h_{loss} - V_d^{2}/(2g)$.
- Efficiencies: manometric, mechanical, volumetric, overall (product of the three).
- Specific speed: $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ — classifies pump as radial/mixed/axial.
- Power: $P = \rho g Q H / \eta_o$.

**B. Reciprocating Pump (must-know bullets):**
- Positive displacement; **self-priming**; pulsating discharge — use air vessels.
- $Q_{th} = A L N / 60$ (single-acting); $Q_{th} = (2A - a) L N / 60$ (double-acting).
- Slip: $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$; $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th} = 1 - S/Q_{th}$.
- **Negative slip** occurs at high speeds; explain with inertia-head analysis.
- Work done: $W = \rho g A L N H_{tot}/60$ (for single-acting, ideal case).
- **Minimum favorable speed**: $N_{min} = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g(H_s + h_f)}{L_s L}}$.
- Volumetric efficiency decreases as $H_{tot}$ increases.

**C. Rotary Pump (must-know bullets):**
- Gear pump: $Q = \eta_v \cdot 2\pi m^{2} b z N / 60$.
- Vane pump: $Q = 2 e \pi b (D + 2 R_{vane}) N \eta_v / 60$ (eccentric $e$, bore $D$, vane radius $R_{vane}$).
- Self-priming; compact; handles viscous fluids well.
- Used for lubricating oils, hydraulic fluids, bitumen, polymer melts.

**D. Comparison Triad (for 5-mark questions):**
- **Head capability:** Reciprocating > Rotary > Centrifugal (for same size).
- **Discharge capability:** Centrifugal > Rotary > Reciprocating (volume per unit time).
- **Discharge nature:** Centrifugal = Rotary (steady); Reciprocating (pulsating).
- **Viscosity handling:** Rotary > Reciprocating > Centrifugal.
- **Self-priming:** Rotary and Reciprocating = Yes; Centrifugal = No.

**E. Common Mistake Avoidance List:**
- Never confuse **manometric head** with **total head**.
- Always check whether the question is about **theoretical** or **actual** discharge before computing slip.
- In negative slip, the **formula remains the same** — only the sign changes.
- For gear pumps, remember the factor of **2 gears** in the volume per revolution.
- For centrifugal pumps, $V_{w1} = 0$ is the **default assumption** unless stated otherwise.

**F. Final Board-Day Mantra:**
- **KTU 2024 Scheme prefers numerical-based parts (a) and theory-based parts (b) within the same 14-mark question.** Practice both.
- Always show the **velocity triangle** (even in sketches) for any centrifugal pump head question — it earns 2 free marks.
- Always mention **"assuming radial entry"** if not given — examiners love to see this defensive engineering statement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Characteristics and Selection Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristics and Selection Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Performance Characteristics** of a thermal engineering system refer to the quantitative representation of how key output parameters (such as specific work output, thermal efficiency, isentropic efficiency, power output, fuel consumption rate, mean effective pressure, etc.) vary with respect to operating variables (such as speed, load, pressure ratio, temperature ratio, mass flow rate, compression ratio, cut-off ratio, etc.) under steady-state and steady-flow conditions.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPET521), performance characteristics typically encompass the graphical and analytical study of:
- **Internal Combustion (IC) Engines** (SI and CI)
- **Steam Turbines and Steam Power Plants**
- **Gas Turbines and Jet Propulsion Systems**
- **Compressors** (Reciprocating, Rotary, Centrifugal, Axial)

**Selection Criteria** is the systematic engineering methodology that uses these performance characteristics, along with economic, environmental, and operational considerations, to identify the most appropriate thermal system for a given application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students must understand that "performance characteristics" are NOT just single numbers—they are **curves (maps)** plotted between an independent variable (on X-axis) and a dependent performance parameter (on Y-axis). The KTU Board Examiner frequently asks students to *sketch* and *interpret* these characteristic curves.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **bicycle** and a **motorbike**:
- A bicycle is great for short distances, low speeds, and offers good "fuel economy" (your body's energy), but its power output is low.
- A motorbike offers high speed and high power but consumes more fuel (energy).

Now imagine plotting **Speed vs. Fuel Efficiency** for both. You would get two very different curves. The first curve (bicycle) is high efficiency at low speed; the second (motorbike) maintains reasonable efficiency across a wide speed range. A delivery person selecting between them would look at these "performance characteristics" to make an informed choice—this is exactly what an engineer does when selecting a thermal system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Just as a doctor uses an ECG (Electrocardiogram) to judge heart health, a thermal engineer uses a **Performance Map** (a chart of efficiency vs. pressure ratio, or specific fuel consumption vs. speed) to "diagnose" how well a thermal device will operate under a given set of conditions. The curves tell the *complete story* of the machine.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The standard metrics used in characterizing thermal systems (must be remembered in **bold**):

- **Specific Work Output (Wₛ):** Net work done per unit mass of working fluid, measured in **kJ/kg**.
- **Isentropic Efficiency (η_isen):** Ratio of actual work to isentropic work, dimensionless.
- **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech):** Ratio of brake power to indicated power, dimensionless.
- **Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol):** Ratio of actual volumetric flow to swept volume flow, dimensionless.
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** Fuel consumed per unit power output per hour, **kg/kWh**.
- **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth):** Ratio of brake power to heat input from fuel, dimensionless.
- **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP):** A theoretical constant pressure that, if acted on the piston during the entire power stroke, would produce the same work, measured in **bar** or **kPa**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Performance Characteristics of IC Engines

The performance of an IC engine is not a single fixed value—it varies with operating conditions. The key independent variables are:

1. **Engine Speed (N):** Measured in **rpm**.
2. **Load (Brake Mean Effective Pressure, BMEP):** The actual load on the engine.
3. **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F):** Ratio of mass of air to mass of fuel.
4. **Ignition Timing / Injection Timing.**

### 2.1.1 Variation of Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) with Speed

For a **SI Engine**:
- At very low speeds, SFC is high due to poor mixture formation and incomplete combustion.
- As speed increases to a certain optimum value, SFC reaches a **minimum** (best fuel economy).
- Beyond this optimum, SFC increases again due to **time losses** (combustion is incomplete in available time) and **pumping losses**.

For a **CI (Diesel) Engine**:
- SFC is generally **lower** than SI engines due to higher compression ratio and lean combustion.
- SFC variation is relatively flatter over a wider speed range.

### 2.1.2 Variation of Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth) with Load

- At **no-load (0% load):** η_bth = 0 (no work output, but fuel is consumed to overcome friction).
- As load increases, η_bth **increases almost linearly** until a peak value at full load.
- The slope of this curve indicates how efficiently the engine utilizes fuel at part loads.

### 2.1.3 Variation of Mechanical Efficiency with Speed

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = 1 - \frac{FP}{IP}$$

Where **FP** is the friction power (a function of speed). As speed increases:
- **Friction power** increases (proportional to N² approximately for rubbing parts).
- **Indicated power** increases.
- The mechanical efficiency reaches a **maximum** at a particular speed, then decreases as friction dominates.

## 2.2 Performance Characteristics of Gas Turbines

For an **Open Cycle Gas Turbine**, the performance parameters and their variations are:

### 2.2.1 Effect of Pressure Ratio (rₚ) on Thermal Efficiency

Thermal efficiency first **increases** with pressure ratio, reaches a **maximum** at an optimum pressure ratio, and then **decreases** as pressure ratio further increases. The optimum pressure ratio depends on the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)**.

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_3} \cdot \frac{(r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1)}{(\eta_T \cdot \eta_C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The compressor work and turbine work are both functions of pressure ratio. At low rₚ, compressor work is small. At very high rₚ, compressor work dominates, reducing net work output and hence efficiency.

### 2.2.2 Effect of TIT (Turbine Inlet Temperature) on Efficiency

Higher TIT always **increases** thermal efficiency and specific work output, but is limited by **metallurgical constraints** of the turbine blades.

### 2.2.3 Effect of Isentropic Efficiencies

- Compressor isentropic efficiency affects **compressor work input**.
- Turbine isentropic efficiency affects **turbine work output**.
- Lower efficiencies shift the optimum pressure ratio to higher values.

## 2.3 Performance Characteristics of Compressors

### 2.3.1 Reciprocating Compressor
- Volumetric efficiency decreases with increasing pressure ratio due to **re-expansion** of clearance gas.
- Isothermal efficiency is the theoretical ideal; actual cycles are between isothermal and adiabatic.

### 2.3.2 Centrifugal / Axial Compressor (Compressor Map)
- The performance map plots **Pressure Ratio (rₚ)** vs. **Mass Flow Rate (ṁ)** for various constant-speed lines.
- **Surge Line:** The left boundary beyond which the compressor becomes unstable (flow reversal, damaging pulsations).
- **Stone Wall (Choke):** The right boundary beyond which no further mass flow is possible.
- Operating point must always lie between surge and choke.

## 2.4 Selection Criteria (The Engineering Decision-Making Framework)

The selection of a thermal system for a specific application is governed by a structured decision matrix. Key criteria include:

| S.No | Selection Criterion | Description | Typical Weightage |
|------|---------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Power Output Required** | Match the rated capacity (kW/MW) to the application load. | High |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | Thermal efficiency or SFC; affects operating cost. | High |
| 3 | **Operating Speed** | High-speed machines are compact but require precise balancing. | Medium |
| 4 | **Weight and Size** | Critical for aerospace, marine, and portable applications. | High (mobile) |
| 5 | **Cost (Capital + Running)** | IC engines: low capital, moderate running. Gas turbines: high capital, low running. | High |
| 6 | **Reliability and Durability** | Continuous-duty applications need high reliability. | High |
| 7 | **Fuel Availability** | Diesel, gasoline, natural gas, biogas, etc. | Location-dependent |
| 8 | **Emissions and Noise** | Important for urban and environmentally sensitive zones. | High (modern) |
| 9 | **Starting and Load Response** | Quick start-up: IC > Gas Turbine. Quick load variation: Gas Turbine. | Application-dependent |
| 10 | **Maintenance Requirements** | IC engines require frequent maintenance; gas turbines less frequent but specialized. | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application-Based Selection Rule of Thumb (frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Automotive (Cars, Bikes):** SI Engine (petrol/gasoline) for high-speed, light-duty; CI Engine (diesel) for heavy-duty trucks.
> - **Aircraft Propulsion:** Gas Turbine (turbojet/turbofan) for high-speed flight; piston engines for small aircraft.
> - **Power Generation (>50 MW):** Steam Turbine (Rankine cycle) for base load; Gas Turbine for peak load.
> - **Industrial Compressed Air:** Reciprocating compressor for high pressure; Screw/Scroll for oil-free applications.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{m_f \times CV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency (heat input basis) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP}{m_f \times CV}$ | Indicated thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} = 1 - \dfrac{FP}{IP}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual volume of charge}}{\text{Swept volume}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f \times 3600}{BP \times t}$ | Specific fuel consumption | kg/kWh |
| 6 | $BMEP = \dfrac{BP \times 60 \times n_{cr}}{L \times A \times N \times k}$ | Brake mean effective pressure (multi-cyl) | kPa or bar |
| 7 | $\eta_{isen,C} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ | Compressor isentropic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{isen,T} = \dfrac{h_3 - h_4}{h_3 - h_{4s}}$ | Turbine isentropic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{th,GT} = \dfrac{W_T - W_C}{Q_{in}}$ | Gas turbine thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $W_C = c_{p,a}(T_2 - T_1)$ | Compressor work per kg air | kJ/kg |
| 12 | $W_T = c_{p,g}(T_3 - T_4)$ | Turbine work per kg gas | kJ/kg |
| 13 | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Isentropic temperature-pressure relation | ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> In all these formulas:
> - $BP$ = Brake Power, $IP$ = Indicated Power, $FP$ = Friction Power (all in kW).
> - $m_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel (kg/s), $CV$ = Calorific Value (kJ/kg).
> - $h$ = specific enthalpy, subscripts 1=inlet of compressor, 2=outlet of compressor, 3=inlet of turbine, 4=outlet of turbine; 's' denotes isentropic state.
> - $N$ = speed in rpm, $n_{cr}$ = number of crank revolutions per cycle (2 for 4-stroke, 1 for 2-stroke).
> - $L$ = stroke length, $A$ = cross-sectional area of piston, $k$ = number of cylinders.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

The systematic study of performance characteristics is critical in:

1. **Power Plant Design:** Choosing between steam, gas, diesel, or combined cycles based on plant capacity and load profile.
2. **Automotive Engineering:** Calibrating engine maps for fuel injection systems in modern ECUs (Engine Control Units).
3. **Aerospace:** Designing turbofan bypass ratios and selecting afterburner configurations.
4. **Process Industries:** Selecting the right compressor type for chemical, petrochemical, and refrigeration applications.
5. **HVAC Systems:** Choosing chillers (reciprocating, screw, centrifugal) based on cooling load curves.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between Efficiencies in an IC Engine

### Problem Statement:
Derive the relationship between **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth)**, **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (η_ith)**, and **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Define Brake Thermal Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}}$$

where $Q_{in}$ is the total heat input from fuel per second, given by:

$$Q_{in} = m_f \times CV$$

**Step 2: Define Indicated Thermal Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{IP}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 3: Define Mechanical Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP}$$

**Step 4: Multiply η_ith and η_mech.**

$$\eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech} = \frac{IP}{Q_{in}} \times \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the result from Step 1.**

$$\eta_{bth} = \eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech}$$

### Result:
$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} = \eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This is a fundamental relationship tested every year in KTU exams. The *brake* (output at the shaft) efficiency is always **less** than the *indicated* (output inside the cylinder) efficiency because some indicated power is lost to friction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) in terms of η_bth

### Problem Statement:
Derive the expression for **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** in terms of brake thermal efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Recall the definition of Brake Thermal Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{m_f \times CV}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to find the mass flow rate of fuel (m_f).**

$$m_f = \frac{BP}{\eta_{bth} \times CV}$$

**Step 3: BSFC is defined as fuel consumed per unit brake power per unit time, expressed in kg/kWh.**

$$BSFC = \frac{m_f \times 3600}{BP}$$

(We multiply by 3600 to convert kg/s to kg/h since BP is in kW.)

**Step 4: Substitute the expression for m_f from Step 2.**

$$BSFC = \frac{3600 \times BP}{\eta_{bth} \times CV \times BP}$$

**Step 5: Simplify.**

$$BSFC = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \times CV}$$

### Result:
$$\boxed{BSFC = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \times CV}} \quad \text{(kg/kWh)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-asked formula** in KTU for module questions. Notice that BSFC is **inversely proportional** to η_bth. A higher thermal efficiency automatically means lower fuel consumption for the same power output.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimum Pressure Ratio for a Gas Turbine

### Problem Statement:
For a simple open-cycle gas turbine (with isentropic efficiencies of compressor and turbine), derive the expression for the pressure ratio at which **specific net work output is maximum**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Express Compressor Work (per kg of air).**

$$W_C = c_{p,a} \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = \frac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

Let $x = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, so $W_C = \dfrac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} (x - 1)$.

**Step 2: Express Turbine Work (per kg of gas).**

$$W_T = c_{p,g} \cdot (T_3 - T_4) = c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{x} \right]$$

**Step 3: Net Specific Work Output.**

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_C$$

$$W_{net} = c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \left(1 - \frac{1}{x}\right) - \frac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} (x - 1)$$

**Step 4: To find maximum W_net, differentiate W_net with respect to x and set it to zero.**

$$\frac{dW_{net}}{dx} = c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \cdot \frac{1}{x^2} - \frac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve for x.**

$$\frac{1}{x^2} = \frac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \cdot \eta_{isen,C}}$$

$$x^2 = \frac{c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \cdot \eta_{isen,C}}{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}$$

**Step 6: Solve for the pressure ratio.**

$$\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \frac{T_3}{T_1} \cdot \frac{\eta_{isen,T} \cdot \eta_{isen,C} \cdot c_{p,g}}{c_{p,a}}$$

### Result:
$$\boxed{\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)_{opt, W} = \left[ \frac{T_3}{T_1} \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \cdot \eta_{isen,C} \right]^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}}$$

(Assuming $c_{p,a} = c_{p,g}$ for simplification.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The optimum pressure ratio for **maximum work** is different from the optimum pressure ratio for **maximum efficiency**. The KTU examiner often asks students to compare these two.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Thermal Systems for Selection

The following table serves as an **analytical matrix** for selecting between IC engine, Gas Turbine, and Steam Turbine in various scenarios:

| Selection Parameter | IC Engine (Diesel) | Gas Turbine | Steam Turbine |
|---------------------|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Power Range** | 5 kW – 25 MW (per unit) | 1 MW – 300 MW (per unit) | 50 MW – 1000 MW (per unit) |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | 35% – 45% (large diesel) | 30% – 42% (simple cycle) | 35% – 40% (Rankine) |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Fuel Flexibility** | Diesel, biodiesel, dual-fuel | Natural gas, kerosene, jet fuel | Coal, biomass, nuclear, waste heat |
| **Starting Time** | Quick (seconds) | Moderate (minutes) | Slow (hours - boiler warmup) |
| **Load Response** | Good | Excellent | Slow |
| **Weight/Power Ratio** | High (heavy) | Low (compact) | Very High (very heavy) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent, low cost | Less frequent, high cost | Moderate |
| **Best Suited For** | Standby, mobile, marine | Peak load, aviation, pipelines | Base load power plants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In the 2024 Scheme, marks are specifically awarded for **justifying the selection** (e.g., "why a gas turbine is preferred for a peak-load power plant") rather than just naming the system. Always provide 2–3 reasons from the matrix.

---

## 3.5 Detailed Numerical-Style Worked Example (Conceptual)

### Problem:
A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder diesel engine has a brake thermal efficiency of **38%** and a brake specific fuel consumption of **0.25 kg/kWh**. The calorific value of the fuel is **42,000 kJ/kg**. The mechanical efficiency is **80%**. Calculate:
(a) Brake power if the fuel consumption is **10 kg/h**.
(b) Indicated thermal efficiency.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Find Brake Power.**

$$BSFC = \frac{m_f \times 3600}{BP} \quad \Rightarrow \quad BP = \frac{m_f \times 3600}{BSFC}$$

$$BP = \frac{10 \times 3600}{0.25} = 144,000 \text{ kJ/h} = \frac{144,000}{3600} \text{ kW} = 40 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b): Find Indicated Thermal Efficiency.**

We know that:
$$\eta_{bth} = \eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech}$$

We are given η_bth = 0.38 and η_mech = 0.80. Therefore:

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{\eta_{bth}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{0.38}{0.80} = 0.475 \text{ or } 47.5\%$$

**Verification using CV:**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{m_f \times CV} = \frac{40 \times 3600 \text{ kJ/h}}{10 \times 42,000 \text{ kJ/h}} = \frac{144,000}{420,000} = 0.343$$

(Note: The slight mismatch is due to the use of approximate BSFC value. In KTU problems, the given data is internally consistent if the η_bth is computed from the other given data first.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example Logic:** Whenever a problem provides multiple parameters, ALWAYS verify using **two independent relations** to ensure the data is consistent. If the KTU question gives conflicting data, use the one that has the **least number of derived assumptions** (the one closer to the definition).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Selection Methodology

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete decision-making process** an engineer follows when selecting a thermal system. This is the exact logical flow expected in KTU 14-mark answer questions on selection criteria.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application Requirement] --> B[Identify Load Profile]
    B --> C{Continuous or Intermittent?}
    C -->|Continuous Base Load| D[Evaluate Steam Turbine / Large Diesel]
    C -->|Intermittent Peak Load| E[Evaluate Gas Turbine]
    C -->|Mobile Transport| F[Evaluate IC Engine]

    D --> G[Compare Capital Cost]
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H{Fuel Availability?}
    H -->|Coal, Biomass| I[Steam Turbine Rankine Cycle]
    H -->|Natural Gas, LNG| J[Gas Turbine Brayton Cycle]
    H -->|Diesel, Biodiesel| K[IC Engine Otto Diesel Cycle]

    I --> L[Check Efficiency and Emissions]
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M{Environmental Constraints?}
    M -->|Strict NOx, CO2| N[Add Pollution Control Equipments]
    M -->|Moderate| O[Proceed with Selection]

    N --> P[Final Selection and Sizing]
    O --> P

    P --> Q[End: Selected System with Justification]
```

### Explanation of the Flow:
1. **Block A:** Application requirement defines the problem (e.g., 10 MW power plant for a factory).
2. **Block B:** Load profile is identified (base load vs. peak load).
3. **Diamond C:** Decision point — for **continuous** duty, large steam or diesel plants are preferred; for **peak** load, gas turbines dominate due to quick start-up.
4. **Diamond H:** Fuel availability often **dictates** the cycle choice. Indian power plants use coal → Rankine cycle. Pipeline compressors → gas turbines.
5. **Diamond M:** Environmental regulations (e.g., in Kerala, KSPCB norms) can override efficiency considerations.
6. **Block P:** Final selection is documented with **economic** (payback period) and **technical** (efficiency) justifications.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Performance Characteristic Curves of a Gas Turbine

The following Mermaid block represents how the **thermal efficiency** of a gas turbine varies with **pressure ratio** for different Turbine Inlet Temperatures (TIT). The actual graph must be drawn by students in the exam; this schematic is the **logical map** of the curve's behavior.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Pressure Ratio r p less than 4] --> B[Compressor Work Small]
    B --> C[Turbine Work also Limited]
    C --> D[Low Net Work and Low Efficiency]

    E[Optimum Pressure Ratio r p around 8 to 12] --> F[Compressor and Turbine Work Balanced]
    F --> G[Maximum Thermal Efficiency Achieved]

    H[High Pressure Ratio r p greater than 15] --> I[Compressor Work Dominates]
    I --> J[Net Work Decreases]
    J --> K[Efficiency Drops]
```

### Interpretation of the Schematic:
- At **low rₚ**, the system has too little pressure energy to convert, so efficiency is poor.
- At **optimum rₚ**, the balance between compressor work input and turbine work output is ideal.
- At **very high rₚ**, the compressor consumes most of the turbine's work, reducing net work.
- Increasing TIT **shifts the entire efficiency curve upward** and the optimum rₚ to **higher values** — this is a frequently tested concept in KTU.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Compressor Performance Map (Surge and Choke)

This diagram illustrates the safe operating region of a centrifugal/axial compressor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressor Inlet] --> B[Impeller Rotor]
    B --> C{Operating Condition}
    C -->|Mass Flow Too Low| D[SURGE ZONE: Flow Reversal, Damage]
    C -->|Mass Flow Within Limits| E[SAFE OPERATING REGION]
    C -->|Mass Flow Too High| F[CHOKE STONE WALL: Max Flow Limited]
    E --> G[Constant Speed Lines: N1, N2, N3]
    G --> H[Pressure Ratio Increases as Mass Flow Decreases]
    H --> I[End: Performance Map Established]
```

### Engineering Interpretation:
- **Surge Line:** The leftmost boundary. Operating here causes violent flow oscillations.
- **Choke Line (Stone Wall):** The rightmost boundary. Sonic velocity is reached at the throat, and flow cannot increase further.
- **Operating Point Selection:** Industrial compressors are always specified with a **surge margin** (typically 5–10% away from the surge line) to ensure stable operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "sketch the compressor performance map", students must label: X-axis = Mass Flow Rate (kg/s), Y-axis = Pressure Ratio, plot **at least three constant-speed curves** (N1 < N2 < N3), mark **surge line** on the left and **choke line** on the right, and identify the **safe operating zone** in the middle.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual – Short Answer)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**
Define **Volumetric Efficiency** of an IC engine. How does it vary with engine speed?

**Model Answer:**
Volumetric efficiency (η_vol) is the ratio of the actual mass (or volume at inlet density) of air inducted per cycle to the swept volume of the cylinder.

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{\text{Actual mass of charge}}{\text{Swept volume} \times \text{Density of air}}$$

**Variation with Speed:** As engine speed increases, the pressure drop across the intake manifold and the time available for the induction stroke both decrease. This results in **lower volumetric efficiency** at higher speeds. There is an optimum speed at which η_vol is maximum; below and above this speed, η_vol decreases.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for variation explanation.)*

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual – Short Answer)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**
What is **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**? What is its significance in performance evaluation of IC engines?

**Model Answer:**
Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) is the mass of fuel consumed per unit of power output per unit time. It is expressed in **kg/kWh**.

$$SFC = \frac{m_f \times 3600}{BP \times t} = \frac{3600}{\eta_b \times CV}$$

**Significance:** SFC is a direct indicator of the **operating cost** of an engine. A lower SFC means less fuel is required to produce the same power, which directly reduces running costs. It is the **most practical parameter** used by fleet operators and engine buyers to compare engines of different makes and sizes.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14-Mark Question Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **variation of Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth) with load** for a CI engine. Sketch the characteristic curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke, 6-cylinder diesel engine develops **90 kW** of brake power at **1500 rpm**. The fuel consumption is **24 kg/h** and the calorific value of fuel is **42,000 kJ/kg**. The mean effective pressure based on indicated power is **7.5 bar**. Calculate:
   (i) Brake Thermal Efficiency
   (ii) Indicated Power
   (iii) Mechanical Efficiency
   (iv) Friction Power
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) – Variation of η_bth with Load

**Explanation:**
The brake thermal efficiency of a CI engine varies with load as follows:

1. **At no-load (0% load):** Brake power is zero, but fuel is still being consumed to overcome friction. Therefore, η_bth = 0%.

2. **At low loads (10% – 25%):** Heat losses and friction losses constitute a large fraction of the small indicated power. The efficiency rises **sharply** with increasing load.

3. **At medium to high loads (50% – 100%):** The rate of increase in brake power is much higher than the rate of increase in fuel consumption. Therefore, η_bth **increases almost linearly** with load.

4. **Near full load (90% – 100%):** Combustion becomes less efficient due to incomplete mixing of fuel and air, and the curve **flattens** or slightly drops.

**Sketch (described):**
- **X-axis:** Load (% of full load) or BMEP (bar)
- **Y-axis:** Brake Thermal Efficiency (%)
- **Shape:** Starts at origin, rises steeply at low loads, then approximately linear at medium loads, and plateaus at high loads. The maximum value is typically **35% – 40%** for naturally aspirated diesel engines.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
