> Many students write "η_bth increases with load" but fail to mention **why**. Always explain that the *rate* of increase in BP is greater than the *rate* of increase in fuel consumption at part loads. Marks are lost if the sketch is not drawn.

### Part (b) – Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Engine type: 4-stroke, 6-cylinder
- BP = 90 kW
- N = 1500 rpm
- m_f = 24 kg/h = 24/3600 kg/s = 0.00667 kg/s
- CV = 42,000 kJ/kg
- IMEP = 7.5 bar = 750 kPa

**Step 1: Calculate Heat Input (Q_in).**

$$Q_{in} = m_f \times CV = 0.00667 \times 42,000 = 280 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth).**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}} = \frac{90}{280} = 0.3214 = 32.14\%$$

**Step 3: Express Indicated Power using IMEP (note: IP = MEP × LA × N × k / 60 for 4-stroke).**

Since BP and η_bth are already known, we use the friction-power relation. From part (a), IMEP = 7.5 bar.

For a 4-stroke engine, Indicated Power is given by:

$$IP = \frac{IMEP \times L \times A \times N \times k}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Friction Power (FP) directly using the definition.**

We know that:
$$\eta_{bth} = \eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech}$$
$$IP = \frac{BP}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{90}{\eta_{mech}}$$

The problem does not provide a direct numerical value of IP. However, by definition:

$$FP = IP - BP$$

For the model answer, let us assume the friction power is to be computed from the indicated efficiency:

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{IP}{Q_{in}} \quad \text{where} \quad IP = BP + FP$$

Without additional geometric data, we provide the **methodology**:

**Methodology (to be shown in exam):**
1. Compute η_bth directly (Step 2) → **32.14%**.
2. Compute IP using the formula with IMEP, swept volume, and speed.
3. Compute FP = IP − BP.
4. Compute η_mech = BP / IP.

For numerical simplicity, if we assume IP = 100 kW (from typical exam data), then:
- FP = IP − BP = 100 − 90 = **10 kW**
- η_mech = BP / IP = 90 / 100 = 0.90 = **90%**

> [!NOTE]
> The actual numerical value of IP and FP would depend on cylinder dimensions (L and A). The KTU exam typically provides these values; the methodology above remains the same.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the **selection criteria** for choosing a prime mover (engine/turbine) for the following applications. Justify your answer with suitable performance characteristics:
   (i) A 200 MW base-load power plant in Kerala.
   (ii) A standby power unit for a hospital.
   (iii) A jet aircraft.
   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas turbine has a **compressor pressure ratio of 8**, a **turbine inlet temperature of 1200 K**, and **isentropic efficiencies of compressor and turbine as 85% and 90%** respectively. The ambient temperature is **300 K**. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate:
   (i) Compressor work per kg of air.
   (ii) Turbine work per kg of air.
   (iii) Net work output.
   (iv) Thermal efficiency.
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) – Selection of Prime Movers

**(i) 200 MW Base-Load Power Plant:**
- **Selection: Steam Turbine (Rankine Cycle)**
- **Justification:**
  - Base-load plants operate continuously (8000+ hours/year), so **high efficiency is critical**.
  - Steam turbines have **thermal efficiencies of 35%–40%**, which is higher than simple gas turbines (~30%).
  - Coal is **abundantly available** in Kerala (and India) — coal-fired Rankine plants are economical.
  - Capital cost is high, but the **long operating life** (30+ years) justifies the investment.
  - Steam turbines can handle **very large capacities** efficiently (up to 1000 MW per unit).

**(ii) Standby Power Unit for a Hospital:**
- **Selection: Diesel Engine (CI Engine) or Diesel Generator Set**
- **Justification:**
  - Standby units require **fast start-up** (within seconds) and quick load acceptance — diesel engines excel at this.
  - Hospitals need **high reliability** — diesel engines are robust and well-proven for emergency duty.
  - Capital cost is **lower** than gas turbines for small capacities (typically 50 kW to 2 MW).
  - Diesel is **easy to store** on-site for emergency use.
  - Specific fuel consumption is reasonable, but since the unit runs only occasionally, fuel cost is not a major factor.

**(iii) Jet Aircraft:**
- **Selection: Gas Turbine (Turbojet or Turbofan)**
- **Justification:**
  - **High power-to-weight ratio** is essential — gas turbines offer the best specific power.
  - Aircraft require **high-speed operation** (thrust at high exhaust velocity).
  - Gas turbines have **excellent reliability** and can operate continuously for long flights.
  - Quick start-up and smooth throttle response are critical for aviation.
  - Piston engines are limited in altitude and speed; gas turbines dominate in jet propulsion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often give **only the name of the system** without proper **justification**. For full 7 marks, you must provide at least **3 valid engineering reasons** for each selection. Generic statements like "it is efficient" or "it is reliable" are not sufficient — quantify the advantage (e.g., "efficiency of 38% vs 28%").

### Part (b) – Gas Turbine Performance Numerical

**Given Data:**
- r_p = P₂/P₁ = 8
- T₃ = 1200 K (Turbine Inlet Temperature)
- T₁ = 300 K (Ambient)
- η_{isen,C} = 0.85
- η_{isen,T} = 0.90
- c_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K
- γ = 1.4

**Step 1: Calculate the Isentropic Temperature Ratio for the Compressor.**

$$\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 8^{0.4/1.4} = 8^{0.2857}$$

Using logarithms: $\log(8) = 0.903$, so $0.2857 \times 0.903 = 0.258$.

$$8^{0.2857} = 10^{0.258} \approx 1.812$$

$$T_{2s} = 1.812 \times 300 = 543.6 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Actual Compressor Outlet Temperature (T₂).**

$$\eta_{isen,C} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$

$$T_2 - T_1 = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} = \frac{543.6 - 300}{0.85} = \frac{243.6}{0.85} = 286.6 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 300 + 286.6 = 586.6 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Compressor Work per kg of air (W_C).**

$$W_C = c_p \times (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times 286.6 = 288.03 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Isentropic Temperature at Turbine Exit (T_{4s}).**

For the turbine, the expansion ratio is the same as the compression ratio (P₄/P₃ = P₁/P₂), so:

$$\frac{T_{4s}}{T_3} = \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{0.2857} = \frac{1}{1.812} = 0.552$$

$$T_{4s} = 0.552 \times 1200 = 662.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Actual Turbine Exit Temperature (T₄).**

$$\eta_{isen,T} = \frac{T_3 - T_4}{T_3 - T_{4s}}$$

$$T_3 - T_4 = \eta_{isen,T} \times (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 0.90 \times (1200 - 662.4) = 0.90 \times 537.6 = 483.84 \text{ K}$$

$$T_4 = 1200 - 483.84 = 716.16 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Turbine Work per kg of air (W_T).**

$$W_T = c_p \times (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times 483.84 = 486.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7: Calculate Net Work Output (W_net).**

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 486.26 - 288.03 = 198.23 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8: Calculate Thermal Efficiency (η_th).**

$$Q_{in} = c_p \times (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 586.6) = 1.005 \times 613.4 = 616.47 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{198.23}{616.47} = 0.3216 = 32.16\%$$

### Final Answers:
- **W_C = 288.03 kJ/kg**
- **W_T = 486.26 kJ/kg**
- **W_net = 198.23 kJ/kg**
- **η_th = 32.16%**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Numerical):**
> 1. **Do not confuse** T_{2s} (isentropic) with T₂ (actual). The isentropic value is calculated first; then the actual value is derived using efficiency.
> 2. **Units check:** Work per kg should be in kJ/kg, not kW (work per kg is not power; mass flow rate is required for power).
> 3. **Sign convention:** W_C is always **work input** (positive in magnitude but subtracted), W_T is work output.
> 4. **Significant figures:** Use 4 significant figures in intermediate steps to avoid compounding errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist:

- ✅ **Performance Characteristics** are graphical/analytical representations showing how output parameters vary with operating variables.
- ✅ Key IC engine parameters: **BSFC, η_bth, η_mech, BMEP, η_vol, FP**.
- ✅ Fundamental efficiency relation: **η_bth = η_ith × η_mech**.
- ✅ BSFC formula: **BSFC = 3600 / (η_bth × CV)** — memorize this.
- ✅ Gas turbine efficiency first **increases** then **decreases** with pressure ratio.
- ✅ Higher **TIT** improves efficiency and shifts optimum rₚ to higher values.
- ✅ Compressor safe operation zone is between **surge line** and **choke (stone wall) line**.
- ✅ Selection of prime mover depends on: **power, efficiency, fuel, cost, weight, response, environment**.
- ✅ **Base load → Steam Turbine; Peak load → Gas Turbine; Mobile/Quick start → IC Engine**.
- ✅ For jet aircraft, only **gas turbines** are practically viable above certain Mach numbers.
- ✅ **Friction power (FP)** increases approximately with N²; mechanical efficiency peaks at an optimum speed.
- ✅ Always specify **surge margin** when operating centrifugal/axial compressors.
- ✅ **CI engines** have lower SFC and higher efficiency than SI engines due to higher compression ratio.
- ✅ For all performance problems, remember to use **isentropic relations** correctly: $\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.
- ✅ In KTU answer scripts, **always sketch the characteristic curve** with **labeled axes** — examiners give 1–2 marks for this.
- ✅ Be thorough with the **comparison table** of IC Engine vs. Gas Turbine vs. Steam Turbine for selection-based questions.

### ⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid:

1. **Mixing up isentropic and actual states** (T_{2s} vs T₂) — always compute isentropic state first.
2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — kJ/kg for work, bar for pressure, K for temperature.
3. **Skipping sketches** in descriptive questions — even a hand-drawn curve fetches full marks.
4. **Using the wrong compression ratio relation** for Otto vs Diesel cycles.
5. **Not justifying** selection in 7-mark questions — bare statements lose marks.
6. **Confusing BSFC with SFC** — SFC is a general term; BSFC specifically uses Brake Power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:**
> For Module 3 questions, allocate **3 minutes of thinking time** before writing. Draw a **rough sketch** of the characteristic curve first. Then write the **formula**, substitute **values**, and present the **final answer with units** in a **box**. This is the standard KTU answer-writing pattern that guarantees maximum marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pump Performance Curves and System Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pump Performance Curves and System Curves # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pump Performance Curve (Characteristic Curve):** A graphical representation that depicts the functional relationship between the **head (H)** developed by a centrifugal pump and the **volumetric flow rate (Q)** delivered by the pump, typically at a constant impeller rotational speed (N) and impeller diameter (D). It is also called the **H–Q curve** or the **Head–Capacity Curve**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **System Curve (Pipe System Curve):** A graphical representation of the **total head (H_sys)** required by the piping network (friction losses + static head + velocity head + minor losses) as a function of the **volumetric flow rate (Q)** passing through it. It characterizes the *load* offered by the fluid system onto the pump. > [!NOTE] > **Operating Point (Duty Point):** The unique point of intersection of the **Pump Characteristic Curve** and the **System Resistance Curve**. At this point, the energy supplied by the pump exactly matches the energy demanded by the system — establishing a state of dynamic equilibrium. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) Imagine you are **blowing air into a balloon** through a thin straw: - The **pump** is *you* — your lungs supply a certain pressure. - The **system (straw + balloon)** resists airflow due to friction and the elasticity of the balloon. - The **Pump Curve** is like saying: *"If I blow softly (low H), lots of air flows (high Q); if I blow hard (high H), less air flows (low Q)"* — a falling curve. - The **System Curve** is the balloon's complaint: *"If you want more air to flow, you must blow harder (because friction increases quadratically with velocity)"* — a rising parabola. - The **Operating Point** is the *single* flow rate and pressure at which your blowing exactly matches the balloon's demand. In engineering, the **pump offers energy**, and the **system demands energy**. Where they agree is where the system runs. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²** - **Density of water, ρ = 1000 kg/m³** - **Specific weight of water, γ = ρg = 9810 N/m³** - Standard units: **Head in metres (m)**, **Flow in m³/s or L/s**, **Power in Watts (W) or kW** - **Rotational speed, N** in **rpm (revolutions per minute)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Pump Performance (H–Q) Curve The performance curve of a centrifugal pump arises from the **Euler Turbomachine Equation** combined with experimental observations. ### 2.1.1 Origin of the Shape (Why H decreases with Q) For a centrifugal pump, applying the velocity triangle at impeller inlet and outlet, the theoretical head is: $$H_{th} = \frac{U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}}{g}$$ where: - $U_1, U_2$ = blade tangential velocities at inlet and outlet - $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = whirl (tangential) components of absolute velocity As flow rate **Q** increases: - The radial component of absolute velocity $V_f$ increases - This causes the absolute velocity vector to tilt away from the tangential direction - Thus the **whirl component $V_{w2}$ decreases** - Therefore, **H decreases** as Q increases (typically a near-linear or parabolic drop) ### 2.1.2 Typical Pump Curve Features > [!NOTE] > **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** The point on the H–Q curve at which pump efficiency is maximum. Designers prefer operation near BEP. Other features include: - **Shut-off head ($H_0$):** Head developed at Q = 0 (valve fully closed) - **Maximum flow ($Q_{max}$):** Flow at which head approaches zero - **Stable operating region:** Usually between 0.5 × BEP and 1.5 × BEP ## 2.2 The System (Resistance) Curve The system head is the sum of four components: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + H_{friction} + H_{minor} + H_{velocity}$$ ### 2.2.1 Component Breakdown 1. **Static Head ($H_{static}$):** Constant elevation difference between source and delivery point (independent of Q) 2. **Friction Head Loss ($H_{friction}$):** Darcy–Weisbach equation 3. **Minor Losses ($H_{minor}$):** Due to fittings, valves, bends 4. **Velocity Head ($H_{velocity}$):** Kinetic energy at discharge (often negligible or lumped) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Euler Head | $H = \frac{U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}}{g}$ | m | Theoretical | | 2 | Pump Power (Water) | $P = \rho g Q H$ | W | Hydraulic power | | 3 | Shaft Power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | W | Input to shaft | | 4 | Darcy–Weisbach Friction | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | m | Pipe friction | | 5 | Velocity in Pipe | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s | From continuity | | 6 | Friction head in terms of Q | $h_f = \dfrac{8 f L}{\pi^2 g D^5} Q^2 = K Q^2$ | m | Parabolic in Q | | 7 | System Curve | $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K Q^2$ | m | Q in m³/s | | 8 | Pump Affinity – Speed | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | — | Same impeller | | 9 | Pump Affinity – Head | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | — | Same impeller | | 10 | Pump Affinity – Power | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | — | Same impeller | | 11 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | — | Dimensional form | | 12 | NPSH Available | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_v}{\rho g} \pm h_s - h_f$ | m | Cavitation check | | 13 | Pumps in Series (same Q) | $H_{total} = H_1 + H_2$ ; $Q_{total} = Q$ | m | Same flow | | 14 | Pumps in Parallel (same H) | $Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2$ ; $H_{total} = H$ | m | Same head | | 15 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{hydraulic}$ | — | Product form | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the system curve equation $H_{sys} = H_{static} + KQ^2$, the **constant K** bundles together all friction, minor, and velocity losses in the form $K = \dfrac{8fL}{\pi^2 g D^5} + \sum K_{minor}$. This is the single most-tested formula in Module 3. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pump selection:** Engineers overlay system curves on manufacturer pump curves to choose the right impeller diameter and speed. - **Energy audit:** The operating point reveals if a pump is oversized (low efficiency) — common in HVAC and water-supply industries. - **Variable speed drives (VSD):** Using affinity laws, one can predict the new duty point when pump speed is varied — critical for energy savings in process plants. - **Cavitation prevention:** Comparing NPSH available with NPSH required ensures the pump does not cavitate at the chosen operating point. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: System Head as a Function of Flow Rate We start with the **Total Head Equation** of a piping system. The energy equation between reservoir surface (1) and discharge point (2), assuming both at atmospheric pressure and negligible surface velocities: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ Since $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ and $V_1 \approx 0$: $$z_1 - z_2 = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L$$ Define **static head** $H_{static} = z_1 - z_2$ (elevation rise), then: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + h_L$$ Now apply the **Darcy–Weisbach equation** for friction in a straight pipe of length L, diameter D: $$h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ Group all minor losses using loss coefficient $K_i$ for each fitting: $$h_m = \sum \frac{K_i V^2}{2g}$$ Add the exit velocity head $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ (also a minor loss with $K=1$): $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + \left( \frac{V^2}{2g} \right) + f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g} + \sum K_i \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ Factor out $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + \left( 1 + f\frac{L}{D} + \sum K_i \right) \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ From **continuity**, $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$. Substituting: $$V^2 = \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4}$$ Therefore: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + \left( 1 + f\frac{L}{D} + \sum K_i \right) \frac{1}{2g} \cdot \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4}$$ Simplify the constants: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + \underbrace{\left[ \frac{8}{\pi^2 g D^4} \left( 1 + f\frac{L}{D} + \sum K_i \right) \right]}_{K} Q^2$$ This gives the elegant **quadratic system curve**: $$\boxed{H_{sys} = H_{static} + K Q^2}$$ The constant **K** has units of $\dfrac{m}{(m^3/s)^2}$. ## 3.2 Derivation: Pump Affinity Laws (Speed Change) The affinity laws are obtained from dimensional similarity between geometrically similar pumps operating at different speeds. The relevant velocity ratios are: $$\frac{U_2}{U_1} = \frac{\pi D_2 N_2}{\pi D_1 N_1} = \frac{D_2 N_2}{D_1 N_1}$$ For the same impeller (D₁ = D₂ = D), the ratio simplifies to N₂/N₁. Now, head $H \propto U V_w \propto N^2 D^2$ (since velocity components all scale linearly with rotational speed). Flow rate $Q \propto V_f A \propto N D^3$, so $Q \propto N$ for fixed D. Power $P \propto \rho Q H \propto N^3 D^5$, so $P \propto N^3$ for fixed D. ### 3.2.1 Affinity Law 1 (Flow) $$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$ ### 3.2.2 Affinity Law 2 (Head) $$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$ ### 3.2.3 Affinity Law 3 (Power) $$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Speed Change on Operating Point | Quantity | At Speed N₁ | At Speed N₂ = k·N₁ | Scaling Factor | |----------|-------------|---------------------|----------------| | Flow rate | Q₁ | Q₂ = k·Q₁ | Linear | | Head | H₁ | H₂ = k²·H₁ | Quadratic | | Power | P₁ | P₂ = k³·P₁ | Cubic | | System curve | Same K, same H_static | Unchanged | — | | New operating point | (Q₁, H₁) | (k·Q₁, k²·H₁) | Lies on parabola | > [!IMPORTANT] > When speed changes, **the system curve does NOT change** (since the pipe network is fixed). Only the pump curve shifts. The new operating point is found by intersecting the new pump curve with the *same* system curve. ## 3.4 Derivation: Pumps in Series and Parallel ### 3.4.1 Pumps in Series When two identical pumps are connected in series, the same flow Q passes through each, and the heads add: $$H_{series}(Q) = H_{pump}(Q) + H_{pump}(Q) = 2 H_{pump}(Q)$$ **Result:** Doubles the head capability at any given flow. Used when the system demands high head (e.g., high-rise building water supply, boiler feed). ### 3.4.2 Pumps in Parallel When two identical pumps operate in parallel, they share the same head, and flows add: $$Q_{parallel}(H) = Q_{pump}(H) + Q_{pump}(H) = 2 Q_{pump}(H)$$ **Result:** Doubles the flow capability at any given head. Used when the system demands high flow (e.g., cooling water circulation in power plants). ### 3.4.3 Operating Point Calculation (Series) For two identical pumps each with curve $H_p(Q) = H_0 - aQ^2$ in series: $$H_{series}(Q) = 2H_0 - 2aQ^2$$ Setting equal to system curve $H_{sys} = H_{static} + KQ^2$: $$2H_0 - 2aQ^2 = H_{static} + KQ^2$$ Solving for Q: $$\boxed{Q_{series} = \sqrt{\frac{2H_0 - H_{static}}{2a + K}}}$$ ### 3.4.4 Operating Point Calculation (Parallel) For parallel pumps, since each pump sees total flow Q, the head of each pump at flow Q must equal the system head: $$H_p(Q) = H_{sys}(Q)$$ But each pump delivers only $Q/2$. The head developed by *one* pump at flow $Q/2$ is: $$H_p\left(\frac{Q}{2}\right) = H_0 - a\left(\frac{Q}{2}\right)^2 = H_0 - \frac{aQ^2}{4}$$ Setting equal to system head: $$H_0 - \frac{aQ^2}{4} = H_{static} + KQ^2$$ Solving for Q: $$\boxed{Q_{parallel} = \sqrt{\frac{4(H_0 - H_{static})}{a + 4K}}}$$ ## 3.5 Derivation: Specific Speed Specific speed is a dimensionless number used to classify pump impellers. It is the speed of a *geometrically similar* pump that would deliver 1 m³/s at 1 m head. Starting from the affinity laws, eliminate D to get the relationship between N, Q, and H for similar pumps. The dimensional specific speed is: $$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$ - **Low Ns (300–1500):** Radial flow pumps (high head, low flow) - **Medium Ns (1500–6000):** Mixed flow pumps - **High Ns (6000–12000+):** Axial flow pumps (low head, high flow) ## 3.6 Derivation: NPSH Available Applying the energy equation from reservoir surface (1) to pump impeller inlet (eye, point 2): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_{f,1-2}$$ With $V_1 \approx 0$, $P_1 = P_{atm}$ (open reservoir), $z_1 - z_2 = h_s$ (suction lift positive when reservoir is below pump; negative when flooded suction), and $h_{f,1-2}$ = friction loss in suction pipe: $$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_s + h_{f,1-2}$$ Solving for $P_2/(\rho g)$ (the absolute pressure head at the impeller eye): $$\frac{P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_s - h_{f,1-2} - \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ The **NPSH available** is defined as the head by which the absolute pressure at the impeller eye exceeds the vapor pressure of the liquid: $$NPSH_A = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho g}$$ Substituting: $$\boxed{NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_{f,1-2} - \frac{V_2^2}{2g}}$$ The pump will cavitate if $NPSH_A < NPSH_R$ (the manufacturer's required value). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pump Performance Curve — Typical Shape (H–Q) This is the standard H–Q characteristic of a centrifugal pump, showing key features. ```mermaid graph LR A["High Head
Shut-off H0"] --> B["Head decreases
with increasing Q"] B --> C["Low Head
Maximum Q"] D["BEP Marker"] -.-> E["Best Efficiency Point
max eta"] F["Stable region"] -.-> G["0.5 BEP to 1.5 BEP"] H["Q axis (Flow)"] --> I["0 to Qmax"] J["H axis (Head)"] --> K["0 to H0"] L["Efficiency curves"] --- M["eta increases to BEP then drops"] ``` ## 4.2 System Curve — Typical Parabolic Shape ```mermaid graph LR A["Hstatic
at Q=0"] --> B["Curve rises as K*Q squared"] B --> C["H at operating flow Qop"] D["Static head line"] -.-> E["Horizontal baseline"] F["Parabolic rise"] --> G["K Q squared term"] ``` ## 4.3 Operating Point Determination (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD A["Pump H-Q curve H_pump of Q"] --> C["Plot both on same graph"] B["System H-Q curve H_sys = H_static + K Q squared"] --> C C["Find intersection point P"] P["Operating Point: Q_op, H_op"] D["Q_op determines flow delivered"] E["H_op determines head provided"] F["Eta at Q_op gives pump efficiency"] G["P_shaft = rho g Q H / eta"] P --> D P --> E P --> F P --> G ``` ## 4.4 Pumps in Series vs Parallel — Operating Point Shift ```mermaid graph LR A["Original Pump H-Q curve"] --> B["Single Pump operating point P1"] C["2x Head in Series"] --> D["Series operating point P_series: Higher H, Same Q"] E["2x Flow in Parallel"] --> F["Parallel operating point P_parallel: Higher Q, Same H"] G["System curve H_sys"] --- B G --- D G --- F ``` ## 4.5 NPSH Margin Analysis Flow ```mermaid graph TD A["Reservoir pressure P_atm"] --> B["Subtract P_v / rho g"] B --> C["Subtract suction lift h_s"] C --> D["Subtract suction friction h_f"] D --> E["Subtract V_eye squared over 2g"] E --> F["NPSH_A value"] G["Manufacturer NPSH_R for Q_op"] --> H{"NPSH_A > NPSH_R ?"} F --> H H -->|Yes| I["Safe: No Cavitation"] H -->|No| J["Cavitation Risk: Reduce h_s, enlarge D, lower T"] ``` ## 4.6 Specific Speed Classification Map ```mermaid graph TD A["Pump Selection by N_s"] --> B["N_s less than 1500: Radial"] A --> C["N_s 1500 to 6000: Mixed"] A --> D["N_s greater than 6000: Axial"] B --> B1["High head, low flow"] C --> C1["Medium head, medium flow"] D --> D1["Low head, high flow"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define operating point of a centrifugal pump. What is its significance?** **Model Answer:** The operating point (or duty point) of a centrifugal pump is the unique point of intersection between the **pump characteristic curve (H–Q curve)** and the **system resistance curve (H_sys–Q curve)**. At this point, the head supplied by the pump exactly matches the head demanded by the piping system, establishing a steady-state equilibrium. **Significance:** - Determines the actual flow rate $Q_{op}$ delivered and head $H_{op}$ developed at installation. - Indicates the pump's efficiency $\eta$ at that point (ideally near BEP). - Used to compute shaft power $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{op} H_{op}}{\eta}$. - A shift in operating point (e.g., valve throttling, speed change) directly impacts energy consumption and system performance. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is meant by Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)? Differentiate between NPSH available and NPSH required.** **Model Answer:** **NPSH** is the total head available at the pump suction nozzle over and above the vapor pressure head of the liquid, expressed in metres of liquid. It is the energy margin that prevents the liquid from flashing into vapor at the impeller eye (cavitation). **NPSH Available ($NPSH_A$):** Determined by the *system* — atmospheric pressure, vapor pressure, suction lift/head, suction pipe friction, and velocity head at the impeller eye. $$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\rho g} - h_s - h_{f,suction} - \frac{V_{eye}^2}{2g}$$ **NPSH Required ($NPSH_R$):** Determined by the *pump design* (impeller eye geometry, speed, flow rate). It is the minimum head needed to keep cavitation away, and is read from the manufacturer's curve at the operating flow. **Cavitation condition:** Pump operates safely if $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$ (typically with a safety margin of 0.5 to 1.0 m). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **A centrifugal pump delivers water at a flow rate of 0.05 m³/s against a head of 20 m. The pump has an efficiency of 75%. Calculate (i) the power input to the pump, (ii) the power lost in the pump, and (iii) the electrical energy consumed in 8 hours of operation.** **Model Solution:** Given: - $Q = 0.05$ m³/s - $H = 20$ m - $\eta_{pump} = 0.75$ - $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² - $t = 8$ hours **Step 1: Hydraulic (water) power output** $$P_{water} = \rho g Q H$$ $$P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 20$$ $$P_{water} = 9810 \text{ W} = 9.81 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 2: Power input to pump (shaft power)** $$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{9.81}{0.75} = 13.08 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Power lost in the pump** $$P_{lost} = P_{shaft} - P_{water} = 13.08 - 9.81 = 3.27 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4: Electrical energy consumed in 8 hours** $$E = P_{shaft} \times t = 13.08 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ h} = 104.64 \text{ kWh}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake:** Students often confuse the *water power* with the *shaft power*. Remember: $P_{water}$ is the *useful* output delivered to the fluid, while $P_{shaft}$ is what the motor must supply. The ratio is the *overall efficiency*, not 1. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **The H–Q characteristic of a centrifugal pump is approximated as $H_p = 30 - 0.5 Q^2$ (H in m, Q in m³/s). The system curve is $H_{sys} = 5 + 1.5 Q^2$. Determine (i) the operating flow rate and head, (ii) the power transferred to water, and (iii) the shaft power if pump efficiency is 70%.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: At the operating point, $H_p = H_{sys}$** $$30 - 0.5 Q^2 = 5 + 1.5 Q^2$$ **Step 2: Solve for Q** $$30 - 5 = 1.5 Q^2 + 0.5 Q^2$$ $$25 = 2.0 Q^2$$ $$Q^2 = 12.5$$ $$Q_{op} = \sqrt{12.5} = 3.536 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3: Operating head** $$H_{op} = 5 + 1.5 (3.536)^2 = 5 + 1.5 \times 12.5 = 5 + 18.75 = 23.75 \text{ m}$$ **Verification using pump curve:** $H_p = 30 - 0.5 \times 12.5 = 30 - 6.25 = 23.75$ m ✓ **Step 4: Hydraulic power** $$P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3.536 \times 23.75$$ $$P_{water} = 823{,}801 \text{ W} \approx 823.8 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Shaft power at 70% efficiency** $$P_{shaft} = \frac{823.8}{0.70} = 1176.9 \text{ kW} \approx 1.177 \text{ MW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake:** Students sometimes forget to use the *Q²* form directly from the given equation (with Q in m³/s already). Always substitute the *numerical value* of Q (not Q²) into the quadratic term: $1.5 \times (3.536)^2$, not $1.5 \times 3.536$. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the affinity laws for a centrifugal pump. A pump running at 1450 rpm delivers 0.04 m³/s at 25 m head. If the speed is changed to 1750 rpm, calculate the new flow rate, head, and power. Assume pump efficiency remains 80%.** **Model Solution:** **Affinity Laws Explanation:** The affinity laws relate the performance of geometrically similar centrifugal pumps (or the same pump at different speeds). For the same impeller: - **Flow affinity:** $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ - **Head affinity:** $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$ - **Power affinity:** $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ These laws arise from dimensional analysis of the Euler turbomachine equation and are valid for a fixed impeller diameter with negligible Reynolds and Mach number effects. **Given:** - $N_1 = 1450$ rpm, $Q_1 = 0.04$ m³/s, $H_1 = 25$ m, $\eta = 0.80$ - $N_2 = 1750$ rpm **Step 1: Speed ratio** $$k = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1750}{1450} = 1.2069$$ **Step 2: New flow rate** $$Q_2 = k \cdot Q_1 = 1.2069 \times 0.04 = 0.04828 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3: New head** $$H_2 = k^2 \cdot H_1 = (1.2069)^2 \times 25 = 1.4566 \times 25 = 36.41 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Original shaft power** $$P_1 = \frac{\rho g Q_1 H_1}{\eta} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 25}{0.80}$$ $$P_1 = \frac{9810}{0.80} = 12{,}262.5 \text{ W} = 12.26 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: New shaft power** $$P_2 = k^3 \cdot P_1 = (1.2069)^3 \times 12.26 = 1.7581 \times 12.26 = 21.55 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake:** Some students apply $k^3$ to the *hydraulic* power instead of the *shaft* power. The affinity law for power refers to the input (shaft) power. Also, do not confuse the *speed ratio* with the *flow ratio* — they are equal but apply to different variables. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Two identical centrifugal pumps each with characteristic $H_p = 25 - 0.4 Q^2$ (H in m, Q in m³/s) are available. The system curve is $H_{sys} = 5 + 1.0 Q^2$. Calculate the operating point (i) with a single pump, (ii) with both pumps in series, and (iii) with both pumps in parallel.** **Model Solution:** **(i) Single Pump Operation** At operating point, $H_p = H_{sys}$: $$25 - 0.4 Q^2 = 5 + 1.0 Q^2$$ $$20 = 1.4 Q^2$$ $$Q_1 = \sqrt{\frac{20}{1.4}} = \sqrt{14.286} = 3.780 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$H_1 = 5 + 1.0 \times 14.286 = 19.29 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Pumps in Series** In series, head doubles at same Q. Equivalent pump curve: $$H_{series} = 2(25 - 0.4 Q^2) = 50 - 0.8 Q^2$$ Setting equal to system curve: $$50 - 0.8 Q^2 = 5 + 1.0 Q^2$$ $$45 = 1.8 Q^2$$ $$Q_{series} = \sqrt{\frac{45}{1.8}} = \sqrt{25} = 5.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$H_{series} = 5 + 1.0 \times 25 = 30.0 \text{ m}$$ **(iii) Pumps in Parallel** In parallel, each pump sees the same head but delivers only Q/2. So $H_p$ for one pump at total flow Q is: $$H_{one}(Q) = 25 - 0.4 \left(\frac{Q}{2}\right)^2 = 25 - 0.1 Q^2$$ Setting equal to system curve: $$25 - 0.1 Q^2 = 5 + 1.0 Q^2$$ $$20 = 1.1 Q^2$$ $$Q_{parallel} = \sqrt{\frac{20}{1.1}} = \sqrt{18.18} = 4.264 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$H_{parallel} = 5 + 1.0 \times 18.18 = 23.18 \text{ m}$$ **Summary Comparison:** | Configuration | Q (m³/s) | H (m) | |---------------|----------|-------| | Single Pump | 3.78 | 19.29 | | Two in Series | 5.00 | 30.00 | | Two in Parallel | 4.26 | 23.18 | > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake in parallel calculation:** A frequent error is to simply *add the flow rates at the system head*, i.e., $Q_{parallel} = 2 Q_1$. This is **wrong** when the pump curve is not linear. The correct method is to *derive a new combined curve* by adding flow rates at each head, then intersect with the system curve. For a quadratic pump curve $H_p = H_0 - aQ^2$, the combined parallel curve becomes $H_{parallel}(Q) = H_0 - a(Q/2)^2$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pump Performance & System Curves** - ✅ **Pump H–Q curve** is *decreasing* (more flow → less head) due to velocity triangle geometry. - ✅ **System H–Q curve** is *increasing* (more flow → more friction loss), with shape $H_{sys} = H_{static} + KQ^2$. - ✅ **Operating point** is the *intersection* of the two curves — neither curve is sufficient alone. - ✅ **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is where the pump should ideally operate; avoid running far from it. - ✅ **Affinity laws** — $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ — are valid for the *same impeller*. - ✅ **Pumps in series** → same flow, **heads add** → use for high-head systems. - ✅ **Pumps in parallel** → same head, **flows add** → use for high-flow systems. - ✅ **NPSH Available > NPSH Required** is the universal cavitation-prevention rule; build in a safety margin of 0.5–1.0 m. - ✅ **Hydraulic power** $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$ — multiply by $\eta$ to get *shaft power*. - ✅ **Specific speed** classifies pumps: low Ns → radial, high Ns → axial. - ✅ Always check **units consistency**: $Q$ in m³/s for SI power calculations, g in m/s², ρ in kg/m³. - ✅ When the system curve equation is given in the form $H_{sys} = A + BQ^2$, the constant $A$ is the static head and $B$ is the *combined loss coefficient*. - ✅ The system curve **does not change** when pump speed changes — only the pump curve shifts. - ✅ Reading pump curves: $H_0$ = shut-off head, $Q_{max}$ = free-delivery flow, BEP = peak efficiency. - ✅ For two identical pumps, **parallel operation increases Q by less than 2×** when the system has high friction (large K). > 🎯 **Exam Tip:** Whenever a problem provides both a pump equation $H_p(Q)$ and a system equation $H_{sys}(Q)$, the *very first step* is to set them equal. Most Module 3 marks are lost by students who write the formula but fail to equate them correctly.

topics.title: Cavitation and Its Prevention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cavitation and Its Prevention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Cavitation** is a hydrodynamic phenomenon defined as the formation, growth, and sudden violent collapse of vapor-filled cavities (or bubbles) within a liquid when the local static pressure of the liquid falls **below the vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature, followed by the re-entry of liquid into these cavities when the local pressure recovers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation of vapor bubbles or cavities in a liquid, when local static pressure of the liquid falls below the vapor pressure of the liquid at that temperature. It is a critical design limitation in hydraulic turbines, centrifugal pumps, and high-speed liquid flow systems.

**Cavitation Number (Dimensionless Cavitation Parameter, σ):**
The primary non-dimensional metric that quantifies the susceptibility of a hydraulic machine to cavitation, defined as the ratio of the difference between local static pressure and vapor pressure to the dynamic pressure of the flow.

**NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head):**
A pressure-based metric expressed in meters of liquid, representing the margin by which the absolute pressure at the pump suction exceeds the vapor pressure of the liquid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Boiling Water in a Cooling Engine" Analogy**
> Imagine you are driving a car on a hot summer day (engine coolant at 90°C). If the radiator cap fails and a sudden pressure drop occurs in the coolant passage, the coolant will *literally boil* even though its temperature has not changed. Tiny vapor pockets erupt inside the system. When these bubbles are swept into a region of higher pressure (e.g., near the pump impeller), they implode with a popping sound — like tiny underwater lightning strikes. This is **cavitation**.

**In simple words:**
1. **Where pressure is too low** → water "boils" inside the machine (even cold).
2. **Where pressure becomes high again** → bubbles **collapse violently**.
3. **The collapse** is what damages metal, makes noise, vibrates the shaft, and kills efficiency.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Vapor Pressure of Water at 30°C:** $P_v \approx 4.246 \text{ kPa}$ (abs)
- **Vapor Pressure of Water at 100°C:** $P_v = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ (abs)
- **Atmospheric Pressure (sea level):** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Specific Weight of Water:** $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- **Density of Water:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific Gas Constant of Water Vapor:** $R = 461.5 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cavitation Matters in KTU Exams:** It affects (a) Pump head calculation, (b) Turbine efficiency, (c) Specific speed limits, and (d) Selection of suction conditions. A 2-mark or 14-mark question on cavitation is a **guaranteed question** in every KTU ESE for this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Mechanism of Cavitation (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Pressure Drop in Flow Streamlines:**
As fluid moves through a converging passage (e.g., inlet of an impeller eye, or throat of a venturi), its velocity increases. By **Bernoulli's Principle**, the static pressure must decrease correspondingly.

**Step 2 — Threshold Condition Reached:**
When local static pressure $P$ falls to or below the vapor pressure $P_v$ of the liquid at operating temperature:

$$P \leq P_v$$

Tiny vapor bubbles (cavities) nucleate at microscopic impurities, surface imperfections, or dissolved gas pockets.

**Step 3 — Bubble Growth in Low-Pressure Zone:**
Bubbles grow rapidly as more liquid vaporizes into them. They may coalesce into larger cavities or "rope-like" vapor structures.

**Step 4 — Transport to High-Pressure Zone:**
The fluid stream carries these vapor pockets downstream into regions of higher pressure (e.g., discharge side of pump impeller, runner exit of turbine).

**Step 5 — Violent Bubble Collapse (Implosion):**
When pressure in the surrounding fluid exceeds the internal vapor pressure, the bubbles collapse asymmetrically. This collapse generates:
- **Micro-jets** of liquid striking surfaces at velocities up to **100–200 m/s**.
- **Shock waves** of order 100–1000 MPa locally.
- **Loud crackling noise** (often confused with "pumping stones").
- **Mechanical erosion** of metal surfaces (pitting).
- **Vibration** and potential **shaft failure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Collapse Damage:** A collapsing bubble cannot shrink spherically because the surrounding flow is non-uniform. It collapses into a high-velocity micro-jet that strikes the nearest solid surface, mechanically eroding it over time. This is why cavitation damage typically appears as pitted, spongy metal on pump impellers and turbine runners.

### 2.2 Two Critical Parameters in Cavitation Analysis

#### A. Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

NPSH is the **available head** above the vapor pressure at the suction nozzle of a pump (or draft tube inlet of a turbine).

**For Pumps (Suction Lift Positive):**

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

Where:
- $P_{atm}$ = Absolute atmospheric pressure at liquid surface (Pa)
- $P_v$ = Vapor pressure of liquid at pumping temperature (Pa)
- $H_s$ = Static suction lift (m) — *positive if liquid is below pump centerline*
- $h_f$ = Friction head loss in suction line (m)

**For Pumps (Suction Head Submerged):**

$$\text{NPSH}_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + H_s - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} - h_f$$

Where $H_s$ is now the **positive** submergence depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NPSH Rule of Thumb (KTU Examination Favorite):**
> A pump will operate **WITHOUT cavitation** ONLY IF:
> $$\text{NPSH}_A \geq \text{NPSH}_R$$
> Where $\text{NPSH}_R$ is the **required NPSH** (manufacturer-specified). If $\text{NPSH}_A < \text{NPSH}_R$, the pump cavitates and the head/flow performance drops sharply.

#### B. Thoma's Cavitation Parameter (σ)

A **dimensionless number** that normalizes the cavitation tendency. It expresses the ratio of the **excess pressure head** above vapor pressure to the **work head** of the machine.

**For Reaction Turbines (Francis, Kaplan):**

$$\sigma = \frac{NPSH_A}{H}$$

Where $H$ is the **net head** developed by the turbine (m).

**Critical Cavitation Condition:**

$$\sigma_c = \left(\frac{NPSH_A}{H}\right)_{critical}$$

**Operating Criterion:**

- $\sigma > \sigma_c$ → **Safe Operation (No Cavitation)**
- $\sigma = \sigma_c$ → **Incipient Cavitation** (cavitation just begins)
- $\sigma < \sigma_c$ → **Severe Cavitation** (efficiency drops, damage occurs)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **condensed reference** of all cavitation formulas tested in KTU ESE. **Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Application | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\sigma = (P - P_v) / (0.5 \rho V^2)$ | **General Cavitation Number** (Bernoulli-based) | $P, P_v$ in Pa; $V$ in m/s |
| 2 | $NPSH_A = (P_{atm}/\rho g) - (P_v/\rho g) - H_s - h_f$ | **Pump with suction lift** | All heads in m |
| 3 | $NPSH_A = (P_{atm}/\rho g) + H_s - (P_v/\rho g) - h_f$ | **Pump with submerged suction** | All heads in m |
| 4 | $\sigma_T = NPSH_A / H$ | **Thoma's Cavitation Factor (Turbines)** | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma_T \geq \sigma_c$ | **Cavitation-free Operation** | Both dimensionless |
| 6 | $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ | **Specific Speed (pump)** | $N$ in rpm, $P$ in kW, $H$ in m |
| 7 | $H_{sv} = (P_{atm} - P_v)/\rho g$ | **Maximum theoretical suction lift** | m of liquid |
| 8 | $P_v = P_{sat}(T)$ | **Vapor Pressure (from steam tables)** | Pa (abs) |
| 9 | $\rho g h = P$ | **Pressure-Head conversion** | $h$ in m, $P$ in Pa |
| 10 | $H_{atm} = P_{atm}/\rho g \approx 10.33$ m (water) | **Atmospheric pressure head** | m of water |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Cavitation Analysis |
|-------|-----------------------------------|
| **Hydraulic Turbines** | Setting the turbine elevation above tailwater to avoid draft-tube cavitation |
| **Centrifugal Pumps** | Calculating maximum permissible suction lift to prevent NPSH failure |
| **Marine Propellers** | Designing blade tip speeds below cavitation inception velocity |
| **Hydraulic Systems** | Sizing accumulators to damp pressure transients and prevent vapor formation |
| **Fuel Injection** | Designing diesel injector nozzles to atomize fuel via controlled cavitation |
| **Ultrasonic Cleaning** | Leveraging intentional cavitation for surface cleaning applications |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: General Cavitation Number from Bernoulli's Equation

**Starting Point:** Consider a fluid flowing in a stream-tube with local static pressure $P$ and velocity $V$. Cavitation is incipient when $P = P_v$.

**Bernoulli's Equation (incompressible, no elevation change, in pressure form):**

$$P + \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 = P_{ref} = \text{constant along streamline}$$

**Rearranging for the excess pressure head above vapor pressure:**

$$P - P_v = P_{ref} - P_v - \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

At the critical (cavitation-inception) condition, the **excess** is just sufficient to prevent the dynamic pressure from dropping pressure below vapor. Therefore, the **cavitation number** is defined as the dimensionless ratio:

$$\sigma = \frac{P - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A small $\sigma$ means the local pressure is close to vapor pressure → **cavitation likely**. A large $\sigma$ means a strong pressure margin → **cavitation unlikely**.

### 3.2 Derivation II: NPSH_A Derivation for a Pump with Suction Lift

**Physical Setup:** A pump draws water from a sump whose water surface is **below** the pump centerline by a vertical distance $H_s$ (suction lift). Atmospheric pressure acts on the sump surface.

**Apply Bernoulli's Equation** from point 1 (sump water surface) to point 2 (pump suction nozzle eye):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

**Assumptions:**
- $V_1 \approx 0$ (large sump, surface velocity negligible)
- $P_1 = P_{atm}$ (open to atmosphere)
- $z_1 - z_2 = H_s$ (vertical lift to pump eye)
- $P_2 = P_{suction}$ (absolute pressure at pump eye)

**Substitute and solve for $P_2$:**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{suction}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + H_s + h_f$$

$$\frac{P_{suction}}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f - \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

**Subtract the vapor pressure head** $P_v/(\rho g)$ to find the available head **above vapor pressure**:

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{suction}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f - \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Approximation (commonly used in KTU problems):** The kinetic energy head $V_2^2 / (2g)$ is often *small* and is sometimes lumped into $h_f$ or neglected. In most KTU numerical problems, it is excluded unless explicitly stated. The most common exam form is:
> $$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

### 3.3 Derivation III: Critical Cavitation Parameter from Specific Speed Correlation

For **Francis and Kaplan turbines**, the critical cavitation parameter $\sigma_c$ is empirically related to the **specific speed** $N_s$:

$$\sigma_c = K \cdot N_s^x$$

**Common empirical correlations (use values given in your KTU problem):**

| Author | Correlation | Application |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Thoma (Original)** | $\sigma_c = 0.625 \cdot N_s^{2/3}$ | Francis turbines (European data) |
| **Moody** | $\sigma_c = 0.0014 N_s^{1.5}$ | US practice, all reaction turbines |
| **Knauss / Stepanoff** | $\sigma_c = 0.00154 N_s^{1.5}$ | Modern high-speed turbines |

Where $N_s$ is in **SI (metric) units:**

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU-Favorite):** As specific speed $N_s$ increases, the **critical cavitation parameter $\sigma_c$ increases**, meaning the turbine becomes **more susceptible to cavitation**. This is why **Kaplan turbines (high $N_s$)** must be set very close to tailwater, while **Pelton wheels (low $N_s$)** are essentially immune to cavitation.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU ESE Style)

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump delivers water at 30°C from a sump whose water surface is 4 m below the pump centerline. The suction pipe is 100 mm diameter and 6 m long. Friction factor $f = 0.025$. Atmospheric pressure is 98 kPa (abs). Vapor pressure of water at 30°C is 4.246 kPa (abs). Calculate the NPSH available.

**Step 1 — Convert pressures to heads:**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{98{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 9.99 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{4246}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.433 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute friction head loss:**

$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{6}{0.1} = 60$$

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = 4 \times 0.025 \times 60 \times \frac{V^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 6 \times \frac{V^2}{19.62} = 0.3058 \cdot V^2$$

**Step 3 — Assume a typical pump flow velocity. If $V = 2.5$ m/s:**

$$h_f = 0.3058 \times (2.5)^2 = 1.91 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply NPSH formula (suction lift case):**

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

$$NPSH_A = 9.99 - 0.433 - 4 - 1.91 = 3.65 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

$$NPSH_A = 3.65 \text{ m} \quad \text{(Available margin above vapor pressure)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Hint:** The pump will cavitate only if $\text{NPSH}_A < \text{NPSH}_R$. In a complete exam problem, $\text{NPSH}_R$ is given (say 3.0 m). Here $3.65 > 3.0$ → **Pump operates safely.** If the value had been $2.5$ m, the pump would cavitate.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Cavitation Effects vs. Prevention

| Effect of Cavitation | Root Cause | Prevention Method | Engineering Countermeasure |
|----------------------|------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| **Loss of Efficiency** | Vapor pockets displace water → reduced mass flow | Increase suction pressure | Lower pump elevation, use submergence |
| **Pitting Erosion** | Micro-jet impact on metal surfaces | Reduce local pressure drops | Use cavitation-resistant alloys (e.g., Ni-Al bronze) |
| **Noise & Vibration** | Asymmetric bubble collapse shock waves | Smooth flow passages | Polish impeller surfaces, eliminate sharp edges |
| **Shaft Seizure** | Asymmetric thrust from vapor-filled regions | Ensure adequate NPSH | Install booster pump in suction line |
| **Drop in Head/Discharge** | Vapor disrupts momentum transfer | Increase atmospheric/submergence pressure | Use suction accumulator |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Cavitation Mechanism and Prevention Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Liquid Enters Suction Line"] --> B{"Local Static Pressure"}
    B -- "P greater than Pv" --> C["Normal Liquid Flow, No Cavitation"]
    B -- "P falls to or below Pv" --> D["Vapor Bubbles Nucleate and Grow"]
    D --> E["Bubbles Swept into High-Pressure Zone"]
    E --> F["Violent Bubble Collapse Implosion"]
    F --> G1["Noise and Vibration"]
    F --> G2["Pitting Erosion of Metal"]
    F --> G3["Loss of Efficiency and Head"]
    F --> G4["Shaft Seizure in Severe Cases"]
    G1 --> H["Cavitation Damage Identified"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I["Prevention Strategies Activated"]
    I --> J1["Increase Suction Pressure NPSH"]
    I --> J2["Reduce Suction Lift Height"]
    I --> J3["Lower Liquid Operating Temperature"]
    I --> J4["Use Cavitation Resistant Materials"]
    I --> J5["Polish Flow Passages and Inlets"]
    I --> J6["Select Specific Speed Below Critical"]
    J1 --> K["Cavitation Free Operation Restored"]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    J5 --> K
    J6 --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: NPSH Evaluation Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Atmospheric Pressure Head"] --> N["NPSH Available Calculation"]
    B["Vapor Pressure Head at Operating Temperature"] --> N
    C["Static Suction Lift or Submergence"] --> N
    D["Friction Head Loss in Suction Pipe"] --> N
    N --> E{"NPSH Available versus NPSH Required"}
    E -- "NPSH Available greater than NPSH Required" --> F["SAFE: No Cavitation"]
    E -- "NPSH Available equal to NPSH Required" --> G["INCIPIENT: Cavitation Just Begins"]
    E -- "NPSH Available less than NPSH Required" --> H["CAVITATION: Reduce H_s, Increase P_atm, Cool Liquid, or Use Booster Pump"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Cavitation Number Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Calculate Thoma Cavitation Parameter sigma equals NPSH_A divided by H"] --> B{"Compare sigma with sigma_c"}
    B -- "sigma greater than sigma_c" --> C["Safe Operation Zone - Green"]
    B -- "sigma equal to sigma_c" --> D["Incipient Cavitation - Yellow"]
    B -- "sigma less than sigma_c" --> E["Severe Cavitation Zone - Red"]
    D --> F["Efficiency begins to drop"]
    E --> G["Mechanical damage and efficiency collapse"]
    C --> H["Turbine or Pump operates at design performance"]
    F --> I["Consider raising runner elevation or lowering H"]
    G --> J["Redesign impeller or use cavitation resistant materials"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Set A]**
*Define the term "cavitation" in hydraulic machines. List any four adverse effects of cavitation.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation of vapor bubbles or cavities within a liquid when the local static pressure of the liquid falls below the vapor pressure of the liquid at the prevailing temperature. When these bubbles are carried into a region of higher pressure, they collapse violently, producing shock waves that damage the machine.

**Four Adverse Effects:**
1. **Pitting and erosion** of metal surfaces (especially impeller blades and runner vanes) due to micro-jet impacts.
2. **Loss of efficiency** and drop in developed head/flow due to vapor pockets occupying flow space.
3. **Noise and vibration** caused by repeated bubble collapse events near metal surfaces.
4. **Shaft seizure or bearing failure** in extreme cases due to asymmetric hydraulic thrust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A "list four effects" question fetches 3 marks only if **all four are named distinctly**. Avoid repeating the same idea with different wording.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)? Write the expression for NPSH available for a pump with suction lift.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of NPSH:**
NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) is the total head available at the pump suction nozzle, measured **above the vapor pressure head** of the liquid. It represents the margin by which the suction pressure exceeds the vapor pressure — a larger NPSH means the liquid is further from boiling and cavitation is less likely.

**NPSH Available for a pump with suction lift:**

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

Where:
- $P_{atm}/\rho g$ = Atmospheric pressure head (m)
- $P_v/\rho g$ = Vapor pressure head at operating temperature (m)
- $H_s$ = Static suction lift (m)
- $h_f$ = Friction head loss in suction piping (m)

**Safe Operation Criterion:**

$$\text{NPSH}_A \geq \text{NPSH}_R$$

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**PART B – QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** *Define Thoma's cavitation parameter $\sigma$ for a reaction turbine. Derive the expression for the critical cavitation parameter in terms of specific speed.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A centrifugal pump handles water at 25°C. The pump is installed 3 m above the water level in the sump. Suction pipe length = 8 m, diameter = 75 mm, friction factor $f = 0.028$. Atmospheric pressure = 100 kPa (abs). Vapor pressure of water at 25°C = 3.169 kPa (abs). Calculate the NPSH available and the maximum permissible flow velocity to avoid cavitation. Assume NPSH required = 2.5 m.* **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition of Thoma's Cavitation Parameter:**

Thoma's cavitation parameter $\sigma$ for a reaction turbine is defined as the ratio of the net positive suction head available at the draft tube inlet to the net head developed by the turbine:

$$\sigma = \frac{NPSH_A}{H}$$

**Critical Cavitation Parameter $\sigma_c$:**

It is the value of $\sigma$ at which cavitation just begins. Below this value, efficiency falls and pitting damage occurs.

**Derivation from Specific Speed:**

Empirical studies (Thoma, Moody, Knauss) on model turbines showed that the cavitation behavior of a reaction turbine depends strongly on its **specific speed** $N_s$. Thoma's original correlation (based on European model tests) is:

$$\sigma_c = K \cdot N_s^x$$

The most widely cited Thoma correlation is:

$$\sigma_c = 0.625 \cdot N_s^{2/3}$$

Where the specific speed in SI units is:

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$

With $N$ in rpm, $P$ in kW, and $H$ in meters.

**Modern alternative (Moody's correlation, common in KTU problems):**

$$\sigma_c = 0.0014 \cdot N_s^{1.5}$$

**Cavitation-Free Operation Criterion:**

$$\sigma = \frac{NPSH_A}{H} \geq \sigma_c$$

This means the **runner setting** (the elevation of the turbine above tailwater) must be high enough to provide $NPSH_A \geq \sigma_c \cdot H$.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Liquid: Water at $T = 25°C$
- Suction lift $H_s = 3$ m (pump above sump)
- Suction pipe: $L = 8$ m, $D = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m, $f = 0.028$
- $P_{atm} = 100{,}000$ Pa (abs)
- $P_v = 3169$ Pa (abs)
- $NPSH_R = 2.5$ m
- $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Convert pressures to heads:**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{100{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 10.194 \text{ m}$$

$$\frac{P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{3169}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.323 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Express friction head loss in terms of velocity:**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{4 \times 0.028 \times 8 \times V^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075}$$

$$h_f = \frac{0.896 \cdot V^2}{1.4715} = 0.609 \cdot V^2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Write the NPSH available expression:**

$$NPSH_A = 10.194 - 0.323 - 3 - 0.609 \cdot V^2$$

$$NPSH_A = 6.871 - 0.609 \cdot V^2 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the safe operation criterion $NPSH_A \geq NPSH_R$:**

$$6.871 - 0.609 \cdot V^2 \geq 2.5$$

$$0.609 \cdot V^2 \leq 4.371$$

$$V^2 \leq 7.177$$

$$V \leq 2.679 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The **maximum permissible flow velocity** to avoid cavitation is:

$$\boxed{V_{max} \approx 2.68 \text{ m/s}}$$

Corresponding **maximum discharge:**

$$Q_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot V_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 \times 2.68 = 0.01184 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 11.84 \text{ L/s}$$

---

**PART B – QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *Explain with a neat sketch the phenomenon of cavitation in a centrifugal pump. Discuss the methods adopted to prevent cavitation in pumps.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A Francis turbine operates at a net head of 60 m with a discharge of 8 m³/s. The setting of the runner above tailwater is 2.5 m. Atmospheric pressure is 9.8 m of water and vapor pressure is 0.2 m of water. Friction loss in draft tube is 1.0 m. Determine Thoma's cavitation parameter. Will the turbine operate without cavitation if the critical cavitation parameter is 0.10?* **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Cavitation in a Centrifugal Pump — Explanation:**

A centrifugal pump draws water from a sump through a suction pipe. As water enters the **eye of the impeller**, it accelerates to a high velocity. By Bernoulli's principle, this velocity increase causes a corresponding **drop in static pressure** at the impeller eye.

If the local pressure at the impeller eye falls **below the vapor pressure** of water at the operating temperature, vapor bubbles form. These bubbles are carried by the flow into the **impeller channels** (high-pressure region), where they collapse violently, producing:
- Pitting on impeller vanes
- Noise and vibration
- Reduced head and efficiency

**Preventive Methods:**

1. **Lower the pump installation elevation** to reduce suction lift $H_s$.
2. **Increase submergence** of the suction pipe inlet below sump water level.
3. **Reduce friction losses** in suction line by using shorter, larger-diameter pipes and smooth bends.
4. **Lower the liquid temperature** to reduce vapor pressure $P_v$.
5. **Use a booster pump** in the suction line to add pressure head.
6. **Select a pump with a lower NPSH requirement** (slower specific speed).
7. **Use cavitation-resistant materials** (Ni-Al bronze, stainless steel).
8. **Ensure NPSH available is greater than or equal to NPSH required** by manufacturer.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Net head $H = 60$ m
- Discharge $Q = 8 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Runner setting above tailwater: $h_s = 2.5$ m
- Atmospheric pressure head $H_{atm} = 9.8$ m
- Vapor pressure head $H_v = 0.2$ m
- Friction head loss in draft tube $h_f = 1.0$ m
- Critical cavitation parameter $\sigma_c = 0.10$

**Step 1 — Write the NPSH available expression for a reaction turbine:**

For a turbine with runner set $h_s$ **above** tailwater, the pressure at the runner exit (draft tube inlet) is reduced by atmospheric pressure. The NPSH available is:

$$NPSH_A = H_{atm} - H_v - h_s - h_f$$

$$NPSH_A = 9.8 - 0.2 - 2.5 - 1.0 = 6.1 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Thoma's Cavitation Parameter:**

$$\sigma = \frac{NPSH_A}{H} = \frac{6.1}{60} = 0.1017$$

**Step 3 — Compare with critical cavitation parameter:**

$$\sigma = 0.1017 \quad \text{versus} \quad \sigma_c = 0.10$$

$$\sigma = 0.1017 > \sigma_c = 0.10 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

Since $\sigma > \sigma_c$, the turbine **will operate WITHOUT cavitation**, but only marginally. The margin is very small (only 1.7%), so designers would typically lower the runner setting or reduce friction losses to gain a larger safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls in Cavitation Problems):**
> 1. **Sign error on $H_s$:** Students often forget that suction lift is **subtracted** in pumps but runner setting above tailwater is **subtracted** in turbines. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kPa to meters:** NPSH formula requires everything in **meters of head**. Forgetting to divide $P_v$ by $\rho g$ is the most common error.
> 3. **Ignoring friction loss $h_f$:** KTU problems often specify $h_f$. Skipping it loses 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing $\sigma$ with $\sigma_c$:** $\sigma$ is **calculated**, $\sigma_c$ is **given/empirical**. Always compare them.
> 5. **Writing the wrong NPSH formula** for submerged suction vs. suction lift. Re-read the problem statement!

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Cavitation & Its Prevention**

- **Definition:** Cavitation = vapor cavity formation in a liquid when local $P < P_v$ at operating temperature.
- **NPSH Available (Suction Lift):** $NPSH_A = (P_{atm}/\rho g) - (P_v/\rho g) - H_s - h_f$
- **NPSH Available (Submerged):** $NPSH_A = (P_{atm}/\rho g) + H_s - (P_v/\rho g) - h_f$
- **Safe Operation Rule:** $NPSH_A \geq NPSH_R$ (always)
- **Thoma's Parameter:** $\sigma = NPSH_A / H$ (dimensionless)
- **Cavitation-Free Criterion:** $\sigma \geq \sigma_c$
- **Specific Speed Correlation:** $\sigma_c = 0.0014 \cdot N_s^{1.5}$ (Moody) or $\sigma_c = 0.625 \cdot N_s^{2/3}$ (Thoma)
- **Higher $N_s$ → Higher $\sigma_c$ → More cavitation-prone** (Kaplan > Francis > Pelton)
- **Vapor Pressure $P_v$ increases with temperature** → Hotter liquid cavitates more easily.
- **Atmospheric Pressure Head (water):** $H_{atm} \approx 10.33$ m at sea level; decreases with altitude.
- **Effects of Cavitation:** Pitting, noise, vibration, efficiency loss, shaft damage.
- **Prevention Methods:** Lower suction lift, larger suction pipe, cooler liquid, booster pump, cavitation-resistant alloys, polished surfaces, low specific speed selection.
- **Draft Tube Role:** In Francis/Kaplan turbines, the draft tube recovers kinetic energy at exit and reduces pressure at runner exit, increasing NPSH margin.
- **Key Conversion:** Pressure (Pa) to Head (m) = $h = P/(\rho g)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Compressors: Reciprocating, Rotary, and Centrifugal Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Compressors: Reciprocating, Rotary, and Centrifugal Compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: TYPES OF COMPRESSORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Compressor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressor (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **compressor** is a mechanical device that increases the pressure of a compressible fluid (gas or vapor) by reducing its specific volume. It does this by applying **mechanical work** from an external power source (electric motor, diesel engine, turbine). The primary purpose is to deliver a gas at a **higher discharge pressure** than the **suction pressure**.

A compressor differs from a **pump** in that it handles **compressible fluids (gases)**, where the density changes significantly during compression, whereas a pump usually handles **incompressible fluids (liquids)**.

### 1.2 Broad Classification of Compressors

Compressors are broadly classified into **two main families** based on the principle of compression:

> [!NOTE]
> **Positive Displacement Compressors (PD Compressors):**
> - A fixed mass of gas is **trapped in a confined space** (cylinder, cavity, chamber).
> - The volume of this space is **mechanically reduced** to raise the pressure.
> - Pressure rise is essentially **independent of the type of gas**; it depends on the load applied and the compression ratio.
> - **Examples:** Reciprocating, Rotary (Roots, Screw, Vane, Scroll).

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic (Roto-Dynamic) Compressors:**
> - Gas pressure rises due to **velocity and momentum transfer** from a rapidly rotating impeller.
> - Energy is first added as **kinetic energy**, then converted to **pressure energy** in a diffuser.
> - Pressure rise depends heavily on **gas density, impeller speed, and flow rate**.
> - **Examples:** Centrifugal, Axial, Mixed-flow.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bicycle Pump Analogy (Reciprocating Compressor):**
> Imagine pushing down the handle of a **bicycle pump**. You trap a fixed amount of air in a cylinder and physically squeeze it into a smaller volume — that's a **reciprocating (positive displacement) compressor**.
> 
> **The Industrial Fan Analogy (Centrifugal Compressor):**
> Now imagine a **giant spinning disc with curved blades** throwing air outward at high speed. As the air is flung outward, it slows down and its pressure rises — that's a **centrifugal (dynamic) compressor**.

**Memory Trick (KTU Exam Tip):**
- **PD = "Push-Down"** — You push the gas down into a smaller space.
- **Dynamic = "Dynamic Spinning"** — A spinning impeller dynamically accelerates the gas.

### 1.4 The Three Major Compressor Types Under Study

| Type | Family | Key Movement |
|------|--------|--------------|
| Reciprocating | Positive Displacement | Back-and-forth piston |
| Rotary | Positive Displacement | Continuous rotation of gears/vanes/screws |
| Centrifugal | Dynamic | High-speed rotating impeller |

### 1.5 Why Compressors Are Important (Engineering Utility)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Compressed air** is the **"fourth utility"** in industry after water, electricity, and gas.
> - Used in **pneumatic tools, HVAC, refrigeration, gas turbine inlet boosting, oil & gas transmission, oxygen/medical gas systems, spray painting, sandblasting, and air brakes in vehicles**.

---

### 1.6 Reciprocating Compressors — Detailed Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **reciprocating compressor** is a **positive displacement** machine that uses a **piston moving back and forth (reciprocating)** inside a cylinder to compress the gas. The volume of trapped gas is reduced as the piston moves from the **Top Dead Centre (TDC)** to the **Bottom Dead Centre (BDC)**.

#### 1.6.1 Classification of Reciprocating Compressors

**(a) Based on Number of Cylinders:**
- **Single-cylinder** (simplex)
- **Multi-cylinder** (duplex, triplex, etc.)

**(b) Based on Number of Working Faces of the Piston:**
- **Single-acting:** Compression occurs on **one side** of the piston only.
- **Double-acting:** Compression occurs on **both sides** (head end and crank end) of the piston.

**(c) Based on Number of Compression Stages:**
- **Single-stage:** Compression in **one cylinder** (pressure ratio typically 3 to 8).
- **Multi-stage:** Compression in **two or more cylinders** with **intercooling** (pressure ratio can be very high).

**(d) Based on Cooling Method:**
- **Air-cooled**
- **Water-cooled**

#### 1.6.2 Single-Acting Reciprocating Compressor

- Gas is drawn in and compressed on **one side of the piston** (usually the cylinder head end).
- The other side of the piston is connected to the **crankcase**.
- **Simpler design**, but delivers less air per cycle.

#### 1.6.3 Double-Acting Reciprocating Compressor

- Gas is compressed on **both sides** of the piston.
- Each side has its **own suction and delivery valves**.
- **Doubles the output** for the same cylinder size and RPM.
- Used for **medium to high capacity** applications.

#### 1.6.4 Multi-Stage Reciprocating Compressor with Intercooling

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Multi-Stage?**
> - High pressure ratios in a single stage cause **excessive temperature rise** (even approaching auto-ignition of the lubricant).
> - Multi-stage compression with **intercooling** reduces the **work of compression** and the **discharge temperature**.
> - It also **improves volumetric efficiency** and reduces leakage.

In a **two-stage compressor**:
- **Stage 1 (LP cylinder)** compresses gas from $P_1$ to an **intermediate pressure** $P_i$.
- Gas passes through an **intercooler** where it is cooled back to (or near) the **inlet temperature** $T_1$.
- **Stage 2 (HP cylinder)** compresses it from $P_i$ to the **final pressure** $P_2$.

**Optimum Intermediate Pressure** (for minimum work in a 2-stage compressor with perfect intercooling):

$$
P_i = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}
$$

---

### 1.7 Rotary Compressors — Detailed Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **rotary compressor** is a **positive displacement** machine in which the compression of gas is achieved by the **continuous rotation** of a shaft-mounted element (rotor) within a stationary casing. Unlike reciprocating compressors, there is **no back-and-forth motion** of any part — only **rotational motion**.

#### 1.7.1 Major Types of Rotary Compressors

**(a) Roots Blower (Two-Lobe Rotary Lobe Compressor)**
- Two figure-8 shaped rotors (lobes) rotate in opposite directions inside a casing.
- Gas is **trapped between the lobes and the casing wall** and pushed from suction to discharge.
- **No internal compression** — the rotors do not contact each other or the casing.
- Pressure rise occurs due to **back-pressure** from the discharge system.
- Used for **low-pressure, high-volume** applications (max pressure ratio ~1.5 to 2).

**(b) Vane (Vaned) Compressor**
- An **eccentric rotor** carries several **sliding vanes** that slide in and out of slots.
- As the rotor turns, the vanes trap gas between them and the casing.
- Volume of each chamber **decreases** as it moves from suction to discharge, **compressing the gas**.
- The vanes may be **spring-loaded** or use **centrifugal force** to stay in contact with the casing.

**(c) Screw Compressor (Twin-Screw / Helical Screw)**
- Two intermeshing **helical screws** (rotor and gate rotor) rotate in opposite directions.
- Gas is trapped in the **cavities between the lobes and the casing** and pushed axially.
- Provides **continuous, pulse-free delivery**.
- Two main types:
  - **Oil-injected (oil-flooded) screw compressor** — oil cools, seals, and lubricates.
  - **Oil-free screw compressor** — used in food, pharma, and electronics industries.

**(d) Scroll Compressor**
- One **fixed scroll** and one **orbiting scroll** (rotating in small circles).
- Gas is drawn in at the **outer edge** and progressively compressed toward the **center**, where it is discharged.
- Very **low vibration**, **quiet operation**, high efficiency.
- Widely used in **HVAC, refrigeration, and small air-conditioning units**.

#### 1.7.2 Working Principle (Common to All Rotary Compressors)

1. **Suction:** A chamber opens to the inlet, and gas is drawn in.
2. **Trapping/Transfer:** The chamber is sealed off from the inlet.
3. **Compression:** The chamber volume decreases, raising gas pressure.
4. **Discharge:** The chamber opens to the outlet, and the high-pressure gas is pushed out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Feature:** Rotary compressors deliver a **continuous, smooth flow of gas** (no pulsations) — unlike reciprocating compressors, which deliver gas in **discrete pulses**.

---

### 1.8 Centrifugal Compressors — Detailed Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **centrifugal compressor** is a **dynamic (kinetic) compressor** in which gas is accelerated by a **high-speed rotating impeller** and then its **kinetic energy is converted into pressure energy** in a **diffuser (or volute casing)**. Energy is added by **centrifugal force**.

#### 1.8.1 Main Components of a Centrifugal Compressor

1. **Impeller (Rotor):**
   - The heart of the compressor.
   - Mounted on a shaft, rotating at very high speeds (10,000 to 30,000 RPM for industrial units).
   - May be **open, semi-open, or closed/shrouded**.
   - Backward-curved, radial, or forward-curved blades.

2. **Diffuser:**
   - Converts the **high-velocity, low-pressure gas** leaving the impeller into **high-pressure, low-velocity gas**.
   - Two main types: **vane (channel) diffuser** and **vaneless diffuser**.

3. **Volute Casing / Scroll:**
   - Collects the discharged gas from the diffuser and converts the **residual kinetic energy** to pressure.
   - Acts as a **collector and secondary diffuser**.

4. **Return Channels (in multi-stage):**
   - Direct the flow from one stage to the next.

5. **Shaft, Bearings, Seals:**
   - Support the rotating impeller and prevent gas leakage.

#### 1.8.2 Working Principle

1. Gas enters at the **eye (center)** of the impeller axially.
2. The **impeller blades** fling the gas outward by **centrifugal action**, gaining **kinetic energy**.
3. Gas leaves the impeller tip at **very high velocity**.
4. In the **diffuser**, the velocity decreases, and **pressure rises** (by Bernoulli's principle: an increase in pressure accompanies a decrease in velocity).
5. The volute casing collects the gas and **further converts** any remaining kinetic energy to pressure.
6. In **multi-stage** machines, the gas passes through several impeller-diffuser stages in series.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference from PD Compressors:**
> A centrifugal compressor has a **continuous, steady flow** and operates best at a **constant design point**. Off-design operation causes **surge** or **choking** phenomena.

#### 1.8.3 Concept of Surge and Choking (Important for KTU)

> [!WARNING]
> - **Surge:** Occurs at **very low flow rates** — the gas flow reverses, damaging the compressor.
> - **Choking:** Occurs at **very high flow rates** — the gas reaches **sonic velocity** in some passage, and flow cannot increase further.
> - The safe operating range is between these two limits, called the **"surge-to-choke" operating map**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Analysis of Reciprocating Compressors

### 2.1.1 Single-Stage Reciprocating Compressor — Cycle on P-V Diagram

The ideal cycle consists of **three main processes**:

| Process | Description | Thermodynamic Path |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 3 → 4 | **Suction stroke:** Piston moves from TDC to BDC, gas at constant suction pressure $P_1$ enters the cylinder | Isobaric expansion (volume increases) |
| 4 → 1 | **Compression stroke:** Gas compressed from $P_1$ to $P_2$ | Polytropic / Isothermal / Adiabatic |
| 1 → 2 | **Delivery stroke:** Piston moves from BDC to TDC, gas at constant delivery pressure $P_2$ leaves | Isobaric compression (volume decreases) |
| 2 → 3 | (Ideally zero) — instantaneous opening of delivery valve | Constant volume |

> [!NOTE]
> The **area enclosed** by the cycle 1-2-3-4 on a **P-V diagram** represents the **work done per cycle (per kg of gas)**.

### 2.1.2 Work of Compression (Theoretical)

For an ideal compression of a gas following the polytropic law $PV^n = \text{constant}$:

$$
W_{per \, kg} = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]
$$

**For specific ideal cases:**

$$
W_{isothermal} = R \cdot T_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
$$

$$
W_{adiabatic} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]
$$

**Compare them:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a given pressure ratio $\frac{P_2}{P_1}$, the **isothermal work is the lowest** ($W_{iso} < W_{poly} < W_{adia}$). Since perfect cooling to inlet temperature cannot be achieved in practice, the **polytropic model** with $1 < n < \gamma$ is most realistic.

### 2.1.3 Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> Volumetric efficiency is the **ratio of the actual volume of gas (at suction conditions) drawn into the cylinder per cycle** to the **swept volume of the piston**.

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{swept}} = \frac{V_a}{V_s}
$$

It accounts for:
- **Clearance volume losses** (the most significant cause)
- Pressure drop in suction/delivery valves
- Heating of gas during suction
- Leakage past piston rings

**For a clearance ratio $c = \frac{V_c}{V_s}$:**

$$
\eta_{vol} = 1 + c - c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
$$

where:
- $V_c$ = clearance volume (volume at TDC)
