- $V_s = V_1 - V_c$ = swept volume
- $V_1$ = total volume at BDC
- $n$ = polytropic index

### 2.1.4 Isothermal Efficiency

$$
\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{isothermal}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{\text{Minimum (ideal) work}}{\text{Actual work}}
$$

### 2.1.5 Mechanical Efficiency

$$
\eta_{mech} = \frac{Indicated \, Power}{Brake \, Power}
$$

### 2.1.6 Free Air Delivery (FAD) — The Most Asked Quantity in KTU Exams

> [!NOTE]
> **FAD** is the **actual volume of air (or gas) delivered by the compressor per unit time, measured at atmospheric (free air) conditions** ($P_a = 1.013 \, \text{bar}$, $T_a = 15°\text{C}$ or $288 \, \text{K}$).

$$
FAD = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_s \cdot N \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]
$$

where $N$ is the number of working strokes per second (or per minute, with unit conversion).

For a **double-acting** compressor:

$$
V_s = (A_1 + A_2 - a) \cdot L
$$

where:
- $A_1$ = area of piston on the head-end side
- $A_2$ = area of piston on the crank-end side
- $a$ = area of piston rod
- $L$ = stroke length

### 2.1.7 Multi-Stage Compression (Detailed Theory)

In a **two-stage** compressor with **perfect intercooling** (gas cooled back to $T_1$ before stage 2):

**Total work per kg:**

$$
W_{total} = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_i}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} + \left(\frac{P_2}{P_i}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 2 \right]
$$

**Optimum Intermediate Pressure (Minimum Work Condition):**

By setting $\frac{dW}{dP_i} = 0$:

$$
P_i = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = (P_1 \cdot P_2)^{1/2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a two-stage compressor with $k$ total stages:**
> The optimum intermediate pressure ratio is **the same for every stage**:
> $$r = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/k}$$
> Each stage compresses the gas by the same ratio $r$.

**Ratio of Work of Multi-Stage to Single-Stage (polytropic):**

$$
\frac{W_{multi}}{W_{single}} = \frac{(n-1) \cdot n \cdot r^{(n-1)/n}}{n \left( r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right)} \quad \text{... requires simplification per case}
$$

A simpler standard result for **isothermal ideal case** between single and two-stage:

$$
\frac{W_{two-stage}}{W_{single-stage}} = \frac{2 \left( r^{1/2} - 1 \right)}{r - 1} \quad \text{for isothermal with } r = \frac{P_2}{P_1}
$$

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of Rotary Compressors

### 2.2.1 Roots Blower (Two-Lobe)

- **No internal compression** — the pressure rise is determined entirely by the **discharge (back) pressure**.
- Used for **pressure ratios up to ~1.5 to 2**.
- Volumetric efficiency close to 1 (small leakage loss).
- For a **Roots blower**, **compression work per kg** is essentially the **isothermal work** (since internal compression is absent):

$$
W = R \cdot T_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)
$$

### 2.2.2 Vane Compressor

- **Continuous compression** as vanes sweep across the casing.
- The compression ratio depends on the **eccentricity, number of vanes, and shaft rotation**.
- Volumetric efficiency decreases with **higher pressure ratios** (more leakage time).
- Power required:

$$
P_{input} = \dot{m} \cdot W_{s,comp} \quad [W]
$$

### 2.2.3 Screw Compressor

- The **male rotor** (driving) typically has 4 lobes; the **female rotor** (driven) has 6 flutes.
- Compression occurs in the **direction of the screw axis** (axial flow).
- Common in **oil-injected** form: oil seals clearances, absorbs heat, and lubricates.
- **Pressure ratio:** up to ~13:1 in industrial two-stage oil-free machines.

### 2.2.4 Scroll Compressor

- Compression occurs in **multiple small chambers** at the same time — very smooth, nearly continuous discharge.
- The **fixed scroll** is stationary; the **orbiting scroll** moves in a circular path of small radius.
- Gas moves from **outer edge to the center** with each orbit, getting smaller in volume.
- **Tip speed** and **orbital speed** are relatively low → very quiet.

---

## 2.3 Theoretical Analysis of Centrifugal Compressors

### 2.3.1 Euler's Work Equation for Turbomachinery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation (Work per unit mass):**
> $$W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$$
> 
> For a centrifugal compressor, the gas enters axially (so $V_{w1} \approx 0$), giving:
> $$W = U_2 \cdot V_{w2}$$
> 
> where:
> - $U_2$ = blade tip speed at impeller exit
> - $V_{w2}$ = tangential (whirl) component of absolute velocity at impeller exit

### 2.3.2 Blade Tip Speed

$$
U_2 = \frac{\pi \cdot D_2 \cdot N}{60}
$$

where:
- $D_2$ = impeller outer diameter [m]
- $N$ = shaft speed [RPM]

### 2.3.3 Energy Transfer with Slip Factor ($\sigma$)

Due to **relative flow** at the impeller exit, a **slip factor** $\sigma$ (less than 1) is introduced:

$$
W = \sigma \cdot U_2^2
$$

**Stodola's Approximation for slip factor:**

$$
\sigma = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos\beta_2}{Z}
$$

where:
- $\beta_2$ = blade exit angle (from tangential direction)
- $Z$ = number of blades

### 2.3.4 Pressure Rise (Simplified)

Using Euler's equation and the relation $W = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ for adiabatic energy transfer:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(1 + \frac{W \cdot \eta_{ad}}{c_p T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}}
$$

### 2.3.5 Centrifugal Compressor Performance (Pressure Coefficient)

$$
\psi = \frac{W}{U_2^2} = \frac{c_p (T_2 - T_1)}{U_2^2}
$$

### 2.3.6 Surging in Centrifugal Compressors

> [!WARNING]
> **Surge Limit:**
> The flow at which the compressor's discharge pressure equals the system's back pressure. Below this flow, the gas flows **backward** momentarily, then forward — causing violent oscillations that can damage the machine.
> 
> **Surge prevention methods:**
> - Anti-surge **blow-off valve** (vents some gas to atmosphere)
> - **Recirculation** of discharge gas to suction
> - Operating **two or more compressors in parallel** to balance flow

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **must-know formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations on Types of Compressors.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Work of compression (polytropic) | $W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$ | J/kg |
| 2 | Work of compression (isothermal) | $W_{iso} = R T_1 \ln \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | J/kg (minimum work) |
| 3 | Work of compression (adiabatic) | $W_{adia} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | J/kg |
| 4 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = 1 + c - c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$ | $c = V_c / V_s$ |
| 5 | Free Air Delivery | $FAD = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_s \cdot N$ | m³/s |
| 6 | Swept volume (single-acting) | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | m³ |
| 7 | Swept volume (double-acting) | $V_s = (A_1 + A_2 - a) L$ | m³ |
| 8 | Isothermal efficiency | $\eta_{iso} = W_{iso} / W_{actual}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = IP / BP$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{iso} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Power required | $P = \dot{m} \cdot W_{actual}$ | W |
| 12 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho_{inlet} \cdot FAD$ | kg/s |
| 13 | Optimum intermediate pressure | $P_i = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ | Pa (2-stage) |
| 14 | Optimum stage pressure ratio (k stages) | $r = (P_2/P_1)^{1/k}$ | Dimensionless |
| 15 | Two-stage total work (polytropic) | $W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ 2 r^{(n-1)/n} - 2 \right]$ | J/kg |
| 16 | Euler's work (centrifugal) | $W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$ | J/kg |
| 17 | Tip speed | $U_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$ | m/s |
| 18 | Slip factor (Stodola) | $\sigma = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos \beta_2}{Z}$ | Dimensionless |
| 19 | Pressure ratio (adiabatic, centrifugal) | $\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(1 + \frac{\eta_{ad} W}{c_p T_1}\right)^{\gamma/(\gamma-1)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 20 | Pressure coefficient | $\psi = W / U_2^2$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to remember (in bold for KTU):**
> - $R_{air} = 287 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$
> - $c_{p,air} = 1005 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$
> - $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$
> - $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$
> - $T_{atm} = 288.15 \, \text{K}$ (sometimes $T_1 = 300 \, \text{K}$ in problems)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter in industry:**
> 
> - **FAD** is the **billing quantity** for compressed air — industries pay for FAD in Nm³/hr.
> - **Volumetric efficiency** determines **how much compressed air you actually get** for a given cylinder size.
> - **Optimum intermediate pressure** is used in **designing multi-stage compressors** to **minimize power consumption** and **avoid high discharge temperatures**.
> - **Euler's equation** is the foundation for **designing impellers** in centrifugal compressors and gas turbines.
> - **Slip factor** is critical for **predicting real performance** — neglecting it gives overly optimistic design calculations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work of Compression for a Polytropic Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Point:** We have a gas trapped in a cylinder by a piston, undergoing polytropic compression:
> $$P V^n = \text{constant} = C$$

### Step 1: Write the expression for work done in a reversible polytropic process

The infinitesimal work done **on the gas** is:

$$
dW = -P \, dV
$$

(The negative sign is because compression means $dV < 0$, but work is done **on** the gas, which we take as positive in compressor convention.)

In compressor sign convention, we use:

$$
W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV
$$

### Step 2: Substitute $P = C V^{-n}$ from the polytropic relation

Since $P V^n = C$, we have $P = C V^{-n}$. Substituting:

$$
W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} C V^{-n} \, dV
$$

### Step 3: Perform the integration

$$
W = -C \left[ \frac{V^{-n+1}}{-n+1} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -C \left[ \frac{V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]
$$

### Step 4: Use $C = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$

Substituting $C$:

$$
W = \frac{P_1 V_1^n \left( V_1^{1-n} - V_2^{1-n} \right)}{1-n}
$$

### Step 5: Use the ideal gas law to convert to temperature form

From the ideal gas law: $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$, so $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$ (per kg: $P_1 v_1 = R T_1$).

Using the polytropic relations: $\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$

After algebraic manipulation:

$$
W = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]
$$

Per unit mass:

$$
\boxed{W = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot R \cdot T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]}
$$

This is the **work done on the gas per kg**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance

> [!NOTE]
> Consider a reciprocating compressor with clearance volume $V_c$ at TDC. The piston sweeps volume $V_s$ from TDC to BDC. Let $V_1 = V_s + V_c$ be the total volume at BDC.

### Step 1: Expansion of trapped clearance gas

After delivery, when the piston moves back, the clearance gas at $P_2$ expands from $V_c$ to a volume $V_3$ (where the suction pressure $P_1$ is reached). Using the polytropic law:

$$
P_2 V_c^n = P_1 V_3^n
$$

Solving for $V_3$:

$$
V_3 = V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
$$

### Step 2: Effective suction volume

The fresh gas drawn in during the next suction stroke occupies the volume:

$$
V_{actual} = V_1 - V_3 = (V_s + V_c) - V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
$$

### Step 3: Define and simplify volumetric efficiency

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_s} = \frac{V_s + V_c - V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}}{V_s}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $V_s$ and using clearance ratio $c = V_c / V_s$:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{vol} = 1 + c - c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimum Intermediate Pressure for a Two-Stage Compressor

### Step 1: Total work expression

For a two-stage compressor with **perfect intercooling** (intermediate temperature = inlet temperature $T_1$):

$$
W_{total} = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_i}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} + \left(\frac{P_2}{P_i}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 2 \right]
$$

### Step 2: Differentiate with respect to $P_i$

Setting $\frac{dW_{total}}{dP_i} = 0$:

$$
\frac{d}{dP_i} \left[ \left(\frac{P_i}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} + \left(\frac{P_2}{P_i}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} \right] = 0
$$

Let $a = \frac{n-1}{n}$ for brevity:

$$
\frac{d}{dP_i} \left[ P_i^a \cdot P_1^{-a} + P_2^a \cdot P_i^{-a} \right] = 0
$$

### Step 3: Apply the power rule

$$
a \cdot P_i^{a-1} \cdot P_1^{-a} + (-a) \cdot P_2^a \cdot P_i^{-a-1} = 0
$$

### Step 4: Rearrange

$$
a \cdot P_i^{a-1} \cdot P_1^{-a} = a \cdot P_2^a \cdot P_i^{-a-1}
$$

Cancel $a$ (non-zero) and multiply by $P_i^{a+1}$:

$$
P_i^{2a} = P_1^a \cdot P_2^a
$$

### Step 5: Solve for $P_i$

$$
P_i^{2a} = (P_1 P_2)^a
$$

Since $a \neq 0$, divide exponents:

$$
P_i^2 = P_1 P_2
$$

$$
\boxed{P_i = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The pressure ratio in each stage is **equal**, and $P_i$ is the **geometric mean** of $P_1$ and $P_2$. This is one of the most frequently asked results in KTU exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Euler's Work Equation for a Centrifugal Compressor

### Step 1: Velocity triangles at inlet and outlet

At any point on the impeller, the **absolute velocity** $V$ has two components:
- Tangential (whirl) component: $V_w$ (in the direction of blade motion)
- Radial (flow) component: $V_f$ (perpendicular to blade motion)

The **relative velocity** $V_r$ is the velocity of the gas **relative to the rotating blade**, and the **blade velocity** is $U = \omega r = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$.

### Step 2: Apply the angular momentum theorem

For a control volume enclosing the impeller, the **torque** $T$ exerted on the gas is equal to the **rate of change of angular momentum**:

$$
T = \dot{m} (r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})
$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate of gas.

### Step 3: Work done per unit mass

Work = Torque × Angular velocity / mass flow rate:

$$
W = \frac{T \cdot \omega}{\dot{m}} = \frac{\omega \cdot \dot{m} (r_2 V_{w2} - r_1 V_{w1})}{\dot{m}}
$$

Since $U = \omega r$:

$$
\boxed{W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}}
$$

### Step 4: Apply inlet condition for centrifugal compressor

Gas enters **axially** at the eye, so $V_{w1} = 0$. Thus:

$$
W = U_2 V_{w2}
$$

This is the **Euler work** for a centrifugal compressor.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Compressor Types

| Parameter | Reciprocating | Rotary | Centrifugal |
|-----------|---------------|--------|-------------|
| Compressor family | Positive displacement | Positive displacement | Dynamic (kinetic) |
| Flow nature | Pulsating, intermittent | Continuous, smooth | Continuous, smooth |
| Pressure ratio capability | **Highest** (up to 30:1 in multi-stage) | Moderate (up to ~13:1) | Moderate (up to ~9:1 per stage) |
| Volumetric efficiency | 60–85% (drops with higher pressure ratio) | 80–95% | Not applicable (flow-based) |
| Isothermal efficiency | 50–70% | 60–75% | 70–85% |
| Discharge temperature | High (in single-stage) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Capacity range | Small to medium (FAD) | Medium to high | High to very high |
| Speed of operation | Low to medium (300–600 RPM) | Medium (3000–10,000 RPM) | Very high (10,000–30,000 RPM) |
| Pulsations in flow | Yes (need pulsation dampeners) | Very low | Very low |
| Sensitivity to dirty gas | Less tolerant | Less tolerant | More tolerant |
| Maintenance | High (valves, rings) | Moderate | Moderate (rotor balancing) |
| Initial cost | Moderate to high | Lower per unit capacity | Lower per unit capacity (at high flow) |
| Best suited for | High pressure, low-medium flow | Continuous medium-pressure air | High flow, moderate pressure |
| Typical applications | CNG, gas pipelines, refrigeration, scuba tanks | HVAC, oil & gas, pneumatic tools | Gas turbines, process air, large industrial plants |

---

## 3.6 Solved Analytical Example (Worked Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Sample, KTU-style):**
> A single-stage reciprocating compressor takes in $0.5 \, \text{m}^3$ of air per second at suction conditions of $100 \, \text{kPa}$ and $300 \, \text{K}$. The compression is polytropic with $n = 1.3$, and the discharge pressure is $900 \, \text{kPa}$. Calculate:
> 1. Work of compression per kg
> 2. Power required
> 3. Isothermal efficiency (given mechanical efficiency = 0.9, and isothermal work = $RT_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$)

### Given:
- $P_1 = 100 \, \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \, \text{K}$
- $P_2 = 900 \, \text{kPa}$, $n = 1.3$
- $V_1 = 0.5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $R = 287 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$
- $\eta_{mech} = 0.9$

### Solution:

**Step 1: Mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \frac{100 \times 10^3 \times 0.5}{287 \times 300}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{50000}{86100} = 0.5807 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2: Pressure ratio**

$$
r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{900}{100} = 9
$$

**Step 3: Work per kg of air (polytropic)**

$$
W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ r_p^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
W = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 287 \times 300 \left[ 9^{0.3/1.3} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
9^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \ln 9} = e^{0.2308 \times 2.197} = e^{0.5071} = 1.6606
$$

$$
W = 4.3333 \times 287 \times 300 \times 0.6606
$$

$$
W = 4.3333 \times 86100 \times 0.6606 = 246,400 \, \text{J/kg} \approx 246.4 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4: Power required (IP)**

$$
IP = \dot{m} \times W = 0.5807 \times 246.4 \times 10^3 = 143,084 \, \text{W} \approx 143.1 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5: Brake Power**

$$
BP = \frac{IP}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{143.1}{0.9} = 159.0 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6: Isothermal work and efficiency**

$$
W_{iso} = R T_1 \ln(r_p) = 287 \times 300 \times \ln 9 = 86100 \times 2.197 = 189,170 \, \text{J/kg} \approx 189.2 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{189.2}{246.4} = 0.768 = 76.8\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:**
> 1. Work of compression = **246.4 kJ/kg**
> 2. Indicated Power = **143.1 kW** (Brake Power = 159 kW)
> 3. Isothermal efficiency = **76.8%**

```

    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1: Compression P1 to Pi]
        B
    end

```

flowchart TD

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Q: Define Free Air Delivery (FAD) of a compressor. Why is it considered the billing quantity for compressed air in industries?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Air Delivery (FAD):**
> 
> FAD is the **actual volume of air delivered by the compressor per unit time, measured at the free (atmospheric) air conditions**, i.e., at standard pressure $P_a = 1.01325 \, \text{bar}$ and standard temperature $T_a = 15°\text{C} = 288 \, \text{K}$ (as per ISO 1217).
> 
> **Mathematically:** $FAD = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_s \cdot N$
> 
> **Why it is the billing quantity:**
> - The **end user pays for usable air at atmospheric conditions**, not for the high-pressure air inside the receiver tank.
> - It accounts for **all losses** (volumetric, leakage, heating, pressure drop in valves) and gives the **true usable output**.
> - All compressor performance ratings (cfm, Nm³/hr) are quoted in terms of FAD.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Q: Distinguish between a positive displacement compressor and a dynamic compressor with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Positive Displacement (PD) | Dynamic (Roto-Dynamic) |
|---------|---------------------------|------------------------|
| Compression mechanism | **Mechanical trapping** and **volume reduction** of a fixed mass of gas | **Velocity and momentum** transfer from a rotating impeller |
| Pressure rise depends on | Back pressure / resistance in discharge system | Gas density, flow rate, impeller speed |
| Flow vs. Pressure | Nearly **constant flow**, variable pressure | Variable flow, limited by surge/choke |
| Examples | **Reciprocating, Rotary** (Roots, Screw, Vane) | **Centrifugal, Axial** |
| Discharge flow | Pulsating (reciprocating) or smooth (rotary) | Always smooth and continuous |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question Set B: Module 3 — Types of Compressors

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

#### QUESTION B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question Paper]

> **A single-stage double-acting reciprocating air compressor has the following specifications:**
> 
> - **Bore (Diameter):** $D = 200 \, \text{mm} = 0.2 \, \text{m}$
> - **Stroke:** $L = 300 \, \text{mm} = 0.3 \, \text{m}$
> - **Piston rod diameter:** $d = 30 \, \text{mm} = 0.03 \, \text{m}$
> - **Clearance volume:** $5\%$ of swept volume on each side
> - **Suction pressure:** $P_1 = 1.0 \, \text{bar}$, **Discharge pressure:** $P_2 = 7.0 \, \text{bar}$
> - **Polytropic index:** $n = 1.3$
> - **Speed:** $N = 200 \, \text{RPM}$
> - **Suction temperature:** $T_1 = 300 \, \text{K}$
> - **Mechanical efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$
> 
> **Calculate:**
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Swept volume per cycle, volumetric efficiency, and Free Air Delivery (FAD).
> 
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Work of compression per kg, indicated power, and brake power required to drive the compressor.

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

### Part (a) Solution — Swept Volume, Volumetric Efficiency, and FAD

**Step 1: Areas of piston on both sides**

Area on head-end side (full piston area):

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.2)^2 = 0.03142 \, \text{m}^2
$$

Area on crank-end side (piston minus rod area):

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.04 - 0.0009) = 0.03069 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Swept volume per cycle (double-acting)**

For a double-acting compressor, swept volume per cycle is:

$$
V_s = (A_1 + A_2) \cdot L
$$

$$
V_s = (0.03142 + 0.03069) \times 0.3 = 0.06211 \times 0.3 = 0.01863 \, \text{m}^3/\text{cycle}
$$

**Step 3: Clearance volume (5% on each side)**

$$
V_c = 0.05 \times V_s = 0.05 \times 0.01863 = 9.317 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Clearance ratio**

$$
c = \frac{V_c}{V_s} = 0.05
$$

**Step 5: Volumetric efficiency**

$$
\eta_{vol} = 1 + c - c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}
$$

$$
\eta_{vol} = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times (7.0)^{1/1.3}
$$

Computing $7.0^{0.7692}$:

$$
7.0^{0.7692} = e^{0.7692 \times \ln 7} = e^{0.7692 \times 1.9459} = e^{1.4969} = 4.469
$$

$$
\eta_{vol} = 1.05 - 0.05 \times 4.469 = 1.05 - 0.2234 = 0.8266
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{vol} \approx 82.66\%}
$$

**Step 6: Free Air Delivery**

For a double-acting compressor at $N = 200 \, \text{RPM}$ (each revolution gives one compression on each side, so the cycle is one revolution):

$$
FAD = \eta_{vol} \times V_s \times N
$$

Converting RPM to per second: $N = 200/60 = 3.333 \, \text{rev/s}$

$$
FAD = 0.8266 \times 0.01863 \times 3.333
$$

$$
FAD = 0.05133 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{FAD \approx 0.0513 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 184.8 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}}
$$

### Part (b) Solution — Work, Indicated Power, Brake Power

**Step 1: Mass of air per cycle**

Using $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$, with $V_1 = V_s + V_c = V_s (1 + c) = 0.01863 \times 1.05 = 0.01956 \, \text{m}^3$

$$
m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^5 \times 0.01956}{287 \times 300} = \frac{1956}{86100} = 0.02272 \, \text{kg/cycle}
$$

**Step 2: Work per kg of air (polytropic compression)**

$$
W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]
$$

Computing $\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} = 7.0^{0.3/1.3} = 7.0^{0.2308}$:

$$
7.0^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times 1.9459} = e^{0.4491} = 1.567
$$

$$
W = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 287 \times 300 \times (1.567 - 1)
$$

$$
W = 4.3333 \times 86100 \times 0.567
$$

$$
W = 211,663 \, \text{J/kg} \approx 211.7 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3: Mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m} = m \times N = 0.02272 \times 3.333 = 0.0757 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 4: Indicated Power**

$$
IP = \dot{m} \times W = 0.0757 \times 211.7 \times 10^3 = 16,026 \, \text{W} \approx 16.03 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5: Brake Power**

$$
BP = \frac{IP}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{16.03}{0.85} = 18.86 \, \text{kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers (Part B-A):**
> 
> **(a)** $V_s = 0.01863 \, \text{m}^3/\text{cycle}$, $\eta_{vol} = 82.66\%$, $FAD = 0.0513 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
> 
> **(b)** $W = 211.7 \, \text{kJ/kg}$, $IP = 16.03 \, \text{kW}$, $BP = 18.86 \, \text{kW}$

---

#### QUESTION B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

> **A two-stage reciprocating air compressor with perfect intercooling compresses air from $P_1 = 1.05 \, \text{bar}$ to $P_2 = 9.0 \, \text{bar}$. The compression is polytropic with $n = 1.25$. The suction temperature is $T_1 = 303 \, \text{K}$ ($30°\text{C}$). The compressor delivers $5 \, \text{kg}$ of air per minute. Take $R = 287 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$.**
> 
> **Calculate:**
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Optimum intermediate pressure, temperature at the end of each stage, and work of compression in each stage per kg.
> 
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Total work of compression per kg, total power required (kW), and isothermal efficiency (if actual work = total work × 1.15).

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

### Part (a) Solution — Optimum Pressure, Stage Temperatures, Stage Works

**Step 1: Optimum Intermediate Pressure**

$$
P_i = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = \sqrt{1.05 \times 9.0} = \sqrt{9.45} = 3.074 \, \text{bar}
$$

**Step 2: Pressure ratio in each stage (equal)**

$$
r = \frac{P_i}{P_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_i} = \sqrt{\frac{P_2}{P_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.0}{1.05}} = \sqrt{8.571} = 2.928
$$

**Step 3: Temperature after stage 1 (polytropic)**

For perfect intercooling, gas enters stage 2 at $T_1 = 303 \, \text{K}$. So both stages have same inlet temperature.

Temperature after stage 1:

$$
T_{2a} = T_1 \cdot r^{(n-1)/n} = 303 \times (2.928)^{(1.25-1)/1.25}
$$

Computing $(2.928)^{0.2}$:

$$
(2.928)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times \ln 2.928} = e^{0.2 \times 1.0745} = e^{0.2149} = 1.2397
$$

$$
T_{2a} = 303 \times 1.2397 = 375.6 \, \text{K} = 102.6°\text{C}
$$

**Step 4: Temperature after intercooler (back to $T_1 = 303 \, \text{K}$)**

**Step 5: Work per kg in each stage (polytropic)**

$$
W_{stage} = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
W_{stage} = \frac{1.25}{0.25} \times 287 \times 303 \times (1.2397 - 1)
$$

$$
W_{stage} = 5 \times 86961 \times 0.2397
$$

$$
W_{stage} = 104,205 \, \text{J/kg} \approx 104.2 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Part (b) Solution — Total Work, Power, Isothermal Efficiency

**Step 1: Total work per kg**

$$
W_{total} = 2 \times W_{stage} = 2 \times 104.2 = 208.4 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

(Or using the combined formula: $W = \frac{2n}{n-1} R T_1 (r^{(n-1)/n} - 1)$)

**Step 2: Total power required**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{5}{60} = 0.0833 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
P = \dot{m} \times W_{total} = 0.0833 \times 208.4 \times 10^3 = 17,366 \, \text{W} \approx 17.37 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3: Isothermal efficiency**

First, isothermal work per kg:

$$
W_{iso} = R T_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = 287 \times 303 \times \ln(8.571)
$$

$$
W_{iso} = 86961 \times 2.1485 = 186,839 \, \text{J/kg} \approx 186.8 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Actual work per kg (given as 1.15 × total):

$$
W_{actual} = 1.15 \times 208.4 = 239.7 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Isothermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{186.8}{239.7} = 0.7793 = 77.93\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers (Part B-B):**
> 
> **(a)** $P_i = 3.074 \, \text{bar}$, $T_{2a} = 375.6 \, \text{K}$, $W_{stage} = 104.2 \, \text{kJ/kg}$ (each)
> 
> **(b)** $W_{total} = 208.4 \, \text{kJ/kg}$, $P = 17.37 \, \text{kW}$, $\eta_{iso} = 77.93\%$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Rod Area in Double-Acting Compressors:** Many students use $V_s = A L$ instead of $V_s = (A_1 + A_2)L$. This loses **2-3 marks** almost every time.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Polytropic Exponent:** Using $n = \gamma = 1.4$ when the problem clearly states $n = 1.3$ or $1.25$ — **major error, 3 marks lost**.
> 
> 3. **Confusing isentropic and polytropic formulas:** Isentropic uses $\gamma$ and $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$ exponent. Polytropic uses $n$ and $\frac{n-1}{n}$. **Read the problem carefully.**
> 
> 4. **Units of FAD:** Always express in both $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and $\text{m}^3/\text{min}$ or $\text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$ for clarity. KTU examiners give partial marks for unit awareness.
> 
> 5. **In two-stage problems, students often forget the "-2" term** in the combined work expression — leading to underestimation of work.
> 
> 6. **Optimum pressure calculation:** A common mistake is using $P_i = (P_2 - P_1)/2$ (arithmetic mean) instead of $\sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ (geometric mean). Only geometric mean is correct.
> 
> 7. **Euler equation sign convention:** For compressors, the formula $W = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$ gives **work done on the gas** (positive for compression).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 — Types of Compressors: Quick-Fire Revision Points**
> 
> **General:**
> - Compressor = raises pressure of compressible fluid (gas) by mechanical work.
> - Two families: **Positive Displacement (PD)** and **Dynamic**.
> - **PD = trapping + squeezing**; **Dynamic = velocity + conversion**.
> - FAD is the **billing quantity** for compressed air.
> 
> **Reciprocating Compressors:**
> - **Piston** moves back and forth; uses **suction and delivery valves**.
> - Single-acting vs **double-acting** (compresses on both sides of piston).
> - Single-stage vs **multi-stage with intercooling**.
> - **Clearance volume** reduces volumetric efficiency.
> - **Swept volume** formula: single-acting $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$; double-acting $V_s = (A_1 + A_2 - a)L$.
> - **Optimum intermediate pressure** in 2-stage: $P_i = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$.
> - **Work of compression (polytropic):** $W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$.
> - **Volumetric efficiency:** $\eta_{vol} = 1 + c - c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$.
> - **FAD** $= \eta_{vol} \times V_s \times N$.
> 
> **Rotary Compressors:**
> - Continuous rotation; **no pulsation**.
> - **Roots blower:** no internal compression; low pressure ratio.
> - **Vane compressor:** eccentric rotor with sliding vanes; medium pressure.
> - **Screw compressor:** two intermeshing helical screws; oil-injected or oil-free.
> - **Scroll compressor:** orbiting + fixed scroll; quiet, smooth flow.
> 
> **Centrifugal Compressors:**
> - **Dynamic**, uses **high-speed impeller** (10,000–30,000 RPM).
> - Components: **impeller → diffuser → volute**.
> - **Euler's equation:** $W = U_2 V_{w2}$ (for axial inlet).
> - **Slip factor (Stodola):** $\sigma = 1 - \frac{\pi \cos \beta_2}{Z}$.
> - **Tip speed:** $U_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$.
> - Operates in a **surge-choke map**; surging is dangerous.
> - Best for **high flow, moderate pressure**.
> 
> **Numerical Solving Strategy (KTU Exam Approach):**
> 1. List given data in symbols with units.
> 2. Identify the formula(s) needed.
> 3. Compute pressure ratio first — it appears in many places.
> 4. Show all intermediate arithmetic steps.
> 5. Maintain unit consistency (Pa vs. bar, J vs. kJ).
> 6. Final answer with **box** and **correct units**.
> 
> **Must-Memorize Constants:**
> - $R_{air} = 287 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$
> - $c_{p,air} = 1005 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$
> - $\gamma_{air} = 1.4$
> - $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \, \text{bar}$
> - $T_{std} = 288.15 \, \text{K}$ (or $15°\text{C}$)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamic Analysis of Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Thermodynamic Analysis of Compressors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Compressor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A compressor is a **positive-displacement or turbomachine** that converts mechanical work from a prime mover (electric motor, IC engine shaft) into **fluid (gas) pressure energy** by *decreasing the specific volume* of the gas. The thermodynamic purpose is to deliver a working fluid at a **higher discharge pressure** than the inlet (suction) pressure. The **Thermodynamic Analysis** of a compressor concerns the application of the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**, the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, and the **Second Law (isentropic/exergy) analysis** to predict: 1. The **work input** required per unit mass of gas compressed. 2. The **temperature rise** during compression. 3. The **volumetric efficiency** (the fraction of swept volume actually occupied by fresh charge). 4. The **effect of clearance volume** on the indicator diagram. 5. The benefits of **multi-stage compression with intercooling**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Specifics (Module 3 – Compressors):** > The course demands analysis of **single-stage reciprocating compressors** under isothermal, polytropic, and isentropic (adiabatic) processes, derivation of **volumetric efficiency** in terms of clearance ratio, and a complete treatment of **multi-stage compression with optimum intercooling** to find the **minimum work condition**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **inflating a bicycle tyre** with a hand pump. - The **down-stroke** of the pump piston draws in atmospheric air (suction). - The **up-stroke** squeezes that trapped air into a much smaller space inside the tyre. This "squeezing" is exactly what a compressor does — it applies **mechanical work** to *force gas molecules closer together*, which *increases their pressure* and *raises their temperature*. The harder you push (more work), the higher the pressure you achieve. > **Three real-world heat-loss scenarios** for this "squeezing" process: > 1. **Isothermal (ideal limit):** You squeeze so *slowly* that heat continuously escapes to the surroundings — temperature stays constant. Requires the **minimum work**. > 2. **Polytropic (real machines):** Heat loss is *partial* and follows $pV^n = $ constant. > 3. **Adiabatic / Isentropic (worst case):** No time for heat loss — all work converts to internal energy, gas heats up the **most**. Requires the **maximum work**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** Since *work input* is minimum for isothermal and maximum for adiabatic, real compressors are designed with **cooling jackets (water-cooled cylinders)** to push the actual process *closer to isothermal*, saving energy and protecting mechanical parts from overheating. ## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical Range | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $W_c$ | Compressor work (per kg) | **kJ/kg** | 100 – 350 | | $r_p$ | Pressure ratio $p_2/p_1$ | **dimensionless** | 3 – 12 | | $V_s$ | Swept volume | **m³** | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-1}$ | | $V_c$ | Clearance volume | **m³** | 2 % – 6 % of $V_s$ | | $\eta_{vol}$ | Volumetric efficiency | **%** | 60 % – 90 % | | $\eta_{iso}$ | Isentropic efficiency | **%** | 70 % – 85 % | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for Compressors For a **control volume** enclosing the compressor (one cylinder or one stage), neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes, and assuming **adiabatic** operation: $$ W_{comp, in} = h_2 - h_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{[per unit mass]} $$ The *actual* work input is always **greater** than the *ideal* (isentropic) work because of internal irreversibilities (friction, throttling across valves, turbulence). ## 2.2 The Three Reference Compression Processes ### Process (a) — Isothermal Compression (Ideal, Heat Removed Continuously) Assumes $T = $ constant. From $pV = mRT$: $$ p \cdot v = \text{constant} \Rightarrow pV = \text{constant} $$ $$ W_{iso} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} p \, dV = p_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = p_1 V_1 \ln r_p $$ $$ \boxed{W_{iso} = RT_1 \ln r_p \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]} $$ ### Process (b) — Polytropic Compression (Real Process) $$ pV^n = \text{constant}, \quad 1 < n < \gamma \quad \text{(when heat is rejected)} $$ $$ W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} \, RT_1 \left[ \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] = \frac{n}{n-1} \, RT_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] $$ ### Process (c) — Isentropic (Adiabatic-Reversible) Compression $$ pV^\gamma = \text{constant} $$ $$ W_{isen} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \, RT_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** Always check whether the question specifies "**with cooling**" (use polytropic with $n < \gamma$) or "**without cooling / adiabatic**" (use isentropic with $\gamma$). The phrase "*isothermal efficiency*" refers to comparing the *isentropic* work to the *isothermal* work — not the *actual* work. ## 2.3 The Reciprocating Compressor Indicator Diagram (P-V Plot) A **single-acting reciprocating compressor** with *clearance volume* has **four distinct processes** forming a closed loop on the $p$–$V$ plane: | Process | Path | Description | Thermodynamic Identity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 3 → 4 | Expansion (re-expansion of trapped gas) | $p_4 V_4^n = p_3 V_3^n$ | Polytropic / isentropic | | 4 → 1 | Suction stroke | $p_1 = p_{\text{suction}}$ | Constant low pressure | | 1 → 2 | Compression stroke | $p V^n = $ const | Polytropic | | 2 → 3 | Discharge stroke | $p_3 = p_{\text{discharge}}$ | Constant high pressure | > **Clearance Ratio ($C$):** > $$ C = \frac{V_c}{V_s} = \frac{V_4}{V_1 - V_4} $$ ## 2.4 Volumetric Efficiency — The KTU Favourite **Volumetric efficiency** is the *ratio of actual volume of free gas drawn in (at suction conditions) to the swept volume*: $$ \eta_{vol} = \frac{\text{Actual volume of gas inducted}}{\text{Swept volume}} = \frac{V_1 - V_4}{V_1 - V_3} $$ Through algebraic manipulation of the re-expansion relation $p_3 V_3^n = p_1 V_4^n$ and using $V_3 = V_c$: $$ \boxed{\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1/n}} $$ where: - $C = V_c / V_s$ is the **clearance ratio** - $p_2 / p_1$ is the **pressure ratio** - $n$ is the **polytropic index** of re-expansion (usually $\approx \gamma$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The two parameters a designer can *control* to maximise $\eta_{vol}$ are (1) **reducing clearance ratio $C$** and (2) **lowering the pressure ratio per stage**. This is precisely the engineering motivation for *multi-stage* compressors with intercooling. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table. Every line below is a guaranteed KTU 2024 mark-earning formula.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $W_{iso} = p_1 V_1 \ln r_p$ | Isothermal work | kJ | | 2 | $W_{iso/kg} = RT_1 \ln r_p$ | Isothermal work per kg | kJ/kg | | 3 | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\, p_1 V_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$ | Polytropic work | kJ | | 4 | $W_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}\, p_1 V_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | Isentropic work | kJ | | 5 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$ | Discharge temperature (polytropic) | K | | 6 | $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \cdot r_p^{1/n}$ | Volumetric efficiency | — | | 7 | $C = V_c / V_s$ | Clearance ratio | — | | 8 | $\eta_{iso-eff} = W_{iso} / W_{act}$ | Isothermal efficiency | — | | 9 | $r_{p,opt} = \left(\dfrac{p_1}{p_{inter}}\right)^{\text{stage}}$ | Per-stage ratio in multi-stage | — | | 10 | $T_{2,\text{after cooler}} = T_1$ | Ideal intercooling | K | > **Note on notation:** For total mass of work on a **volumetric** basis, multiply $W$ (in kJ) by $\rho_1$ or use the specific gas constant $R$ with the absolute inlet temperature $T_1$ (in **K** — *not °C*). This is a common KTU valuation pitfall. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning (HVAC):** Reciprocating compressors in domestic refrigerators; multi-stage with intercooling in large chillers. - **Gas Pipeline Transport:** Multi-stage centrifugal compressors station every 80–120 km to maintain line pressure. - **Petrochemical Industry:** Isothermal compressors for ethylene, propylene, and LNG. - **Scuba Diving / Breathing Air:** High-pressure multi-stage (200–300 bar) reciprocating compressors with inter-stage cooling and drying. - **Pneumatic Tools & Tyre Inflation:** Single-stage reciprocating units with $r_p \leq 8$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Polytropic Compression Work (Per kg of gas) We start from the **SFEE** for an open system (control volume) of a compressor cylinder. **Step 1 — Apply SFEE neglecting KE, PE and heat transfer (adiabatic):** $$ w_s = -\int_1^2 v \, dp $$ The negative sign appears because the boundary work done *on* the gas is **positive** (work input). **Step 2 — For a reversible polytropic process,** the equation of state is: $$ p \cdot v^n = \text{constant} = p_1 v_1^n = p_2 v_2^n $$ Differentiate both sides to get $v$ as a function of $p$: $$ n \, p^{n-1} \cdot v^n \, dp + p^n \cdot n \, v^{n-1} \, dv = 0 $$ Wait — easier approach: directly use the relationship $p \, v^n = K$: $$ v = \left(\frac{K}{p}\right)^{1/n} = K^{1/n} \cdot p^{-1/n} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the SFEE integral:** $$ w_s = -\int_{p_1}^{p_2} K^{1/n} \, p^{-1/n} \, dp $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:** $$ w_s = -K^{1/n} \left[ \frac{p^{1 - 1/n}}{1 - 1/n} \right]_{p_1}^{p_2} = \frac{-n \, K^{1/n}}{n-1} \left[ p_2^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - p_1^{\frac{n-1}{n}} \right] $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $K^{1/n} = p_1^{1/n} v_1$ (using $p_1 v_1^n = K$):** $$ w_s = \frac{-n \, p_1^{1/n} v_1}{n-1} \left[ p_2^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - p_1^{\frac{n-1}{n}} \right] $$ **Step 6 — Factor out $p_1^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$:** $$ w_s = \frac{-n \, p_1 v_1}{n-1} \left[ \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] $$ **Step 7 — Recognise $p_1 v_1 = RT_1$ for an ideal gas:** $$ \boxed{w_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} \, RT_1 \left[ \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] \quad \text{kJ/kg}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention Check:** The expression inside the brackets is **positive** when $p_2 > p_1$ and $n > 1$. The expression gives a **positive value**, which correctly represents the *work input required by the compressor*. KTU valuation expects a *positive* magnitude. ## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Efficiency We use the indicator diagram notation: $V_1$ = volume at start of compression, $V_2$ = volume at end of compression, $V_3 = V_c$ = clearance volume, $V_4$ = volume after re-expansion of clearance gas. **Step 1 — Define volumetric efficiency:** $$ \eta_{vol} = \frac{V_1 - V_4}{V_s} = \frac{V_1 - V_4}{V_1 - V_3} $$ **Step 2 — Re-expansion process (3 → 4) follows the same polytropic law:** $$ p_3 V_3^n = p_4 V_4^n $$ But $p_3 = p_2$ (discharge pressure) and $p_4 = p_1$ (suction pressure), and $V_3 = V_c$: $$ p_2 \, V_c^n = p_1 \, V_4^n \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_4 = V_c \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1/n} $$ **Step 3 — Express $V_1$ in terms of clearance and swept volumes:** $$ V_1 = V_s + V_c $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the $\eta_{vol}$ expression:** $$ \eta_{vol} = \frac{(V_s + V_c) - V_c \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1/n}}{V_s} $$ **Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $V_s$ and introduce $C = V_c / V_s$:** $$ \eta_{vol} = \frac{V_s(1 + C) - C \cdot V_s \cdot r_p^{1/n}}{V_s} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1/n}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Optimum Pressure Ratio for Multi-Stage Compression with Intercooling For a **two-stage reciprocating compressor with intercooling to initial temperature $T_1$**, the total work input is the sum of work in stage 1 and stage 2. **Step 1 — Let $r_p = p_2 / p_1$ be the overall pressure ratio and $x = p_x / p_1$ be the inter-stage pressure ratio.** Then stage-1 ratio is $x$ and stage-2 ratio is $r_p / x$. **Step 2 — Total isentropic work (per kg) for both stages with $n = \gamma$:** $$ W_{total} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} RT_1 \left[ \left(\frac{p_x}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] + \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} RT_1 \left[ \left(\frac{p_2}{p_x}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$ **Step 3 — Minimise $W_{total}$ with respect to $x$ (set $dW_{total}/dx = 0$):** $$ \frac{dW_{total}}{dx} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} RT_1 \left[ \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} \cdot x^{-1/\gamma} \cdot \frac{1}{p_1^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} \right] + \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} RT_1 \left[ \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} \left(\frac{p_2}{x}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{x}\right) \right] = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Simplify and rearrange:** $$ x^{-1/\gamma} = \left(\frac{p_2}{x}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \cdot \frac{1}{x} $$ Multiply both sides by $x$: $$ x^{1 - 1/\gamma} = \left(\frac{p_2}{x}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = p_2^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \cdot x^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} $$ **Step 5 — Collect $x$ terms on the left:** $$ x^{1 - 1/\gamma + \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = p_2^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} $$ The exponent of $x$ becomes $1 - 1/\gamma + (\gamma-1)/\gamma = 1$. Therefore: $$ x = p_2^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \cdot p_1^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} $$ **Step 6 — Recognise that $p_x = x \cdot p_1$, hence:** $$ p_x = p_1 \cdot \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = \sqrt{p_1 \cdot p_2} \quad \text{(geometric mean)} $$ **Step 7 — Therefore the optimum inter-stage pressure is the *geometric mean*:** $$ \boxed{p_x = \sqrt{p_1 \cdot p_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_{p,\text{opt}} = \frac{p_x}{p_1} = \frac{p_2}{p_x} = \sqrt{\frac{p_2}{p_1}} = \sqrt{r_p}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Extension — Minimum work condition:** The minimum work obtained by *ideal intercooling* and *equal pressure ratios per stage* is: > $$ W_{min} = \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma-1} RT_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{2\gamma}} - 1 \right] = \frac{2n}{n-1} RT_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{n-1}{2n}} - 1 \right] $$ > For $N$ stages, replace the exponent $\frac{n-1}{2n}$ with $\frac{n-1}{N \cdot n}$ and the leading factor $\frac{2n}{n-1}$ with $\frac{N \cdot n}{n-1}$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Stages of a Multi-Stage Compressor with Intercooling ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Atmospheric Air
p1, T1] --> B[Stage 1
Cylinder with
Water Jacket] B --> C[Hot Compressed Air
p_x, T2 > T1] C --> D[Intercooler 1
Heat Exchanger] D --> E[Cooled Air
p_x, T1 Approx] E --> F[Stage 2
Cylinder with
Water Jacket] F --> G[Hot Compressed Air
p2, T3 > T1] G --> H[After Cooler
Final Cooling] H --> I[Delivery
p2, T near T1] H --> J[Moisture Separator
Drain Trap] J --> I ``` > **Engineering Note:** The **intercooler** is the *heart* of multi-stage efficiency. It returns the gas to the original temperature $T_1$ (or as close as possible), which *reduces the specific volume* entering the next stage. The **moisture separator** prevents condensed water from entering the next stage — a critical KTU point often overlooked. ## 4.2 P-V Indicator Diagram (Reciprocating Compressor with Clearance) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Point 4: Start of Suction
p = p1, V = V4"] -->|"Suction stroke 4 to 1
Constant low pressure"| P2["Point 1: End of Suction
p = p1, V = V1"] P2 -->|"Compression 1 to 2
Polytropic pV^n = K"| P3["Point 2: End of Compression
p = p2, V = V2"] P3 -->|"Discharge 2 to 3
Constant high pressure"| P4["Point 3: End of Discharge
p = p2, V = Vc = V3"] P4 -->|"Re-expansion 3 to 4
Polytropic pV^n = K"| P1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - $V_c = V_3$ is the clearance volume (small, top-right corner). - $V_s = V_1 - V_3$ is the swept volume (full piston displacement). - $V_4 - V_3$ represents the *expanded clearance gas* that *reduces* the volume available for fresh charge. - The area enclosed by the loop equals the **net indicated work per cycle**. ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Effect of Clearance Ratio on Volumetric Efficiency ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Increase Clearance Ratio C] --> B[Larger Re-expansion
V4 increases] B --> C[Smaller Inducted
Volume per cycle] C --> D[Volumetric Efficiency
eta_vol decreases] E[Increase Pressure Ratio rp] --> F[More Re-expansion
V4 increases] F --> C G[Reduce C and
Use Multi-stage] --> H[eta_vol increases
eta_iso improves] H --> I[Lower Discharge
Temperature] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Flow — Isothermal vs Polytropic vs Isentropic Work ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Given: p1, p2, T1, Gas Constant R] --> B{Process Specified?} B -->|Isothermal| C["W_iso = p1 V1 ln(p2/p1)
Lowest work input"] B -->|Polytropic, index n| D["W_poly = n/(n-1) RT1 (rp^((n-1)/n) - 1)
Intermediate work"] B -->|Isentropic, gamma| E["W_isen = gamma/(gamma-1) RT1 (rp^((gamma-1)/gamma) - 1)
Highest work input"] C --> F[Compare with W_actual] D --> F E --> F F --> G["Isothermal Efficiency = W_iso / W_actual
Isentropic Efficiency = W_isen / W_actual"] ``` > **KTU Memory Aid:** *I*so < *P*oly < *A*diabatic in terms of work — think **IPA** = "**I**deal small, **P**roper, **A**ctual large". # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 3 – Compressors):** > 1. **Temperature Unit Error:** Students frequently plug $T_1$ in **°C** instead of **K** in $W = RT_1 \ln r_p$. This single mistake *invalidates the entire numerical answer* and costs **3–4 marks**. > 2. **Confusing the Three Efficiencies:** KTU exam setters *deliberately* test if you distinguish $\eta_{vol}$ (volumetric), $\eta_{iso-eff}$ (isothermal), and $\eta_{mech}$ (mechanical). Mark loss if used interchangeably. > 3. **Sign Convention on Work:** Compressor work is *work input* — always **report as positive magnitude**, but the *direction* must be shown as an *arrow into* the control volume on the schematic. > 4. **Polytropic Index vs Specific Heat Ratio:** If a question says "**with cooling**", use $n$ (polytropic) — *not* $\gamma$. If it says "**adiabatic reversible**", use $\gamma$ (isentropic). > 5. **Optimum Pressure Ratio in Multi-stage:** Students forget to take the *Nth root* for an $N$-stage machine. The general form is $r_{p,\text{per stage}} = r_p^{1/N}$. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor. How does it vary with clearance ratio and pressure ratio?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the actual volume of free gas inducted at suction conditions to the swept volume of the piston per cycle. > $$ \eta_{vol} = \frac{V_1 - V_4}{V_s} = 1 + C - C \cdot r_p^{1/n} $$ > > **Variation (2 marks):** > - $\eta_{vol}$ **decreases** as clearance ratio $C$ increases. > - $\eta_{vol}$ **decreases** as pressure ratio $r_p$ increases. > - As $C \to 0$, $\eta_{vol} \to 1$ (100 %). ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Why is multi-stage compression with intercooling preferred over single-stage compression for high pressure ratios?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Reduction in work input (1 mark):** For a given overall pressure ratio, the total work is minimised when each stage has an *equal pressure ratio* $r_p^{1/N}$, and the gas is *cooled back to $T_1$* between stages. 2. **Lower discharge temperature (1 mark):** Intercooling limits the temperature rise, protecting cylinder walls, valves, and lubricant from thermal degradation. 3. **Improved volumetric efficiency (1 mark):** A lower *per-stage* pressure ratio increases $\eta_{vol}$ per stage, so the machine *sweeps* more gas per cycle. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A: (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** A single-stage reciprocating compressor compresses **0.05 kg/s of air** from **1 bar, 300 K** to **8 bar**. Assuming a **polytropic process with $n = 1.3$**, calculate: (i) The power required to drive the compressor, in **kW**. (4 marks) (ii) The discharge temperature of air. (3 marks) **(b)** For the same compressor, if a **clearance ratio of 4 %** exists, and the polytropic re-expansion index is **$n = 1.3$**, determine: (i) The **volumetric efficiency**. (3 marks) (ii) The **actual volume of air inducted per cycle** if the swept volume is **$V_s = 0.005$ m³**. (4 marks) --- ### Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a)(i) — Power required (4 marks) **Given:** - $\dot{m} = 0.05$ kg/s - $p_1 = 1$ bar $= 1 \times 10^5$ Pa - $T_1 = 300$ K - $p_2 = 8$ bar - $n = 1.3$ - $R_{\text{air}} = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K $= 287$ J/kg·K **Step 1 — Work per kg (polytropic):** $$ w = \frac{n}{n-1} RT_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right] $$ **Step 2 — Compute the exponent:** $$ \frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{1.3 - 1}{1.3} = \frac{0.3}{1.3} = 0.2308 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $r_p^{0.2308}$:** $$ 8^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \ln 8} = e^{0.2308 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.4799} = 1.6158 $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the formula:** $$ w = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 0.287 \times 300 \times (1.6158 - 1) $$ $$ w = 4.333 \times 0.287 \times 300 \times 0.6158 $$ $$ w = 4.333 \times 86.1 \times 0.6158 = 4.333 \times 53.02 $$ $$ \boxed{w = 229.74 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ **Step 5 — Power input:** $$ P = \dot{m} \times w = 0.05 \times 229.74 $$ $$ \boxed{P = 11.49 \text{ kW}} $$ #### Part (a)(ii) — Discharge temperature (3 marks) $$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{n-1}{n}} = 300 \times 1.6158 $$ $$ \boxed{T_2 = 484.7 \text{ K} \;\; (\approx 211.7\,°\text{C})} $$ > **Comment:** A discharge temperature of 484.7 K exceeds the safe limit for most lubricating oils (~ 420 K). This justifies using *multi-stage compression with intercooling* in practice. #### Part (b)(i) — Volumetric efficiency (3 marks) **Step 1 — Clearance ratio and pressure ratio:** $$ C = 0.04, \quad r_p = 8 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $r_p^{1/n}$:** $$ \frac{1}{n} = \frac{1}{1.3} = 0.7692 $$ $$ 8^{0.7692} = e^{0.7692 \ln 8} = e^{0.7692 \times 2.0794} = e^{1.5995} = 4.950 $$ **Step 3 — Apply the formula:** $$ \eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \cdot r_p^{1/n} = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04 \times 4.950 $$ $$ \eta_{vol} = 1.04 - 0.198 = 0.842 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{vol} = 84.2\%} $$ #### Part (b)(ii) — Actual volume inducted (4 marks) $$ V_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{vol} \times V_s = 0.842 \times 0.005 $$ $$ \boxed{V_{\text{actual}} = 4.21 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative verification:** $V_4 = V_c \cdot r_p^{1/n} = (0.04 \times 0.005) \times 4.950 = 9.9 \times 10^{-4}$ m³. Then $V_1 - V_4 = 0.005 \times 1.04 - 9.9 \times 10^{-4} = 5.2 \times 10^{-3} - 9.9 \times 10^{-4} = 4.21 \times 10^{-3}$ m³. ✓ --- ### Question B (Internal Choice — Alternative to A): (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat $p$–$V$ diagram, explain the **working of a single-stage reciprocating compressor with clearance**. Derive an expression for its **volumetric efficiency**. (7 marks) **(b)** A two-stage reciprocating compressor with **complete intercooling** compresses air from **1 bar, 20 °C** to **20 bar**. The compression in both stages follows the **law $pV^{1.3} = $ constant**. Calculate: (i) The **optimum intermediate pressure**. (3 marks) (ii) The **work of compression per kg of air**. (4 marks) --- ### Complete Step-by-Step Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Theory + Derivation (7 marks) > **Working (3 marks):** A single-stage reciprocating compressor has a piston moving inside a cylinder fitted with *suction* and *delivery* valves. Due to the **clearance volume** $V_c$ left between the piston crown and the cylinder head at the end of the delivery stroke, a small amount of high-pressure air remains trapped. As the piston moves back, this trapped air **re-expands** until its pressure falls to the suction pressure $p_1$. Only *after* this re-expansion does the suction valve open, and fresh air is inducted. Thus the *effective* volume of fresh charge is **less than the swept volume**. > **P-V Diagram (1 mark):** Closed loop with four processes — re-expansion (3→4), suction (4→1), compression (1→2), delivery (2→3). > **Derivation (3 marks):** See Section 3.2 of these notes. The final expression is: > $$ \eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{1/n} $$ #### Part (b)(i) — Optimum Intermediate Pressure (3 marks) **Step 1 — Apply the geometric mean rule:** $$ p_x = \sqrt{p_1 \cdot p_2} = \sqrt{1 \times 20} = \sqrt{20} $$ $$ \boxed{p_x = 4.472 \text{ bar}} $$ **Step 2 — Verify per-stage pressure ratio is the square root:** $$ r_{p,\text{per stage}} = \sqrt{20} = 4.472 $$ #### Part (b)(ii) — Work of Compression per kg (4 marks) **Step 1 — Use the minimum work formula for 2-stage with intercooling to $T_1$:** $$ w_{min} = \frac{2n}{n-1} RT_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{n-1}{2n}} - 1 \right] $$ **Step 2 — Convert temperature:** $$ T_1 = 20 + 273 = 293 \text{ K} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the exponent:** $$ \frac{n-1}{2n} = \frac{0.3}{2.6} = 0.1154 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $r_p^{0.1154}$:** $$ 20^{0.1154} = e^{0.1154 \times \ln 20} = e^{0.1154 \times 2.9957} = e^{0.3457} = 1.4131 $$ **Step 5 — Substitute:** $$ w_{min} = \frac{2 \times 1.3}{0.3} \times 0.287 \times 293 \times (1.4131 - 1) $$ $$ w_{min} = 8.667 \times 0.287 \times 293 \times 0.4131 $$ $$ w_{min} = 8.667 \times 84.091 \times 0.4131 $$ $$ w_{min} = 8.667 \times 34.73 = 301.0 $$ $$ \boxed{w_{min} \approx 301 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ > **Comparison (optional 1-mark insight):** A *single-stage adiabatic* compression to the same ratio would require $\frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 0.287 \times 293 \times (20^{0.2857} - 1) = 3.5 \times 84.1 \times (2.395 - 1) = 410.8$ kJ/kg. **Two-stage intercooled saves ~ 27 % of work.** This is the central engineering motivation — write this in your answer to earn the highest KTU descriptor for "**Apply / Analyse**". --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):** - [x] **Compressor work is *work INPUT*** — always positive, *arrow into* the control volume. - [x] **Three reference processes** (memorise all three formulas): *Isothermal* ($pV=$ const), *Polytropic* ($pV^n=$ const, $1

topics.title: Performance Characteristics and Selection Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Characteristics and Selection Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Performance Characteristics** of a thermal engineering system refer to the quantitative representation of how key output parameters (such as specific work output, thermal efficiency, isentropic efficiency, power output, fuel consumption rate, mean effective pressure, etc.) vary with respect to operating variables (such as speed, load, pressure ratio, temperature ratio, mass flow rate, compression ratio, cut-off ratio, etc.) under steady-state and steady-flow conditions.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPET521), performance characteristics typically encompass the graphical and analytical study of:
- **Internal Combustion (IC) Engines** (SI and CI)
- **Steam Turbines and Steam Power Plants**
- **Gas Turbines and Jet Propulsion Systems**
- **Compressors** (Reciprocating, Rotary, Centrifugal, Axial)

**Selection Criteria** is the systematic engineering methodology that uses these performance characteristics, along with economic, environmental, and operational considerations, to identify the most appropriate thermal system for a given application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Students must understand that "performance characteristics" are NOT just single numbers—they are **curves (maps)** plotted between an independent variable (on X-axis) and a dependent performance parameter (on Y-axis). The KTU Board Examiner frequently asks students to *sketch* and *interpret* these characteristic curves.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **bicycle** and a **motorbike**:
- A bicycle is great for short distances, low speeds, and offers good "fuel economy" (your body's energy), but its power output is low.
- A motorbike offers high speed and high power but consumes more fuel (energy).

Now imagine plotting **Speed vs. Fuel Efficiency** for both. You would get two very different curves. The first curve (bicycle) is high efficiency at low speed; the second (motorbike) maintains reasonable efficiency across a wide speed range. A delivery person selecting between them would look at these "performance characteristics" to make an informed choice—this is exactly what an engineer does when selecting a thermal system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Just as a doctor uses an ECG (Electrocardiogram) to judge heart health, a thermal engineer uses a **Performance Map** (a chart of efficiency vs. pressure ratio, or specific fuel consumption vs. speed) to "diagnose" how well a thermal device will operate under a given set of conditions. The curves tell the *complete story* of the machine.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The standard metrics used in characterizing thermal systems (must be remembered in **bold**):

- **Specific Work Output (Wₛ):** Net work done per unit mass of working fluid, measured in **kJ/kg**.
- **Isentropic Efficiency (η_isen):** Ratio of actual work to isentropic work, dimensionless.
- **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech):** Ratio of brake power to indicated power, dimensionless.
- **Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol):** Ratio of actual volumetric flow to swept volume flow, dimensionless.
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** Fuel consumed per unit power output per hour, **kg/kWh**.
- **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth):** Ratio of brake power to heat input from fuel, dimensionless.
- **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP):** A theoretical constant pressure that, if acted on the piston during the entire power stroke, would produce the same work, measured in **bar** or **kPa**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Performance Characteristics of IC Engines

The performance of an IC engine is not a single fixed value—it varies with operating conditions. The key independent variables are:

1. **Engine Speed (N):** Measured in **rpm**.
2. **Load (Brake Mean Effective Pressure, BMEP):** The actual load on the engine.
3. **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F):** Ratio of mass of air to mass of fuel.
4. **Ignition Timing / Injection Timing.**

### 2.1.1 Variation of Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) with Speed

For a **SI Engine**:
- At very low speeds, SFC is high due to poor mixture formation and incomplete combustion.
- As speed increases to a certain optimum value, SFC reaches a **minimum** (best fuel economy).
- Beyond this optimum, SFC increases again due to **time losses** (combustion is incomplete in available time) and **pumping losses**.

For a **CI (Diesel) Engine**:
- SFC is generally **lower** than SI engines due to higher compression ratio and lean combustion.
- SFC variation is relatively flatter over a wider speed range.

### 2.1.2 Variation of Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth) with Load

- At **no-load (0% load):** η_bth = 0 (no work output, but fuel is consumed to overcome friction).
- As load increases, η_bth **increases almost linearly** until a peak value at full load.
- The slope of this curve indicates how efficiently the engine utilizes fuel at part loads.

### 2.1.3 Variation of Mechanical Efficiency with Speed

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = 1 - \frac{FP}{IP}$$

Where **FP** is the friction power (a function of speed). As speed increases:
- **Friction power** increases (proportional to N² approximately for rubbing parts).
- **Indicated power** increases.
- The mechanical efficiency reaches a **maximum** at a particular speed, then decreases as friction dominates.

## 2.2 Performance Characteristics of Gas Turbines

For an **Open Cycle Gas Turbine**, the performance parameters and their variations are:

### 2.2.1 Effect of Pressure Ratio (rₚ) on Thermal Efficiency

Thermal efficiency first **increases** with pressure ratio, reaches a **maximum** at an optimum pressure ratio, and then **decreases** as pressure ratio further increases. The optimum pressure ratio depends on the **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)**.

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_3} \cdot \frac{(r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1)}{(\eta_T \cdot \eta_C)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The compressor work and turbine work are both functions of pressure ratio. At low rₚ, compressor work is small. At very high rₚ, compressor work dominates, reducing net work output and hence efficiency.

### 2.2.2 Effect of TIT (Turbine Inlet Temperature) on Efficiency

Higher TIT always **increases** thermal efficiency and specific work output, but is limited by **metallurgical constraints** of the turbine blades.

### 2.2.3 Effect of Isentropic Efficiencies

- Compressor isentropic efficiency affects **compressor work input**.
- Turbine isentropic efficiency affects **turbine work output**.
- Lower efficiencies shift the optimum pressure ratio to higher values.

## 2.3 Performance Characteristics of Compressors

### 2.3.1 Reciprocating Compressor
- Volumetric efficiency decreases with increasing pressure ratio due to **re-expansion** of clearance gas.
- Isothermal efficiency is the theoretical ideal; actual cycles are between isothermal and adiabatic.

### 2.3.2 Centrifugal / Axial Compressor (Compressor Map)
- The performance map plots **Pressure Ratio (rₚ)** vs. **Mass Flow Rate (ṁ)** for various constant-speed lines.
- **Surge Line:** The left boundary beyond which the compressor becomes unstable (flow reversal, damaging pulsations).
- **Stone Wall (Choke):** The right boundary beyond which no further mass flow is possible.
- Operating point must always lie between surge and choke.

## 2.4 Selection Criteria (The Engineering Decision-Making Framework)

The selection of a thermal system for a specific application is governed by a structured decision matrix. Key criteria include:

| S.No | Selection Criterion | Description | Typical Weightage |
|------|---------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Power Output Required** | Match the rated capacity (kW/MW) to the application load. | High |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | Thermal efficiency or SFC; affects operating cost. | High |
| 3 | **Operating Speed** | High-speed machines are compact but require precise balancing. | Medium |
| 4 | **Weight and Size** | Critical for aerospace, marine, and portable applications. | High (mobile) |
| 5 | **Cost (Capital + Running)** | IC engines: low capital, moderate running. Gas turbines: high capital, low running. | High |
| 6 | **Reliability and Durability** | Continuous-duty applications need high reliability. | High |
| 7 | **Fuel Availability** | Diesel, gasoline, natural gas, biogas, etc. | Location-dependent |
| 8 | **Emissions and Noise** | Important for urban and environmentally sensitive zones. | High (modern) |
| 9 | **Starting and Load Response** | Quick start-up: IC > Gas Turbine. Quick load variation: Gas Turbine. | Application-dependent |
| 10 | **Maintenance Requirements** | IC engines require frequent maintenance; gas turbines less frequent but specialized. | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application-Based Selection Rule of Thumb (frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Automotive (Cars, Bikes):** SI Engine (petrol/gasoline) for high-speed, light-duty; CI Engine (diesel) for heavy-duty trucks.
> - **Aircraft Propulsion:** Gas Turbine (turbojet/turbofan) for high-speed flight; piston engines for small aircraft.
> - **Power Generation (>50 MW):** Steam Turbine (Rankine cycle) for base load; Gas Turbine for peak load.
> - **Industrial Compressed Air:** Reciprocating compressor for high pressure; Screw/Scroll for oil-free applications.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{m_f \times CV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency (heat input basis) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP}{m_f \times CV}$ | Indicated thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} = 1 - \dfrac{FP}{IP}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual volume of charge}}{\text{Swept volume}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $SFC = \dfrac{m_f \times 3600}{BP \times t}$ | Specific fuel consumption | kg/kWh |
| 6 | $BMEP = \dfrac{BP \times 60 \times n_{cr}}{L \times A \times N \times k}$ | Brake mean effective pressure (multi-cyl) | kPa or bar |
| 7 | $\eta_{isen,C} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$ | Compressor isentropic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{isen,T} = \dfrac{h_3 - h_4}{h_3 - h_{4s}}$ | Turbine isentropic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{th,GT} = \dfrac{W_T - W_C}{Q_{in}}$ | Gas turbine thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $W_C = c_{p,a}(T_2 - T_1)$ | Compressor work per kg air | kJ/kg |
| 12 | $W_T = c_{p,g}(T_3 - T_4)$ | Turbine work per kg gas | kJ/kg |
| 13 | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Isentropic temperature-pressure relation | ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> In all these formulas:
> - $BP$ = Brake Power, $IP$ = Indicated Power, $FP$ = Friction Power (all in kW).
> - $m_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel (kg/s), $CV$ = Calorific Value (kJ/kg).
> - $h$ = specific enthalpy, subscripts 1=inlet of compressor, 2=outlet of compressor, 3=inlet of turbine, 4=outlet of turbine; 's' denotes isentropic state.
> - $N$ = speed in rpm, $n_{cr}$ = number of crank revolutions per cycle (2 for 4-stroke, 1 for 2-stroke).
> - $L$ = stroke length, $A$ = cross-sectional area of piston, $k$ = number of cylinders.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

The systematic study of performance characteristics is critical in:

1. **Power Plant Design:** Choosing between steam, gas, diesel, or combined cycles based on plant capacity and load profile.
2. **Automotive Engineering:** Calibrating engine maps for fuel injection systems in modern ECUs (Engine Control Units).
3. **Aerospace:** Designing turbofan bypass ratios and selecting afterburner configurations.
4. **Process Industries:** Selecting the right compressor type for chemical, petrochemical, and refrigeration applications.
5. **HVAC Systems:** Choosing chillers (reciprocating, screw, centrifugal) based on cooling load curves.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between Efficiencies in an IC Engine

### Problem Statement:
Derive the relationship between **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth)**, **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (η_ith)**, and **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Define Brake Thermal Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}}$$

where $Q_{in}$ is the total heat input from fuel per second, given by:

$$Q_{in} = m_f \times CV$$

**Step 2: Define Indicated Thermal Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{IP}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 3: Define Mechanical Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP}$$

**Step 4: Multiply η_ith and η_mech.**

$$\eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech} = \frac{IP}{Q_{in}} \times \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the result from Step 1.**

$$\eta_{bth} = \eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech}$$

### Result:
$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} = \eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This is a fundamental relationship tested every year in KTU exams. The *brake* (output at the shaft) efficiency is always **less** than the *indicated* (output inside the cylinder) efficiency because some indicated power is lost to friction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) in terms of η_bth

### Problem Statement:
Derive the expression for **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** in terms of brake thermal efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Recall the definition of Brake Thermal Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{m_f \times CV}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to find the mass flow rate of fuel (m_f).**

$$m_f = \frac{BP}{\eta_{bth} \times CV}$$

**Step 3: BSFC is defined as fuel consumed per unit brake power per unit time, expressed in kg/kWh.**

$$BSFC = \frac{m_f \times 3600}{BP}$$

(We multiply by 3600 to convert kg/s to kg/h since BP is in kW.)

**Step 4: Substitute the expression for m_f from Step 2.**

$$BSFC = \frac{3600 \times BP}{\eta_{bth} \times CV \times BP}$$

**Step 5: Simplify.**

$$BSFC = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \times CV}$$

### Result:
$$\boxed{BSFC = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \times CV}} \quad \text{(kg/kWh)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-asked formula** in KTU for module questions. Notice that BSFC is **inversely proportional** to η_bth. A higher thermal efficiency automatically means lower fuel consumption for the same power output.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Optimum Pressure Ratio for a Gas Turbine

### Problem Statement:
For a simple open-cycle gas turbine (with isentropic efficiencies of compressor and turbine), derive the expression for the pressure ratio at which **specific net work output is maximum**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Express Compressor Work (per kg of air).**

$$W_C = c_{p,a} \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = \frac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

Let $x = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, so $W_C = \dfrac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} (x - 1)$.

**Step 2: Express Turbine Work (per kg of gas).**

$$W_T = c_{p,g} \cdot (T_3 - T_4) = c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{x} \right]$$

**Step 3: Net Specific Work Output.**

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_C$$

$$W_{net} = c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \left(1 - \frac{1}{x}\right) - \frac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} (x - 1)$$

**Step 4: To find maximum W_net, differentiate W_net with respect to x and set it to zero.**

$$\frac{dW_{net}}{dx} = c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \cdot \frac{1}{x^2} - \frac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve for x.**

$$\frac{1}{x^2} = \frac{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}{c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \cdot \eta_{isen,C}}$$

$$x^2 = \frac{c_{p,g} \cdot T_3 \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \cdot \eta_{isen,C}}{c_{p,a} \cdot T_1}$$

**Step 6: Solve for the pressure ratio.**

$$\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \frac{T_3}{T_1} \cdot \frac{\eta_{isen,T} \cdot \eta_{isen,C} \cdot c_{p,g}}{c_{p,a}}$$

### Result:
$$\boxed{\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)_{opt, W} = \left[ \frac{T_3}{T_1} \cdot \eta_{isen,T} \cdot \eta_{isen,C} \right]^{\frac{\gamma}{2(\gamma-1)}}}$$

(Assuming $c_{p,a} = c_{p,g}$ for simplification.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The optimum pressure ratio for **maximum work** is different from the optimum pressure ratio for **maximum efficiency**. The KTU examiner often asks students to compare these two.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Thermal Systems for Selection

The following table serves as an **analytical matrix** for selecting between IC engine, Gas Turbine, and Steam Turbine in various scenarios:

| Selection Parameter | IC Engine (Diesel) | Gas Turbine | Steam Turbine |
|---------------------|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Power Range** | 5 kW – 25 MW (per unit) | 1 MW – 300 MW (per unit) | 50 MW – 1000 MW (per unit) |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | 35% – 45% (large diesel) | 30% – 42% (simple cycle) | 35% – 40% (Rankine) |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Fuel Flexibility** | Diesel, biodiesel, dual-fuel | Natural gas, kerosene, jet fuel | Coal, biomass, nuclear, waste heat |
| **Starting Time** | Quick (seconds) | Moderate (minutes) | Slow (hours - boiler warmup) |
| **Load Response** | Good | Excellent | Slow |
| **Weight/Power Ratio** | High (heavy) | Low (compact) | Very High (very heavy) |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent, low cost | Less frequent, high cost | Moderate |
| **Best Suited For** | Standby, mobile, marine | Peak load, aviation, pipelines | Base load power plants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In the 2024 Scheme, marks are specifically awarded for **justifying the selection** (e.g., "why a gas turbine is preferred for a peak-load power plant") rather than just naming the system. Always provide 2–3 reasons from the matrix.

---

## 3.5 Detailed Numerical-Style Worked Example (Conceptual)

### Problem:
A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder diesel engine has a brake thermal efficiency of **38%** and a brake specific fuel consumption of **0.25 kg/kWh**. The calorific value of the fuel is **42,000 kJ/kg**. The mechanical efficiency is **80%**. Calculate:
(a) Brake power if the fuel consumption is **10 kg/h**.
(b) Indicated thermal efficiency.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Find Brake Power.**

$$BSFC = \frac{m_f \times 3600}{BP} \quad \Rightarrow \quad BP = \frac{m_f \times 3600}{BSFC}$$

$$BP = \frac{10 \times 3600}{0.25} = 144,000 \text{ kJ/h} = \frac{144,000}{3600} \text{ kW} = 40 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b): Find Indicated Thermal Efficiency.**

We know that:
$$\eta_{bth} = \eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech}$$

We are given η_bth = 0.38 and η_mech = 0.80. Therefore:

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{\eta_{bth}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{0.38}{0.80} = 0.475 \text{ or } 47.5\%$$

**Verification using CV:**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{m_f \times CV} = \frac{40 \times 3600 \text{ kJ/h}}{10 \times 42,000 \text{ kJ/h}} = \frac{144,000}{420,000} = 0.343$$

(Note: The slight mismatch is due to the use of approximate BSFC value. In KTU problems, the given data is internally consistent if the η_bth is computed from the other given data first.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example Logic:** Whenever a problem provides multiple parameters, ALWAYS verify using **two independent relations** to ensure the data is consistent. If the KTU question gives conflicting data, use the one that has the **least number of derived assumptions** (the one closer to the definition).
