# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Selection Methodology

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete decision-making process** an engineer follows when selecting a thermal system. This is the exact logical flow expected in KTU 14-mark answer questions on selection criteria.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application Requirement] --> B[Identify Load Profile]
    B --> C{Continuous or Intermittent?}
    C -->|Continuous Base Load| D[Evaluate Steam Turbine / Large Diesel]
    C -->|Intermittent Peak Load| E[Evaluate Gas Turbine]
    C -->|Mobile Transport| F[Evaluate IC Engine]

    D --> G[Compare Capital Cost]
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H{Fuel Availability?}
    H -->|Coal, Biomass| I[Steam Turbine Rankine Cycle]
    H -->|Natural Gas, LNG| J[Gas Turbine Brayton Cycle]
    H -->|Diesel, Biodiesel| K[IC Engine Otto Diesel Cycle]

    I --> L[Check Efficiency and Emissions]
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M{Environmental Constraints?}
    M -->|Strict NOx, CO2| N[Add Pollution Control Equipments]
    M -->|Moderate| O[Proceed with Selection]

    N --> P[Final Selection and Sizing]
    O --> P

    P --> Q[End: Selected System with Justification]
```

### Explanation of the Flow:
1. **Block A:** Application requirement defines the problem (e.g., 10 MW power plant for a factory).
2. **Block B:** Load profile is identified (base load vs. peak load).
3. **Diamond C:** Decision point — for **continuous** duty, large steam or diesel plants are preferred; for **peak** load, gas turbines dominate due to quick start-up.
4. **Diamond H:** Fuel availability often **dictates** the cycle choice. Indian power plants use coal → Rankine cycle. Pipeline compressors → gas turbines.
5. **Diamond M:** Environmental regulations (e.g., in Kerala, KSPCB norms) can override efficiency considerations.
6. **Block P:** Final selection is documented with **economic** (payback period) and **technical** (efficiency) justifications.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Performance Characteristic Curves of a Gas Turbine

The following Mermaid block represents how the **thermal efficiency** of a gas turbine varies with **pressure ratio** for different Turbine Inlet Temperatures (TIT). The actual graph must be drawn by students in the exam; this schematic is the **logical map** of the curve's behavior.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Pressure Ratio r p less than 4] --> B[Compressor Work Small]
    B --> C[Turbine Work also Limited]
    C --> D[Low Net Work and Low Efficiency]

    E[Optimum Pressure Ratio r p around 8 to 12] --> F[Compressor and Turbine Work Balanced]
    F --> G[Maximum Thermal Efficiency Achieved]

    H[High Pressure Ratio r p greater than 15] --> I[Compressor Work Dominates]
    I --> J[Net Work Decreases]
    J --> K[Efficiency Drops]
```

### Interpretation of the Schematic:
- At **low rₚ**, the system has too little pressure energy to convert, so efficiency is poor.
- At **optimum rₚ**, the balance between compressor work input and turbine work output is ideal.
- At **very high rₚ**, the compressor consumes most of the turbine's work, reducing net work.
- Increasing TIT **shifts the entire efficiency curve upward** and the optimum rₚ to **higher values** — this is a frequently tested concept in KTU.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Compressor Performance Map (Surge and Choke)

This diagram illustrates the safe operating region of a centrifugal/axial compressor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressor Inlet] --> B[Impeller Rotor]
    B --> C{Operating Condition}
    C -->|Mass Flow Too Low| D[SURGE ZONE: Flow Reversal, Damage]
    C -->|Mass Flow Within Limits| E[SAFE OPERATING REGION]
    C -->|Mass Flow Too High| F[CHOKE STONE WALL: Max Flow Limited]
    E --> G[Constant Speed Lines: N1, N2, N3]
    G --> H[Pressure Ratio Increases as Mass Flow Decreases]
    H --> I[End: Performance Map Established]
```

### Engineering Interpretation:
- **Surge Line:** The leftmost boundary. Operating here causes violent flow oscillations.
- **Choke Line (Stone Wall):** The rightmost boundary. Sonic velocity is reached at the throat, and flow cannot increase further.
- **Operating Point Selection:** Industrial compressors are always specified with a **surge margin** (typically 5–10% away from the surge line) to ensure stable operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "sketch the compressor performance map", students must label: X-axis = Mass Flow Rate (kg/s), Y-axis = Pressure Ratio, plot **at least three constant-speed curves** (N1 < N2 < N3), mark **surge line** on the left and **choke line** on the right, and identify the **safe operating zone** in the middle.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual – Short Answer)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**
Define **Volumetric Efficiency** of an IC engine. How does it vary with engine speed?

**Model Answer:**
Volumetric efficiency (η_vol) is the ratio of the actual mass (or volume at inlet density) of air inducted per cycle to the swept volume of the cylinder.

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{\text{Actual mass of charge}}{\text{Swept volume} \times \text{Density of air}}$$

**Variation with Speed:** As engine speed increases, the pressure drop across the intake manifold and the time available for the induction stroke both decrease. This results in **lower volumetric efficiency** at higher speeds. There is an optimum speed at which η_vol is maximum; below and above this speed, η_vol decreases.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for variation explanation.)*

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual – Short Answer)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**
What is **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**? What is its significance in performance evaluation of IC engines?

**Model Answer:**
Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) is the mass of fuel consumed per unit of power output per unit time. It is expressed in **kg/kWh**.

$$SFC = \frac{m_f \times 3600}{BP \times t} = \frac{3600}{\eta_b \times CV}$$

**Significance:** SFC is a direct indicator of the **operating cost** of an engine. A lower SFC means less fuel is required to produce the same power, which directly reduces running costs. It is the **most practical parameter** used by fleet operators and engine buyers to compare engines of different makes and sizes.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14-Mark Question Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **variation of Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth) with load** for a CI engine. Sketch the characteristic curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke, 6-cylinder diesel engine develops **90 kW** of brake power at **1500 rpm**. The fuel consumption is **24 kg/h** and the calorific value of fuel is **42,000 kJ/kg**. The mean effective pressure based on indicated power is **7.5 bar**. Calculate:
   (i) Brake Thermal Efficiency
   (ii) Indicated Power
   (iii) Mechanical Efficiency
   (iv) Friction Power
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) – Variation of η_bth with Load

**Explanation:**
The brake thermal efficiency of a CI engine varies with load as follows:

1. **At no-load (0% load):** Brake power is zero, but fuel is still being consumed to overcome friction. Therefore, η_bth = 0%.

2. **At low loads (10% – 25%):** Heat losses and friction losses constitute a large fraction of the small indicated power. The efficiency rises **sharply** with increasing load.

3. **At medium to high loads (50% – 100%):** The rate of increase in brake power is much higher than the rate of increase in fuel consumption. Therefore, η_bth **increases almost linearly** with load.

4. **Near full load (90% – 100%):** Combustion becomes less efficient due to incomplete mixing of fuel and air, and the curve **flattens** or slightly drops.

**Sketch (described):**
- **X-axis:** Load (% of full load) or BMEP (bar)
- **Y-axis:** Brake Thermal Efficiency (%)
- **Shape:** Starts at origin, rises steeply at low loads, then approximately linear at medium loads, and plateaus at high loads. The maximum value is typically **35% – 40%** for naturally aspirated diesel engines.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students write "η_bth increases with load" but fail to mention **why**. Always explain that the *rate* of increase in BP is greater than the *rate* of increase in fuel consumption at part loads. Marks are lost if the sketch is not drawn.

### Part (b) – Numerical Solution

**Given Data:**
- Engine type: 4-stroke, 6-cylinder
- BP = 90 kW
- N = 1500 rpm
- m_f = 24 kg/h = 24/3600 kg/s = 0.00667 kg/s
- CV = 42,000 kJ/kg
- IMEP = 7.5 bar = 750 kPa

**Step 1: Calculate Heat Input (Q_in).**

$$Q_{in} = m_f \times CV = 0.00667 \times 42,000 = 280 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bth).**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}} = \frac{90}{280} = 0.3214 = 32.14\%$$

**Step 3: Express Indicated Power using IMEP (note: IP = MEP × LA × N × k / 60 for 4-stroke).**

Since BP and η_bth are already known, we use the friction-power relation. From part (a), IMEP = 7.5 bar.

For a 4-stroke engine, Indicated Power is given by:

$$IP = \frac{IMEP \times L \times A \times N \times k}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Friction Power (FP) directly using the definition.**

We know that:
$$\eta_{bth} = \eta_{ith} \times \eta_{mech}$$
$$IP = \frac{BP}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{90}{\eta_{mech}}$$

The problem does not provide a direct numerical value of IP. However, by definition:

$$FP = IP - BP$$

For the model answer, let us assume the friction power is to be computed from the indicated efficiency:

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{IP}{Q_{in}} \quad \text{where} \quad IP = BP + FP$$

Without additional geometric data, we provide the **methodology**:

**Methodology (to be shown in exam):**
1. Compute η_bth directly (Step 2) → **32.14%**.
2. Compute IP using the formula with IMEP, swept volume, and speed.
3. Compute FP = IP − BP.
4. Compute η_mech = BP / IP.

For numerical simplicity, if we assume IP = 100 kW (from typical exam data), then:
- FP = IP − BP = 100 − 90 = **10 kW**
- η_mech = BP / IP = 90 / 100 = 0.90 = **90%**

> [!NOTE]
> The actual numerical value of IP and FP would depend on cylinder dimensions (L and A). The KTU exam typically provides these values; the methodology above remains the same.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the **selection criteria** for choosing a prime mover (engine/turbine) for the following applications. Justify your answer with suitable performance characteristics:
   (i) A 200 MW base-load power plant in Kerala.
   (ii) A standby power unit for a hospital.
   (iii) A jet aircraft.
   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas turbine has a **compressor pressure ratio of 8**, a **turbine inlet temperature of 1200 K**, and **isentropic efficiencies of compressor and turbine as 85% and 90%** respectively. The ambient temperature is **300 K**. Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate:
   (i) Compressor work per kg of air.
   (ii) Turbine work per kg of air.
   (iii) Net work output.
   (iv) Thermal efficiency.
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) – Selection of Prime Movers

**(i) 200 MW Base-Load Power Plant:**
- **Selection: Steam Turbine (Rankine Cycle)**
- **Justification:**
  - Base-load plants operate continuously (8000+ hours/year), so **high efficiency is critical**.
  - Steam turbines have **thermal efficiencies of 35%–40%**, which is higher than simple gas turbines (~30%).
  - Coal is **abundantly available** in Kerala (and India) — coal-fired Rankine plants are economical.
  - Capital cost is high, but the **long operating life** (30+ years) justifies the investment.
  - Steam turbines can handle **very large capacities** efficiently (up to 1000 MW per unit).

**(ii) Standby Power Unit for a Hospital:**
- **Selection: Diesel Engine (CI Engine) or Diesel Generator Set**
- **Justification:**
  - Standby units require **fast start-up** (within seconds) and quick load acceptance — diesel engines excel at this.
  - Hospitals need **high reliability** — diesel engines are robust and well-proven for emergency duty.
  - Capital cost is **lower** than gas turbines for small capacities (typically 50 kW to 2 MW).
  - Diesel is **easy to store** on-site for emergency use.
  - Specific fuel consumption is reasonable, but since the unit runs only occasionally, fuel cost is not a major factor.

**(iii) Jet Aircraft:**
- **Selection: Gas Turbine (Turbojet or Turbofan)**
- **Justification:**
  - **High power-to-weight ratio** is essential — gas turbines offer the best specific power.
  - Aircraft require **high-speed operation** (thrust at high exhaust velocity).
  - Gas turbines have **excellent reliability** and can operate continuously for long flights.
  - Quick start-up and smooth throttle response are critical for aviation.
  - Piston engines are limited in altitude and speed; gas turbines dominate in jet propulsion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often give **only the name of the system** without proper **justification**. For full 7 marks, you must provide at least **3 valid engineering reasons** for each selection. Generic statements like "it is efficient" or "it is reliable" are not sufficient — quantify the advantage (e.g., "efficiency of 38% vs 28%").

### Part (b) – Gas Turbine Performance Numerical

**Given Data:**
- r_p = P₂/P₁ = 8
- T₃ = 1200 K (Turbine Inlet Temperature)
- T₁ = 300 K (Ambient)
- η_{isen,C} = 0.85
- η_{isen,T} = 0.90
- c_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K
- γ = 1.4

**Step 1: Calculate the Isentropic Temperature Ratio for the Compressor.**

$$\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 8^{0.4/1.4} = 8^{0.2857}$$

Using logarithms: $\log(8) = 0.903$, so $0.2857 \times 0.903 = 0.258$.

$$8^{0.2857} = 10^{0.258} \approx 1.812$$

$$T_{2s} = 1.812 \times 300 = 543.6 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Actual Compressor Outlet Temperature (T₂).**

$$\eta_{isen,C} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$$

$$T_2 - T_1 = \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_{isen,C}} = \frac{543.6 - 300}{0.85} = \frac{243.6}{0.85} = 286.6 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 300 + 286.6 = 586.6 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Compressor Work per kg of air (W_C).**

$$W_C = c_p \times (T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times 286.6 = 288.03 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Isentropic Temperature at Turbine Exit (T_{4s}).**

For the turbine, the expansion ratio is the same as the compression ratio (P₄/P₃ = P₁/P₂), so:

$$\frac{T_{4s}}{T_3} = \left(\frac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{0.2857} = \frac{1}{1.812} = 0.552$$

$$T_{4s} = 0.552 \times 1200 = 662.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Actual Turbine Exit Temperature (T₄).**

$$\eta_{isen,T} = \frac{T_3 - T_4}{T_3 - T_{4s}}$$

$$T_3 - T_4 = \eta_{isen,T} \times (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 0.90 \times (1200 - 662.4) = 0.90 \times 537.6 = 483.84 \text{ K}$$

$$T_4 = 1200 - 483.84 = 716.16 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Turbine Work per kg of air (W_T).**

$$W_T = c_p \times (T_3 - T_4) = 1.005 \times 483.84 = 486.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7: Calculate Net Work Output (W_net).**

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_C = 486.26 - 288.03 = 198.23 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8: Calculate Thermal Efficiency (η_th).**

$$Q_{in} = c_p \times (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1200 - 586.6) = 1.005 \times 613.4 = 616.47 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{198.23}{616.47} = 0.3216 = 32.16\%$$

### Final Answers:
- **W_C = 288.03 kJ/kg**
- **W_T = 486.26 kJ/kg**
- **W_net = 198.23 kJ/kg**
- **η_th = 32.16%**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Numerical):**
> 1. **Do not confuse** T_{2s} (isentropic) with T₂ (actual). The isentropic value is calculated first; then the actual value is derived using efficiency.
> 2. **Units check:** Work per kg should be in kJ/kg, not kW (work per kg is not power; mass flow rate is required for power).
> 3. **Sign convention:** W_C is always **work input** (positive in magnitude but subtracted), W_T is work output.
> 4. **Significant figures:** Use 4 significant figures in intermediate steps to avoid compounding errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist:

- ✅ **Performance Characteristics** are graphical/analytical representations showing how output parameters vary with operating variables.
- ✅ Key IC engine parameters: **BSFC, η_bth, η_mech, BMEP, η_vol, FP**.
- ✅ Fundamental efficiency relation: **η_bth = η_ith × η_mech**.
- ✅ BSFC formula: **BSFC = 3600 / (η_bth × CV)** — memorize this.
- ✅ Gas turbine efficiency first **increases** then **decreases** with pressure ratio.
- ✅ Higher **TIT** improves efficiency and shifts optimum rₚ to higher values.
- ✅ Compressor safe operation zone is between **surge line** and **choke (stone wall) line**.
- ✅ Selection of prime mover depends on: **power, efficiency, fuel, cost, weight, response, environment**.
- ✅ **Base load → Steam Turbine; Peak load → Gas Turbine; Mobile/Quick start → IC Engine**.
- ✅ For jet aircraft, only **gas turbines** are practically viable above certain Mach numbers.
- ✅ **Friction power (FP)** increases approximately with N²; mechanical efficiency peaks at an optimum speed.
- ✅ Always specify **surge margin** when operating centrifugal/axial compressors.
- ✅ **CI engines** have lower SFC and higher efficiency than SI engines due to higher compression ratio.
- ✅ For all performance problems, remember to use **isentropic relations** correctly: $\frac{T_{2s}}{T_1} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.
- ✅ In KTU answer scripts, **always sketch the characteristic curve** with **labeled axes** — examiners give 1–2 marks for this.
- ✅ Be thorough with the **comparison table** of IC Engine vs. Gas Turbine vs. Steam Turbine for selection-based questions.

### ⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid:

1. **Mixing up isentropic and actual states** (T_{2s} vs T₂) — always compute isentropic state first.
2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — kJ/kg for work, bar for pressure, K for temperature.
3. **Skipping sketches** in descriptive questions — even a hand-drawn curve fetches full marks.
4. **Using the wrong compression ratio relation** for Otto vs Diesel cycles.
5. **Not justifying** selection in 7-mark questions — bare statements lose marks.
6. **Confusing BSFC with SFC** — SFC is a general term; BSFC specifically uses Brake Power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:**
> For Module 3 questions, allocate **3 minutes of thinking time** before writing. Draw a **rough sketch** of the characteristic curve first. Then write the **formula**, substitute **values**, and present the **final answer with units** in a **box**. This is the standard KTU answer-writing pattern that guarantees maximum marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency and Work Input of Compressors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Efficiency and Work Input of Compressors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Efficiency and Work Input of Compressors

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **compressor** is a mechanical device that increases the pressure of a gas (typically air) by reducing its volume. The **work input** of a compressor is the actual mechanical energy that must be supplied (usually via a prime mover like an electric motor or engine) to achieve a desired pressure rise. The **efficiency** of a compressor is a dimensionless metric that compares the *ideal (minimum) work* required for a specified compression process against the *actual work* delivered to the gas.

Mathematically, efficiency is expressed as:

$$\eta = \frac{W_{\text{ideal}}}{W_{\text{actual}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** The KTU syllabus explicitly asks students to evaluate the **Isothermal Efficiency (η_iso)**, **Adiabatic (Isentropic) Efficiency (η_ad)**, **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)**, and **Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol)** of reciprocating compressors, along with the analytical derivation of work input with and without clearance volume.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **pushing a heavily loaded bicycle up a steep hill (Actual Work)**. Now imagine a perfectly smooth, frictionless ramp that allows the bicycle to glide up using the *bare minimum* effort (Ideal Work). The **ratio** of the smooth-ramp effort to your real effort is the "efficiency." The difference between them is "wasted" energy — in compressors, this wasted energy appears as **heat (due to friction, turbulence, and non-ideal compression paths)** rather than useful pressure rise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook:** Think of compressor efficiency as a *report card* for how well a real machine converts the electrical energy you pay for into useful compressed air pressure. A poor efficiency means you pay more on your electricity bill for the same amount of air.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Specific heat ratio for air:** $\gamma = 1.4$
- **Polytropic index for actual compression:** $n$ (typically $1.25 < n < 1.35$ for air)
- **Gas constant for air:** $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently set numericals using **air as the working fluid** unless otherwise specified. Always default to $\gamma = 1.4$ for air in adiabatic (isentropic) calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Compressor Efficiencies

The performance of a reciprocating compressor is judged by **four principal efficiencies**, each measuring a different loss:

### A. Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{vol}}$)
Measures the *suction effectiveness* of the compressor — i.e., how much fresh charge enters per cycle versus the swept volume.

- **Without clearance:** $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1.0$ (theoretical maximum)
- **With clearance:** $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n}$

  where $C$ is the **clearance ratio** ($C = V_c / V_s$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Clearance volume is the dead space left between the piston crown and the cylinder head to prevent mechanical impact. This trapped air re-expands on the suction stroke, *blocking* fresh air from entering. Hence, $\eta_{\text{vol}}$ always drops as pressure ratio rises.

### B. Isothermal Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{iso}}$)
Compares the *ideal isothermal work* (which is the **minimum possible** work for any compression process) to the actual work. Since the actual process is not isothermal (heat is generated but not removed), $\eta_{\text{iso}}$ is always less than 1.

- $\eta_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{actual}}}$

### C. Adiabatic (Isentropic) Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{ad}}$)
Compares the *isentropic work* (reversible adiabatic work) to the actual work. This is the most widely used metric in industry.

- $\eta_{\text{ad}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{ad}}}{W_{\text{actual}}}$

### D. Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{mech}}$)
Accounts for friction in bearings, piston rings, and other moving parts. Compares the *indicated work* (gas-side work) to the *brake work* (shaft work).

- $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{indicated}}}{W_{\text{brake}}}$

### E. Overall Efficiency
The cumulative product:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{vol}} \times \eta_{\text{ad}} \times \eta_{\text{mech}}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Swept Volume | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times N$ | $m^3/s$ (for $N$ strokes/sec) |
| 2 | Clearance Ratio | $C = \dfrac{V_c}{V_s}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Volumetric Efficiency (with clearance) | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n}$ | Valid for polytropic index $n$ |
| 4 | Isothermal Work (per kg) | $W_{\text{iso}} = R T_1 \ln \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$ | kJ/kg |
| 5 | Isothermal Work (per cycle) | $W_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \ln \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$ | kJ |
| 6 | Polytropic / Adiabatic Work (per cycle) | $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | kJ |
| 7 | Isentropic Work (per cycle, $\gamma$ for air) | $W_{\text{ad}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | kJ |
| 8 | Isothermal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{actual}}}$ | $0 < \eta_{\text{iso}} < 1$ |
| 9 | Adiabatic Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{ad}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{ad}}}{W_{\text{actual}}}$ | $0 < \eta_{\text{ad}} < 1$ |
| 10 | Multi-stage Optimum Pressure Ratio | $r_{\text{opt}} = \left( \dfrac{P_d}{P_1} \right)^{1/N}$ | $N$ = number of stages |
| 11 | Mass Flow Rate | $m = \eta_{\text{vol}} \times \rho_1 \times V_s \times N$ | kg/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Rule:** When the KTU question states "compression follows the law $PV^n = \text{constant}$" without specifying $n$, **derive the general expression and substitute $n = 1$ for isothermal** or **$n = \gamma$ for isentropic** in the final step. Examiners *strictly* deduct marks for premature substitution.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial pneumatic systems:** Selecting compressor size based on $\eta_{\text{ad}}$ to minimize energy cost.
- **Gas turbine power plants:** The *compressor section* of a gas turbine consumes ~60% of turbine output — efficiency directly governs net plant output.
- **Refrigeration and HVAC:** Compressor work input determines the coefficient of performance (COP).
- **CNG dispensing stations:** Multi-stage compression with intercooling relies on the $r_{\text{opt}}$ formula to minimize shaft work.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Work Input for Polytropic Compression (Without Clearance)

**Starting Premise:** A piston compresses a gas following the law $PV^n = \text{constant}$.

**Step 1 — Write the boundary condition:**

For a closed system, the differential work done *on* the gas is:

$$\delta W = -P \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Express P as a function of V using the polytropic law:**

From $PV^n = C_1$ (a constant), we isolate $P$:

$$P = \dfrac{C_1}{V^n} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n}$$

where $C_1 = P_1 V_1^n$ (initial state evaluation).

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the work integral:**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \delta W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n} \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Pull out the constants and integrate:**

$$W = -P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

The integral $\int V^{-n} \, dV = \dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n}$ for $n \neq 1$.

$$W = -P_1 V_1^n \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = -P_1 V_1^n \left( \dfrac{V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}}{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the signs:**

$$W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{n-1} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $V_1^{1-n}$:**

$$W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n V_1^{1-n}}{n-1} \left[ \left( \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right)^{1-n} - 1 \right] = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[ \left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right)^{n-1} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 7 — Convert volume ratio to pressure ratio:**

Since $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$, we have $\left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right)^n = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$, hence:

$$\left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \right)^{n-1} = \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n}$$

**Step 8 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Cases Verification:**
> - For $n = 1$ (isothermal): Use L'Hôpital's rule to obtain $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$. ✔
> - For $n = \gamma$ (isentropic): Substitute $n = 1.4$ for air. ✔
> - $W > 0$ for $P_2 > P_1$ (positive work input). ✔

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance Volume

**Step 1 — Define clearance volume geometry:**

At end of delivery stroke, the clearance volume $V_c$ contains gas at pressure $P_2$. During the *expansion* stroke (which begins the return cycle), this gas expands following the same polytropic law down to suction pressure $P_1$.

**Step 2 — Re-expansion volume:**

The gas re-expands to a volume $V_4$:

$$P_2 V_c^n = P_1 V_4^n \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_4 = V_c \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 3 — Effective suction volume:**

The volume of *fresh* gas drawn in during the suction stroke:

$$V_s' = V_1 - V_4 = (V_c + V_s) - V_c \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the geometric relation** $V_1 = V_c + V_s$:

$$V_s' = V_c + V_s - V_c \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n} = V_s + V_c \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Define and simplify $\eta_{\text{vol}}$:**

$$\eta_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{V_s'}{V_s} = 1 + \dfrac{V_c}{V_s} \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n} \right]$$

Substituting $C = V_c / V_s$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** As $P_2/P_1 \to \infty$, $\eta_{\text{vol}} \to 1 - C$. This is the **asymptotic limit** — beyond a certain pressure ratio, increasing $P_2$ yields *zero* additional suction, forcing the use of multi-stage compression.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Input WITH Clearance Volume (Using η_vol)

**Step 1 — Use the actual inducted volume per cycle:**

$$V_1 = \eta_{\text{vol}} \times V_s$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the general work formula:**

$$W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 \left( \eta_{\text{vol}} V_s \right) \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Work per unit time (Power):**

$$P_{\text{input}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 \eta_{\text{vol}} V_s N \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

where $N$ = number of working strokes per second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $N$ not $2N$?** A double-acting cylinder does deliver twice per revolution, so $N = 2 \times \text{RPS}$ for double-acting. KTU problems often specify this — *read carefully* to avoid the most common 1-mark deduction.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Minimum Work for Two-Stage Compression (with Optimum Intercooling)

**Step 1 — Work of stage 1:**

$$W_1 = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_x}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Work of stage 2 (intercooled to $T_1$, hence $V_3 = V_1 \cdot P_1/P_x$):**

$$W_2 = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_x V_1 \dfrac{P_1}{P_x} \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_x} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right] = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_x} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Total work:**

$$W_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_x}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} + \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_x} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 2 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $P_x$ and set to zero** (for minimum work):

$$\dfrac{dW_{\text{total}}}{dP_x} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left( \dfrac{P_x}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} \cdot \dfrac{1}{P_x} = \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_x} \right)^{(n-1)/n} \cdot \dfrac{1}{P_x}$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two pressure ratios:**

$$\dfrac{P_x}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{P_x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_x^2 = P_1 P_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$$

**Step 6 — Generalize to $N$ stages:**

$$\boxed{r_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{P_x}{P_1} = \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/N}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The optimum pressure ratio per stage is the *geometric mean* of the boundary pressures. Setting $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ for two stages ensures that the work is shared equally between stages, minimizing total shaft work by **15–30%** compared to a single stage at the same final pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Compressor Efficiency Relationship Block Diagram

The following block diagram illustrates the **hierarchy and interaction** of the four principal efficiencies used in KTU 2024 compressor analysis, from input power to delivered useful work.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Input Power kW] --> B[Mechanical Efficiency nmech]
    B --> C[Indicated Power Gas Side]
    C --> D[Adiabatic Isentropic Efficiency nad]
    D --> E[Ideal Adiabatic Work]
    E --> F[Isothermal Work Bound Lower Limit]
    D --> G[Volumetric Efficiency nvol]
    G --> H[Effective Suction Volume]
    H --> I[Actual Mass Flow Rate]
    I --> J[Final Pressure Output P2]
    F --> K[Isothermal Efficiency niso]
    E --> K
    K --> L[Overall Performance Report]
    J --> L
```

## 4.2 Multi-Stage Compression Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Fresh Air Inlet P1 T1] --> STG1[Stage 1 Cylinder Compression]
    STG1 --> IC[Intercooler Heat Removed]
    IC --> STG2[Stage 2 Cylinder Compression]
    STG2 --> OUT[Final Delivery P2 T cool]
    IC -.->|Px sqrt of P1P2| STG1
    STG2 -.->|Px sqrt of P1P2| IC
    OUT --> AP[Application Pneumatics Tools]
```

## 4.3 Compressor Work Comparison Schematic (P-V Diagram Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[P1 Suction Pressure] --> ISO[Isothermal Curve PV const]
    P1 --> AD[Adiabatic Curve PV gamma const]
    P1 --> POL[Polytropic Curve PVn const]
    ISO --> W1[Isothermal Work Area]
    AD --> W2[Adiabatic Work Area]
    POL --> W3[Polytropic Work Area]
    W1 --> COMP[Comparison Conclusion]
    W2 --> COMP
    W3 --> COMP
    COMP --> RES[Isothermal Work is Minimum bound]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The P-V indicator diagram in a reciprocating compressor plot shows that the *isothermal curve* lies *below* the adiabatic curve for the same pressure limits — visually confirming that isothermal compression requires the **least work**. This is the theoretical foundation for water-jacketed cylinders and intercoolers in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define isothermal efficiency and volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor. State the formula for volumetric efficiency of a compressor with clearance volume.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Isothermal Efficiency:** It is defined as the ratio of isothermal work input (which is the minimum theoretical work required for compression) to the actual work input delivered to the compressor.

$$\eta_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{isothermal}}}{W_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Volumetric Efficiency:** It is defined as the ratio of the actual volume of gas inducted per cycle to the swept volume of the piston.

$$\eta_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_s}$$

**Formula for $\eta_{\text{vol}}$ with clearance:**

$$\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n}$$

where $C = V_c / V_s$ is the clearance ratio and $n$ is the polytropic index.

> **Marking Key:** 1 mark each for the two definitions, 1 mark for the volumetric efficiency formula with clearance. ✔

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is meant by clearance volume in a reciprocating compressor? Why is it provided, and what is its effect on volumetric efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Clearance Volume:** The clearance volume $V_c$ is the small volume left between the piston crown (at the end of the delivery stroke) and the cylinder head. It is expressed as a ratio $C = V_c / V_s$.

**Why Provided:** Clearance is provided to:
1. **Accommodate the valve assembly** — mechanical space is needed for suction and delivery valve mechanisms.
2. **Prevent piston-to-head impact** — avoids catastrophic mechanical damage due to thermal expansion or manufacturing tolerances.
3. **Allow for thermal expansion** of piston and rods at high operating temperatures.

**Effect on Volumetric Efficiency:** As clearance volume increases, the re-expansion of trapped air on the return stroke *reduces* the available volume for fresh induction, thereby *decreasing* $\eta_{\text{vol}}$. The relationship is:

$$\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n}$$

> **Marking Key:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for reasons, 1 mark for effect with formula. ✔

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: A single-stage, single-acting reciprocating compressor compresses air from 1 bar to 8 bar. The clearance ratio is 5% and the compression follows the law $PV^{1.3} = C$. The bore and stroke are 200 mm and 300 mm respectively, and the compressor runs at 300 RPM. The ambient temperature is 27°C.**

**(a) Calculate the volumetric efficiency and the mass of air inducted per minute.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Calculate the actual work input per cycle, the indicated power, and the isothermal efficiency of the compressor. Take the actual indicated work to be 10% higher than the ideal polytropic work.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**Given Data:**
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$
- $P_2 = 8 \text{ bar} = 800 \text{ kPa}$
- $C = V_c / V_s = 0.05$
- $n = 1.3$
- Bore $D = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $N = 300 \text{ RPM} = 5 \text{ RPS}$
- $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}$
- $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Swept Volume per cycle:**

$$V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.2)^2 (0.3) = 9.4248 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n} = 1 + 0.05 - 0.05 \times (8)^{1/1.3}$$

Computing the pressure-ratio term:

$$(8)^{1/1.3} = (8)^{0.7692} = e^{0.7692 \ln 8} = e^{0.7692 \times 2.0794} = e^{1.5995} = 4.951$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1.05 - 0.05 \times 4.951 = 1.05 - 0.2476 = 0.8024 = 80.24\%$$

**Step 3: Effective Suction Volume per cycle:**

$$V_1 = \eta_{\text{vol}} \times V_s = 0.8024 \times 9.4248 \times 10^{-3} = 7.563 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Density at inlet:**

$$\rho_1 = \dfrac{P_1}{R T_1} = \dfrac{100}{0.287 \times 300} = \dfrac{100}{86.1} = 1.1614 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Mass per cycle and per minute:**

$$m_{\text{cycle}} = \rho_1 \times V_1 = 1.1614 \times 7.563 \times 10^{-3} = 8.785 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

$$\dot{m} = m_{\text{cycle}} \times N \times 60 = 8.785 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 60 = 2.6355 \text{ kg/min}$$

> **Part (a) Answer:** $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 80.24\%$, $\dot{m} = 2.636 \text{ kg/min}$ ✔

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Polytropic Work per cycle (ideal):**

$$W_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

Compute $(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n} = (8)^{0.3/1.3} = (8)^{0.2308}$

$$(8)^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.4799} = 1.6158$$

$$W_{\text{poly}} = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} \times 100 \times 7.563 \times 10^{-3} \times (1.6158 - 1)$$

$$W_{\text{poly}} = 4.333 \times 100 \times 7.563 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6158$$

$$W_{\text{poly}} = 4.333 \times 0.7563 \times 0.6158 = 2.018 \text{ kJ/cycle}$$

**Step 2: Actual Work per cycle** (10% higher):

$$W_{\text{actual}} = 1.10 \times 2.018 = 2.2198 \text{ kJ/cycle}$$

**Step 3: Indicated Power:**

For a single-acting compressor, 1 working stroke per revolution, so 5 strokes/sec:

$$P_{\text{ind}} = W_{\text{actual}} \times N = 2.2198 \times 5 = 11.099 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Isothermal Work per cycle (for isothermal efficiency):**

$$W_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \ln \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = 100 \times 7.563 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln(8)$$

$$W_{\text{iso}} = 0.7563 \times 2.0794 = 1.5727 \text{ kJ/cycle}$$

**Step 5: Isothermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{iso}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{iso}}}{W_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{1.5727}{2.2198} = 0.7085 = 70.85\%$$

> **Part (b) Answer:** $W_{\text{actual}} = 2.22 \text{ kJ/cycle}$, $P_{\text{ind}} = 11.1 \text{ kW}$, $\eta_{\text{iso}} = 70.85\%$ ✔

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q: A two-stage reciprocating compressor compresses air from 1.013 bar and 20°C to a final pressure of 60 bar. The compression follows $PV^{1.25} = C$ in both stages, with complete intercooling back to 20°C. The free air delivery is 0.25 m³/min measured at inlet conditions.**

**(a) Determine the optimum intermediate pressure and the minimum work of compression per kg of air.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Calculate the work of compression if the intermediate pressure deviates by ±10% from the optimum, and comment on the sensitivity of work to this deviation.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Given Data:**
- $P_1 = 1.013 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 293 \text{ K}$
- $P_2 = 60 \text{ bar}$
- $n = 1.25$
- FAD $= 0.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$
- Intercooling is complete → $T_x = T_1$

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Optimum Intermediate Pressure:**

For minimum work in a two-stage compressor with intercooling back to inlet temperature:

$$P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \times P_2} = \sqrt{1.013 \times 60} = \sqrt{60.78} = 7.795 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2: Specific gas constant and density:**

$$R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$
$$\rho_1 = \dfrac{P_1}{R T_1} = \dfrac{101.3}{0.287 \times 293} = \dfrac{101.3}{84.091} = 1.2047 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_1 \times \text{FAD} = 1.2047 \times \dfrac{0.25}{60} = 5.0196 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4: Work per kg (total for both stages):**

Since both stages have equal pressure ratio $r = P_x/P_1 = P_2/P_x$:

$$r = \sqrt{P_2/P_1} = \sqrt{60/1.013} = \sqrt{59.23} = 7.696$$

$$W_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

Compute $r^{(n-1)/n} = (7.696)^{0.25/1.25} = (7.696)^{0.2}$

$$(7.696)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times \ln 7.696} = e^{0.2 \times 2.0405} = e^{0.4081} = 1.5041$$

$$W_{\text{stage}} = \dfrac{1.25}{0.25} \times 0.287 \times 293 \times (1.5041 - 1)$$

$$W_{\text{stage}} = 5 \times 84.091 \times 0.5041 = 211.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5: Total work per kg:**

$$W_{\text{total}} = 2 \times W_{\text{stage}} = 2 \times 211.93 = 423.86 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> **Part (a) Answer:** $P_x = 7.795 \text{ bar}$, $W_{\text{min}} = 423.86 \text{ kJ/kg}$ ✔

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Define a 10% deviation range.** For +10% deviation:

$$P_x' = 1.10 \times 7.795 = 8.5745 \text{ bar}$$

For -10% deviation:

$$P_x'' = 0.90 \times 7.795 = 7.0155 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 2: Work for +10% case** ($P_x' = 8.5745$):

Pressure ratio stage 1: $r_1 = 8.5745/1.013 = 8.464$
Pressure ratio stage 2: $r_2 = 60/8.5745 = 6.997$

Compute $r_1^{0.2} = (8.464)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times 2.1358} = e^{0.4272} = 1.5330$
Compute $r_2^{0.2} = (6.997)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times 1.9453} = e^{0.3891} = 1.4756$

$$W_1 = 5 \times 84.091 \times (1.5330 - 1) = 5 \times 84.091 \times 0.5330 = 224.10 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_2 = 5 \times 84.091 \times (1.4756 - 1) = 5 \times 84.091 \times 0.4756 = 199.94 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_{\text{total}}' = 224.10 + 199.94 = 424.04 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3: Work for -10% case** ($P_x'' = 7.0155$):

Pressure ratio stage 1: $r_1 = 7.0155/1.013 = 6.925$
Pressure ratio stage 2: $r_2 = 60/7.0155 = 8.552$

Compute $r_1^{0.2} = (6.925)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times 1.9351} = e^{0.3870} = 1.4724$
Compute $r_2^{0.2} = (8.552)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times 2.1463} = e^{0.4293} = 1.5360$

$$W_1 = 5 \times 84.091 \times (1.4724 - 1) = 5 \times 84.091 \times 0.4724 = 198.62 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_2 = 5 \times 84.091 \times (1.5360 - 1) = 5 \times 84.091 \times 0.5360 = 225.36 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_{\text{total}}'' = 198.62 + 225.36 = 423.98 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Sensitivity Comparison:**

| Parameter | Optimum | +10% Deviation | -10% Deviation |
|-----------|---------|----------------|----------------|
| $P_x$ (bar) | 7.795 | 8.5745 | 7.0155 |
| $W_{\text{total}}$ (kJ/kg) | 423.86 | 424.04 | 423.98 |
| Change from optimum | 0% | +0.042% | +0.028% |

> **Part (b) Answer:** Both deviations produce only a marginal increase in work (~0.04%), demonstrating that the **optimum intermediate pressure is a "shallow minimum"** — small deviations do not penalize work input significantly. This is a key practical advantage of multi-stage design: it is *robust* to imperfect intercooler pressure settings. ✔

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
>
> 1. **Substituting $n = 1.4$ for "polytropic" problems:** The KTU question MUST state $n$ explicitly. If given $PV^{1.3} = C$, use $n = 1.3$ in **all** formulas. Premature use of $\gamma$ loses 2-3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $\eta_{\text{vol}}$:** When the problem says "with clearance volume," the work formula MUST use $V_1 = \eta_{\text{vol}} \times V_s$, NOT $V_s$ alone. This is the most common 2-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Using $P_1$ in bar without conversion:** $P_1 V_1$ in SI units requires $P$ in **kPa (or Pa)** and $V$ in **m³** to yield work in **kJ (or J)**. Mixing bar with m³ produces dimensional errors — examiners deduct 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Double-acting confusion:** If a problem says "double-acting," there are **2 working strokes per revolution** — so $N_{\text{working}} = 2 \times \text{RPS}$, not just RPS. Read the problem statement twice.
>
> 5. **Intercooling assumption:** KTU questions often say "intercooled to initial temperature" — this is the **ideal** intercooling assumption (necessary for $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ to hold). If not stated, you cannot apply the formula blindly.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four Efficiencies:** Volumetric ($\eta_{\text{vol}}$), Isothermal ($\eta_{\text{iso}}$), Adiabatic/Isentropic ($\eta_{\text{ad}}$), Mechanical ($\eta_{\text{mech}}$). Each measures a distinct loss mechanism.
- **Master Formula (Polytropic Work):** $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$. Know how to derive it — KTU loves derivation questions worth 4-5 marks.
- **Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance:** $\eta_{\text{vol}} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{1/n}$. **Decreases** as $P_2/P_1$ or $C$ increases.
- **Isothermal Work:** $W_{\text{iso}} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ — derived from the limit $n \to 1$ of the polytropic formula. **Always the minimum possible work** for a given pressure rise.
- **Optimum Intermediate Pressure:** $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ for two stages; $\left( P_2/P_1 \right)^{1/N}$ for $N$ stages. Setting this correctly **minimizes total work**.
- **Work Decreases with Multi-Staging:** Two-stage compression with intercooling reduces work by ~15% over single-stage for the same pressure ratio.
- **Units Consistency:** $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³, $W$ in kJ. Use $\ln$ (natural log) for isothermal work formulas.
- **$\eta_{\text{vol}}$ Asymptote:** As $P_2/P_1 \to \infty$, $\eta_{\text{vol}} \to 1 - C$. This is why **very high pressure ratios always need multi-stage compression**.
- **Relationship $\eta_{\text{iso}} < \eta_{\text{ad}} < 1$:** Since isothermal work < isentropic work < actual work, dividing the ideal by actual always gives isothermal efficiency < isentropic efficiency < 1.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Revision Strategy:** Memorize the **master polytropic work formula** and the **four efficiency formulas** in one sitting. Then re-derive them on a blank sheet 3 times. KTU 2024 numericals test these four formulas in 80% of compressor questions — pattern recognition is your best friend.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Heat Exchangers: Shell and Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types of Heat Exchangers: Shell and Tube # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heat Exchanger (HX):** A device that transfers thermal energy between two or more fluids (or between a fluid and a solid surface) that are at different temperatures, without allowing them to mix physically. A **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** is the most common and versatile type of industrial heat exchanger. It consists of a **cylindrical pressure vessel (shell)** containing a bundle of **tubes** through which one fluid flows, while the second fluid flows over the outside of the tubes (through the shell) absorbing or releasing heat. Tube sheets at either end separate the shell-side and tube-side fluids. The designation of STHEs follows the **Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association (TEMA)** standards, which classify the three major regions: - **Stationary Head (Front)** - **Shell** - **Rear Head** > [!NOTE] > **Why STHE dominates industry:** It handles high pressures, high temperatures, large temperature differentials, and corrosive/phase-changing fluids (boiling, condensation) far better than compact or plate exchangers. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Think of a **car radiator**. The engine coolant (hot) circulates inside thin metal tubes, while outside air (cool) is forced past the tubes by a fan. Heat naturally flows from the hot fluid to the cool air through the tube walls — the fluids never touch. That is a shell-and-tube exchanger in miniature: - **Tubes** = drinking straws carrying hot tea - **Shell** = the outer cup of cold water surrounding them - **Baffles** = spoons you place inside the cup to make the cold water swirl around every straw (so it doesn't just slip past) The goal is to extract **maximum heat** from the hot fluid before it leaves, using minimum material and pump power. > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick-Fire Definition (3-mark ready):** *A shell-and-tube heat exchanger is a compact, indirect-contact type of heat exchanger in which one fluid flows through a bundle of tubes enclosed in a shell, while the second fluid flows through the shell over the tube surfaces, facilitating heat transfer across the tube walls.* ## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters & Standards | Symbol | Parameter | Typical Magnitude / Standard | |--------|-----------|------------------------------| | $D_s$ | Shell inside diameter | **100 mm – 3000 mm** | | $D_o$ | Tube outside diameter | **6.35 mm – 50.8 mm** (BWG 24–BWG 16) | | $L$ | Tube length | **1 m – 6 m** (commonly 2.44 m / 8 ft) | | $t$ | Tube wall thickness | **0.5 mm – 3.5 mm** (Birmingham Wire Gauge) | | $p_t$ | Tube pitch | **1.25 $D_o$** to **1.5 $D_o$** | | $p_b$ | Baffle pitch / spacing | **0.2 $D_s$** to **1.0 $D_s$** | | $N_t$ | Total number of tubes | Determined by $Q = UA \Delta T_m$ | | $N_p$ | Number of tube passes | 1, 2, 4, 6, 8 (must be even) | > [!CAUTION] > **Tube pitch** is the center-to-center distance between adjacent tubes. Two standard layouts exist — **Triangular (30°, 60°, 90°)** and **Square (90°)**. Triangular gives more tubes in the same shell diameter; square eases mechanical cleaning. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Heat Exchangers (Module-4 Foundation) Heat exchangers are categorized by three criteria — **flow arrangement**, **construction**, and **compactness**. ### (A) By Flow Arrangement 1. **Parallel Flow (Co-current):** Both fluids enter at the same end, flow in the same direction. - $T_{h,out} > T_{c,out}$ always; cannot cross the fluid outlet temperature. 2. **Counter Flow (Counter-current):** Fluids flow in opposite directions. - Highest $\Delta T_{LMTD}$, can approach the *cold inlet* temperature with the hot outlet. 3. **Cross Flow:** Fluids flow perpendicular to each other (typical of STHE with multiple passes). 4. **Multi-pass / Hybrid:** Combinations of the above (e.g., 2-4 STHE = two shell passes, four tube passes). ### (B) By Construction 1. **Recuperators:** Both fluids on opposite sides of a solid wall (e.g., STHE, plate HX). 2. **Regenerators:** Same fluid alternately heated and cooled by a solid matrix. 3. **Direct Contact:** Fluids mix during heat transfer (e.g., cooling towers). ### (C) By Compactness $$\beta = \frac{A}{V_{HX}}$$ - $\beta \ge 700 \text{ m}^2/\text{m}^3$ → **Compact HX** - $\beta < 700 \text{ m}^2/\text{m}^3$ → **Conventional HX** (STHE falls here, $\beta \approx 50$–$150$) ## 2.2 Major Components of an STHE 1. **Tubes:** Carry tube-side fluid. Common materials — copper, admiralty brass, carbon steel, stainless steel (SS 304, SS 316), titanium, Inconel. 2. **Tube Sheets (Tubesheets):** Thick plates into which tubes are rolled, welded, or expanded. Connect tubes to channels. 3. **Shell:** Cylindrical pressure vessel; contains shell-side fluid. 4. **Baffles:** Plates that direct the shell-side flow across the tubes, increasing velocity and turbulence. 5. **Channel (Front/Head):** Houses the tube-side inlet/outlet. 6. **Nozzles:** Inlet/outlet connections on the shell and channel. 7. **Tie Rods & Spacers:** Hold baffles at correct spacing. 8. **Expansion Joints:** Compensate for differential thermal expansion between shell and tubes. ## 2.3 TEMA Designation System TEMA codifies STHEs using **three letters** — one for each region: | Region | Letter | Type | Description | |--------|--------|------|-------------| | **Front Head** | A | Channel & Removable Cover | Most common; allows tube access | | | B | Bonnet (Integral Cover) | Cheaper, no removable cover | | | C | Channel Integral with Tube Sheet | High-pressure, no gaskets | | | D | Special High-Pressure Closures | — | | | N | Bonnet with Pipe Integral | — | | **Shell** | E | One-Pass Shell | Simplest, most common | | | F | Two-Pass Shell with Longitudinal Baffle | — | | | G | Split-Flow | — | | | H | Double Split-Flow | — | | | J | Divided Flow | — | | | K | Kettle Vaporizer | Boiling/condensing | | | X | Cross-Flow | — | | **Rear Head** | L, M, N, P, S, T, U, W | Various fixed/floating heads | Determines thermal expansion handling | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Example:** **AEL** → A (removable channel front head) + E (one-pass shell) + L (fixed tubesheet rear head). This is a *fixed tubesheet* exchanger — cheapest, but cannot handle large $\Delta T$ between fluids. **Floating-head types (S, T, U, W)** allow the tube bundle to expand freely, handling large temperature differences and thermal stresses. ## 2.4 Baffles — The Heart of Shell-Side Performance Baffles serve three purposes: 1. **Support the tubes** (prevent sagging and vibration) 2. **Direct flow** across the tube bundle (forcing cross-flow) 3. **Increase shell-side velocity and turbulence** → higher $h_o$ (outside heat transfer coefficient) ### Common Baffle Types - **Segmental Baffles (most common):** A segment of the shell is cut out. Creates cross-flow + leakage. - **Disc-and-Donut Baffles:** Alternate disc (smaller) and donut (larger) baffles — better heat transfer, less dead zones. - **Orifice Baffles:** Segmental with holes — act as nozzles, creating high-velocity jets. - **Longitudinal Baffles:** Create multi-pass shell (F-shell). > [!NOTE] > **Baffle Cut** is the height of the cut segment, expressed as a percentage of the shell ID. Optimal baffle cut: **20%–25%** of $D_s$. Too small → high pressure drop. Too large → low velocity, poor heat transfer. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Description / Use | Units | |---|----------|------------------|-------| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})$ | Energy balance (no losses) | W | | 2 | $Q = UA\, \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | LMTD-based rate equation | W | | 3 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Counter-flow LMTD | K | | 4 | $Q = UA\, F\, \Delta T_{LMTD,\,CF}$ | Multi-pass correction with $F$-factor | W | | 5 | $F = \dfrac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\,\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-P}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-P(R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1})}{2-P(R+1+\sqrt{R^2+1})}\right]}$ | TEMA LMTD correction factor | dimensionless | | 6 | $R = \dfrac{T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}}$ | Heat-capacity ratio | dimensionless | | 7 | $P = \dfrac{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}}$ | Temperature effectiveness | dimensionless | | 8 | $\dfrac{1}{UA} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_o h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L N_t} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | Total resistance (fouling ignored) | K/W | | 9 | $\dfrac{1}{U A} = \dfrac{1}{U_i A_i} = \dfrac{1}{U_o A_o} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_i h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L N_t} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o} + R_{f,i}/\!A_i + R_{f,o}/\!A_o$ | Overall resistance with **fouling** | K/W | | 10 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}}$ (when $C_{min}$ = hot) | Effectiveness | dimensionless | | 11 | $C_{min} = (\dot{m} c_p)_{min}$ | Minimum heat-capacity rate | W/K | | 12 | $C_R = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ | Heat-capacity ratio | dimensionless | | 13 | $NTU = \dfrac{UA}{C_{min}}$ | Number of transfer units | dimensionless | | 14 | $\varepsilon_{CF} = \dfrac{1-\exp[-NTU(1-C_R)]}{1-C_R \exp[-NTU(1-C_R)]}$ | Counter-flow effectiveness | dimensionless | | 15 | $\Delta P = 4f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}\, N_p$ | Tube-side pressure drop (multi-pass) | Pa | | 16 | $\Delta P_{shell} = 4f_s \dfrac{D_s}{D_e}\dfrac{\rho V_s^2}{2}\,(N_B+1)$ | Shell-side pressure drop | Pa | | 17 | $\eta_o = 1 - \dfrac{(n D_o)}{p_t} \left[1 - \dfrac{1.82}{\pi}\!\left(\dfrac{p_t}{D_o} - 1\right)\!\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi D_o}{2p_t}\right)\!\right]$ | Tube-bundle bypass factor | dimensionless | > [!TIP] > **Fouling factors ($R_f$)**: typical values — water 0.0001–0.0002, oil 0.0002–0.001, gases 0.0001 m²K/W. **Always include** in a KTU exam problem unless explicitly told otherwise. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | STHE Configuration | |-------------|--------------------| | Power plant condensers | AKT (titanium tubes, seawater) | | Oil refineries (crude preheat) | AES, BEM with U-tubes | | Chemical reactors (feed-effluent) | Floating head (BES) | | LNG vaporizers | Kettle reboilers (AKK) | | HVAC chillers | Brazed plate (not STHE), but water-cooled condensers use BEM | | Marine propulsion | Fixed tubesheet (AEL, removable bundle) | The selection of a STHE type influences **cleanability**, **thermal expansion tolerance**, **pressure rating**, and **cost** — the TEMA system is therefore an industry lingua franca. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) **Start with a differential element** of a single-pass counter-flow heat exchanger. Let $T_h(x)$ and $T_c(x)$ be local bulk temperatures. > **Step 1 — Energy balance on the differential element $dx$** Heat transferred from hot fluid to cold fluid: $$ dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}\, dT_h = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}\, dT_c $$ > **Step 2 — Rate equation across the element** $$ dQ = U\, (\pi D_o N_t)\, dx\, (T_h - T_c) $$ > **Step 3 — Form the temperature difference $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$** Differentiate $\Delta T$ along $x$: $$ d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c $$ From Step 1: $$ dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}, \qquad dT_c = \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} $$ Therefore: $$ d(\Delta T) = -dQ \!\left[\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right] $$ Let $C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}$ and $C_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}$. Then: $$ d(\Delta T) = -dQ \!\left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right] $$ > **Step 4 — Substitute $dQ$ from Step 2** $$ d(\Delta T) = -U(\pi D_o N_t)(T_h - T_c)\, dx \!\left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right] $$ But $T_h - T_c = \Delta T$, so: $$ \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U(\pi D_o N_t) \!\left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right] dx $$ > **Step 5 — Integrate from inlet ($x=0$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to outlet ($x=L$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$)** $$ \int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U(\pi D_o N_t) \!\left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right] \int_0^L dx $$ $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U(\pi D_o N_t) L \!\left[\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right] $$ > **Step 6 — Solve for $Q$** The total heat transfer is $Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$. From the energy balance: $$ \Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}, \qquad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} $$ Manipulating the integrated expression algebraically: $$ Q = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \cdot U (\pi D_o N_t L) $$ > **Final Result — LMTD Definition** $$ \boxed{\;\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Validity:** This derivation assumes **constant $U$**, **constant $c_p$**, **no phase change**, **no axial conduction**, and **negligible kinetic/potential energy changes**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the F-Factor for Multi-Pass Shell-and-Tube Exchangers For a 1-2 STHE (one shell pass, two tube passes), the true mean temperature difference is **less** than the counter-flow LMTD because the flow is not purely counter-current. The **F-factor** is defined as: $$ F = \frac{\Delta T_{m,\,actual}}{\Delta T_{LMTD,\,CF}} $$ **Start with the temperature-effectiveness and heat-capacity ratios:** $$ P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}, \qquad R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} $$ > **Step 1 — Express actual $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ for the 1-2 STHE** In a 1-2 exchanger, the hot shell-side fluid makes one pass; the tube-side fluid makes two passes. Tracking the temperature nodes: - **At tube-pass-1 inlet (shell inlet):** $\Delta T_a = T_{h,in} - T_{c,mid}$ (where $T_{c,mid}$ is the temperature at the bend) - **At tube-pass-2 outlet (shell outlet):** $\Delta T_b = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ These cannot be expressed in closed form without iteration; the F-factor is the result of the **exact analytical integration by Bowman, Mueller, and Nagle (1940)**. > **Step 2 — Apply the Bowman-M Mueller-Nagle equation** $$ \boxed{\;F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\,\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-P}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-P\!\left(R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1}\right)}{2-P\!\left(R+1+\sqrt{R^2+1}\right)}\right]}\;} $$ > **Step 3 — Validity envelope (TEMA)** The 1-2 F-factor is valid when: $$ F \ge 0.75 \quad \text{(acceptable)}, \qquad F \ge 0.90 \quad \text{(no penalty)} $$ If $F < 0.75$, the design must be modified (more shells, or a different pass arrangement). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Trap:** For $R = 1$, the formula has a $0/0$ form. Apply **L'Hôpital's rule** to obtain the limit: $$ \lim_{R \to 1} F = \frac{P}{(1-P)\ln[(2-P)/2]} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Counter-Flow LMTD) **Problem:** Hot oil enters at $T_{h,in}=150°C$ and leaves at $T_{h,out}=80°C$. Cold water enters at $T_{c,in}=30°C$ and leaves at $T_{c,out}=70°C$ in a counter-flow exchanger. Compute $\Delta T_{LMTD}$. > **Step 1 — Identify end temperature differences** $$ \Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 70 = 80\,°C $$ $$ \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 30 = 50\,°C $$ > **Step 2 — Apply LMTD formula** $$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{80 - 50}{\ln(80/50)} = \frac{30}{\ln 1.6} $$ $$ \ln 1.6 = 0.4700 $$ $$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{30}{0.4700} = 63.83\,°C $$ > **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{\;\Delta T_{LMTD} \approx 63.83\,°C\;} $$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (F-Factor Application, 1-2 STHE) **Problem:** In the same 1-2 STHE, compute $P$, $R$, and $F$. $$ P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{70 - 30}{150 - 30} = \frac{40}{120} = 0.3333 $$ $$ R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{150 - 80}{70 - 30} = \frac{70}{40} = 1.75 $$ > **Step 1 — Compute $R^2 + 1$** $$ R^2 + 1 = 1.75^2 + 1 = 3.0625 + 1 = 4.0625 $$ $$ \sqrt{R^2+1} = \sqrt{4.0625} = 2.0156 $$ > **Step 2 — Compute the numerator logarithm** $$ 1 - P = 1 - 0.3333 = 0.6667 $$ $$ 1 - PR = 1 - (0.3333 \times 1.75) = 1 - 0.5833 = 0.4167 $$ $$ \frac{1-P}{1-PR} = \frac{0.6667}{0.4167} = 1.6000 $$ $$ \ln(1.6000) = 0.4700 $$ Numerator: $$ \sqrt{R^2+1} \times \ln\!\left[\frac{1-P}{1-PR}\right] = 2.0156 \times 0.4700 = 0.9473 $$ > **Step 3 — Compute the denominator logarithm** $$ R + 1 = 2.75, \quad R + 1 - \sqrt{R^2+1} = 2.75 - 2.0156 = 0.7344 $$ $$ R + 1 + \sqrt{R^2+1} = 2.75 + 2.0156 = 4.7656 $$ $$ P(R+1 - \sqrt{R^2+1}) = 0.3333 \times 0.7344 = 0.2448 $$ $$ 2 - 0.2448 = 1.7552 $$ $$ P(R+1 + \sqrt{R^2+1}) = 0.3333 \times 4.7656 = 1.5885 $$ $$ 2 - 1.5885 = 0.4115 $$ $$ \frac{1.7552}{0.4115} = 4.2653 $$ $$ \ln(4.2653) = 1.4501 $$ Denominator: $$ (R-1) \times \ln(4.2653) = 0.75 \times 1.4501 = 1.0876 $$ > **Step 4 — Compute $F$** $$ F = \frac{0.9473}{1.0876} = 0.8709 $$ > **Step 5 — Apply corrected mean temperature difference** $$ \Delta T_m = F \times \Delta T_{LMTD,\,CF} = 0.8709 \times 63.83 = 55.59\,°C $$ > **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{\;F \approx 0.871, \quad \Delta T_m \approx 55.6\,°C\;} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Practical Note:** Since $F \ge 0.75$, this design is **acceptable** under TEMA. If $F < 0.75$, increase the number of shells in series. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow architecture** of a 1-2 STHE, showing how hot and cold streams interact with the major components. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["HOT FLUID INLET
