The largest resistance dominates — typically the **convective** term on the gas side or the **fouling** term after long service. The wall conduction term is usually negligible for metallic tubes. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *List and explain briefly the TEMA designations for the front head, shell, and rear head of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. State the significance of a "floating head" type.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Front Head (A, B, C, D, N):** Defines how the channel and tube sheet at the inlet are sealed. 'A' (removable channel) allows tube-side access for cleaning; 'B' (bonnet) is welded, cheaper; 'C' (integral channel) for high-pressure service. - **Shell (E, F, G, H, J, K, X):** Describes the shell-side flow path. 'E' = one-pass (most common); 'F' = two-pass with longitudinal baffle; 'K' = kettle reboiler; 'X' = cross-flow. - **Rear Head (L, M, N, P, S, T, U, W):** Defines how the tube bundle terminates. 'L' = fixed tubesheet (cheap, limited to low $\Delta T$); 'U' = U-tube (handles thermal expansion); 'S', 'T', 'W' = floating-head designs (free expansion, fully cleanable). - **Significance of floating head:** It allows the tube bundle to expand independently of the shell, accommodating large temperature differences between tube-side and shell-side fluids. It also permits removal of the entire bundle for cleaning — preferred in refinery and chemical service. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Why are baffles used in a shell-and-tube heat exchanger? What is an "optimum baffle cut" and what is its typical range?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Purpose of baffles:** Baffles (1) support the tubes mechanically and prevent vibration, (2) direct the shell-side fluid in a **cross-flow** pattern across the tubes, and (3) increase the shell-side velocity and turbulence, thereby raising the outside heat transfer coefficient $h_o$. - **Baffle cut definition:** The fraction of the shell diameter removed in a segmental baffle, expressed as a percentage of $D_s$. - **Optimum baffle cut:** Typically **20% to 25%** of the shell inside diameter. Cuts below 15% cause excessive pressure drop; cuts above 35% allow too much flow bypassing the tubes (streamlining), reducing $h_o$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Always include fouling factors** unless the problem explicitly says "clean surfaces." Skipping $R_f$ loses 1–2 marks. > - **Apply the F-factor correctly** for 1-2, 2-4 STHE configurations. Showing the F-formula with all three steps (numerator $\ln$, denominator $\ln$, final division) is mandatory. > - **State assumptions** at the start: constant $c_p$, constant $U$, no heat loss, negligible kinetic energy, no phase change. > - **Unit conversion traps:** $\ln$ arguments must be dimensionless; $k$ must be in W/m·K (not W/m·°C, but they are numerically equal); $A$ in m²; flow rates in kg/s. > - **End with a boxed final answer** for the rate equation result. --- ### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 4]** *In a 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger, hot oil ($c_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) flows at 5 kg/s and is cooled from 150°C to 90°C. Cold water ($c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) enters at 30°C and is heated to 70°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on outer area is $U_o = 850$ W/m²·K. The outer surface area of the tubes is 12 m². Take suitable fluid arrangement and verify with the LMTD approach.* #### (a) Calculate the heat transfer rate, log-mean temperature difference, and the F-factor for the 1-2 STHE. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** > **Step 1 — Heat duty from energy balance (hot side)** $$ Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 5 \times 2.1 \times (150 - 90) = 5 \times 2.1 \times 60 $$ $$ Q = 630 \text{ kW} = 630{,}000 \text{ W} $$ > **Step 2 — Counter-flow LMTD** $$ \Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 70 = 80\,°C $$ $$ \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 90 - 30 = 60\,°C $$ $$ \Delta T_{LMTD,\,CF} = \frac{80 - 60}{\ln(80/60)} = \frac{20}{\ln 1.3333} = \frac{20}{0.2877} = 69.52\,°C $$ > **Step 3 — F-factor parameters** $$ P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{70-30}{150-30} = \frac{40}{120} = 0.3333 $$ $$ R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{150-90}{70-30} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5 $$ > **Step 4 — Numerator of F** $$ \sqrt{R^2+1} = \sqrt{2.25+1} = \sqrt{3.25} = 1.8028 $$ $$ 1 - P = 0.6667, \quad 1 - PR = 1 - 0.3333 \times 1.5 = 0.5 $$ $$ \ln(0.6667/0.5) = \ln 1.3333 = 0.2877 $$ Numerator = $1.8028 \times 0.2877 = 0.5186$ > **Step 5 — Denominator of F** $$ R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1} = 2.5 - 1.8028 = 0.6972 $$ $$ R+1+\sqrt{R^2+1} = 2.5 + 1.8028 = 4.3028 $$ $$ P \times 0.6972 = 0.2324, \quad P \times 4.3028 = 1.4343 $$ $$ 2 - 0.2324 = 1.7676, \quad 2 - 1.4343 = 0.5657 $$ $$ \frac{1.7676}{0.5657} = 3.1250, \quad \ln(3.1250) = 1.1394 $$ Denominator = $(R-1) \times 1.1394 = 0.5 \times 1.1394 = 0.5697$ > **Step 6 — F-factor** $$ F = \frac{0.5186}{0.5697} = 0.910 $$ > **Final Answer for (a):** $$ \boxed{\;Q = 630 \text{ kW}, \quad \Delta T_{LMTD,CF} = 69.52\,°C, \quad F = 0.910\;} $$ --- #### (b) Calculate the actual mean temperature difference and verify if the heat exchanger can meet the design heat duty. Comment on the F-factor. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** > **Step 1 — Corrected mean temperature difference** $$ \Delta T_m = F \times \Delta T_{LMTD,\,CF} = 0.910 \times 69.52 = 63.26\,°C $$ > **Step 2 — Required overall heat transfer coefficient from design** $$ Q_{design} = U_o A_o \Delta T_m \Rightarrow U_{o,required} = \frac{Q_{design}}{A_o \Delta T_m} $$ $$ U_{o,required} = \frac{630{,}000}{12 \times 63.26} = \frac{630{,}000}{759.12} = 830 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} $$ > **Step 3 — Compare with available $U_o$** Available: $U_o = 850$ W/m²·K. Required: $U_{o,required} = 830$ W/m²·K. Since $850 > 830$, the exchanger is **adequate** — there is even a small margin of $20$ W/m²·K. > **Step 4 — Check the F-factor envelope** Since $F = 0.910 \ge 0.90$, the design is in the **"no penalty"** zone per TEMA. No re-design required. > **Step 5 — Final comment** The 1-2 STHE is suitable. The F-factor is high, indicating a near-counter-flow temperature profile, and the available $U_o$ exceeds the minimum required by a small margin (about 2.4%). > **Final Answer for (b):** $$ \boxed{\;\Delta T_m = 63.26\,°C, \quad U_{o,required} = 830 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} < 850 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K} \Rightarrow \textbf{Design is adequate.}\;} $$ --- ### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]** *A shell-and-tube heat exchanger with one shell pass and two tube passes is used to condense saturated steam at 2.0 bar (saturation temperature $T_{sat}=120.23°C$) by cooling water entering at 25°C and leaving at 60°C. The steam condenses at a rate of 0.4 kg/s. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U_o = 1500$ W/m²·K. The latent heat of vaporization at 2.0 bar is $h_{fg} = 2201.6$ kJ/kg.* #### (a) Calculate the heat duty, LMTD (counter-flow basis), and F-factor. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** > **Step 1 — Heat duty (condenser is single-fluid system; hot side at constant $T_h$)** $$ Q = \dot{m}_{steam} \times h_{fg} = 0.4 \times 2201.6 = 880.64 \text{ kW} = 880{,}640 \text{ W} $$ > **Step 2 — Counter-flow LMTD** Since hot side temperature is constant: $$ \Delta T_1 = T_h - T_{c,out} = 120.23 - 60 = 60.23\,°C $$ $$ \Delta T_2 = T_h - T_{c,in} = 120.23 - 25 = 95.23\,°C $$ $$ \Delta T_{LMTD,\,CF} = \frac{95.23 - 60.23}{\ln(95.23/60.23)} = \frac{35.0}{\ln 1.5811} = \frac{35.0}{0.4582} = 76.39\,°C $$ > **Step 3 — F-factor parameters** For a condenser with one fluid undergoing phase change, $R = 0$ (since $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = T_{sat}$). The F-factor simplifies for $R = 0$: $$ F = \frac{P}{(1-P)\ln[1/(1-P)]} \quad \text{(limiting form of Bowman equation as } R \to 0\text{)} $$ where: $$ P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_h - T_{c,in}} = \frac{60-25}{120.23-25} = \frac{35}{95.23} = 0.3675 $$ > **Step 4 — Compute F** $$ 1 - P = 0.6325, \quad \frac{1}{1-P} = 1.5811 $$ $$ \ln(1.5811) = 0.4582 $$ $$ (1-P)\ln[1/(1-P)] = 0.6325 \times 0.4582 = 0.2898 $$ $$ F = \frac{0.3675}{0.2898} = 1.268 $$ > **Note:** $F > 1$ occurs for **condensers** because the temperature profile of the hot fluid is flat (constant $T_{sat}$), which is more favorable than counter-flow. The F-factor is allowed to exceed 1 in this regime; the design is unconditionally acceptable. > **Final Answer for (a):** $$ \boxed{\;Q = 880.64 \text{ kW}, \quad \Delta T_{LMTD,CF} = 76.39\,°C, \quad F = 1.268\;} $$ --- #### (b) Determine the required heat transfer area and recommend a tube-side fluid arrangement to maximize the heat transfer. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** > **Step 1 — Required area** $$ A_{o,required} = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD,CF}} = \frac{880{,}640}{1500 \times 1.268 \times 76.39} $$ $$ A_{o,required} = \frac{880{,}640}{145{,}304} = 6.06 \text{ m}^2 $$ > **Step 2 — Recommended tube-side arrangement** For maximum heat transfer in a **condenser**: - **Run the steam on the shell side** (one pass). This allows proper distribution of condensate across the entire tube bundle and natural draining. - **Run cooling water on the tube side** (two or more passes). This raises the water velocity, increasing $h_i$ and $U$. - **Include segmental baffles** on the shell side at 20%–25% cut to direct condensate flow and improve $h_o$ on the upper tube rows. > **Step 3 — Justification for multi-pass tube side** Counter-flow is impossible for a single-pass shell (one fluid must occupy each pass). However, with a **1-2 STHE** the tube-side water flows in the opposite direction to the shell-side steam bulk motion in each successive pass, providing a quasi-counter-flow arrangement with $F \to 1$. > **Step 4 — Practical specification** Choose standard 3/4" (19.05 mm OD), 14 BWG tubes of 2.44 m length. Number of tubes: $$ N_t = \frac{A_o}{\pi D_o L} = \frac{6.06}{\pi \times 0.01905 \times 2.44} = \frac{6.06}{0.1460} \approx 42 \text{ tubes} $$ Round to 48 tubes (2 passes × 24 tubes/pass) for a clean bundle layout with triangular pitch 1.25 $D_o$ = 23.8 mm. > **Final Answer for (b):** $$ \boxed{\;A_{o,required} \approx 6.06 \text{ m}^2, \quad \text{Recommendation: 1-2 STHE, steam on shell side, water multi-pass on tube side, 48 tubes.}\;} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shell and Tube Heat Exchangers (KTU PEPET521, Module 4)** - **Definition:** A device with one fluid inside a tube bundle, another fluid flowing in the surrounding shell, separated by tube walls. - **TEMA code:** Three letters (Front-Shell-Rear). Example: **AEL** = Removable channel + One-pass shell + Fixed tubesheet. - **Most common type:** 1-2 STHE (one shell pass, two tube passes). - **Floating-head (S, T, W)** = handles thermal expansion; bundle removable. **Fixed tubesheet (L, M)** = cheapest, but limited $\Delta T$. - **Baffle cut** = 20–25% of $D_s$ (optimal). Baffles raise $h_o$ by 2–5×. - **LMTD formula** valid for counter-flow; for multi-pass, apply the **F-factor (Bowman-M Mueller-Nagle)**. - **F-factor envelope:** $F \ge 0.75$ acceptable; $F \ge 0.90$ no penalty. $F < 0.75$ → redesign (more shells). - **Effectiveness-NTU method** is preferred for variable specific heat or off-design analysis; counter-flow $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1-\exp[-NTU(1-C_R)]}{1-C_R\exp[-NTU(1-C_R)]}$. - **Fouling factor $R_f$** must be included in KTU design problems — water $\sim 0.0001$–$0.0002$, oil $\sim 0.0002$–$0.001$ m²K/W. - **Tube pitch** — triangular (1.25–1.5 $D_o$) for compactness; square (1.25 $D_o$) for cleanability. - **Pressure drop:** Tube-side $\Delta P_t \propto N_p V_t^2$; Shell-side $\Delta P_s \propto (N_B+1) V_s^2$ — baffle spacing directly controls $\Delta P_s$. - **Tube-side velocity** typically 1–2.5 m/s; **shell-side** typically 0.5–1.5 m/s. - **Assumptions to declare** in KTU solutions: constant $c_p$, constant $U$, no heat loss, no phase change (or specify phase change), negligible kinetic energy. - **For condensers/boilers**, $F > 1$ is acceptable; $R = 0$ simplifies the Bowman equation. - **Key reference standards:** TEMA (mechanical design), ASME Section VIII Div 1 (pressure vessel code), IS 2825 (Indian STHE standard). > [!TIP] > **Top 3 KTU Exam Mistakes to Avoid:** > 1. Forgetting the $F$-factor for 1-2, 2-4, etc. configurations (loses up to 4 marks). > 2. Mixing up $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ in the LMTD — always take the **inlet-end** and **outlet-end** differences in the **counter-flow** direction. > 3. Reporting $U_o$ in W/m²·K vs. W/m²·°C — they are numerically equal, but the textbook convention is K.

topics.title: Plate and Finned Tube Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plate and Finned Tube Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) — Formal Definition

A **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** is a compact, high-efficiency heat transfer device composed of a series of thin, corrugated metal plates clamped together within a rigid frame. The two fluid streams (hot and cold) flow in alternate channels formed between adjacent plates, exchanging heat through the plate walls. Unlike conventional shell-and-tube exchangers, PHEs achieve very high overall heat transfer coefficients (typically **3000–7000 W/m²K**) due to large surface area-to-volume ratios and induced turbulence from plate corrugations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Plate heat exchangers are classified as **compact heat exchangers** because they provide a heat transfer surface area per unit volume greater than **700 m²/m³**, vastly exceeding shell-and-tube designs (≈ 50–150 m²/m³).

### 1.2 Finned Tube Heat Exchanger — Formal Definition

A **Finned Tube Heat Exchanger (FTHE)** is a heat transfer apparatus in which tubes carrying one fluid are externally augmented with **fins (extended surfaces)** to enhance heat transfer to or from a surrounding gas stream. Fins increase the effective heat transfer area on the gas side, which is otherwise the controlling thermal resistance. They are widely employed in **air-cooled condensers, radiators, HVAC coils, and gas heaters**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** Fins are added on the side with the **lower heat transfer coefficient** (typically gases) to balance the thermal resistances on both sides of the tube wall.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

**Analogy for Plate Heat Exchanger:**
Imagine a **deck of cards pressed together in a frame**. Each card is a heat-conducting plate, and hot and cold water flow through the gaps between alternate cards. The wavy grooves on the cards (corrugations) act like a washboard, forcing the fluid to swirl and tumble, dramatically increasing the contact between fluid and plate. This is exactly how a PHE works.

**Analogy for Finned Tube Heat Exchanger:**
Think of a **bicycle radiator** with thin metal sheets glued to tubes. Hot water flows inside the tube; air blows across the fins. The fins act like a peacock's tail feathers — they spread out the heat-receiving surface so that a large area of air is in thermal contact with the tube, even though the tube itself is small. Without fins, the tube would have barely any surface to "talk" to the air, since air is a poor conductor of heat.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Plate material | **Stainless Steel (SS 316/304)**, Titanium |
| Plate thickness | **0.5 – 1.0 mm** |
| Chevron angle | **30° – 65°** (most common: 45°) |
| Fin material | **Aluminum**, Copper |
| Fin efficiency (typical) | **0.7 – 0.95** |
| Compactness factor (PHE) | **β > 700 m²/m³** |
| Compactness factor (FTHE) | **β ≈ 200–700 m²/m³** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Keyword Checklist:** Compactness factor (β), Heat transfer area per unit volume, Cheveron/corrugation angle, Fin efficiency (η_fin), Overall heat transfer coefficient (U).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Plate Heat Exchanger — Operational Concept

**Constructional Features:**
- A **fixed frame plate** and a **movable pressure plate** hold the plate pack together using **tie rods and clamping bolts**.
- Each plate is pressed with a **chevron (corrugated) pattern** that promotes turbulence.
- **Gaskets** (typically nitrile or EPDM rubber) are fitted around plate edges and ports to direct flow into alternate channels and prevent cross-contamination.
- **Four corner ports** in each plate act as inlet/outlet for the two fluids (e.g., Ports 1 & 3 for hot fluid, Ports 2 & 4 for cold fluid in single-pass configuration).

**Working Principle:**
1. Hot fluid enters through port 1, distributes across channel 1, exits via port 3.
2. Cold fluid enters through port 2, flows in the **opposite direction** (counterflow arrangement) in channel 2, exits via port 4.
3. Heat transfers through the thin plate wall from hot to cold fluid.
4. The plate corrugations create highly turbulent flow even at low Reynolds numbers, significantly enhancing convective heat transfer.

**Advantages of PHE:**
- Very high overall heat transfer coefficient (**U** up to 7000 W/m²K).
- Easy to dismantle for **cleaning and inspection** — hence called "gasketed PHE."
- Easy to **add/remove plates** to alter heat transfer capacity.
- Small hold-up volume → fast thermal response.
- Reduced **fouling** due to high turbulence.

**Disadvantages of PHE:**
- Limited pressure and temperature ratings (gaskets fail above ~180 °C and ~25 bar).
- Gaskets may leak over time.
- Not suitable for slurries or fibrous fluids (clog plate channels).
- Larger pressure drop compared to shell-and-tube.

### 2.2 Types of Plate Heat Exchangers

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Gasketed PHE** | Removable plates with gaskets; most common |
| **Brazed PHE (BPHE)** | Plates vacuum-brazed with copper; sealed, high pressure |
| **Welded PHE** | Plates welded in pairs; for aggressive/pressurized fluids |
| **Plate-and-Frame (Spiral)** | Two long plates rolled around a central core |
| **Plate-Fin (PFHE)** | Plates separated by corrugated fins; aerospace & cryogenic |

### 2.3 Finned Tube Heat Exchanger — Operational Concept

**Why fins are needed:**
- Heat transfer coefficient on gas side (h_gas ≈ 30–300 W/m²K) is **much lower** than on liquid side (h_liquid ≈ 500–10,000 W/m²K).
- This creates a **dominant gas-side thermal resistance** (R_gas = 1/(h_gas·A_gas)).
- By attaching fins, the gas-side area A_gas increases, reducing R_gas.

**Types of Fins:**
| Fin Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Annular (circular) fins** | Concentric ring-shaped fins on a tube (most common) |
| **Longitudinal fins** | Flat strips running parallel to tube axis (used in cross-flow) |
| **Helical fins** | Continuous spiral fin wound around the tube |
| **Pin fins (stud fins)** | Cylindrical pins welded on tube (used in fired heaters) |
| **Plate fins (flat fins)** | Rectangular flat plates attached radially (used in radiators) |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are examinable directly in KTU ESE — memorize with units!**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$ | Basic heat transfer equation (W) |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) in **K** |
| 3 | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} ; \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$ | End temperature differences |
| 4 | $F = \dfrac{\Delta T_m \,(\text{actual})}{\Delta T_{LM} \,(\text{counterflow})}$ | LMTD Correction Factor (dimensionless, $F \leq 1$) |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}$ | Cold-fluid capacity rate ratio |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}$ | Capacity rate ratio (R ≠ 1) |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{C_h(T_{h,i}-T_{h,o})}{C_{min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})}$ | Effectiveness of HX |
| 8 | $NTU = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units (dimensionless) |
| 9 | $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c}$ | Fin efficiency of an annular/rectangular fin |
| 10 | $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{h \cdot P}{k_{fin} \cdot A_c}}$ | Fin parameter (m⁻¹) |
| 11 | $L_c = L + \dfrac{D_o}{4}$ (annular) ; $L_c = L + \dfrac{t}{2}$ (rectangular) | Corrected fin length (m) |
| 12 | $\eta_o = 1 - \dfrac{A_{fin}}{A_{total}}(1 - \eta_{fin})$ | Overall surface efficiency |
| 13 | $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ | Heat capacity rate (W/K) |
| 14 | $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ | Heat capacity ratio ($0 \leq C_r \leq 1$) |
| 15 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left[-NTU \cdot (1 - C_r)\right]$ | Effectiveness for C_min side, C_max side fluid unmixed (cross-flow) |
| 16 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{C_r}\left[1 - \exp\left\{-C_r[1 - \exp(-NTU)]\right\}\right]$ | Effectiveness for C_max side fluid unmixed (cross-flow) |
| 17 | $C_r = 0: \varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU}$ | Condenser (C_max → ∞) |
| 18 | $C_r = 1: \varepsilon = \dfrac{NTU}{1+NTU}$ | Balanced counterflow |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Do not confuse **R** (in LMTD correction) with the universal gas constant. In heat exchanger design, **R = C_min/C_max**.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Application | Type Used |
|---|---|
| Dairy & food pasteurization | Gasketed PHE |
| District heating systems | Brazed PHE |
| Aircraft oil coolers | Brazed PHE |
| Car radiators | **Finned tube** (cross-flow over tubes) |
| HVAC evaporator/condenser coils | **Finned tube** (helical fins) |
| Air-cooled steam condensers in power plants | **Finned tube** (high-finned) |
| LNG vaporizers | Plate-fin HX (cryogenic) |
| Refrigeration condensers | Brazed PHE / Finned tube |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

**Starting Point:** For a differential element of heat exchanger at distance *x* from the inlet:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the cold fluid:**

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c = C_c \cdot (T_{c,o} - T_c)$$

Rearranging:

$$dT_c = -\dfrac{dQ}{C_c}$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance on the hot fluid:**

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot dT_h = C_h \cdot (T_h - T_{h,o})$$

Rearranging:

$$dT_h = -\dfrac{dQ}{C_h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the difference d(T_h − T_c):**

$$d(T_h - T_c) = dT_h - dT_c = -\dfrac{dQ}{C_h} + \dfrac{dQ}{C_c} = -dQ \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} - \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Define ΔT = T_h − T_c. Substitute into the first equation:**

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$$

Therefore:

$$dA = \dfrac{dQ}{U \cdot \Delta T}$$

**Step 5 — Express d(ΔT) in terms of dQ:**

$$d(\Delta T) = -dQ \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} - \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

Solving for dQ:

$$dQ = \dfrac{-d(\Delta T)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} - \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Equate both expressions for dA:**

$$\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} - \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right) dA$$

**Step 7 — Integrate from inlet (ΔT₁) to outlet (ΔT₂) over the total area A:**

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} - \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right) \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U \cdot A \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} - \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

**Step 8 — Express the bracket in terms of ΔT₁ and ΔT₂:**

Using the overall energy balance $Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$:

$$\dfrac{1}{C_h} - \dfrac{1}{C_c} = \dfrac{T_{h,o} - T_{c,o} - (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}{Q} = -\dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$

Substituting back:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = U \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$

**Step 9 — Solve for Q:**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}, \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}}$$

where for **counterflow** configuration:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Fin Efficiency for an Annular Fin

**Step 1 — Consider a differential annular fin element at radius r, of thickness t and width dr:**

Heat conducted in at radius r minus heat conducted out at radius r + dr equals heat convected from the side surfaces:

$$Q_r - Q_{r+dr} = dQ_{conv}$$

**Step 2 — Express each term:**

$$Q_r = -k_{fin} \cdot A_c \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dr} = -k_{fin} \cdot (2\pi r t) \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dr}$$

$$Q_{r+dr} = Q_r + \dfrac{dQ_r}{dr} dr$$

**Step 3 — Convective loss from sides:**

$$dQ_{conv} = h \cdot dA \cdot (T - T_\infty) = h \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) \cdot (T - T_\infty)$$

**Step 4 — Energy balance:**

$$\dfrac{d}{dr}\left[k_{fin} \cdot 2\pi r t \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dr}\right] dr = h \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \cdot (T - T_\infty)$$

**Step 5 — Let θ = T − T∞ and assume uniform k_fin:**

$$\dfrac{d}{dr}\left(r \dfrac{d\theta}{dr}\right) = \dfrac{2h}{k_{fin} \cdot t} \cdot r \cdot \theta$$

Let $m^2 = \dfrac{2h}{k_{fin} \cdot t}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{d}{dr}\left(r \dfrac{d\theta}{dr}\right) - m^2 \theta = 0$$

This is the **modified Bessel equation of order zero** with solution:

$$\theta(r) = C_1 I_0(mr) + C_2 K_0(mr)$$

**Step 6 — Apply boundary conditions:**
- At r = r₁ (base): θ(r₁) = θ_b
- At r = r₂ (tip, with insulated tip): $\dfrac{d\theta}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=r_2} = 0$

**Step 7 — Solve the constants (algebra omitted, see Kays & London, Holman):**

$$\theta(r) = \theta_b \cdot \dfrac{I_1(m r_2) \cdot K_0(m r) + K_1(m r_2) \cdot I_0(m r)}{I_1(m r_2) \cdot K_0(m r_1) + K_1(m r_2) \cdot I_0(m r_1)}$$

**Step 8 — Heat dissipated by the fin:**

$$Q_{fin} = -k_{fin} \cdot (2\pi r_1 t) \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=r_1}$$

After differentiation and simplification:

$$Q_{fin} = \theta_b \cdot k_{fin} \cdot (2\pi r_1 t) \cdot m \cdot \dfrac{I_1(m r_1) K_1(m r_2) - K_1(m r_1) I_1(m r_2)}{I_1(m r_2) K_0(m r_1) + K_1(m r_2) I_0(m r_1)}$$

**Step 9 — Ideal heat transfer (entire fin at base temperature):**

$$Q_{ideal} = h \cdot A_{fin} \cdot \theta_b = h \cdot 2\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2) \cdot \theta_b$$

**Step 10 — Fin efficiency:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{Q_{fin}}{Q_{ideal}} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c} \quad \text{(for long thin fin approximation)}}$$

where $L_c = r_2 - r_1 + \dfrac{t}{2}$ and $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{k_{fin} t}}$ for an annular fin.

> [!NOTE]
> The exact Bessel-function solution reduces to the **tanh(mLc) form** for **thin annular fins** where $(r_2 - r_1) \gg t/2$. For KTU problems, use the tanh formula unless the fin is very short and stubby.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Effectiveness–NTU Relation for a Cross-Flow Heat Exchanger (Both Fluids Unmixed)

**Step 1 — Differential energy balance on a small area dA:**

For the hot fluid (capacity rate C_h) and cold fluid (C_c):

$$C_h \, dT_h = -U \cdot (T_h - T_c) \, dA$$
$$C_c \, dT_c = +U \cdot (T_h - T_c) \, dA$$

**Step 2 — Define dimensionless temperatures:**

$$T_h^* = \dfrac{T_h - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}, \quad T_c^* = \dfrac{T_c - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting and dividing by C_min:**

Let C* = C/C_min. Assuming C_h is the min:

$$\dfrac{dT_h^*}{dA^*} = -(T_h^* - T_c^*), \quad \dfrac{dT_c^*}{dA^*} = \dfrac{1}{C_r}(T_h^* - T_c^*)$$

where $A^* = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} = NTU$ and $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$.

**Step 4 — Define local capacity rate parameter β:**

For cross-flow, an additional parameter is needed because flow streams mix at different rates. Define:

$$\beta = \dfrac{A_{cross}}{A_{total}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the coupled PDE (both fluids unmixed, β = 1):**

The standard result (Kays & London, Table of ε-NTU):

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left\{\dfrac{NTU^{0.22}}{C_r}\left[\exp(-C_r \cdot NTU^{0.78}) - 1\right]\right\}}$$

**For C_max-side unmixed (one fluid cross-flow, other in pure cross with no mixing):**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}$$

**For C_min-side unmixed (other fluid fully mixed):**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{C_r}\left\{1 - \exp\left[-C_r\left(1 - \exp(-NTU)\right)\right]\right\}}$$

**Step 6 — Special cases:**

- **C_r → 0** (condenser): $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU}$
- **C_r → 1** (balanced counterflow): $\varepsilon = \dfrac{NTU}{1+NTU}$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A finned-tube cross-flow heat exchanger heats air (C_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K) from 15 °C to 35 °C using hot water (C_p = 4.18 kJ/kg·K) entering at 80 °C and leaving at 50 °C. Mass flow of air = 0.5 kg/s, water = 0.2 kg/s. If the overall heat transfer coefficient U = 80 W/m²K based on bare tube area, determine the required area using NTU method.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heat capacity rates:**

$$C_{air} = 0.5 \times 1.005 = 0.5025 \text{ kW/K} = 502.5 \text{ W/K}$$
$$C_{water} = 0.2 \times 4.18 = 0.836 \text{ kW/K} = 836 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 2 — Identify C_min and C_max:**

$$C_{min} = C_{air} = 502.5 \text{ W/K}, \quad C_{max} = C_{water} = 836 \text{ W/K}$$
$$C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{502.5}{836} = 0.6011$$

**Step 3 — Compute effectiveness ε:**

$$Q = C_{air} \cdot (T_{a,o} - T_{a,i}) = 502.5 \times 20 = 10050 \text{ W}$$
$$Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 502.5 \times (80 - 15) = 32712.5 \text{ W}$$
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{10050}{32712.5} = 0.3072$$

**Step 4 — Apply cross-flow ε-NTU (C_min unmixed, C_max mixed):**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]$$
$$0.3072 = 1 - \exp\left[-NTU \times (1 - 0.6011)\right]$$
$$\exp(-0.3989 \cdot NTU) = 0.6928$$
$$NTU = -\dfrac{\ln(0.6928)}{0.3989} = \dfrac{0.3669}{0.3989} = 0.9198$$

**Step 5 — Required area:**

$$A = \dfrac{NTU \cdot C_{min}}{U} = \dfrac{0.9198 \times 502.5}{80} = \dfrac{462.2}{80} = 5.78 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 5.78 \text{ m}^2 \text{ (bare tube area)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **total finned area** would be larger: $A_{total} = A_{bare} + A_{fin} \times \eta_{fin}$. For KTU, always specify whether the area is on bare or finned side.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plate Heat Exchanger — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HF1(["HOT FLUID INLET"]) --> PP1["PORT 1 TOP LEFT"]
    PP1 --> PCH1["PLATE CHANNEL H1 BETWEEN PLATES 1 AND 2"]
    PCH1 --> PP3["PORT 3 BOTTOM LEFT"]
    PP3 --> HF2(["HOT FLUID OUTLET"])

    CF1(["COLD FLUID INLET"]) --> PP2["PORT 2 TOP RIGHT"]
    PP2 --> PCH2["PLATE CHANNEL C1 BETWEEN PLATES 2 AND 3"]
    PCH2 --> PP4["PORT 4 BOTTOM RIGHT"]
    PP4 --> CF2(["COLD FLUID OUTLET"])

    PCH1 -.HEAT TRANSFER.-> PCH2
    PCH2 -.HEAT TRANSFER.-> PCH3["PLATE CHANNEL H2 BETWEEN PLATES 3 AND 4"]
    PCH3 -.HEAT TRANSFER.-> PCH4["PLATE CHANNEL C2 BETWEEN PLATES 4 AND 5"]
    PCH4 --> PCH5["PLATE CHANNEL H3 BETWEEN PLATES 5 AND 6"]

    FRAME["FIXED FRAME PLATE"] --- PCH1
    PCH5 --- MP["MOVABLE PRESSURE PLATE"]
    CLAMP["CLAMPING BOLTS AND TIE RODS"] --- MP
```

### 4.2 Plate Geometry — Corrugation Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PLT["SINGLE PLATE 0.5 TO 1.0 MM STAINLESS STEEL"]
    CH["CHEVRON CORRUGATION ANGLE 30 TO 65 DEG"]
    PORT1["PORT A TOP LEFT"]
    PORT2["PORT B TOP RIGHT"]
    PORT3["PORT C BOTTOM LEFT"]
    PORT4["PORT D BOTTOM RIGHT"]
    GSK["GASKET CHANNEL AROUND EDGE"]
    PLT --- CH
    PLT --- PORT1
    PLT --- PORT2
    PLT --- PORT3
    PLT --- PORT4
    PLT --- GSK
```

### 4.3 Finned Tube Heat Exchanger — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["TUBE CARRYING FLUID 1 INTERNAL FLOW"] --> T2["TUBE CARRYING FLUID 1 INTERNAL FLOW"]
    T2 --> T3["TUBE CARRYING FLUID 1 INTERNAL FLOW"]
    T3 --> T4["TUBE CARRYING FLUID 1 INTERNAL FLOW"]
    F1["ANNULAR FIN 1 RADIATING FROM TUBE"] --- T1
    F2["ANNULAR FIN 2"] --- T2
    F3["ANNULAR FIN 3"] --- T3
    F4["ANNULAR FIN 4"] --- T4
    GAS1(["EXTERNAL GAS FLOW IN CROSS DIRECTION"]) --> GAS2["GAS PASSES OVER FINS RECEIVES HEAT"]
    GAS2 --> GAS3(["HEATED GAS OUTLET"])
    GAS2 --- F1
    GAS2 --- F2
    GAS2 --- F3
    GAS2 --- F4
```

### 4.4 Cross-Flow Configuration for Finned Tube HX

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INLETHOT["HOT FLUID INLET TUBE HEADER TOP"] --> TUBEARRAY["BANK OF FINNED TUBES ARRANGED IN STAGGERED PATTERN"]
    TUBEARRAY --> OUTLETHOT["HOT FLUID OUTLET BOTTOM HEADER"]
    AIRCROSS(["AMBIENT AIR BLOWN PERPENDICULAR BY FAN"]) --> TUBEARRAY
    TUBEARRAY --> AIRHEATED(["HEATED AIR DISCHARGED TO ATMOSPHERE"])
    FINS["EXTENDED SURFACE FINS COVER TUBES AIR SIDE"] --- TUBEARRAY
```

### 4.5 Heat Transfer Path in Finned Tube (Thermal Resistance Network)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["R_i INSIDE FILM RESISTANCE 1 BY h_i A_i"] --> R2["R_w TUBE WALL RESISTANCE ln D_o D_i BY 2 PI k L"]
    R2 --> R3["R_contact CONTACT RESISTANCE FIN TUBE JOINT"]
    R3 --> R4["R_fin FIN EFFICIENCY MODIFIED 1 BY h_o A_fin eta_fin"]
    R4 --> R5["R_o OUTSIDE AIR FILM RESISTANCE"]
    R5 --> Q(["Q TOTAL HEAT TRANSFER RATE"])
```

### 4.6 Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Plate HX | Finned Tube HX |
|---|---|---|
| Typical U (W/m²K) | 3000–7000 | 30–300 |
| Compactness β (m²/m³) | > 700 | 200–700 |
| Operating pressure | Low–Medium | Medium–High |
| Best suited for | Liquid–liquid | Gas–liquid |
| Maintenance | Easy (gasketed) | Moderate |
| Pressure drop | Moderate–High | Low (gas side) |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a Plate Heat Exchanger. List any four advantages of a plate heat exchanger over a conventional shell-and-tube heat exchanger.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** is a compact heat exchanger consisting of a series of thin corrugated metal plates clamped together in a frame, with two fluid streams flowing in alternate channels between the plates, exchanging heat through the plate walls.

**Four advantages over shell-and-tube HX:**
1. **High heat transfer coefficient** (U up to 7000 W/m²K) due to large area and turbulence from corrugations.
2. **Compact** — area per unit volume > 700 m²/m³.
3. **Easy maintenance** — gasketed plates can be removed, cleaned, and replaced individually.
4. **Flexible capacity** — heat transfer area can be increased/decreased by adding/removing plates.
5. *(Any other valid point: less fouling, lower hold-up, closer temperature approach)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the significance of adding fins to a tube in a heat exchanger? Define fin efficiency and state the formula for a rectangular fin.**

**Model Answer:**
**Significance of fins:**
When one fluid has a much lower heat transfer coefficient (e.g., a gas), it dominates the overall thermal resistance. Adding fins on that side **increases the effective heat transfer area**, reducing the resistance on the gas side and thereby increasing the overall heat transfer rate.

**Fin efficiency (η_fin):** It is defined as the ratio of the actual heat dissipated by the fin to the heat that would be dissipated if the entire fin were at the base temperature.

$$\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{ideal}} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{h \cdot A_{fin} \cdot \theta_b}$$

For a **rectangular fin** with corrected length $L_c$ and fin parameter $m$:

$$\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(m L_c)}{m L_c}$$

where $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{k_{fin} A_c}}$ and $L_c = L + \dfrac{t}{2}$ for a thin rectangular fin.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and working of a gasketed plate heat exchanger with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of chevron angle on heat transfer and pressure drop.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A plate heat exchanger with 30 plates heats 2 kg/s of water from 35 °C to 80 °C using hot oil (C_p = 2.1 kJ/kg·K) entering at 150 °C and leaving at 90 °C. If U = 4500 W/m²K (based on single plate area of 0.35 m²) and the flow is counter-current single pass, determine:
   1. The mass flow rate of oil.
   2. The LMTD.
   3. The total effective heat transfer area and number of effective plates.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction:**
- A plate HX consists of a **fixed frame plate** and a **movable pressure plate** held together by **tie rods with clamping nuts**.
- **Gasketed plates** (SS 316, 0.5–1 mm thick) are pressed with chevron corrugations and stacked between the two frame plates.
- Each plate has **four corner ports** (two for inlet, two for outlet) for the two fluids.
- **Gaskets** (NBR or EPDM) seal the plate edges and direct fluid flow into alternate channels.

**Working:**
- Hot fluid enters port 1 → flows in even-numbered channels → exits port 3.
- Cold fluid enters port 2 → flows in odd-numbered channels in opposite direction → exits port 4.
- Heat passes through each plate from hot to cold side.
- The plate pack is compressed using a hydraulic jack and clamping bolts to required torque.

**Role of Chevron Angle (β):**

| Chevron Angle | Heat Transfer | Pressure Drop | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 30° (low) | Lower h | Low ΔP | Viscous / shear-sensitive fluids |
| 45° (medium) | Moderate | Moderate | General purpose |
| 65° (high) | High h | High ΔP | Low-viscosity, clean fluids |

A **larger chevron angle** induces stronger vortices and cross-flow, increasing the heat transfer coefficient and pressure drop. Optimum is around **45°** for most applications.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(1) Heat duty and oil mass flow rate:**

$$Q = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} (T_{w,o} - T_{w,i}) = 2 \times 4.18 \times (80 - 35) = 376.2 \text{ kW}$$

$$\dot{m}_{oil} = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,oil}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})} = \dfrac{376.2}{2.1 \times 60} = \dfrac{376.2}{126} = 2.986 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{oil} \approx 2.99 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**(2) LMTD (counterflow):**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 150 - 80 = 70 \text{ °C}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 90 - 35 = 55 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{70 - 55}{\ln(70/55)} = \dfrac{15}{\ln(1.2727)} = \dfrac{15}{0.2412} = 62.2 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LM} \approx 62.2 \text{ K}}$$

**(3) Effective area and effective plate count:**

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \dfrac{376{,}200}{4500 \times 62.2} = \dfrac{376{,}200}{279{,}900} = 1.344 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 1.344 \text{ m}^2}$$

Number of effective plates:

$$N_{eff} = \dfrac{A}{A_{plate}} = \dfrac{1.344}{0.35} = 3.84 \Rightarrow 4 \text{ plates}$$

$$\boxed{N_{eff} = 4 \text{ effective plates}}$$

The remaining 26 plates in the pack include the head/tail plates (which don't transfer heat) and additional plates in the pack for flow distribution.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the fin efficiency of a rectangular fin of uniform cross-section, with the tip assumed to be insulated. State the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An annular fin of inner diameter 25 mm, outer diameter 75 mm, and thickness 1.5 mm is fitted on a tube. The fin is made of aluminum (k = 205 W/m·K) and is exposed to air at h = 50 W/m²K. The fin base temperature is 100 °C and air temperature is 25 °C. Calculate:
   1. The fin parameter m.
   2. The fin efficiency η_fin.
   3. The heat dissipated by a single fin.
   4. The overall efficiency if 100 such fins are attached to a tube of length 0.5 m (bare tube h_inside = 5000 W/m²K).

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, 1-D heat conduction along the fin length.
2. Constant thermal conductivity k_fin.
3. Uniform fin thickness t.
4. Constant convective coefficient h on fin surface.
5. Tip is insulated (no heat loss at tip) — or adiabatic at tip.
6. No heat generation inside the fin.
7. Constant ambient temperature T_∞.

**Derivation:**

For a rectangular fin of width w, thickness t, the differential element at distance x has:

Heat conducted in at x = $Q_x = -k_{fin} A_c \dfrac{dT}{dx}$

Heat conducted out at x+dx = $Q_{x+dx} = Q_x + \dfrac{dQ_x}{dx} dx$

Heat convected from the element = $h \cdot P \cdot (T - T_\infty) dx$, where P = 2(w + t) is the perimeter.

**Energy balance:**

$$Q_x = Q_{x+dx} + hP(T - T_\infty) dx$$

$$-\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(k_{fin} A_c \dfrac{dT}{dx}\right) = hP(T - T_\infty)$$

Let $\theta = T - T_\infty$ and $m^2 = \dfrac{hP}{k_{fin} A_c}$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0$$

**General solution:**

$$\theta(x) = C_1 \cosh(mx) + C_2 \sinh(mx)$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- At x = 0: θ(0) = θ_b → $C_1 = \theta_b$
- At x = L: $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg\vert_{L} = 0$ → $C_2 \sinh(mL) m = 0$ → $C_2 = -\theta_b \tanh(mL)$

**Solution:**

$$\theta(x) = \theta_b \dfrac{\cosh[m(L - x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$$

**Heat dissipated:**

$$Q_{fin} = -k_{fin} A_c \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = k_{fin} A_c \cdot m \cdot \theta_b \tanh(mL)$$

**Ideal heat (entire fin at base):**

$$Q_{ideal} = h A_{fin} \theta_b = h \cdot (2wL) \cdot \theta_b$$

**Fin efficiency:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{Q_{fin}}{Q_{ideal}} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c}, \quad \text{where } L_c = L + \dfrac{t}{2}}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $r_1 = 0.0125$ m, $r_2 = 0.0375$ m, $t = 0.0015$ m, $k_{Al} = 205$ W/m·K, $h = 50$ W/m²K, $\theta_b = 100 - 25 = 75$ °C.

**(1) Fin parameter m for annular fin:**

$$m = \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{k_{fin} t}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 50}{205 \times 0.0015}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{0.3075}} = \sqrt{325.2} = 18.03 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Corrected length:**

$$L_c = (r_2 - r_1) + \dfrac{t}{2} = (0.0375 - 0.0125) + 0.00075 = 0.025 + 0.00075 = 0.02575 \text{ m}$$

**Argument of tanh:**

$$mL_c = 18.03 \times 0.02575 = 0.4643$$

$$\tanh(0.4643) = \dfrac{e^{0.4643} - e^{-0.4643}}{e^{0.4643} + e^{-0.4643}} = \dfrac{1.5908 - 0.6287}{1.5908 + 0.6287} = \dfrac{0.9621}{2.2195} = 0.4335$$

**(2) Fin efficiency:**

$$\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{0.4335}{0.4643} = 0.9336$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{fin} \approx 0.934}$$

**(3) Heat dissipated by single fin:**

Fin surface area (both sides, neglecting edges):

$$A_{fin} = 2\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2) = 2\pi (0.0375^2 - 0.0125^2) = 2\pi (0.001406 - 0.000156) = 2\pi \times 0.00125 = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{fin} = \eta_{fin} \cdot h \cdot A_{fin} \cdot \theta_b = 0.9336 \times 50 \times 0.007854 \times 75$$

$$Q_{fin} = 0.9336 \times 50 \times 0.5890 = 27.49 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{fin} \approx 27.5 \text{ W}}$$

**(4) Overall surface efficiency:**

For 100 fins, total fin area = 100 × 0.007854 = 0.7854 m².

Bare tube area (between fins): the tube length is 0.5 m and the tube is interrupted by 100 fins occupying $100 \times 0.0015 = 0.15$ m along the length. The remaining bare length per fin gap = $\dfrac{0.5 - 0.15}{100} = 0.0035$ m per gap.

Bare area = $\pi D_o \times \text{bare length} = \pi \times 0.05 \times (0.5 - 0.15) = \pi \times 0.05 \times 0.35 = 0.05498$ m².

Actually, a simpler approach assuming fins are tightly packed and bare area is negligible compared to fin area, or given tube OD is $D_o = 2r_2 = 0.075$ m:

$$A_{total} = A_{fin} + A_{bare} = 0.7854 + 0.05498 = 0.8404 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\eta_o = 1 - \dfrac{A_{fin}}{A_{total}}(1 - \eta_{fin}) = 1 - \dfrac{0.7854}{0.8404}(1 - 0.9336) = 1 - 0.9344 \times 0.0664$$

$$\eta_o = 1 - 0.0620 = 0.9380$$

$$\boxed{\eta_o \approx 0.938}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Don't confuse the outer tube diameter with the fin OD.** The tube outside diameter is the fin inner diameter ($D_i = 2r_1$).
> 2. **Use L_c, not L**, in the tanh formula. The corrected length accounts for the heat transfer at the fin tip.
> 3. **Always state assumptions** at the start of the derivation — students lose up to **2 marks** in KTU for skipping assumptions.
> 4. **Convert temperatures to Kelvin only if ΔT differences are needed**; for efficiency questions, °C and K give identical answers (since differences are the same).
> 5. **Bessel functions (I₀, K₀, I₁, K₁)** appear for annular fins. For KTU, the **tanh(mLc) approximation** is acceptable for thin fins ($L_c/t \gg 5$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Plate & Finned Tube Heat Exchangers**

**🔑 Key Definitions:**
- **Plate HX:** Stack of corrugated plates; alternate channels carry hot/cold fluids.
- **Finned Tube HX:** Tubes fitted with extended surfaces (fins) to augment gas-side heat transfer.
- **Fin efficiency (η_fin):** Ratio of actual heat dissipated by fin to heat if entire fin were at base temperature.
- **Overall surface efficiency (η_o):** Accounts for the fact that the fin is not at uniform base temperature.
- **Compactness factor (β):** Heat transfer area per unit volume of HX.
- **LMTD correction factor (F):** Accounts for deviation from pure counterflow.

**🔑 Critical Formulas:**
- LMTD: $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$
- Fin efficiency: $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL_c)}{mL_c}$
- NTU: $NTU = \dfrac{UA}{C_{min}}$
- Effectiveness: $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU(1-C_r)}$ (cross-flow, C_min unmixed)

**🔑 Design Rules of Thumb:**
- Fin efficiency should be **> 0.6** for a fin to be "worth it."
- **LMTD correction factor F** must lie in **0.8–1.0**; if F < 0.8, redesign with multiple shells.
- For plate HX, **chevron angle 30°–45°** is best; **65°** for clean, low-viscosity fluids.
- Add fins on the side with the **lower h** (gas side), not the liquid side.

**🔑 Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:**
1. ❌ Forgetting to use the corrected fin length $L_c$ instead of actual length $L$.
2. ❌ Treating shell-and-tube LMTD correction formula as applicable to plate HX (use F = 1 for true counterflow plate HX).
3. ❌ Using $C_p$ for water as 4.186 kJ/kg·K vs 4.18 kJ/kg·K (both acceptable, but be consistent).
4. ❌ Confusing the "cold fluid unmixed" vs "hot fluid unmixed" formulas in cross-flow.
5. ❌ Not specifying the basis of area (bare or finned) when reporting U or A.
6. ❌ Writing $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2}{2}$ (this is AMTD, used only when correction factor is close to 1 and the error is acceptable).

**🔑 Numerical Constants to Memorize:**
- $c_{p,water} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K
- $c_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
- $k_{Aluminum} = 205$ W/m·K
- $k_{Copper} = 385$ W/m·K
- $k_{Stainless Steel 316} = 16.2$ W/m·K
- 1 kcal/h = 1.163 W

**🔑 Typical KTU Question Patterns:**
- **3-mark Q:** Definition + 4 advantages/disadvantages; definition of fin efficiency with formula.
- **7-mark Q:** Derivation of LMTD or fin efficiency; design of plate HX with given inlet/outlet temperatures.
- **14-mark Q:** Combination of (a) construction/derivation + (b) numerical design using LMTD or NTU method.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer Principles and Design Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer Principles and Design Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heat Transfer** is the branch of thermal engineering that deals with the generation, conversion, storage, and transport of thermal energy across physical systems. In applied thermal engineering, it provides the governing framework for sizing boilers, condensers, evaporators, heat exchangers, insulations, and fins used in power plants, refrigeration cycles, and process industries.

The three fundamental modes of heat transfer as prescribed by the KTU 2024 scheme are:

1. **Conduction** — Transfer of thermal energy through a medium by molecular interactions and electron drift, without bulk motion of the medium. Governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**.

2. **Convection** — Transfer of thermal energy between a solid surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas) due to combined effects of conduction, advection, and fluid motion. Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**.

3. **Radiation** — Transfer of thermal energy in the form of electromagnetic waves that does not require a medium. Governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Equations** in heat transfer are mathematical relations that allow an engineer to predict the rate of heat exchange ($Q$ in Watts), select appropriate heat transfer surface area ($A$ in m²), and determine the operating temperatures of thermal equipment under given boundary conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Heat Flow is Like Electrical Current Flow**

Imagine a hydraulic/electrical circuit analogy:

- **Temperature difference** ($\Delta T$) acts as the **voltage** (driving potential).
- **Heat flow rate** ($Q$) acts as the **electric current**.
- **Thermal resistance** ($R_{th}$) acts as the **electrical resistance**.

Just as Ohm's Law states $I = V/R$, Fourier's law analogously gives:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$$

A wall that easily passes heat is like a thick copper wire (low resistance). An insulator is like a thin constantan wire (high resistance). This analogy is foundational to **thermal network analysis** and is heavily used in KTU board problems.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Thermal conductivity** of copper $k \approx 401$ **W/m·K**
- **Thermal conductivity** of fiberglass insulation $k \approx 0.04$ **W/m·K**
- **Standard gravitational acceleration** $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** (for natural convection correlations)
- **Standard atmospheric pressure** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ **kPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant mode of heat transfer.**
- Solid stationary medium → **Conduction**
- Fluid in motion over a surface → **Convection**
- High temperature surfaces with no intervening medium → **Radiation**

**Step 2 — Establish the temperature field and boundary conditions.**
- Steady state: $\partial T / \partial t = 0$
- Boundary conditions: prescribed temperature (Dirichlet) or heat flux (Neumann)

**Step 3 — Compute the relevant thermal resistance.**
- Conduction: $R_{cond} = L / (kA)$
- Convection: $R_{conv} = 1 / (hA)$
- Radiation: $R_{rad} = 1 / (h_r A)$ where $h_r = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s + T_\infty)(T_s^2 + T_\infty^2)$

**Step 4 — Combine resistances using series/parallel rules.**
- Series: $R_{total} = \sum R_i$
- Parallel: $1/R_{total} = \sum (1/R_i)$

**Step 5 — Apply the design equation $Q = \Delta T / R_{total}$ to find heat flow.**

**Why?** Because in steady state, the heat flow through each layer in a composite system must be equal (conservation of energy), and the temperature drops add up — exactly as voltage drops across series resistors.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Heat Transfer Design Equation

The generalized **heat transfer design equation** used in KTU problems is:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

where:
- $U$ = Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = Heat transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T_{lm}$ = Log Mean Temperature Difference (K)

**How it works:**
1. $U$ bundles up the conduction, convection, and sometimes radiation resistances into a single coefficient for ease of design.
2. $A$ is the physical contact area between the hot and cold fluid streams.
3. $\Delta T_{lm}$ accounts for the fact that in most heat exchangers, the temperature difference between fluids varies along the flow path — it is the *effective* average temperature difference.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 90% of KTU board problems on this topic.**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| Fourier's Law (1-D Conduction) | $q = -k \, dT/dx$ | $q$: W/m², $k$: W/m·K |
| Steady Conduction through Plane Wall | $Q = kA(T_1 - T_2)/L$ | $Q$: W, $L$: m |
| Conduction through Cylindrical Wall | $Q = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2) / \ln(r_2/r_1)$ | $r$: radius (m) |
| Convection (Newton's Law) | $Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | $h$: W/m²·K |
| Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann) | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | $\varepsilon$: emissivity |
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $1/(UA) = 1/h_i A_i + L/(kA) + 1/h_o A_o$ | For composite wall |
| LMTD | $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$ | $\Delta T_1, \Delta T_2$: end temperature differences |
| NTU | $NTU = UA / C_{min}$ | $C$: heat capacity rate (W/K) |
| Heat Exchanger Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} / Q_{max}$ | Dimensionless |
| Fin Parameter | $m = \sqrt{h P / (k A_c)}$ | $P$: perimeter, $A_c$: cross-section |
| Fin Efficiency (Insulated Tip) | $\eta_f = \tanh(m L) / (m L)$ | Dimensionless |
| Fin Effectiveness | $\varepsilon_f = Q_{fin} / Q_{no\,fin}$ | Dimensionless |
| Critical Radius (Cylinder) | $r_c = k / h$ | $r_c$: m |
| Critical Radius (Sphere) | $r_c = 2k / h$ | $r_c$: m |

> [!NOTE]
> In all of the above, $C_{min}$ refers to the smaller of the two fluid heat capacity rates $C_h$ and $C_c$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Power plant boilers** use the LMTD approach to size evaporator tubes that transfer heat from combustion gases to water.
- **Refrigeration condensers and evaporators** are sized using NTU-effectiveness ($\varepsilon$-NTU) charts.
- **Insulation design** for steam pipes requires the critical radius of insulation concept — adding insulation below the critical radius *increases* heat loss, a counterintuitive but exam-favorite result.
- **CPU and transformer cooling** rely on fin design equations to maximize heat dissipation per unit mass.
- **Solar thermal collectors** use the overall heat transfer coefficient to model losses from absorber plates to ambient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient for a Composite Plane Wall

**Given:** A composite wall of three layers in series. Hot fluid at $T_{h,\infty}$ outside, cold fluid at $T_{c,\infty}$ on the other side. Surface coefficients are $h_h$ and $h_c$. Layer thicknesses $L_1, L_2, L_3$ and conductivities $k_1, k_2, k_3$.

**Find:** Expression for the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$.

**Step 1 — Write heat flux through each resistance in series.**

In steady state, the heat flux $q$ (W/m²) is the same through all layers:

$$q = h_h (T_{h,\infty} - T_{s1})$$

$$q = \frac{k_1}{L_1} (T_{s1} - T_{s2})$$

$$q = \frac{k_2}{L_2} (T_{s2} - T_{s3})$$

$$q = \frac{k_3}{L_3} (T_{s3} - T_{s4})$$

$$q = h_c (T_{s4} - T_{c,\infty})$$

**Step 2 — Add the temperature drops.**

Adding all five equations, the intermediate surface temperatures cancel out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{h,\infty} - T_{c,\infty} &= (T_{h,\infty} - T_{s1}) + (T_{s1} - T_{s2}) + (T_{s2} - T_{s3}) + (T_{s3} - T_{s4}) + (T_{s4} - T_{c,\infty}) \\
&= \frac{q}{h_h} + \frac{q L_1}{k_1} + \frac{q L_2}{k_2} + \frac{q L_3}{k_3} + \frac{q}{h_c} \\
&= q \left[ \frac{1}{h_h} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_c} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify $U$ from the overall form $q = U (T_{h,\infty} - T_{c,\infty})$.**

Comparing with the standard form, we get the **design equation**:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_h} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_c}$$

This is the **fundamental design equation for composite wall heat transfer** in KTU exams.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

**Given:** A counter-flow heat exchanger with hot fluid entering at $T_{h,i}$ and leaving at $T_{h,o}$, cold fluid entering at $T_{c,i}$ and leaving at $T_{c,o}$.

**Find:** Expression for $\Delta T_{lm}$.

**Step 1 — Heat transfer in a differential element $dA$.**

Let the hot fluid temperature at position $x$ be $T_h$ and the cold fluid temperature be $T_c$. The local temperature difference is:

$$\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$$

The differential heat transferred:

$$dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T = U \, dA \, (T_h - T_c)$$

**Step 2 — Apply energy balance to each fluid stream.**

For the hot fluid losing heat:

$$dQ = -C_h \, dT_h \quad \Rightarrow \quad dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{C_h}$$

For the cold fluid gaining heat:

$$dQ = C_c \, dT_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad dT_c = \frac{dQ}{C_c}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $\Delta T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\Delta T) &= dT_h - dT_c \\
&= -\frac{dQ}{C_h} - \frac{dQ}{C_c} \\
&= -dQ \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T$ and separate variables.**

$$
d(\Delta T) = -U \, dA \, \Delta T \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) dA
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from inlet to outlet.**

$$
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) \int_0^A dA
$$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -UA \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Use the overall energy balance.**

Total heat transferred: $Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$

This gives $1/C_h + 1/C_c = (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o} - T_{c,o} + T_{c,i})/Q = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/Q$

**Step 7 — Substitute back and solve for $Q$.**

$$
\ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -UA \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}
$$

$$
Q = UA \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

Defining the **Log Mean Temperature Difference**:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$

This yields the **master heat exchanger design equation**:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Radius of Insulation

**Given:** A cylindrical pipe of outer radius $r_1$ carrying hot fluid, covered with insulation of outer radius $r_2$ and thermal conductivity $k$. Outer surface exposed to ambient with convection coefficient $h$.

**Find:** The critical radius $r_c$ at which adding insulation *maximizes* heat loss.

**Step 1 — Total thermal resistance of the insulated cylinder (per unit length).**

$$
R_{th} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_2 L)}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat loss per unit length.**

$$
q = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{R_{th}} = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k} + \dfrac{1}{h (2\pi r_2)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $R_{th}$ with respect to $r_2$ and set $dR_{th}/dr_2 = 0$ for maximum heat loss (minimum resistance).**

$$
\frac{dR_{th}}{dr_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi k r_2} - \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_2^2)} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $r_2$.**

$$
\frac{1}{2\pi k r_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi h r_2^2}
$$

$$
r_2 = \frac{k}{h}
$$

This is the **critical radius of insulation**:

$$\boxed{r_c = \frac{k}{h}}$$

**Conclusion:** For $r_2 < r_c$, adding insulation *increases* heat loss. For $r_2 > r_c$, adding insulation *decreases* heat loss as expected. This counterintuitive result is a favorite KTU viva question.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fin Heat Transfer and Fin Efficiency

**Given:** A rectangular fin of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A_c$, perimeter $P$, and thermal conductivity $k_f$, attached to a base at temperature $T_b$, exposed to convection at $h$ in ambient $T_\infty$.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on a differential element $dx$.**

Heat in at $x$ minus heat out at $x + dx$ equals heat lost by convection from the surface:

$$
q_x - q_{x+dx} = h P \, dx \, (T - T_\infty)
$$

**Step 2 — Express $q_x$ using Fourier's law.**

$$
q_x = -k A_c \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

So:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\left( k A_c \frac{dT}{dx} \right) = h P (T - T_\infty)
$$

**Step 3 — Define excess temperature $\theta = T - T_\infty$ and assume constant $k, A_c, P$.**

$$
\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0, \quad \text{where } m^2 = \frac{h P}{k A_c}
$$

**Step 4 — General solution.**

$$
\theta(x) = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{-mx}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions for a fin with insulated tip.**

- At $x = 0$: $\theta = \theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$
- At $x = L$: $d\theta/dx = 0$ (insulated tip approximation)

This gives:

$$
\theta(x) = \theta_b \frac{\cosh[m(L - x)]}{\cosh(mL)}
$$

**Step 6 — Heat transfer rate from the fin.**

$$
q_f = -k A_c \left. \frac{d\theta}{dx} \right|_{x=0} = \sqrt{h P k A_c} \, \theta_b \, \tanh(mL)
$$

**Step 7 — Fin efficiency.**

Fin efficiency is the ratio of actual heat dissipated to the heat that would be dissipated if the entire fin were at base temperature:

$$
\eta_f = \frac{q_f}{h A_f (T_b - T_\infty)} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}
$$

This is the **fin efficiency equation** used in KTU design problems.
