## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the overall heat transfer coefficient. Mention its physical significance in the design of a composite wall.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature difference between the two fluid streams separated by a composite wall. For a composite wall, it is given by:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{L_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

**Significance:**
- (1 mark) It is a single coefficient that combines the effect of convection on both sides and conduction through the wall layers.
- (1 mark) It is used directly in the design equation $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ for sizing heat exchangers.
- (1 mark) The inverse $1/U$ represents the total thermal resistance per unit area, allowing easy analysis of composite walls using the electrical resistance analogy.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the concept of critical radius of insulation. Why does heat loss initially increase when insulation is added to small diameter pipes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The **critical radius of insulation** $r_c = k/h$ is the outer radius of insulation at which the heat loss from an insulated cylinder is maximum (i.e., the total thermal resistance is minimum).
- (1 mark) For $r_2 < r_c$, the outer surface area available for convection increases faster than the conductive resistance offered by the added insulation, causing net heat loss to **increase**.
- (1 mark) This phenomenon is significant for small diameter wires and tubes (e.g., electrical cables, $r_1 \approx 1$ mm) where $r_1 < r_c$. The minimum radius of insulation to be effective is therefore slightly greater than $r_c$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the overall heat transfer coefficient for a composite plane wall comprising three solid layers in series, with convective boundaries on both sides. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A furnace wall is constructed of a 100 mm thick fireclay brick ($k = 1.0$ W/m·K) lined on the inner side with 75 mm of insulating brick ($k = 0.21$ W/m·K). The inside and outside surface coefficients are 29 W/m²·K and 11.6 W/m²·K respectively. The inner and outer surface temperatures are 1100 K and 330 K. Calculate:
   (i) The overall heat transfer coefficient
   (ii) The heat loss per square metre of wall area
   (iii) The temperature at the interface between the two brick layers **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state, one-dimensional heat conduction
2. Constant thermal conductivities
3. Perfect thermal contact between layers (no contact resistance)
4. Constant surface coefficients $h_i$ and $h_o$
5. No heat generation within the layers

Let the layers be numbered 1, 2, 3 with thicknesses $L_1, L_2, L_3$ and conductivities $k_1, k_2, k_3$. Hot fluid at $T_{\infty,1}$, cold fluid at $T_{\infty,2}$. Surface temperatures $T_{s1}, T_{s2}, T_{s3}, T_{s4}$ in sequence.

Heat flux in steady state is the same through each resistance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= h_i (T_{\infty,1} - T_{s1}) \\
q &= \frac{k_1}{L_1} (T_{s1} - T_{s2}) \\
q &= \frac{k_2}{L_2} (T_{s2} - T_{s3}) \\
q &= \frac{k_3}{L_3} (T_{s3} - T_{s4}) \\
q &= h_o (T_{s4} - T_{\infty,2})
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding all five equations:

$$
T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2} = q \left[ \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right]
$$

Define $U$ such that $q = U (T_{\infty,1} - T_{\infty,2})$:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Fireclay: $L_1 = 0.100$ m, $k_1 = 1.0$ W/m·K
- Insulation: $L_2 = 0.075$ m, $k_2 = 0.21$ W/m·K
- $h_i = 29$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 11.6$ W/m²·K
- $T_{s1} = 1100$ K, $T_{s4} = 330$ K

**(i) Overall heat transfer coefficient:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U} &= \frac{1}{29} + \frac{0.100}{1.0} + \frac{0.075}{0.21} + \frac{1}{11.6} \\
&= 0.03448 + 0.1000 + 0.35714 + 0.08621 \\
&= 0.57783 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$U = \frac{1}{0.57783} = 1.731 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**(ii) Heat loss per m²:**

$$q = U (T_{s1} - T_{s4}) = 1.731 \times (1100 - 330) = 1.731 \times 770 = 1332.87 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**(iii) Interface temperature $T_{s2}$:**

Heat flow through the fireclay layer: $q = (k_1/L_1)(T_{s1} - T_{s2})$

$$
1332.87 = \frac{1.0}{0.100} (1100 - T_{s2})
$$

$$
1332.87 = 10 \times (1100 - T_{s2})
$$

$$
1100 - T_{s2} = 133.287
$$

$$
T_{s2} = 966.71 \text{ K}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Define the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). Derive its expression for a counter-flow heat exchanger. Mention its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel pipe of outer diameter 60 mm is covered with a 40 mm thick layer of insulation ($k = 0.05$ W/m·K). The outside surface temperature of the insulated pipe is 35°C and the ambient temperature is 20°C with convection coefficient 10 W/m²·K. Calculate:
   (i) The heat loss per metre length of pipe
   (ii) Whether the insulation is effectively reducing heat loss, given the critical radius condition **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Derivation of LMTD (7 marks):**

**Definition:** LMTD is the effective average temperature difference between the hot and cold fluid streams in a heat exchanger, accounting for the variation of $\Delta T$ along the flow path.

**Derivation:**

Consider a counter-flow heat exchanger. Let $T_h$ and $T_c$ be local temperatures. The temperature difference $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$.

Differential energy balance gives:

$$
dQ = -C_h dT_h = C_c dT_c
$$

Differentiating $\Delta T$:

$$
d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{C_h} - \frac{dQ}{C_c} = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)
$$

From local heat transfer:

$$
dQ = U \, \Delta T \, dA
$$

So:

$$
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) dA
$$

Integrating from inlet to outlet:

$$
\ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -UA \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)
$$

Using overall energy balance $Q = C_h(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$:

$$
\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} = \frac{(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) - (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})}{Q} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -UA \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}
$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$
Q = UA \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

Defining:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$

we obtain the **design equation**: $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$.

**Significance:** (1 mark) LMTD represents the true driving force for heat transfer in a heat exchanger where the temperature difference varies along the length. It allows direct use of the simple linear heat transfer equation for design.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Outer radius of pipe: $r_1 = 0.060 / 2 = 0.030$ m
- Insulation thickness: $0.040$ m
- Outer radius of insulation: $r_2 = 0.030 + 0.040 = 0.070$ m
- $k_{ins} = 0.05$ W/m·K
- $T_{s2} = 35°C = 308$ K
- $T_\infty = 20°C = 293$ K
- $h_o = 10$ W/m²·K

**(i) Heat loss per metre length:**

Resistances per metre length:

$$
R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k} = \frac{\ln(0.070/0.030)}{2\pi \times 0.05} = \frac{\ln(2.333)}{0.3142} = \frac{0.8459}{0.3142} = 2.692 \text{ m·K/W}
$$

$$
R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r_2)} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2\pi \times 0.070} = \frac{1}{4.398} = 0.2274 \text{ m·K/W}
$$

$$
R_{total} = 2.692 + 0.2274 = 2.9194 \text{ m·K/W}
$$

Heat loss per metre:

$$
q = \frac{T_{s2} - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{35 - 20}{2.9194} = \frac{15}{2.9194} = 5.138 \text{ W/m}
$$

**(ii) Critical radius check:**

$$
r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.05}{10} = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $r_2 = 70$ mm $\gg r_c = 5$ mm, the insulation is well above the critical radius, so it is **effectively reducing heat loss**. The pipe radius (30 mm) is also greater than $r_c$, so adding insulation from the start would have increased heat loss until $r_2$ exceeded $r_c = 5$ mm, after which heat loss began to decrease.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **LMTD requires $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$** — if they are equal (e.g., both fluids have the same capacity rate in parallel flow), LMTD equals $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ by L'Hôpital's rule. Students often blindly apply the formula.
> 2. **For cross-flow and multipass heat exchangers**, LMTD must be multiplied by a **correction factor F** (0 to 1). Using uncorrected LMTD leads to over-design.
> 3. **In the critical radius derivation**, students frequently confuse the *outer* radius of insulation with the *added thickness*. The critical radius $r_c$ is the *outer* radius, not the thickness.
> 4. **Overall heat transfer coefficient U** must be referred to a specific area (inner, outer, or mean). Students should always state which area is being used.
> 5. **For fin problems**, the insulated tip assumption $\eta = \tanh(mL)/mL$ is *not* valid for long fins. Use exact solution for accuracy: $\eta = \tanh(mL_c)/(mL_c)$ where $L_c = L + A_c/P$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three modes of heat transfer**: conduction, convection, radiation — identify the dominant mode first in any problem.
- **Fourier's Law** is to heat conduction what **Ohm's Law** is to electric current — both follow $Q = \Delta T / R$.
- **Thermal resistances in series add directly**: $R_{total} = \sum R_i$. In parallel: $1/R_{total} = \sum 1/R_i$.
- **Overall heat transfer coefficient** $U$ is governed by the sum of all per-unit-area resistances; the **smallest resistance dominates** the heat flow.
- **LMTD derivation** assumes constant $C_h$, $C_c$, and $U$ along the heat exchanger. It is the effective $\Delta T$ for design.
- **NTU method** is preferred when outlet temperatures are unknown; LMTD method is preferred when outlet temperatures are known.
- **Effectiveness $\varepsilon$** is the ratio of actual heat transfer to the maximum possible heat transfer ($C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$).
- **Critical radius of insulation** $r_c = k/h$ is a key concept — insulation can paradoxically *increase* heat loss for small-radius pipes.
- **Fin efficiency** $\eta_f = \tanh(mL)/mL$ applies to the **insulated tip** case; for long fins, this approaches $1/(mL)$.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ must be used in Kelvin for temperatures in radiation calculations.
- **For composite cylinders**, thermal resistance through insulation is $R = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)$ — note the natural log, not $\log_{10}$.
- **Heat exchanger correction factor F** must be applied to LMTD for all configurations except pure counter-flow.
- **Always use SI units** in KTU board exams: W, m, K, kg, s. Mixed units cause immediate mark deductions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Effectiveness: NTU Method and LMTD Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Effectiveness: NTU Method and LMTD Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Effectiveness Measurement in Heat Exchangers

The **performance of a heat exchanger** can be quantified by two principal analytical methods that allow engineers to predict thermal behavior under varying operating conditions. These are the **LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference) Method** and the **NTU (Number of Transfer Units) Method**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LMTD Method** is used when the **inlet and outlet temperatures** of both fluids are known (or specified). It yields the heat exchanger surface area.
>
> **NTU Method** is used when only the **inlet temperatures** and heat capacity rates are known. It yields the **outlet temperatures** and the **effectiveness** of the heat exchanger.

## 1.2 Definition of Heat Exchanger Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$)

The **effectiveness** ($\varepsilon$) of a heat exchanger is defined as the ratio of the **actual heat transfer rate** ($Q_{actual}$) to the **maximum possible heat transfer rate** ($Q_{max}$) that could be transferred in an infinitely long exchanger (counter-flow limit).

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}}$$

The maximum possible heat transfer is governed by the **smaller heat capacity rate** ($C_{min}$):

$$Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Water Bucket" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine two buckets** — one containing hot water and the other cold water, connected by a pipe with a valve. The valve opening represents the **heat transfer area ($A$)**, and the flow rate of water through the pipe represents the **overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$)**. The **smaller bucket** (limiting fluid) determines the *maximum* water that can be exchanged. The **effectiveness** is simply *how much water actually got transferred* compared to that theoretical maximum. The **NTU method** tells you "given a valve size, how much water flows?" while the **LMTD method** tells you "given a desired water flow, what valve size do you need?"

## 1.4 Definition of Heat Capacity Rate Ratio ($C_r$)

The ratio of the minimum to maximum heat capacity rate is a dimensionless parameter that classifies fluid behavior:

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} \leq 1$$

where $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ (mass flow rate $\times$ specific heat).

## 1.5 Definition of Number of Transfer Units (NTU)

NTU is a dimensionless measure of the heat exchanger's heat transfer capability:

$$NTU = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$$

where **$U$ = Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient** (W/m²K) and **$A$ = Heat Transfer Area** (m²).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LMTD Method — Operational Logic

The LMTD method assumes that:
1. The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ is **constant** over the entire heat exchanger.
2. The specific heats $c_p$ of both fluids are **constant**.
3. There is **no heat loss** to the surroundings.
4. There is **no phase change** in either fluid (single-phase heat transfer).

The fundamental LMTD equation:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F$$

For **pure counter-flow** and **pure parallel-flow** (no correction needed, $F = 1$):

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

The end-temperature differences are:

| Configuration | $\Delta T_1$ | $\Delta T_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel Flow** | $T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$ | $T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$ |
| **Counter Flow** | $T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ | $T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$ |

## 2.2 Correction Factor $F$ for Multi-Pass Exchangers

For shell-and-tube and cross-flow heat exchangers, the flow pattern is not purely counter-current. A **correction factor $F \leq 1$** is applied:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$$

The LMTD is computed assuming counter-flow, then multiplied by $F$ to account for the deviation from ideal counter-current behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> The Correction Factor $F$ is a function of two parameters:
> 1. **P-parameter:** $P = \dfrac{T_{h,o} - T_{h,i}}{T_{c,i} - T_{h,i}}$
> 2. **R-parameter:** $R = \dfrac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}} = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = C_r$

For a **1-shell pass, 2-tube pass** heat exchanger:

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2 + 1} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{1-P}{1-PR}\right)}{(R-1) \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{2-P\left(R+1+\sqrt{R^2+1}\right)}{2-P\left(R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1}\right)}\right)}$$

## 2.3 NTU Method — Operational Logic

When outlet temperatures are unknown, the **effectiveness-NTU ($\varepsilon$-NTU)** relationship is used. The key equations are:

$$NTU = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} \quad ; \quad C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} \quad ; \quad \varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Variables** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Capacity Rate | $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate, $c_p$ = specific heat | W/K |
| Capacity Ratio | $C_r = C_{min} \cdot C_{max}^{-1}$ | $C_{min}$, $C_{max}$ | dimensionless |
| LMTD | $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) \cdot \left[\ln(\Delta T_1 \cdot \Delta T_2^{-1})\right]^{-1}$ | $\Delta T_1$, $\Delta T_2$ | K or °C |
| Heat Transfer Rate (LMTD) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F$ | $U$, $A$, $F$ | W |
| Maximum Heat Transfer | $Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ | $T_{h,i}$, $T_{c,i}$ | W |
| Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} \cdot Q_{max}^{-1}$ | $Q_{actual}$ | dimensionless |
| NTU | $NTU = U \cdot A \cdot C_{min}^{-1}$ | $U$, $A$ | dimensionless |
| $\varepsilon$ from temps | $\varepsilon = (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})^{-1}$ | Hot fluid side | dimensionless |
| $\varepsilon$ from temps | $\varepsilon = (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})^{-1}$ | Cold fluid side | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Effectiveness Relations for Common Heat Exchanger Configurations

| **Heat Exchanger Type** | **Effectiveness Equation $\varepsilon(NTU, C_r)$** |
|---|---|
| **Counter-Flow** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}$ |
| **Counter-Flow** ($C_r = 1$) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{NTU}{1+NTU}$ |
| **Parallel-Flow** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1+C_r)]}{1+C_r}$ |
| **Shell & Tube (1-2)** | $\varepsilon = 2 \cdot \left[(1+C_r) + \sqrt{1+C_r^2} \cdot \coth\left(\dfrac{NTU\sqrt{1+C_r^2}}{2}\right) \cdot 2^{-1} \right]^{-1}$ |
| **Cross-Flow ($C_{max}$ mixed)** | $\varepsilon = C_r^{-1} \cdot \left\{1 - \exp[-C_r(1 - \exp(-NTU))]\right\}$ |
| **Cross-Flow ($C_{min}$ mixed)** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left\{-C_r^{-1} \cdot [1 - \exp(-C_r \cdot NTU)]\right\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **counter-flow** exchanger provides the **highest possible effectiveness** for given $NTU$ and $C_r$, making it the most thermally efficient configuration. The **parallel-flow** is the least effective among flow arrangements.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Field** | **Application** |
|---|---|
| Power Plants | Condensers, feedwater heaters, economizers |
| HVAC Systems | Chillers, cooling coils, air-handling units |
| Chemical Industry | Reactor cooling, reboilers, condensers |
| Refrigeration | Evaporators, condensers in vapor-compression cycles |
| Automotive | Engine oil coolers, radiators, EGR coolers |
| Process Industries | Distillation column reboilers, plate heat exchangers |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of LMTD for a Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

### Step 1: Define an Elemental Heat Transfer

Consider an elemental area $dA$ in a counter-flow heat exchanger. The heat transferred across this area is:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

where $T_h$ and $T_c$ are local hot and cold fluid temperatures at position $x$.

### Step 2: Energy Balance for Hot Fluid

As the hot fluid loses heat $dQ$, its temperature decreases by $dT_h$:

$$dQ = -C_h \cdot dT_h$$

### Step 3: Energy Balance for Cold Fluid

The cold fluid gains heat $dQ$ over the same elemental area (in counter-flow, cold fluid moves opposite to hot fluid):

$$dQ = C_c \cdot dT_c$$

### Step 4: Form the Temperature Difference Derivative

Differentiating $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ along the exchanger:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -dQ \cdot C_h^{-1} - dQ \cdot C_c^{-1}$$

$$d(\Delta T) = -dQ \cdot \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

### Step 5: Substitute $dQ$ and Separate Variables

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

Let $K = \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right)$. Then:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \cdot K \cdot dA$$

### Step 6: Integrate Across the Heat Exchanger

Integrating from the inlet (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to the outlet (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$), and from $A = 0$ to $A = A$:

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \cdot K \cdot \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U \cdot K \cdot A$$

### Step 7: Solve for $U \cdot A$ and Apply to $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$

Multiplying both sides by $-(C_h \cdot C_c) \cdot (C_h + C_c)^{-1}$ and combining with the total energy balance $Q = C_h(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot A}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness for a Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

### Step 1: Express the Energy Balance in Terms of Effectiveness

From the definitions:

$$Q = \varepsilon \cdot C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$$

For a counter-flow exchanger, the actual heat transfer can be written as:

$$Q = C_h \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c \cdot (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$$

### Step 2: Express Outlet Temperatures via Effectiveness

For the hot fluid (assumed to be $C_{min}$):

$$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \varepsilon \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$$

For the cold fluid (assumed to be $C_{max}$):

$$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \varepsilon \cdot \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = T_{c,i} + \varepsilon \cdot C_r \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$$

### Step 3: Compute the End Temperature Differences for Counter-Flow

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})(1 - \varepsilon \cdot C_r)$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})(1 - \varepsilon)$$

### Step 4: Apply the LMTD Equation

$$Q = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) \cdot [(1 - \varepsilon \cdot C_r) - (1 - \varepsilon)]}{\ln\left(\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon \cdot C_r}{1 - \varepsilon}\right)}$$

### Step 5: Simplify Using $Q = \varepsilon \cdot C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ and $NTU = U \cdot A / C_{min}$

$$\varepsilon \cdot C_{min} = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (C_r - 1)}{\ln\left(\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon C_r}{1 - \varepsilon}\right)}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{NTU \cdot (1 - C_r)}{\ln\left(\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon C_r}{1 - \varepsilon}\right)}$$

### Step 6: Solve for $\varepsilon$ Analytically

Rearranging and exponentiating:

$$\frac{1 - \varepsilon C_r}{1 - \varepsilon} = \exp[NTU(1 - C_r)]$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}}$$

For the special case $C_r = 1$, applying L'Hôpital's Rule:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: LMTD Method

> **Problem Statement:**
> A counter-flow double-pipe heat exchanger cools oil ($c_p = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K) from 150°C to 50°C at a flow rate of 2 kg/s using water ($c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) entering at 20°C with a flow rate of 1.5 kg/s. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 1000$ W/m²K. Find the **LMTD**, **heat transfer rate**, and **required heat transfer area**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute heat capacity rates**

$$C_h = \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot c_{p,oil} = 2 \times 2.0 = 4.0 \text{ kW/K} = 4000 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C_c = \dot{m}_{water} \cdot c_{p,water} = 1.5 \times 4.18 = 6.27 \text{ kW/K} = 6270 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 2: Compute heat transfer rate from oil side**

$$Q = C_h \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 4.0 \times (150 - 50) = 400 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Find water outlet temperature**

$$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{Q}{C_c} = 20 + \frac{400}{6.27} = 20 + 63.80 = 83.80°C$$

**Step 4: Compute end temperature differences (counter-flow)**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 150 - 83.80 = 66.20°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 50 - 20 = 30.00°C$$

**Step 5: Compute LMTD**

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{66.20 - 30.00}{\ln(66.20 / 30.00)} = \frac{36.20}{\ln(2.2067)} = \frac{36.20}{0.7918} = 45.72°C$$

**Step 6: Compute required heat transfer area**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{400{,}000}{1000 \times 45.72} = 8.75 \text{ m}^2$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: NTU Method

> **Problem Statement:**
> A shell-and-tube heat exchanger (1 shell pass, 2 tube passes) is to heat water ($C_c = 5000$ W/K) from 30°C to 80°C using engine oil ($C_h = 2000$ W/K) entering at 150°C. The overall $UA = 6000$ W/K. Find the **effectiveness**, **outlet temperature of oil**, and **heat transfer rate**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$, compute $C_r$**

$$C_{min} = C_h = 2000 \text{ W/K} \quad ; \quad C_{max} = C_c = 5000 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{2000}{5000} = 0.40$$

**Step 2: Compute NTU**

$$NTU = \frac{UA}{C_{min}} = \frac{6000}{2000} = 3.0$$

**Step 3: Compute $\sqrt{1 + C_r^2}$ and intermediate terms**

$$\sqrt{1 + C_r^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.16} = \sqrt{1.16} = 1.0770$$

$$NTU \cdot \sqrt{1 + C_r^2} = 3.0 \times 1.0770 = 3.231$$

$$e^{-3.231} = 0.03954$$

**Step 4: Apply the 1-2 shell-and-tube effectiveness formula**

Using $\coth(x) = \dfrac{e^x + e^{-x}}{e^x - e^{-x}}$:

$$\coth\left(\frac{3.231}{2}\right) = \coth(1.6155) = \frac{e^{1.6155} + e^{-1.6155}}{e^{1.6155} - e^{-1.6155}} = \frac{5.0300 + 0.1988}{5.0300 - 0.1988} = \frac{5.2288}{4.8312} = 1.0822$$

**Step 5: Substitute and compute $\varepsilon$**

$$(1+C_r) + \sqrt{1+C_r^2} \cdot \coth\left(\frac{NTU\sqrt{1+C_r^2}}{2}\right)$$

$$= 1.40 + 1.0770 \times 1.0822 = 1.40 + 1.1655 = 2.5655$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{2}{2.5655} = 0.7795$$

**Step 6: Compute $Q_{actual}$ and outlet temperature of oil**

$$Q_{actual} = \varepsilon \cdot C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 0.7795 \times 2000 \times (150 - 30) = 0.7795 \times 240{,}000 = 187{,}080 \text{ W}$$

$$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}} = 150 - \frac{187{,}080}{2000} = 150 - 93.54 = 56.46°C$$

**Step 7: Verification using energy balance on cold side**

$$Q = C_c \cdot (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) \Rightarrow 187{,}080 = 5000 \times (80 - 30) = 250{,}000$$

> [!WARNING]
> The given outlet water temperature is **inconsistent** with the stated $C_c$. In practice, the NTU method would be used to **predict** $T_{c,o}$, not to verify a given one. This example illustrates that the problem specification must be physically consistent.

## 3.5 Comparison: LMTD vs NTU Method

| **Aspect** | **LMTD Method** | **NTU Method** |
|---|---|---|
| **Known Inputs** | Inlet & outlet temperatures | Inlet temperatures & heat capacity rates |
| **Unknown Output** | Heat transfer area $A$ | Outlet temperatures, effectiveness |
| **Best for** | Design (sizing) | Performance (rating) |
| **Correction Factor** | Required for non-counterflow ($F$) | Not required |
| **Iterative?** | Sometimes (if $C_r$ depends on $T$) | No, direct from $\varepsilon$-NTU chart/equation |
| **Computational Difficulty** | Medium (logarithmic) | Easy to Moderate (lookup or formula) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LMTD Method — Solution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start: Given T_h,i, T_h,o, T_c,i, T_c,o, U"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Identify Flow Configuration"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2: Compute Heat Transfer Rate Q = m_dot * c_p * deltaT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3: Calculate End Temperature Differences delta_T1 and delta_T2"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 4: Compute LMTD using logarithmic formula"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"Step 5: Is flow pure counterflow?"}
    nodeF -- "Yes" --> nodeG["Use LMTD directly with F = 1"]
    nodeF -- "No: Shell-Tube or Cross-Flow" --> nodeH["Step 6: Compute P-parameter and R-parameter"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Step 7: Read Correction Factor F from chart or formula"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Step 8: Apply Q = U * A * F * deltaT_lm, solve for A"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["End: Report Heat Transfer Area A"]
```

## 4.2 NTU Method — Solution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Start: Given T_h,i, T_c,i, C_h, C_c, UA"] --> step2["Step 1: Compute Capacity Ratio C_r = C_min / C_max"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 2: Compute NTU = UA / C_min"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 3: Identify Heat Exchanger Configuration Type"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 4: Use Correct epsilon-NTU Formula or Chart"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 5: Compute Effectiveness epsilon"]
    step6 --> step7["Step 6: Compute Q_actual = epsilon * C_min * deltaT_inlet"]
    step7 --> step8["Step 7: Compute Outlet Temperatures using energy balance"]
    step8 --> step9["End: Report T_h,o, T_c,o, and Q"]
```

## 4.3 Heat Exchanger Configuration Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Heat Exchanger Flow Arrangement"] --> branch1["Parallel Flow"]
    rootA --> branch2["Counter Flow"]
    rootA --> branch3["Cross Flow"]
    rootA --> branch4["Shell and Tube Multi-Pass"]
    branch3 --> subX["Both Fluids Unmixed"]
    branch3 --> subY["One Fluid Mixed: C_max mixed"]
    branch3 --> subZ["One Fluid Mixed: C_min mixed"]
    branch4 --> subM["1-2 Arrangement"]
    branch4 --> subN["2-4 Arrangement"]
    subM --> note1["epsilon = 2 divided by 1 + C_r + sqrt 1 + C_r squared times coth NTU sqrt 1 + C_r squared by 2"]
    subY --> note2["epsilon = 1 by C_r times bracket 1 - exp -C_r times 1 - exp -NTU"]
    subZ --> note3["epsilon = 1 - exp -1 by C_r times bracket 1 - exp -C_r NTU"]
```

## 4.4 Effectiveness vs NTU Conceptual Behavior (Textual Chart)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pointA["Low NTU, Low Cr: epsilon increases steeply"] --> pointB["Mid NTU, Mid Cr: epsilon grows more slowly"]
    pointB --> pointC["High NTU: epsilon approaches 1 asymptotically"]
    pointD["Counter-Flow: Highest epsilon curve"] --> pointE["Shell-Tube 1-2: Slightly lower than counter"]
    pointE --> pointF["Cross-Flow: Between parallel and counter"]
    pointF --> pointG["Parallel-Flow: Lowest epsilon curve"]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Heat Exchanger Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputBlock["Input Data Block: Temperatures, Flow rates, cp, U, A"] --> computeBlock["Computation Engine: C, Cr, NTU, LMTD"]
    computeBlock --> methodSwitch{"Analysis Method Selector"}
    methodSwitch -- "Rating Problem" --> ntuPath["NTU Path: epsilon from chart or formula, solve for outlet temps"]
    methodSwitch -- "Sizing Problem" --> lmtdPath["LMTD Path: solve for Area A using Q = UA * deltaT_lm * F"]
    ntuPath --> outputBlock["Output: Outlet Temperatures and Q"]
    lmtdPath --> outputBlock
    outputBlock --> verifyBlock["Verification: Energy balance check on both fluid streams"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition of Heat Exchanger Effectiveness `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The effectiveness ($\varepsilon$) of a heat exchanger is defined as the ratio of the actual heat transfer rate to the maximum possible heat transfer rate that could be achieved in a counter-flow heat exchanger of infinite length.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from 0 (no heat transfer) to 1 (theoretically perfect heat transfer). The effectiveness quantifies how closely the heat exchanger approaches its thermodynamic ideal.

### Question 2: Difference Between LMTD and NTU Methods `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **LMTD Method** | **NTU Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Used to find heat transfer **area** | Used to find **outlet temperatures** |
| Required Input | Inlet and outlet temperatures of both fluids | Inlet temperatures and heat capacity rates |
| Primary Equation | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | $\varepsilon = f(NTU, C_r)$ |
| Correction Factor | Required for multi-pass exchangers | Not required |

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

### Question A:
**(a)** Derive the expression for the LMTD of a counter-flow heat exchanger, clearly stating the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a counter-flow heat exchanger, oil ($c_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) is cooled from 200°C to 80°C using water ($c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) flowing at 1.2 kg/s. The water enters at 25°C. If $U = 1500$ W/m²K, determine the **length** of the exchanger if the pipe has an inner diameter of 20 mm. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Derivation**

The derivation steps are as listed in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps:
1. Energy balance on elemental area: $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$
2. Temperature gradient: $d(\Delta T) = -dQ(C_h^{-1} + C_c^{-1})$
3. Separation of variables and integration
4. Final result: $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$

**Assumptions:**
- $U$ is constant throughout the exchanger
- Specific heats $c_p$ are constant
- No heat loss to surroundings
- No phase change
- No axial conduction
- Steady-state operation

**Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Heat capacity rates**

$$C_h = \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot c_{p,oil} = \dot{m}_{oil} \times 2.1 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

$$Q = C_h \cdot (200 - 80) = C_h \times 120 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Energy balance on water side**

$$Q = C_c \cdot (T_{c,o} - 25) = 1.2 \times 4.18 \times (T_{c,o} - 25) = 5.016 \times (T_{c,o} - 25) \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Equating and solving**

We need another constraint. Assume $\dot{m}_{oil} = 0.5$ kg/s (typical for such problems):

$$C_h = 0.5 \times 2.1 = 1.05 \text{ kW/K}$$

$$Q = 1.05 \times 120 = 126 \text{ kW} = 126{,}000 \text{ W}$$

$$T_{c,o} = 25 + \frac{126}{5.016} = 25 + 25.12 = 50.12°C$$

**Step 4: End temperature differences**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 200 - 50.12 = 149.88°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 80 - 25 = 55.00°C$$

**Step 5: LMTD**

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{149.88 - 55.00}{\ln(149.88/55.00)} = \frac{94.88}{\ln(2.7251)} = \frac{94.88}{1.0025} = 94.65°C$$

**Step 6: Heat transfer area**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{126{,}000}{1500 \times 94.65} = 0.8876 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 7: Length of pipe**

$$L = \frac{A}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{0.8876}{\pi \times 0.020} = 14.13 \text{ m}$$

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

### Question B (Alternative Choice):
**(a)** Define NTU and effectiveness. Derive the effectiveness expression for a counter-flow heat exchanger. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hot oil ($C_h = 1500$ W/K) enters a 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger at 130°C and is to be cooled by water ($C_c = 3000$ W/K) entering at 25°C. The overall $UA = 4000$ W/K. Determine: (i) the effectiveness, (ii) the heat transfer rate, and (iii) the outlet temperature of both fluids. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Derivation of Counter-Flow Effectiveness**

The complete derivation is given in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The key steps involve:
1. Writing the energy balance in terms of effectiveness
2. Expressing outlet temperatures
3. Computing the end temperature differences
4. Equating LMTD and effectiveness expressions
5. Solving for $\varepsilon$ analytically

**Final Result:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}$$

**Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Capacity ratio and NTU**

$$C_{min} = C_h = 1500 \text{ W/K} \quad ; \quad C_{max} = C_c = 3000 \text{ W/K}$$

$$C_r = \frac{1500}{3000} = 0.50 \quad ; \quad NTU = \frac{4000}{1500} = 2.667$$

**Step 2: For 1-2 shell-and-tube configuration**

$$\sqrt{1 + C_r^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.25} = \sqrt{1.25} = 1.1180$$

$$NTU \cdot \sqrt{1 + C_r^2} = 2.667 \times 1.1180 = 2.982$$

$$\coth(1.491) = \frac{e^{1.491} + e^{-1.491}}{e^{1.491} - e^{-1.491}} = \frac{4.442 + 0.2251}{4.442 - 0.2251} = \frac{4.667}{4.217} = 1.1067$$

**Step 3: Effectiveness**

$$(1+C_r) + \sqrt{1+C_r^2} \cdot \coth\left(\frac{NTU\sqrt{1+C_r^2}}{2}\right) = 1.50 + 1.1180 \times 1.1067 = 1.50 + 1.2373 = 2.7373$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{2}{2.7373} = 0.7306$$

**Step 4: Heat transfer rate**

$$Q = \varepsilon \cdot C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 0.7306 \times 1500 \times (130 - 25) = 0.7306 \times 157{,}500 = 115{,}070 \text{ W} \approx 115.07 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Outlet temperatures**

$$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \frac{Q}{C_{min}} = 130 - \frac{115{,}070}{1500} = 130 - 76.71 = 53.29°C$$

$$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{Q}{C_{max}} = 25 + \frac{115{,}070}{3000} = 25 + 38.36 = 63.36°C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the correction factor $F$** in LMTD method for shell-and-tube or cross-flow exchangers will result in **incorrect area calculations** (commonly 2-3 marks deduction).
> 2. **Confusing $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$**: Always identify the minimum heat capacity rate correctly. Using $C_r = C_{max}/C_{min}$ (which is > 1) will give a wrong answer.
> 3. **Not specifying the flow arrangement** in NTU method: The effectiveness formula is **configuration-specific**. Using a counter-flow formula for a parallel-flow problem loses 3-4 marks.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency**: $U$ in W/m²K and area in m² give $Q$ in W. Mixing kW and W is a common error.
> 5. **Forgetting to check $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ case**: When temperatures cross (in parallel flow) or are nearly equal, $\Delta T_{lm}$ requires special handling to avoid $\ln(1) = 0$ division.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist — Measurement of Effectiveness**

- **LMTD Method** → Use when **both inlet AND outlet temperatures** are known → Find **Area $A$**.
- **NTU Method** → Use when **only inlet temperatures** and heat capacity rates are known → Find **outlet temperatures and effectiveness $\varepsilon$**.
- **Effectiveness** = $Q_{actual} / Q_{max}$ is **dimensionless**, ranging from 0 to 1.
- **$Q_{max}$** = $C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ is the thermodynamic limit of an infinitely long counter-flow exchanger.
- **NTU** = $UA/C_{min}$ measures heat exchanger **thermal size**.
- **Capacity Ratio** $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max} \leq 1$ is a **measure of stream imbalance**.
- **Counter-flow** gives **highest $\varepsilon$** for given $NTU$ and $C_r$.
- **Parallel-flow** always has $\varepsilon < 0.5$ (cannot exceed 50% effectiveness at infinite $NTU$).
- **Correction Factor $F$** must be applied for **shell-and-tube** and **cross-flow** configurations in the LMTD method.
- For $\varepsilon$-NTU relationship: **as $NTU \to \infty$, $\varepsilon \to 1$** for all configurations.
- The **LMTD** is the **logarithmic mean** of the two end-temperature differences; when they are equal, $\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$.
- **$C_{min}$** is on the **hot side** if $C_h < C_c$, else on the **cold side**.
- For **1-2 shell-and-tube** exchangers, the $\coth$ function appears in the effectiveness formula.
- **Cross-flow with $C_{max}$ mixed** has higher $\varepsilon$ than cross-flow with $C_{min}$ mixed.
- **Effectiveness is symmetric** — it can be computed from either the hot fluid side or the cold fluid side; both yield the same value.
- **$P$ and $R$ parameters** (used in $F$ factor charts) are $P = (T_{h,o} - T_{h,i})/(T_{c,i} - T_{h,i})$ and $R = C_{min}/C_{max} = C_r$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fouling and Maintenance of Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET521 - APPLIED THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fouling and Maintenance of Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fouling** in a heat exchanger is defined as the progressive accumulation of undesirable material (solid, semi-solid, or biological deposits) on the heat transfer surfaces, which results in an additional resistance to heat flow and degrades the thermal-hydraulic performance of the equipment over its operating life.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEPET521 - Module 4)**, fouling is classified as an *operational degradation phenomenon* and is quantified using the **Fouling Factor ($R_f$)** or **Fouling Resistance**, with units of **m²·K/W**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Reference)**: Fouling is the ratio of the temperature drop across the deposit layer to the heat flux through the surface, mathematically expressed as $R_f = \Delta T_{deposit} / q$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen chimney (exhaust hood) at home**. Over a few months, a greasy, oily layer builds up on the inner walls. Initially, the chimney exhausts smoke perfectly. After six months, the smoke escapes slowly because the grease layer acts as an *insulator* blocking the heat. You must scrub it clean to restore performance.

A heat exchanger behaves identically:
- **Smoke (Heat)** = The thermal energy being transferred.
- **Grease Layer (Fouling)** = The unwanted deposit on the tubes/plates.
- **Chimney Walls (Heat Transfer Surface)** = The tube walls of the heat exchanger.

Just like grease **insulates** the chimney, fouling **insulates** the heat transfer surface, making the exchanger work harder to achieve the same heating/cooling duty.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Fouling Resistance ($R_f$)**: **0.0001 to 0.002 m²·K/W** (typical industrial range).
- **Fouling Factor (U-degradation)**: A drop in $U$ of **10% to 50%** is considered economically significant.
- **Critical Thickness ($\delta_f$)**: The deposit layer thickness beyond which cleaning is mandatory, usually **0.5 mm to 2 mm**.
- **Heat Conductivity of Typical Deposits ($k_f$)**: **0.1 to 2.0 W/(m·K)** (very low compared to steel tube $k \approx 50$ W/(m·K)).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $k_f$ so low?** Fouling deposits are porous, loosely packed, and often contain water, oil, or biological matter — all of which are inherently poor thermal conductors compared to metallic surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

### Step 1: Identifying the Origin of Fouling
Fouling does not appear spontaneously. It is the cumulative result of one or more physical, chemical, or biological processes occurring inside the heat exchanger. The hot and cold fluids carry dissolved/particulate matter that deposits when:
- **Temperature gradients** are high.
- **Flow velocities** are low (boundary layer stagnation).
- **Surface roughness** of tubes is high.
- **Chemical reactivity** between fluid and wall material is present.

### Step 2: The "Why" — How Fouling Degrades Performance
- Adds an **extra thermal resistance** in series with the wall.
- Reduces the **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)** since $U \propto 1/\Sigma R$.
- Increases the **required surface area** for a given heat duty, or reduces the **heat duty** for a fixed area.
- Increases **pumping power** due to flow area reduction and surface roughness.

### Step 3: The "How" — Mathematical Quantification
Fouling is treated as a *resistance in series* added to the convective and conductive resistances in the **Fourier's Law** network.

## 2.2 Types of Fouling (KTU 2024 Classification)

1. **Particulate Fouling** — Accumulation of suspended solid particles (dust, soot, scale fragments) on the heat transfer surface.
2. **Crystallization / Scaling Fouling** — Precipitation of dissolved salts (e.g., $CaCO_3$, $CaSO_4$) when solubility decreases with temperature. Common in **boilers and cooling towers**.
3. **Chemical Reaction Fouling** — Deposits formed by chemical reactions at the wall (e.g., polymerization, coking in petroleum refining).
4. **Corrosion Fouling** — Corrosion products (iron oxides, $Fe_2O_3$) deposited on the surface.
5. **Biological Fouling (Biofouling)** — Growth of microorganisms, algae, or biofilms. Critical in **seawater-cooled exchangers**.
6. **Freezing Fouling** — Solidification of a fluid component on a sub-cooled surface.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R_f = \dfrac{\delta_f}{k_f}$ | Fouling resistance (Fourier's Law applied to deposit) | m²·K/W |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | **Dirty** overall heat transfer coefficient (with fouling) | W/(m²·K) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{U_c} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | **Clean** overall heat transfer coefficient (no fouling) | W/(m²·K) |
| 4 | $R_{f,total} = R_{f,i} + R_{f,o} = \dfrac{1}{U_d} - \dfrac{1}{U_c}$ | Total fouling resistance from $U$ drop | m²·K/W |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | Area required (note that $U$ drops as fouling grows) | m² |
| 6 | $\% \text{ Penalty} = \dfrac{U_c - U_d}{U_c} \times 100$ | Percentage degradation in $U$ due to fouling | % |
| 7 | $\text{Cleaning Frequency} = \dfrac{t_{op}}{\delta_{crit}}$ | Maintenance scheduling metric | hours/cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Board**: Always use the **subscript 'd' for dirty** and **subscript 'c' for clean** in your equations. Examiners specifically look for this notation in derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants**: Scaling in condenser tubes reduces turbine back-pressure, lowering cycle efficiency by 2–5%.
- **Petrochemical Refineries**: Coking in furnace tubes can cause tube rupture — a safety hazard.
- **HVAC & Refrigeration**: Biofouling in chilled water systems increases chiller power consumption by **up to 30%**.
- **Marine Engineering**: Biofouling in seawater coolers increases fuel consumption in ships.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient with Fouling Resistance

### Step 1 — Start with the Basic Heat Transfer Equation

For a cylindrical tube heat exchanger, the steady-state heat transfer through concentric cylindrical layers is governed by Fourier's Law in series.

$$q = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{\sum R_{thermal}}$$

### Step 2 — Identify All Thermal Resistances in Series

The total thermal resistance from the inner fluid to the outer fluid consists of:
- Inner convective resistance: $\dfrac{1}{h_i A_i}$
- Inner fouling resistance: $\dfrac{R_{f,i}}{A_i}$
- Conductive resistance of the tube wall: $\dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k L}$
- Outer fouling resistance: $\dfrac{R_{f,o}}{A_o}$
- Outer convective resistance: $\dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$

### Step 3 — Write the Total Resistance

For a tube of length $L$, area ratio $A_o / A_i = D_o / D_i$ :

$$\sum R = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{R_{f,i}}{A_i} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k L} + \frac{R_{f,o}}{A_o} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Area-Based Form

Dividing the entire resistance by the **outer area $A_o$** to express everything per unit outer surface area:

$$\frac{\sum R}{A_o} = \frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{h_i (D_i/D_o)} + \frac{R_{f,i}}{(D_i/D_o)} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k L \cdot A_o / A_o} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

### Step 5 — Final Clean Form (KTU Standard Result)

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{h_i} \cdot \frac{D_i}{D_o} + R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{D_i}{D_o} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$

**Logic Used**: Each resistance is divided by the local area. Since fouling is a *surface phenomenon*, the fouling resistance must be referenced to the area on which the deposit lies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Heat Transfer Area with and without Fouling

### Step 1 — Clean Condition

The heat transfer rate for a clean exchanger using LMTD is:

$$Q = U_c A_c \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

Solving for the clean area:

$$A_c = \frac{Q}{U_c \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

### Step 2 — Dirty Condition

When fouling develops, $U$ drops to $U_d$. If the heat exchanger is to deliver the **same heat duty $Q$**, it would need a larger area:

$$A_d = \frac{Q}{U_d \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

### Step 3 — Ratio of Areas (Excess Area / Fouling Allowance)

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{U_c}{U_d}$$

This ratio is called the **Overdesign Ratio** or **Fouling Allowance Factor**.

### Step 4 — Surface Area Penalty

$$A_{excess} = A_d - A_c = \frac{Q}{\Delta T_{LMTD}} \left( \frac{1}{U_d} - \frac{1}{U_c} \right) = \frac{Q \cdot R_{f,total}}{\Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

**Logic Used**: The fouling resistance $R_{f,total}$ added to the denominator forces the area to grow proportionally.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Heat Duty Drop for a Fixed-Area Exchanger

If the area $A$ is fixed (no overdesign) and fouling occurs, the **delivered heat duty** drops.

### Step 1 — Initial Clean State

$$Q_c = U_c A \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

### Step 2 — Fouled State

$$Q_d = U_d A \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

### Step 3 — Heat Duty Loss

$$Q_{loss} = Q_c - Q_d = (U_c - U_d) A \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

### Step 4 — Percentage Heat Loss

$$\boxed{\% Q_{loss} = \left( 1 - \frac{U_d}{U_c} \right) \times 100 = \frac{R_{f,total}}{\frac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,total}} \times 100}$$

**Logic Used**: As $U$ drops, the heat flux falls in direct proportion because $Q \propto U$ for constant $A$ and $\Delta T_{LMTD}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Fouling Resistance Values (TEMA Standards)

| Fluid Type | $R_f$ (m²·K/W) | Typical Application |
|------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Distilled water | 0.0001 | Pure water systems |
| Seawater (below 50°C) | 0.0002 – 0.0004 | Marine condensers |
| Cooling tower water | 0.0002 – 0.0006 | HVAC chillers |
| Industrial cooling water | 0.0005 – 0.0010 | Process plants |
| Crude oil | 0.0003 – 0.0010 | Refinery preheaters |
| Heavy fuel oil | 0.0010 – 0.0020 | Fired heaters |
| Vegetable oil | 0.0005 – 0.0015 | Food processing |
| Exhaust gas | 0.0020 – 0.0050 | Waste heat recovery |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Classification of Fouling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FOULING IN HEAT EXCHANGERS] --> B[Particulate Fouling]
    A --> C[Crystallization Scaling]
    A --> D[Chemical Reaction Fouling]
    A --> E[Corrosion Fouling]
    A --> F[Biological Fouling]
    A --> G[Freezing Fouling]

    B --> B1[Suspended solids settle on walls]
    C --> C1[Dissolved salts precipitate at high T]
    D --> D1[Polymerization and coking on hot walls]
    E --> E1[Oxide layer formation on metal]
    F --> F1[Microbial colonies and biofilm growth]
    G --> G1[Congealing of high pour point fluids]

    C1 --> C2[Example: CaCO3 in hard water]
    F1 --> F2[Example: Algae in seawater]
    D1 --> D2[Example: Coke in refinery furnaces]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Maintenance Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[HEAT EXCHANGER MAINTENANCE] --> N[Preventive Maintenance]
    M --> O[Predictive Maintenance]
    M --> P[Corrective Maintenance]

    N --> N1[Scheduled Cleaning]
    N --> N2[Chemical Dosing]
    N --> N3[Filter Replacement]

    O --> O1[Performance Monitoring]
    O --> O2[Online U Calculation]
    O --> O3[Thermography Inspection]

    P --> P1[On-line Cleaning]
    P --> P2[Mechanical Cleaning]
    P --> P3[Chemical Cleaning]

    N1 --> N1a[Frequency based on TEMA tables]
    O2 --> O2a[Compare U_d with U_design]
    P2 --> P2a[Hydroblasting and rodding]
    P3 --> P3a[Acid pickling CIP]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heat Transfer Resistance Network with Fouling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Inner Hot Fluid T_hi] --> R1[Convective 1/h_i]
    R1 --> Rf1[Fouling R_f_i]
    Rf1 --> Rw[Wall Conduction ln Do/Di / 2 pi k L]
    Rw --> Rf2[Fouling R_f_o]
    Rf2 --> R2[Convective 1/h_o]
    R2 --> T2[Outer Cold Fluid T_ho]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read the Resistance Diagram**: Heat must pass through *five* resistances in series. The two fouling resistances ($R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$) are inserted between the convective and conductive layers, representing the deposit layer on the inner and outer tube surfaces respectively.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define fouling factor. State its typical values for seawater and crude oil.**

**Model Answer:**

The **fouling factor** ($R_f$), also called **fouling resistance**, is defined as the ratio of the temperature drop across the deposit layer to the heat flux through the surface.

$$R_f = \frac{\delta_f}{k_f} = \frac{\Delta T_{deposit}}{q}$$

Its units are **m²·K/W**.

**Typical Values (TEMA Standard):**
- Seawater (below 50°C): $R_f = 0.0002$ to $0.0004$ m²·K/W.
- Crude oil: $R_f = 0.0003$ to $0.0010$ m²·K/W.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six types of fouling observed in industrial heat exchangers.**

**Model Answer:**

The six types of fouling are:
1. **Particulate Fouling** — Deposition of suspended solids.
2. **Crystallization / Scaling Fouling** — Precipitation of dissolved salts.
3. **Chemical Reaction Fouling** — Polymerization, coking.
4. **Corrosion Fouling** — Oxide layer buildup.
5. **Biological Fouling (Biofouling)** — Microbial growth.
6. **Freezing Fouling** — Solidification of components.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the overall heat transfer coefficient of a **dirty** heat exchanger, including the fouling resistances on both sides. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger handles crude oil and cooling water. The clean overall heat transfer coefficient is $U_c = 850$ W/(m²·K). After 6 months of operation, the measured $U_d$ drops to **620 W/(m²·K)**. Calculate:
- (i) The total fouling resistance $R_{f,total}$.
- (ii) The percentage drop in $U$.
- (iii) The percentage heat duty loss if the area is constant.
- (iv) The required percentage increase in area to restore the original duty. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1**: The total thermal resistance network is the **series sum** of:
- Inner convective resistance.
- Inner fouling resistance.
- Tube wall conductive resistance.
- Outer fouling resistance.
- Outer convective resistance.

**Step 2**: Writing them per unit outer area and summing:

$$\frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{h_i} \cdot \frac{D_i}{D_o} + R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{D_i}{D_o} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

**Step 3**: The clean version omits $R_{f,i}$ and $R_{f,o}$:

$$\frac{1}{U_c} = \frac{1}{h_i} \cdot \frac{D_i}{D_o} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$

**Step 4**: Subtracting the two equations:

$$\boxed{R_{f,total} = R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{D_i}{D_o} + R_{f,o} = \frac{1}{U_d} - \frac{1}{U_c}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $U_c = 850$ W/(m²·K), $U_d = 620$ W/(m²·K)

**(i) Total Fouling Resistance:**

$$R_{f,total} = \frac{1}{U_d} - \frac{1}{U_c} = \frac{1}{620} - \frac{1}{850}$$

$$R_{f,total} = 0.001613 - 0.001176 = 0.000437 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**(ii) Percentage Drop in $U$:**

$$\% \text{ drop} = \frac{850 - 620}{850} \times 100 = 27.06\%$$

**(iii) Percentage Heat Duty Loss (Constant Area):**

$$\% Q_{loss} = \left(1 - \frac{U_d}{U_c}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{620}{850}\right) \times 100 = 27.06\%$$

**(iv) Required Percentage Area Increase:**

$$\frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{U_c}{U_d} = \frac{850}{620} = 1.371$$

$$\% \text{ area increase} = (1.371 - 1) \times 100 = 37.10\%$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the various **types of fouling** with one industrial example for each. Discuss the **preventive and corrective maintenance** strategies for heat exchangers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A condenser has a clean overall heat transfer coefficient of $U_c = 1200$ W/(m²·K) and operates with seawater whose design fouling resistance is $R_f = 0.0003$ m²·K/W. Calculate:
- (i) The dirty overall heat transfer coefficient $U_d$.
- (ii) The percentage drop in $U$ due to fouling.
- (iii) The percentage overdesign area required.
- (iv) If the heat duty to be transferred is $Q = 5 \times 10^6$ W and $\Delta T_{LMTD} = 25$ K, find the **dirty area** $A_d$ required. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Types of Fouling** (See Section 2.2) — Discuss any 5 types with one example each (e.g., Scaling — $CaCO_3$ in cooling water; Biofouling — Algae in seawater; Coking — Cracking furnaces in refineries).

**Maintenance Strategies:**

| Strategy | Activity | Frequency |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| Preventive | Scheduled cleaning, chemical dosing, tube brushing | Quarterly |
| Predictive | $U$ monitoring, IR thermography, pressure drop trending | Continuous |
| Corrective | Hydroblasting, acid cleaning (CIP), tube replacement | As needed |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $U_c = 1200$ W/(m²·K), $R_f = 0.0003$ m²·K/W

**(i) Dirty Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$\frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{U_c} + R_f = \frac{1}{1200} + 0.0003$$

$$\frac{1}{U_d} = 0.000833 + 0.000300 = 0.001133 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$U_d = \frac{1}{0.001133} = 882.6 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

**(ii) Percentage Drop in $U$:**

$$\% \text{ drop} = \frac{1200 - 882.6}{1200} \times 100 = 26.45\%$$

**(iii) Percentage Overdesign Area:**

$$\frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{U_c}{U_d} = \frac{1200}{882.6} = 1.360$$

$$\% \text{ overdesign} = 36.0\%$$

**(iv) Dirty Area Required:**

$$A_d = \frac{Q}{U_d \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{882.6 \times 25}$$

$$A_d = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{22065} = 226.6 \text{ m}^2$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Subscript Confusion**: Writing $R_f$ in the denominator of $U_d$ directly *without* the area correction factor $D_i/D_o$ for cylindrical tubes — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Unit Error**: Forgetting that $R_f$ is in m²·K/W and not K/W. Always add the area units.
> 3. **Formula Misuse**: Using $R_f$ as the *reciprocal* of $U$ instead of the *difference* of reciprocals: $R_f = 1/U_d - 1/U_c$.
> 4. **LMTD Sign Convention**: Not maintaining temperature difference as positive magnitude in heat duty calculations.
> 5. **TEMA Values**: Memorize at least 4 typical $R_f$ values for common fluids (water, oil, gas). Examiners love these one-liners.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Fouling** is the accumulation of unwanted material on heat transfer surfaces causing additional thermal resistance.
- **Fouling Factor / Resistance** $R_f = \delta_f / k_f$, units **m²·K/W**.
- The **dirty overall heat transfer coefficient** is *always less* than the clean one: $U_d < U_c$.
- Key relation: $\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,total}$.
- **Six types of fouling**: Particulate, Crystallization, Chemical Reaction, Corrosion, Biological, Freezing.
- **TEMA Standard values** of $R_f$ are the most commonly tested numerical data.
- **Fouling Allowance**: Extra area built into the design to compensate for $U$ drop — usually 15% to 40%.
- **Percentage $U$ drop** = Percentage heat duty drop *for constant area* — a frequently asked concept.
- **Maintenance types**: Preventive (scheduled), Predictive (condition-based), Corrective (breakdown-based).
- **Cleaning methods**: Mechanical (rodding, hydroblasting), Chemical (acid CIP), Thermal (steam sparging).
- Always use the **dirty area $A_d$** in design calculations to ensure the exchanger meets duty *over its entire life*, not just when clean.
- **Critical Fouling Thickness** is the operational limit; cleaning must be performed before this is reached to prevent tube overheating and failure.
